s: The TPMS receiver reports that the internal battery of sensor 4 is at end of life. The sensor must be replaced as the battery is sealed inside the housing.

t: TPMS Receiver Circuit Malfunction
s: The body or chassis module has detected a fault in the TPMS RF receiver — for example open antenna or internal failure. No tire pressure data can be received.

t: TPMS Initiator Front Circuit Malfunction
s: The TPMS module has detected an electrical fault in the front low-frequency initiator (used to wake and locate sensors). Position learning of the front sensors fails.

t: Active Roll Stabilisation Front Actuator Circuit
s: The front active anti-roll bar actuator reports an electrical fault.

t: Active Roll Stabilisation Rear Actuator Circuit
s: The rear active anti-roll bar actuator reports an electrical fault.

t: Suspension Control Module Internal Fault
s: The suspension control module fails its internal self-test. Adaptive damping or air suspension is disabled.

t: Tire Pressure Monitoring Sensor Front Left
s: The TPMS sensor at the front left wheel does not transmit a valid signal. Indirect TPMS variants raise this when the wheel-speed-based estimation fails for that wheel.

t: Tire Pressure Monitoring Sensor Front Right
s: The TPMS sensor at the front right wheel does not transmit a valid signal.

t: Tire Pressure Monitoring Sensor Rear Left
s: The TPMS sensor at the rear left wheel does not transmit a valid signal.

t: Tire Pressure Monitoring Sensor Rear Right
s: The TPMS sensor at the rear right wheel does not transmit a valid signal.

t: Tire Pressure Monitoring Module Internal Fault
s: The tire pressure monitoring module fails its internal self-test.

t: Fuel Volume Regulator A Control Circuit/Open
s: The ECU detects an open circuit in the control of fuel volume regulator A (often the metering valve on the high-pressure fuel pump). The actuator does not respond, so rail pressure cannot be controlled accurately.

t: Fuel Volume Regulator A Control Circuit Range/Performance
s: The control signal of fuel volume regulator A is electrically present but the actual rail pressure does not follow the target value within tolerance. Indicates a mechanical or hydraulic issue rather than pure wiring.

t: Fuel Volume Regulator A Control Circuit Low
s: The ECU sees the control voltage of fuel volume regulator A pulled below the expected range, typically a short to ground or a low-resistance fault in the wiring or coil.

t: Fuel Volume Regulator A Control Circuit High
s: The ECU sees the control voltage of fuel volume regulator A above the expected range, typically a short to power or open ground return.

t: Fuel Shutoff Valve A Control Circuit/Open
s: Open circuit detected at the fuel shutoff valve A solenoid (used on diesels to stop fuel flow at engine-off and as anti-theft). Engine may not start or not stop cleanly.

t: Fuel Shutoff Valve A Control Circuit Low
s: Control voltage of fuel shutoff valve A is below the expected range, typically caused by a short to ground or low-resistance fault in the solenoid circuit.

t: Fuel Shutoff Valve A Control Circuit High
s: Control voltage of fuel shutoff valve A is above the expected range, typically caused by a short to power or open ground return.

t: Engine Position System Performance Bank 1
s: The ECU detects an implausible relationship between crankshaft and camshaft signals on bank 1, indicating timing chain stretch, jumped tooth, or sensor reluctor wear.

t: Engine Position System Performance Bank 2
s: Same fault pattern as P0008 but on bank 2 of a V or boxer engine. Crank/cam correlation outside spec on the second cylinder bank.

t: A Camshaft Position Slow Response Bank 1
s: The intake camshaft phaser on bank 1 responds too slowly to commanded position changes. Often caused by VVT solenoid sticking, oil aging, or low oil pressure.

t: B Camshaft Position Slow Response Bank 1
s: The exhaust camshaft phaser on bank 1 responds too slowly to commanded position changes. Same root causes as P000A but on the exhaust cam.

t: A Camshaft Position Slow Response Bank 2
s: Intake camshaft phaser on bank 2 responds too slowly. Same fault pattern as P000A on the second cylinder bank.

t: B Camshaft Position Slow Response Bank 2
s: Exhaust camshaft phaser on bank 2 responds too slowly. Same fault pattern as P000B on the second cylinder bank.

t: Fuel Volume Regulator Control Exceeded Learning Limit
s: The ECU's adaptive correction of fuel volume regulator A has reached its learning limit. Indicates a long-term drift between commanded and actual rail pressure that could no longer be compensated.

t: Fuel System Over Pressure Relief Valve Activated
s: The mechanical pressure relief valve on the fuel rail has opened due to overpressure. Indicates a malfunction of the rail pressure regulation that drove pressure beyond the safe limit.

t: "A" Camshaft Position Actuator Circuit (Bank 1)
s: An electrical fault in the bank 1 intake camshaft position actuator solenoid (variable valve timing). The ECU monitors current and feedback on the oil control valve and sets this code when the circuit is open, shorted, or out of range.

t: "A" Camshaft Position - Timing Over-Advanced or System Performance (Bank 1)
s: The bank 1 intake camshaft is more advanced than commanded, or the actuator does not respond as expected. Often caused by stuck-open oil control valves, sludge in VVT passages, or low oil pressure.

t: "A" Camshaft Position - Timing Over-Retarded (Bank 1)
s: The bank 1 intake camshaft remains retarded compared to commanded position. Common causes include a stuck-closed oil control valve, blocked oil passages, or a worn cam phaser.

t: "B" Camshaft Position Actuator Circuit (Bank 1)
s: Electrical fault in the bank 1 exhaust camshaft position actuator solenoid. Same diagnostic principle as P0010 but on the exhaust cam.

t: "B" Camshaft Position - Timing Over-Advanced (Bank 1)
s: Exhaust camshaft position bank 1 over-advanced or system performance — the actual cam phase exceeds the commanded value. Usually caused by a stuck-open oil control valve, low oil pressure, sludged passages, or a worn cam phaser. Confirm with a scan tool live data: command 0° advance and watch actual.

t: "B" Camshaft Position - Timing Over-Retarded (Bank 1)
s: The bank 1 exhaust camshaft remains retarded relative to commanded position. Same root causes as P0012 but on the exhaust cam.

t: Crankshaft Position - Camshaft Position Correlation (Bank 1 Sensor A)
s: The relative timing between the crankshaft and bank 1 intake camshaft is outside the expected window. Often caused by a stretched timing chain, jumped tooth, worn tensioner, or failed cam/crank sensor.

t: Crankshaft Position - Camshaft Position Correlation (Bank 1 Sensor B)
s: The relative timing between the crankshaft and bank 1 intake camshaft is outside the expected window. Often caused by a stretched timing chain, jumped tooth, worn tensioner, or failed cam/crank sensor.

t: Crankshaft Position - Camshaft Position Correlation (Bank 2 Sensor A)
s: The relative timing between the crankshaft and bank 2 intake camshaft is outside the expected window. Often caused by a stretched timing chain, jumped tooth, worn tensioner, or failed cam/crank sensor.

t: Crankshaft Position - Camshaft Position Correlation (Bank 2 Sensor B)
s: The relative timing between the crankshaft and bank 2 intake camshaft is outside the expected window. Often caused by a stretched timing chain, jumped tooth, worn tensioner, or failed cam/crank sensor.

t: A Camshaft Profile Actuator A Control Circuit/Open Bank 1
s: Open circuit at the camshaft profile actuator (intake, profile A, bank 1). Used by systems with discrete cam-lift levels (e.g. BMW Valvetronic, Honda VTEC, Audi AVS) — the actuator that selects the lift profile cannot be driven.

t: A Camshaft Profile Actuator A Control Circuit Low Bank 1
s: Low control voltage at the cam profile actuator (intake, profile A, bank 1). Typically a short to ground in the wiring or actuator coil.

t: A Camshaft Profile Actuator A Control Circuit High Bank 1
s: Excessively high control voltage at the cam profile actuator (intake, profile A, bank 1). Typically a short to power or open ground.

t: A Camshaft Profile Actuator A Control Circuit/Open Bank 2
s: Open circuit at the cam profile actuator (intake, profile A, bank 2). Same fault as P001A but on bank 2. The cam phaser actuator adjusts intake/exhaust timing under load — a stuck or slow phaser kills mid-rpm torque and can throw correlation codes between cam and crank.

t: A Camshaft Profile Actuator A Control Circuit Low Bank 2
s: Low control voltage at the cam profile actuator (intake, profile A, bank 2). Same fault as P001B on bank 2. The cam phaser actuator adjusts intake/exhaust timing under load — a stuck or slow phaser kills mid-rpm torque and can throw correlation codes between cam and crank.

t: A Camshaft Profile Actuator A Control Circuit High Bank 2
s: Excessively high control voltage at the cam profile actuator (intake, profile A, bank 2). Same fault as P001C on bank 2. The cam phaser actuator adjusts intake/exhaust timing under load — a stuck or slow phaser kills mid-rpm torque and can throw correlation codes between cam and crank.

t: "A" Camshaft Position Actuator Circuit (Bank 2)
s: Electrical fault in the bank 2 intake camshaft position actuator. Same diagnostic principle as P0010 but on the second cylinder bank.

t: "A" Camshaft Position - Timing Over-Advanced (Bank 2)
s: The bank 2 intake camshaft is more advanced than commanded, or the actuator does not respond as expected. Often caused by stuck-open oil control valves, sludge in VVT passages, or low oil pressure.

t: "A" Camshaft Position - Timing Over-Retarded (Bank 2)
s: The bank 2 intake camshaft remains retarded compared to commanded position. Common causes include a stuck-closed oil control valve, blocked oil passages, or a worn cam phaser.

t: "B" Camshaft Position Actuator Circuit (Bank 2)
s: Electrical fault in the bank 2 exhaust camshaft position actuator. Same diagnostic principle as P0013 but on the second cylinder bank.

t: "B" Camshaft Position - Timing Over-Advanced (Bank 2)
s: The bank 2 exhaust camshaft is more advanced than commanded. Same root causes as P0011 but on the exhaust cam.

t: "B" Camshaft Position - Timing Over-Retarded (Bank 2)
s: The bank 2 exhaust camshaft remains retarded relative to commanded position. Same root causes as P0012 but on the exhaust cam.

t: Intake Valve Control Solenoid Circuit Range/Performance (Bank 1)
s: The ECU detected that the bank 1 intake valve oil control solenoid does not respond as expected. Common causes are sludge in the oil passages, a sticking solenoid, or low oil pressure that prevents proper VVT phasing on the intake side.

t: Exhaust Valve Control Solenoid Circuit Range/Performance Bank 1
s: The ECU detects that the exhaust camshaft oil control valve on bank 1 does not respond to commanded duty cycle within expected limits. Camshaft phase deviates from target during phaser actuation tests.

t: Intake Valve Control Solenoid Circuit Range/Performance (Bank 2)
s: The ECU detected that the bank 2 intake valve oil control solenoid does not respond as expected. Common causes are sludge in the oil passages, a sticking solenoid, or low oil pressure that prevents proper VVT phasing on the intake side.

t: Exhaust Valve Control Solenoid Circuit Range/Performance Bank 2
s: The ECU detects that the exhaust camshaft oil control valve on bank 2 does not respond to commanded duty cycle within expected limits. Camshaft phase deviates from target during phaser actuation tests.

t: B Camshaft Profile Actuator A Control Circuit/Open Bank 1
s: Open circuit at the cam profile actuator on the exhaust cam (profile A, bank 1). Same diagnosis as P001A but for the exhaust profile actuator.

t: B Camshaft Profile Actuator A Control Circuit Low Bank 1
s: Low control voltage at the exhaust cam profile actuator (profile A, bank 1). Short to ground in wiring or coil.

t: B Camshaft Profile Actuator A Control Circuit High Bank 1
s: Excessively high control voltage at the exhaust cam profile actuator (profile A, bank 1). Short to power or open ground.

t: B Camshaft Profile Actuator A Control Circuit/Open Bank 2
s: Open circuit at the exhaust cam profile actuator (profile A, bank 2). Same as P002A on bank 2. The cam phaser actuator adjusts intake/exhaust timing under load — a stuck or slow phaser kills mid-rpm torque and can throw correlation codes between cam and crank.

t: B Camshaft Profile Actuator A Control Circuit Low Bank 2
s: Low control voltage at the exhaust cam profile actuator (profile A, bank 2). Same as P002B on bank 2. The cam phaser actuator adjusts intake/exhaust timing under load — a stuck or slow phaser kills mid-rpm torque and can throw correlation codes between cam and crank.

t: B Camshaft Profile Actuator A Control Circuit High Bank 2
s: Excessively high control voltage at the exhaust cam profile actuator (profile A, bank 2). Same as P002C on bank 2. The cam phaser actuator adjusts intake/exhaust timing under load — a stuck or slow phaser kills mid-rpm torque and can throw correlation codes between cam and crank.

t: HO2S Heater Control Circuit (Bank 1 Sensor 1)
s: Generic electrical fault in the heater circuit of the upstream oxygen sensor on bank 1. The ECU monitors the heater current and resistance to ensure the sensor reaches operating temperature for closed-loop fueling.

t: HO2S Heater Control Circuit Low (Bank 1 Sensor 1)
s: O2 sensor heater control circuit on bank 1, sensor 1 reports low voltage. Likely shorted to ground or open in the heater control wire. The heater warms the sensor element to operating temperature (~600 °C); without it the sensor stays inactive in closed loop and the ECM cannot trim mixture.

t: HO2S Heater Control Circuit High (Bank 1 Sensor 1)
s: O2 sensor heater control circuit on bank 1, sensor 1 reports high voltage. Likely shorted to B+ or open in the heater element itself. The heater warms the sensor element to operating temperature (~600 °C); without it the sensor stays inactive in closed loop and the ECM cannot trim mixture.

t: Turbocharger Bypass Valve Control Circuit
s: The ECU detects an electrical fault in the turbocharger bypass (wastegate) valve control circuit. Voltage or current measured on the solenoid output deviates from expected values during commanded actuation.

t: Turbocharger Bypass Valve Control Circuit Low
s: The bypass valve solenoid control circuit reports a voltage below threshold, indicating a short to ground or open driver. The ECU disables boost control until the fault clears.

t: Turbocharger Bypass Valve Control Circuit High
s: The bypass valve solenoid control circuit reports a voltage above threshold, indicating a short to power or open circuit. Boost control is disabled while the fault is active.

t: HO2S Heater Control Circuit (Bank 1 Sensor 2)
s: Generic electrical fault in the heater circuit of the upstream oxygen sensor on bank 1. The ECU monitors the heater current and resistance to ensure the sensor reaches operating temperature for closed-loop fueling.

t: HO2S Heater Control Circuit Low (Bank 1 Sensor 2)
s: O2 sensor heater control circuit on bank 1, sensor 2 reports low voltage. Likely shorted to ground or open in the heater control wire. The heater warms the sensor element to operating temperature (~600 °C); without it the sensor stays inactive in closed loop and the ECM cannot trim mixture.

t: HO2S Heater Control Circuit High (Bank 1 Sensor 2)
s: The heater control line for the upstream bank 1 oxygen sensor reads above the expected voltage, typical of a short to supply.

t: Turbocharger/Supercharger Bypass Valve A Control Circuit Range/Performance
s: The wastegate or bypass valve A on the turbo/supercharger does not respond mechanically as expected to its control signal. Boost pressure deviates from target.

t: Turbocharger/Supercharger Boost Control A Position Exceeded Learning Limit
s: Adaptive learning of boost control actuator A position has hit its limit. Long-term mechanical drift (wastegate wear, linkage stretched) outside compensable range.

t: Turbocharger/Supercharger Boost Control B Position Exceeded Learning Limit
s: Adaptive learning of boost control actuator A position has hit its limit. Long-term mechanical drift (wastegate wear, linkage stretched) outside compensable range.

t: A Camshaft Profile Actuator A Control Performance/Stuck Off Bank 1
s: The intake cam profile actuator (profile A, bank 1) does not engage when commanded — stays in low-lift profile. Often hydraulic pin sticking or low oil pressure.

t: A Camshaft Profile Actuator A Control Stuck On Bank 1
s: The intake cam profile actuator (profile A, bank 1) stays engaged when commanded off — stuck in high-lift profile. Idle quality and emissions affected.

t: A Camshaft Profile Actuator A Control Performance/Stuck Off Bank 2
s: The intake cam profile actuator (profile A, bank 2) does not engage when commanded — stays in low-lift profile. Often hydraulic pin sticking or low oil pressure.

t: A Camshaft Profile Actuator A Control Stuck On Bank 2
s: The intake cam profile actuator (profile A, bank 2) stays engaged when commanded off — stuck in high-lift profile. Idle quality and emissions affected.

t: O2 Sensor Signals Swapped Bank 1 Sensor 1/Bank 2 Sensor 1
s: The pre-cat oxygen sensors of bank 1 and bank 2 appear to be wired the wrong way around — bank 1 reacts when bank 2 should and vice versa. Typically after recent service.

t: O2 Sensor Signals Swapped Bank 1 Sensor 2/Bank 2 Sensor 2
s: The post-cat oxygen sensors of bank 1 and bank 2 appear to be wired the wrong way around — bank 1 reacts when bank 2 should and vice versa. Typically after recent service.

t: HO2S Heater Control Circuit Bank 1 Sensor 3
s: Generic electrical fault in the heater control of the bank 1, sensor 3 oxygen sensor (typically a third sensor downstream of multi-stage cat or NOx trap).

t: HO2S Heater Control Circuit Low Bank 1 Sensor 3
s: Heater control voltage of the bank 1, sensor 3 oxygen sensor is below the expected range — short to ground or low-resistance fault.

t: HO2S Heater Control Circuit High Bank 1 Sensor 3
s: Heater control voltage of the bank 1, sensor 3 oxygen sensor is above the expected range — short to power or open ground.

t: Turbocharger/Supercharger Boost Control A Circuit/Open
s: Open circuit at the boost control solenoid/actuator A. The ECU cannot drive the wastegate or VGT vanes.

t: Turbocharger/Supercharger Boost Control A Circuit Range/Performance
s: Boost control actuator A is electrically active but boost pressure deviates from target. Mechanical or hydraulic limitation.

t: Turbocharger/Supercharger Boost Control A Circuit Low
s: Boost control circuit A voltage below expected range — short to ground or low-resistance fault in solenoid wiring.

t: Turbocharger/Supercharger Boost Control A Circuit High
s: Boost control circuit A voltage above expected range — short to power or open ground in solenoid wiring.

t: Turbocharger/Supercharger A Turbine Overspeed
s: The turbocharger A turbine speed exceeded its safe limit. Risk of bearing damage and shaft fracture. Often caused by a stuck-closed wastegate or boost control fault.

t: Turbocharger/Supercharger Boost Control B Circuit/Open
s: Open circuit at boost control solenoid B (twin-turbo or two-stage system). Same diagnosis as P0045 on actuator B.

t: Turbocharger/Supercharger Boost Control B Circuit Range/Performance
s: Boost control B mechanical/hydraulic deviation from target. Same as P0046 on actuator B. Boost control tracks commanded vs. actual manifold pressure; persistent deviation means wastegate, vanes, hose or actuator are not delivering the requested pressure.

t: Turbocharger/Supercharger Boost Control B Circuit Low
s: Boost control B circuit voltage below expected range. Same as P0047 on actuator B. Boost control tracks commanded vs. actual manifold pressure; persistent deviation means wastegate, vanes, hose or actuator are not delivering the requested pressure.

t: Turbocharger/Supercharger Boost Control B Circuit High
s: Boost control B circuit voltage above expected range. Same as P0048 on actuator B. Boost control tracks commanded vs. actual manifold pressure; persistent deviation means wastegate, vanes, hose or actuator are not delivering the requested pressure.

t: Turbocharger/Supercharger Boost Control A Circuit Intermittent/Erratic
s: Intermittent dropouts or noise on boost control A circuit. Loose connector, partial wiring break, or solenoid degrading.

t: Turbocharger/Supercharger Boost Control B Circuit Intermittent/Erratic
s: Same intermittent fault pattern as P004E on boost control B. Boost control tracks commanded vs. actual manifold pressure; persistent deviation means wastegate, vanes, hose or actuator are not delivering the requested pressure.

t: HO2S Heater Control Circuit (Bank 2 Sensor 1)
s: Generic electrical fault in the heater circuit of the upstream oxygen sensor on bank 2. The ECU monitors the heater current and resistance to ensure the sensor reaches operating temperature for closed-loop fueling.

t: HO2S Heater Control Circuit Low (Bank 2 Sensor 1)
s: The heater control line for the upstream bank 2 oxygen sensor reads below the expected voltage, typical of a short to ground or low driver current.

t: HO2S Heater Control Circuit High (Bank 2 Sensor 1)
s: The heater control line for the upstream bank 2 oxygen sensor reads above the expected voltage, typical of a short to supply.

t: HO2S Heater Resistance Bank 1 Sensor 1
s: The heater resistance of the bank 1, sensor 1 oxygen sensor is outside its specified range. Sensor heater aging or short between heater and signal.

t: HO2S Heater Resistance Bank 1 Sensor 2
s: The heater resistance of the bank 1, sensor 2 oxygen sensor is outside its specified range. Sensor heater aging or short between heater and signal.

t: HO2S Heater Resistance Bank 1 Sensor 3
s: The heater resistance of the bank 1, sensor 3 oxygen sensor is outside its specified range. Sensor heater aging or short between heater and signal.

t: HO2S Heater Control Circuit (Bank 2 Sensor 2)
s: Generic electrical fault in the heater circuit of the upstream oxygen sensor on bank 2. The ECU monitors the heater current and resistance to ensure the sensor reaches operating temperature for closed-loop fueling.

t: HO2S Heater Control Circuit Low (Bank 2 Sensor 2)
s: The heater control line for the upstream bank 2 oxygen sensor reads below the expected voltage, typical of a short to ground or low driver current.

t: HO2S Heater Control Circuit High (Bank 2 Sensor 2)
s: The heater control line for the upstream bank 2 oxygen sensor reads above the expected voltage, typical of a short to supply.

t: HO2S Heater Resistance Bank 2 Sensor 1
s: Heater resistance out of range on bank 2 sensor 1 (pre-cat). Same diagnostic as P0053 on the second bank.

t: B Camshaft Profile Actuator A Control Performance/Stuck Off Bank 1
s: Exhaust cam profile actuator A on bank 1 stuck disengaged. Same fault pattern as P003C but on the exhaust cam. The cam phaser actuator adjusts intake/exhaust timing under load — a stuck or slow phaser kills mid-rpm torque and can throw correlation codes between cam and crank.

t: B Camshaft Profile Actuator A Control Stuck On Bank 1
s: Exhaust cam profile actuator A on bank 1 stuck engaged. Same fault pattern as P003D on the exhaust cam. The cam phaser actuator adjusts intake/exhaust timing under load — a stuck or slow phaser kills mid-rpm torque and can throw correlation codes between cam and crank.

t: B Camshaft Profile Actuator A Control Performance/Stuck Off Bank 2
s: Exhaust cam profile actuator A on bank 2 stuck disengaged. Same fault pattern as P003C but on the exhaust cam. The cam phaser actuator adjusts intake/exhaust timing under load — a stuck or slow phaser kills mid-rpm torque and can throw correlation codes between cam and crank.

t: B Camshaft Profile Actuator A Control Stuck On Bank 2
s: Exhaust cam profile actuator A on bank 2 stuck engaged. Same fault pattern as P003D on the exhaust cam. The cam phaser actuator adjusts intake/exhaust timing under load — a stuck or slow phaser kills mid-rpm torque and can throw correlation codes between cam and crank.

t: Turbocharger/Supercharger Boost Control B Supply Voltage Circuit Low
s: Supply voltage feeding boost control actuator B is below the expected range. Likely fuse, supply wiring or relay fault.

t: Turbocharger/Supercharger Boost Control B Supply Voltage Circuit High
s: Supply voltage feeding boost control actuator B is above the expected range. Likely charging system overvoltage or wiring shorted to a higher potential.

t: HO2S Heater Resistance Bank 2 Sensor 2
s: Heater resistance out of range on bank 2 sensor 2 (post-cat). Same diagnostic as P0053 on second bank, post-cat.

t: HO2S Heater Resistance Bank 2 Sensor 3
s: Heater resistance out of range on bank 2 sensor 3. The sensor heater warms the element to operating temperature (~600 °C); without it the lambda sensor stays inactive in closed loop and the ECM cannot trim mixture.

t: HO2S Heater Control Circuit Bank 2 Sensor 3
s: Generic electrical fault in the heater control of bank 2 sensor 3. The sensor heater warms the element to operating temperature (~600 °C); without it the lambda sensor stays inactive in closed loop and the ECM cannot trim mixture.

t: HO2S Heater Control Circuit Low Bank 2 Sensor 3
s: Heater control voltage of bank 2 sensor 3 below the expected range. The sensor heater warms the element to operating temperature (~600 °C); without it the lambda sensor stays inactive in closed loop and the ECM cannot trim mixture.

t: HO2S Heater Control Circuit High Bank 2 Sensor 3
s: Heater control voltage of bank 2 sensor 3 above the expected range. The sensor heater warms the element to operating temperature (~600 °C); without it the lambda sensor stays inactive in closed loop and the ECM cannot trim mixture.

t: Air Assisted Injector Control Range/Performance
s: The air-assist injector control system (used on some lean-burn or direct-injection engines to atomize fuel with air) shows a performance deviation from the expected behavior.

t: Air Assisted Injector Control Circuit Low
s: Control voltage of the air-assist injector valve is below the expected range — short to ground or low-resistance fault.

t: Air Assisted Injector Control Circuit High
s: Control voltage of the air-assist injector valve is above the expected range — short to power or open ground. A flow-deviating injector throws off cylinder balance — adjacent cylinders run leaner/richer to compensate, often masking the root cause until the trim limit is hit.

t: MAP/MAF - Throttle Position Correlation
s: The ECU detects implausibility between throttle position and the airflow or intake manifold pressure measured. At a given throttle angle the air mass deviates beyond rationality limits.

t: Manifold Absolute Pressure - Barometric Pressure Correlation
s: At engine cranking or key-on the intake manifold pressure should equal barometric pressure within tolerance. The ECU detects a deviation indicating sensor drift or vacuum line issues.

t: MAP - Mass or Volume Air Flow Correlation Bank 1
s: The expected relationship between manifold absolute pressure (MAP) and mass airflow (MAF) on bank 1 is violated. One of the two sensors is reading incorrectly.

t: MAP - Exhaust Pressure Correlation
s: The relationship between manifold absolute pressure and exhaust back pressure is implausible. One of the two pressure sensors is faulty or there is a major leak in either path.

t: MAP - Turbocharger/Supercharger Inlet Pressure Correlation
s: The relationship between manifold absolute pressure and turbocharger inlet pressure is implausible. Indicates a leak between turbo inlet and intake manifold or a sensor fault.

t: Barometric Pressure - Turbocharger/Supercharger Inlet Pressure Correlation Bank 1
s: The relationship between barometric pressure and turbo inlet pressure (bank 1) is implausible — at engine off the two should match. Indicates an inlet sensor or barometric sensor fault.

t: Turbocharger/Supercharger Boost Control A Supply Voltage Circuit Low
s: Supply voltage to boost control actuator A below expected range. Same as P005E but on actuator A. Boost control tracks commanded vs. actual manifold pressure; persistent deviation means wastegate, vanes, hose or actuator are not delivering the requested pressure.

t: Turbocharger/Supercharger Boost Control A Supply Voltage Circuit High
s: Supply voltage to boost control actuator A above expected range. Same as P005F but on actuator A. Boost control tracks commanded vs. actual manifold pressure; persistent deviation means wastegate, vanes, hose or actuator are not delivering the requested pressure.

t: Ambient Air Temperature Sensor Circuit A
s: Generic electrical fault in the ambient air temperature sensor A circuit. Used by the ECU for cold-start corrections, climate logic, and intake-air calculations.

t: Ambient Air Temperature Sensor Circuit A Range/Performance
s: The ambient air temperature reading is electrically valid but implausible compared to other temperature sources (intake air temp, coolant at cold start).

t: Ambient Air Temperature Sensor Circuit A Low
s: Ambient air temperature sensor A signal voltage below the expected range — typically a short to ground.

t: Ambient Air Temperature Sensor Circuit A High
s: Ambient air temperature sensor A signal voltage above the expected range — typically open circuit (broken wire or sensor) or short to power.

t: Ambient Air Temperature Sensor Circuit A Intermittent/Erratic
s: Intermittent dropouts or noise on the ambient air temperature sensor A signal — typically a loose connector or chafed wiring.

t: Intake Valve Control Solenoid Circuit Bank 1
s: Generic electrical fault on the intake-valve control solenoid for bank 1 (used to switch valve lift, deactivation or VVL profiles).

t: Intake Valve Control Solenoid Circuit Low Bank 1
s: Intake valve solenoid bank 1 control voltage below expected range. Short to ground or low-resistance fault.

t: Intake Valve Control Solenoid Circuit High Bank 1
s: Intake valve solenoid bank 1 control voltage above expected range. Short to power or open ground. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Exhaust Valve Control Solenoid Circuit Bank 1
s: Generic electrical fault on the exhaust-valve control solenoid for bank 1. The exhaust control valve modulates exhaust backpressure or routing (EGR feed, sound, regen support); a malfunction shifts torque mapping and may trigger limp mode.

t: Exhaust Valve Control Solenoid Circuit Low Bank 1
s: Exhaust valve solenoid bank 1 control voltage below expected range. The exhaust control valve modulates exhaust backpressure or routing (EGR feed, sound, regen support); a malfunction shifts torque mapping and may trigger limp mode.

t: Charge Air Cooler Temperature Sensor Circuit Bank 1
s: Generic electrical fault on the charge air cooler (intercooler) outlet temperature sensor on bank 1. Used to monitor intercooler effectiveness and to correct boost calculations.

t: Charge Air Cooler Temperature Sensor Circuit Range/Performance Bank 1
s: Charge air cooler temperature sensor reading on bank 1 is electrically valid but implausible — does not match expected behavior under load, ambient conditions or after long soak.

t: Charge Air Cooler Temperature Sensor Circuit Low Bank 1
s: Charge air cooler temperature sensor signal voltage on bank 1 below expected range. Typically a short to ground. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Charge Air Cooler Temperature Sensor Circuit High Bank 1
s: Charge air cooler temperature sensor signal voltage on bank 1 above expected range. Typically open circuit or short to power.

t: Charge Air Cooler Temperature Sensor Circuit Intermittent/Erratic Bank 1
s: Intermittent dropouts or noise on the charge air cooler temperature sensor signal on bank 1. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Charge Air Cooler Temperature Sensor Bank1/Bank2 Correlation
s: The charge air cooler temperature sensors of bank 1 and bank 2 deviate from each other beyond plausible limits. One of the two has drifted.

t: Exhaust Valve Control Solenoid Circuit High Bank 1
s: Exhaust valve solenoid bank 1 control voltage above expected range. Short to power or open ground. The exhaust control valve modulates exhaust backpressure or routing (EGR feed, sound, regen support); a malfunction shifts torque mapping and may trigger limp mode.

t: Intake Valve Control Solenoid Circuit Bank 2
s: Generic electrical fault on the intake-valve control solenoid for bank 2 (used to switch valve lift, deactivation or VVL profiles).

t: Intake Valve Control Solenoid Circuit Low Bank 2
s: Intake valve solenoid bank 2 control voltage below expected range. Short to ground or low-resistance fault.

t: Intake Valve Control Solenoid Circuit High Bank 2
s: Intake valve solenoid bank 2 control voltage above expected range. Short to power or open ground. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Exhaust Valve Control Solenoid Circuit Bank 2
s: Generic electrical fault on the exhaust-valve control solenoid for bank 2. The exhaust control valve modulates exhaust backpressure or routing (EGR feed, sound, regen support); a malfunction shifts torque mapping and may trigger limp mode.

t: Exhaust Valve Control Solenoid Circuit Low Bank 2
s: Exhaust valve solenoid bank 2 control voltage below expected range. The exhaust control valve modulates exhaust backpressure or routing (EGR feed, sound, regen support); a malfunction shifts torque mapping and may trigger limp mode.

t: Exhaust Valve Control Solenoid Circuit High Bank 2
s: Exhaust valve solenoid bank 2 control voltage above expected range. Short to power or open ground. The exhaust control valve modulates exhaust backpressure or routing (EGR feed, sound, regen support); a malfunction shifts torque mapping and may trigger limp mode.

t: Fuel Rail/System Pressure Too Low
s: The ECU detects fuel rail pressure below the desired setpoint despite maximum pump and metering valve commands. Common in direct injection diesel and gasoline systems where high-pressure pump or rail pressure regulator fails.

t: Fuel Rail/System Pressure Too High
s: The ECU detects fuel rail pressure above the desired setpoint and cannot reduce it through pressure regulator commands. Risk of injector damage and rail seal failure if not addressed.

t: Fuel Pressure Regulator 1 Performance
s: The ECU detects that fuel rail pressure does not respond to commanded changes of the pressure regulator within expected dynamics. Indicates a sluggish or partially failed regulator.

t: Low Pressure Fuel System Pressure - Too Low
s: The low-pressure (lift pump) fuel pressure is below the expected value. Insufficient supply to the high-pressure pump leads to power loss and possible HP pump cavitation.

t: Low Pressure Fuel System Pressure - Too High
s: The low-pressure fuel pressure is above the expected value. Often caused by a stuck-closed fuel return valve or a faulty pressure regulator.

t: Fuel Cooler Pump Control Circuit/Open
s: Open circuit at the fuel cooler pump control. The pump that circulates fuel through a cooler (used on diesels and some hot-fuel applications) cannot be driven.

t: Fuel Cooler Pump Control Circuit Low
s: Control voltage of the fuel cooler pump is below the expected range — short to ground or low-resistance fault.

t: Fuel Cooler Pump Control Circuit High
s: Control voltage of the fuel cooler pump is above the expected range — short to power or open ground. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Engine Coolant Temperature/Fuel Temperature Correlation
s: Engine coolant temperature and fuel temperature readings are inconsistent — at long soak both should be near ambient and converge under load. One sensor has drifted.

t: Fuel Pressure Regulator A Control Circuit/Open
s: Open circuit at fuel pressure regulator A. The actuator that controls rail pressure cannot be driven.

t: Fuel Pressure Regulator A Control Circuit Low
s: Fuel pressure regulator A control voltage below expected range — short to ground. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Fuel Pressure Regulator A Control Circuit High
s: Fuel pressure regulator A control voltage above expected range — short to power or open ground. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Fuel System Leak Detected - Large Leak
s: The ECU detected a large leak in the fuel system. Rail pressure cannot be maintained, often visible as fuel odor or visible drips.

t: Fuel System Leak Detected - Small Leak
s: A small fuel system leak detected by long-term pressure drop pattern. Often a slowly weeping injector or hairline crack in a fitting.

t: Intake Air Temperature Sensor 2 Circuit Bank 1
s: Generic electrical fault on intake air temperature sensor 2 (typically post-intercooler) on bank 1. The intake air temperature sensor feeds the ECM's density calculation — a drifted reading miscalculates fuel mass and pushes mixture lean (cold reading) or rich (hot reading).

t: Intake Air Temperature Sensor 2 Circuit Range/Performance Bank 1
s: IAT sensor 2 reading on bank 1 is electrically valid but implausible — does not match other temperature sources or expected behavior.

t: Intake Air Temperature Sensor 2 Circuit Low Bank 1
s: IAT sensor 2 bank 1 signal voltage below expected range — short to ground. The intake air temperature sensor feeds the ECM's density calculation — a drifted reading miscalculates fuel mass and pushes mixture lean (cold reading) or rich (hot reading).

t: Intake Air Temperature Sensor 2 Circuit High Bank 1
s: IAT sensor 2 bank 1 signal voltage above expected range — open circuit or short to power. The intake air temperature sensor feeds the ECM's density calculation — a drifted reading miscalculates fuel mass and pushes mixture lean (cold reading) or rich (hot reading).

t: Intake Air Temperature Sensor 2 Circuit Intermittent/Erratic Bank 1
s: Intermittent dropouts or noise on IAT sensor 2 bank 1 signal. The intake air temperature sensor feeds the ECM's density calculation — a drifted reading miscalculates fuel mass and pushes mixture lean (cold reading) or rich (hot reading).

t: Intake Air Temperature/Ambient Air Temperature Correlation
s: Intake air temperature and ambient air temperature readings deviate beyond plausible limits — at engine off both should match within tolerance.

t: Fuel Pressure Relief Control Circuit/Open
s: Open circuit at fuel pressure relief solenoid (used to bleed off rail pressure during certain operating modes).

t: Fuel Pressure Relief Control Circuit Low
s: Pressure relief solenoid control voltage below expected range — short to ground. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Fuel Pressure Relief Control Circuit High
s: Pressure relief solenoid control voltage above expected range — short to power or open ground. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Fuel Pressure Relief Control Performance/Stuck Off
s: Pressure relief solenoid does not relieve pressure when commanded — stuck closed.

t: Fuel Pressure Relief Control Stuck On
s: Pressure relief solenoid stays open when not commanded — rail pressure cannot build up properly.

t: Charge Air Cooler Temperature Sensor Circuit Bank 2
s: Generic electrical fault on charge air cooler temperature sensor on bank 2. Same as P007A on bank 2.

t: Charge Air Cooler Temperature Sensor Circuit Range/Performance Bank 2
s: CAC temperature sensor reading on bank 2 implausible. Same as P007B on bank 2. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Charge Air Cooler Temperature Sensor Circuit Low Bank 2
s: Charge air cooler temperature sensor signal voltage on bank 2 below expected range. Typically a short to ground. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Charge Air Cooler Temperature Sensor Circuit High Bank 2
s: Charge air cooler temperature sensor signal voltage on bank 2 above expected range. Typically open circuit or short to power.

t: Charge Air Cooler Temperature Sensor Circuit Intermittent/Erratic Bank 2
s: Intermittent dropouts or noise on the charge air cooler temperature sensor signal on bank 2. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Intake Air Temperature Sensor 2 Circuit Bank 2
s: Generic electrical fault on intake air temperature sensor 2 (typically post-intercooler) on bank 2. The intake air temperature sensor feeds the ECM's density calculation — a drifted reading miscalculates fuel mass and pushes mixture lean (cold reading) or rich (hot reading).

t: Intake Air Temperature Sensor 2 Circuit Range/Performance Bank 2
s: IAT sensor 2 reading on bank 2 is electrically valid but implausible — does not match other temperature sources or expected behavior.

t: Intake Air Temperature Sensor 2 Circuit Low Bank 2
s: IAT sensor 2 bank 2 signal voltage below expected range — short to ground. The intake air temperature sensor feeds the ECM's density calculation — a drifted reading miscalculates fuel mass and pushes mixture lean (cold reading) or rich (hot reading).

t: Intake Air Temperature Sensor 2 Circuit High Bank 2
s: IAT sensor 2 bank 2 signal voltage above expected range — open circuit or short to power. The intake air temperature sensor feeds the ECM's density calculation — a drifted reading miscalculates fuel mass and pushes mixture lean (cold reading) or rich (hot reading).

t: Intake Air Temperature Sensor 2 Circuit Intermittent/Erratic Bank 2
s: Intermittent dropouts or noise on IAT sensor 2 bank 2 signal. The intake air temperature sensor feeds the ECM's density calculation — a drifted reading miscalculates fuel mass and pushes mixture lean (cold reading) or rich (hot reading).

t: Intake Air Temperature Sensor 1 Circuit Bank 2
s: Generic electrical fault on intake air temperature sensor 1 (typically pre-intercooler) on bank 2. The intake air temperature sensor feeds the ECM's density calculation — a drifted reading miscalculates fuel mass and pushes mixture lean (cold reading) or rich (hot reading).

t: Intake Air Temperature Sensor 1 Circuit Range/Performance Bank 2
s: IAT sensor 1 reading on bank 2 implausible. The intake air temperature sensor feeds the ECM's density calculation — a drifted reading miscalculates fuel mass and pushes mixture lean (cold reading) or rich (hot reading).

t: Intake Air Temperature Sensor 1 Circuit Low Bank 2
s: IAT sensor 1 bank 2 signal voltage below expected range. The intake air temperature sensor feeds the ECM's density calculation — a drifted reading miscalculates fuel mass and pushes mixture lean (cold reading) or rich (hot reading).

t: Intake Air Temperature Sensor 1 Circuit High Bank 2
s: IAT sensor 1 bank 2 signal voltage above expected range. The intake air temperature sensor feeds the ECM's density calculation — a drifted reading miscalculates fuel mass and pushes mixture lean (cold reading) or rich (hot reading).

t: Intake Air Temperature Sensor 1 Circuit Intermittent Bank 2
s: Intermittent signal from IAT sensor 1 on bank 2. The intake air temperature sensor feeds the ECM's density calculation — a drifted reading miscalculates fuel mass and pushes mixture lean (cold reading) or rich (hot reading).

t: Turbocharger/Supercharger Boost Control A Module Performance
s: The dedicated boost control module A reports a performance fault — internal driver, sensor or self-diagnostic flagged an issue.

t: Turbocharger/Supercharger Boost Control B Module Performance
s: The dedicated boost control module A reports a performance fault — internal driver, sensor or self-diagnostic flagged an issue.

t: Radiator Coolant Temperature Sensor Circuit
s: Generic electrical fault at the radiator coolant temperature sensor (downstream of radiator, used for thermostat diagnosis).

t: Radiator Coolant Temperature Sensor Circuit Range/Performance
s: Radiator coolant sensor reading implausible compared to engine coolant or expected behavior. The engine coolant temperature sensor feeds warm-up enrichment, fan control and closed-loop entry — a stuck-cold reading keeps the engine in open loop and rich forever.

t: Radiator Coolant Temperature Sensor Circuit Low
s: Radiator coolant temperature sensor signal voltage below expected range. The engine coolant temperature sensor feeds warm-up enrichment, fan control and closed-loop entry — a stuck-cold reading keeps the engine in open loop and rich forever.

t: Radiator Coolant Temperature Sensor Circuit High
s: Radiator coolant temperature sensor signal voltage above expected range. The engine coolant temperature sensor feeds warm-up enrichment, fan control and closed-loop entry — a stuck-cold reading keeps the engine in open loop and rich forever.

t: Radiator Coolant Temperature Sensor Circuit Intermittent/Erratic
s: Intermittent signal from radiator coolant temperature sensor. The engine coolant temperature sensor feeds warm-up enrichment, fan control and closed-loop entry — a stuck-cold reading keeps the engine in open loop and rich forever.

t: Radiator Coolant Temperature/Engine Coolant Temperature Correlation
s: Difference between radiator outlet and engine coolant temperatures is implausible — typically indicates a stuck thermostat.

t: Engine Coolant Flow Low/Performance
s: Coolant flow through the engine is below the expected level. Risk of overheating.

t: MAP - Mass or Volume Air Flow Correlation Bank 2
s: The expected relationship between manifold absolute pressure (MAP) and mass airflow (MAF) on bank 2 is violated. One of the two sensors is reading incorrectly.

t: Low Pressure Fuel System Pressure - Too Low. Low Ambient Temperature
s: Low-pressure fuel circuit pressure below target under cold ambient conditions. Often diesel waxing or gelled fuel filter.

t: Low Fuel Pressure - Forced Limited Power
s: Fuel pressure too low — ECU has activated forced power reduction (limp mode) to protect the fuel system.

t: Fuel Injector Insufficient Flow - Forced Limited Power
s: One or more injectors deliver less fuel than commanded — ECU has activated forced power reduction. A flow-deviating injector throws off cylinder balance — adjacent cylinders run leaner/richer to compensate, often masking the root cause until the trim limit is hit.

t: Mass or Volume Air Flow A Circuit Range/Performance - Air Flow Too Low
s: MAF sensor A reads air flow lower than physically expected for the operating point. Often contaminated MAF or large vacuum leak after the MAF.

t: Mass or Volume Air Flow A Circuit Range/Performance - Air Flow Too High
s: MAF sensor A reads air flow higher than physically expected — sensor drift to high or signal noise. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Mass or Volume Air Flow B Circuit Range/Performance - Air Flow Too Low
s: MAF sensor A reads air flow lower than physically expected for the operating point. Often contaminated MAF or large vacuum leak after the MAF.

t: Mass or Volume Air Flow B Circuit Range/Performance - Air Flow Too High
s: MAF sensor A reads air flow higher than physically expected — sensor drift to high or signal noise. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Turbocharger/Supercharger Bypass Valve B Control Circuit
s: Generic electrical fault on bypass valve B control. Same family as P0039 on actuator B. Boost control tracks commanded vs. actual manifold pressure; persistent deviation means wastegate, vanes, hose or actuator are not delivering the requested pressure.

t: Turbocharger/Supercharger Bypass Valve B Control Circuit Low
s: Bypass valve B control voltage below expected range — short to ground. Boost control tracks commanded vs. actual manifold pressure; persistent deviation means wastegate, vanes, hose or actuator are not delivering the requested pressure.

t: Turbocharger/Supercharger Bypass Valve B Control Circuit High
s: Bypass valve B control voltage above expected range. Boost control tracks commanded vs. actual manifold pressure; persistent deviation means wastegate, vanes, hose or actuator are not delivering the requested pressure.

t: Turbocharger/Supercharger Bypass Valve B Control Circuit Range/Performance
s: Bypass valve B does not respond to commands as expected. Mechanical issue. Boost control tracks commanded vs. actual manifold pressure; persistent deviation means wastegate, vanes, hose or actuator are not delivering the requested pressure.

t: Turbocharger/Supercharger Bypass Valve B - Mechanical
s: Mechanical fault detected at bypass valve B — broken linkage, jammed valve, or seized actuator rod. Boost control tracks commanded vs. actual manifold pressure; persistent deviation means wastegate, vanes, hose or actuator are not delivering the requested pressure.

t: Turbocharger/Supercharger B Turbine Overspeed
s: Turbocharger B turbine speed exceeded its safe limit. Same as P0049 on turbo B. Boost control tracks commanded vs. actual manifold pressure; persistent deviation means wastegate, vanes, hose or actuator are not delivering the requested pressure.

t: Fuel Rail Pressure Too Low - Engine Cranking Bank 1
s: During engine cranking on bank 1, fuel rail pressure does not reach the level needed for start. Hard or no-start condition.

t: Intake Air Pressure Measurement System - Multiple Sensor Correlation Bank 1
s: Multiple intake air pressure sensors on bank 1 disagree with each other beyond the plausible deviation. One has drifted.

t: Fuel Pressure Regulator A Solenoid Supply Voltage Control Circuit/Open
s: Open circuit on the supply voltage feed to the fuel pressure regulator A solenoid.

t: Fuel Pressure Regulator A Solenoid Supply Voltage Control Circuit Low
s: Supply voltage to fuel pressure regulator A solenoid below expected range — fuse, relay or wiring fault.

t: Fuel Pressure Regulator A Solenoid Supply Voltage Control Circuit High
s: Supply voltage to fuel pressure regulator A solenoid above expected range — typically charging system overvoltage. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Fuel Volume Regulator Solenoid Supply Voltage Control Circuit/Open
s: Open circuit on the supply voltage feed to the fuel volume regulator solenoid.

t: Fuel Volume Regulator Solenoid Supply Voltage Control Circuit Low
s: Supply voltage to fuel volume regulator solenoid below expected range. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Fuel Volume Regulator Solenoid Supply Voltage Control Circuit High
s: Supply voltage to fuel volume regulator solenoid above expected range. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Intake Air Temperature Measurement System - Multiple Sensor Correlation Bank 1
s: Multiple intake air temperature sensors on bank 1 disagree beyond plausible limits. The intake air temperature sensor feeds the ECM's density calculation — a drifted reading miscalculates fuel mass and pushes mixture lean (cold reading) or rich (hot reading).

t: Barometric Pressure - Turbocharger/Supercharger Boost Sensor A Correlation
s: Barometric pressure and boost sensor A readings disagree at engine off — they should match without boost.

t: Barometric Pressure - Turbocharger/Supercharger Boost Sensor B Correlation
s: Barometric pressure and boost sensor B readings disagree at engine off — they should match without boost.

t: HO2S Heater Control Circuit Range/Performance Bank 1 Sensor 1
s: Heater control of bank 1 sensor 1 is electrically OK but does not heat the sensor as expected — element aged or wiring resistance high.

t: HO2S Heater Control Circuit Range/Performance Bank 1 Sensor 2
s: Heater control of bank 1 sensor 2 is electrically OK but does not heat the sensor as expected — element aged or wiring resistance high.

t: HO2S Heater Control Circuit Range/Performance Bank 2 Sensor 1
s: Heater control of bank 2 sensor 1 is electrically OK but does not heat the sensor as expected — element aged or wiring resistance high.

t: HO2S Heater Control Circuit Range/Performance Bank 2 Sensor 2
s: Heater control of bank 2 sensor 2 is electrically OK but does not heat the sensor as expected — element aged or wiring resistance high.

t: HO2S Sensor Correlation Bank 1 Sensor 1/Bank 1 Sensor 2
s: Pre- and post-cat oxygen sensors on bank 1 do not correlate as expected — typical sign of catalyst inefficiency or one failed sensor.

t: HO2S Sensor Correlation Bank 2 Sensor 1/Bank 2 Sensor 2
s: Pre- and post-cat oxygen sensors on bank 2 do not correlate as expected — typical sign of catalyst inefficiency or one failed sensor.

t: Turbocharger/Supercharger Bypass Valve A Temperature Too High
s: Bypass valve A temperature exceeded its safe limit — heat soak from prolonged high-load operation. Boost control tracks commanded vs. actual manifold pressure; persistent deviation means wastegate, vanes, hose or actuator are not delivering the requested pressure.

t: Turbocharger/Supercharger Bypass Valve B Temperature Too High
s: Bypass valve A temperature exceeded its safe limit — heat soak from prolonged high-load operation. Boost control tracks commanded vs. actual manifold pressure; persistent deviation means wastegate, vanes, hose or actuator are not delivering the requested pressure.

t: Ambient Air Temperature Sensor Circuit B
s: Generic electrical fault on ambient air temperature sensor B (second sensor for redundancy or second measurement location).

t: Ambient Air Temperature Sensor Circuit B Range/Performance
s: Ambient air temperature sensor B reading implausible compared to other sources.

t: Ambient Air Temperature Sensor Circuit B Low
s: Ambient air temp sensor B signal voltage below expected range.

t: Ambient Air Temperature Sensor Circuit B High
s: Ambient air temp sensor B signal voltage above expected range.

t: Ambient Air Temperature Sensor Circuit B Intermittent/Erratic
s: Intermittent signal from ambient air temp sensor B.

t: Charge Air Cooler Coolant Temperature Sensor A Circuit
s: Generic electrical fault at the charge air cooler coolant temperature sensor A (water-cooled CAC system).

t: Charge Air Cooler Coolant Temperature Sensor A Circuit Range/Performance
s: CAC coolant temp sensor A reading implausible. The engine coolant temperature sensor feeds warm-up enrichment, fan control and closed-loop entry — a stuck-cold reading keeps the engine in open loop and rich forever.

t: Charge Air Cooler Coolant Temperature Sensor A Circuit Low
s: CAC coolant temp sensor A signal voltage below expected range. The engine coolant temperature sensor feeds warm-up enrichment, fan control and closed-loop entry — a stuck-cold reading keeps the engine in open loop and rich forever.

t: Charge Air Cooler Coolant Temperature Sensor A Circuit High
s: CAC coolant temp sensor A signal voltage above expected range. The engine coolant temperature sensor feeds warm-up enrichment, fan control and closed-loop entry — a stuck-cold reading keeps the engine in open loop and rich forever.

t: Charge Air Cooler Coolant Temperature Sensor A Intermittent/Erratic
s: Intermittent signal from CAC coolant temp sensor A. The engine coolant temperature sensor feeds warm-up enrichment, fan control and closed-loop entry — a stuck-cold reading keeps the engine in open loop and rich forever.

t: Exhaust Heat Exchanger Exhaust Bypass Valve A Stuck Open
s: The exhaust heat exchanger bypass valve A is stuck open. Engine warms up too slowly because exhaust does not heat the heat exchanger.

t: Exhaust Heat Exchanger Exhaust Bypass Valve A Stuck Closed
s: The exhaust heat exchanger bypass valve A is stuck closed. Risk of overheating because exhaust always passes through the heat exchanger.

t: Intake Air Temperature/Fuel Temperature Correlation
s: Intake air and fuel temperature readings are inconsistent — at long soak both should match ambient. The intake air temperature sensor feeds the ECM's density calculation — a drifted reading miscalculates fuel mass and pushes mixture lean (cold reading) or rich (hot reading).

t: Intake Air Pressure Measurement System - Multiple Sensor Correlation Bank 2
s: Multiple intake air pressure sensors on bank 2 disagree with each other beyond the plausible deviation. One has drifted.

t: Intake Air Temperature Measurement System - Multiple Sensor Correlation Bank 2
s: Multiple intake air temperature sensors on bank 2 disagree beyond plausible limits. The intake air temperature sensor feeds the ECM's density calculation — a drifted reading miscalculates fuel mass and pushes mixture lean (cold reading) or rich (hot reading).

t: Intake Air Temperature Sensor 3 Circuit Bank 1
s: Generic electrical fault on intake air temperature sensor 3 (third measurement location, e.g. in throttle or after intercooler) on bank 1.

t: Intake Air Temperature Sensor 3 Circuit Range/Performance Bank 1
s: IAT sensor 3 reading on bank 1 implausible. The intake air temperature sensor feeds the ECM's density calculation — a drifted reading miscalculates fuel mass and pushes mixture lean (cold reading) or rich (hot reading).

t: Intake Air Temperature Sensor 3 Circuit Low Bank 1
s: IAT sensor 3 bank 1 signal voltage below expected range. The intake air temperature sensor feeds the ECM's density calculation — a drifted reading miscalculates fuel mass and pushes mixture lean (cold reading) or rich (hot reading).

t: Intake Air Temperature Sensor 3 Circuit High Bank 1
s: IAT sensor 3 bank 1 signal voltage above expected range. The intake air temperature sensor feeds the ECM's density calculation — a drifted reading miscalculates fuel mass and pushes mixture lean (cold reading) or rich (hot reading).

t: Intake Air Temperature Sensor 3 Circuit Intermittent/Erratic Bank 1
s: Intermittent signal from IAT sensor 3 on bank 1. The intake air temperature sensor feeds the ECM's density calculation — a drifted reading miscalculates fuel mass and pushes mixture lean (cold reading) or rich (hot reading).

t: Intake Air Temperature Sensor 3 Circuit Bank 2
s: Generic electrical fault on intake air temperature sensor 3 (third measurement location, e.g. in throttle or after intercooler) on bank 2.

t: Intake Air Temperature Sensor 3 Circuit Range/Performance Bank 2
s: IAT sensor 3 reading on bank 2 implausible. The intake air temperature sensor feeds the ECM's density calculation — a drifted reading miscalculates fuel mass and pushes mixture lean (cold reading) or rich (hot reading).

t: Intake Air Temperature Sensor 3 Circuit Low Bank 2
s: IAT sensor 3 bank 2 signal voltage below expected range. The intake air temperature sensor feeds the ECM's density calculation — a drifted reading miscalculates fuel mass and pushes mixture lean (cold reading) or rich (hot reading).

t: Intake Air Temperature Sensor 3 Circuit High Bank 2
s: IAT sensor 3 bank 2 signal voltage above expected range. The intake air temperature sensor feeds the ECM's density calculation — a drifted reading miscalculates fuel mass and pushes mixture lean (cold reading) or rich (hot reading).

t: Intake Air Temperature Sensor 3 Circuit Intermittent/Erratic Bank 2
s: Intermittent signal from IAT sensor 3 on bank 2. The intake air temperature sensor feeds the ECM's density calculation — a drifted reading miscalculates fuel mass and pushes mixture lean (cold reading) or rich (hot reading).

t: Humidity Sensor Circuit/Open
s: Open circuit at the humidity sensor (used by ECU for combustion correction, especially on direct-injection engines).

t: Humidity Sensor Circuit Range/Performance
s: Humidity sensor reading implausible. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Humidity Sensor Circuit Low
s: Humidity sensor signal voltage below expected range. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Humidity Sensor Circuit High
s: Humidity sensor signal voltage above expected range. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Humidity Sensor Circuit Intermittent/Erratic
s: Intermittent signal from humidity sensor. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Barometric Pressure - Turbocharger/Supercharger Inlet Pressure Correlation Bank 2
s: The relationship between barometric pressure and turbo inlet pressure (bank 2) is implausible — at engine off the two should match. Indicates an inlet sensor or barometric sensor fault.

t: Turbocharger Outlet Pressure Sensor A Circuit
s: Generic electrical fault on turbocharger outlet pressure sensor A (post-compressor pressure measurement).

t: Turbocharger Outlet Pressure Sensor A Range/Performance
s: Turbo outlet pressure sensor A reading implausible. Boost control tracks commanded vs. actual manifold pressure; persistent deviation means wastegate, vanes, hose or actuator are not delivering the requested pressure.

t: Turbocharger Outlet Pressure Sensor A Circuit Low
s: Turbo outlet pressure sensor A signal voltage below expected range. Boost control tracks commanded vs. actual manifold pressure; persistent deviation means wastegate, vanes, hose or actuator are not delivering the requested pressure.

t: Turbocharger Outlet Pressure Sensor A Circuit High
s: Turbo outlet pressure sensor A signal voltage above expected range. Boost control tracks commanded vs. actual manifold pressure; persistent deviation means wastegate, vanes, hose or actuator are not delivering the requested pressure.

t: Turbocharger Outlet Pressure Sensor A Intermittent/Erratic
s: Intermittent signal from turbo outlet pressure sensor A. Boost control tracks commanded vs. actual manifold pressure; persistent deviation means wastegate, vanes, hose or actuator are not delivering the requested pressure.

t: Battery B State of Charge Performance
s: State of charge of battery B (typically auxiliary or hybrid battery) shows performance fault — does not behave according to charging model.

t: EVAP System Tank Vapor Line Restricted/Blocked
s: The EVAP tank vapor line is restricted or blocked. Tank pressure cannot equalize properly. The evaporative system captures fuel vapor from the tank and purges it into intake; leaks or stuck purge valves leak hydrocarbons and cause emissions failures.

t: Body Control Module Requested MIL Illumination
s: The Body Control Module (BCM) sent a request to the engine ECU to illuminate the MIL — actual fault is in a body system; this is a forwarded request, not a powertrain fault.

t: Mass or Volume Air Flow Circuit Malfunction
s: The ECU detects a general electrical malfunction in the mass air flow (MAF) sensor circuit. The signal is missing, out of plausible range, or the wiring shows continuity issues. Used as a generic fault when no more specific MAF code applies.

t: Mass or Volume Air Flow Circuit Range/Performance
s: The mass air flow (MAF) sensor signal is outside the expected range or does not track engine load plausibly. The ECU compares MAF readings against load calculated from RPM, throttle position and intake pressure; persistent disagreement triggers this code.

t: Mass or Volume Air Flow Circuit Low Input
s: The MAF sensor reports an air flow value below the plausible minimum for current engine speed and load. The ECU substitutes a calculated load value while the fault is active.

t: Mass or Volume Air Flow Circuit High Input
s: The MAF sensor reports an air flow value above the plausible maximum for current engine speed and load. Often indicates a sensor that is failing in a way that produces excess output, or a short to supply voltage in the signal path.

t: Mass or Volume Air Flow Circuit Intermittent
s: The ECU detects intermittent signal dropouts or noise on the mass air flow (MAF) sensor circuit. The fault is not constantly present, suggesting a loose connection, intermittent wiring break, or a failing sensor under specific conditions (vibration, temperature).

t: Manifold Absolute Pressure / Barometric Pressure Circuit Malfunction
s: The ECU detects a general electrical malfunction in the manifold absolute pressure (MAP) or barometric pressure sensor circuit. Signal missing, implausible, or wiring broken. Generic fault when no more specific MAP code applies.

t: Manifold Absolute Pressure Sensor Range/Performance
s: The manifold absolute pressure (MAP) sensor signal is implausible relative to throttle position and engine speed. The ECU expects MAP to rise with throttle opening and fall under deceleration; deviation triggers the code.

t: Manifold Absolute Pressure Sensor Low Input
s: The MAP sensor reports a pressure below the lower threshold the ECU expects at any engine state. Typically indicates a hard electrical fault rather than a slow drift.

t: Manifold Absolute Pressure Sensor High Input
s: The MAP sensor reports a pressure above the upper threshold the ECU expects in any engine state. Typically caused by a short in the signal circuit or a sensor that has failed producing a fixed high output.

t: Manifold Absolute Pressure / Barometric Pressure Circuit Intermittent
s: The ECU detects intermittent signal dropouts on the MAP/BARO sensor circuit. Fault is not constantly present — typically caused by loose connectors, chafed wiring, or a sensor failing under thermal load.

t: Mass or Volume Air Flow Sensor B Circuit
s: Generic electrical fault on MAF sensor B (second sensor for redundancy or twin-intake systems).

t: Mass or Volume Air Flow Sensor B Circuit Range/Performance
s: MAF sensor B reading implausible. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Mass or Volume Air Flow Sensor B Circuit Low
s: MAF B signal voltage below expected range. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Mass or Volume Air Flow Sensor B Circuit High
s: MAF B signal voltage above expected range. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Mass or Volume Air Flow Sensor B Circuit Intermittent/Erratic
s: Intermittent signal from MAF sensor B. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Mass or Volume Air Flow Sensor A/B Correlation
s: MAF sensor A and B disagree beyond plausible deviation. One is reading incorrectly.

t: Intake Air Temperature Sensor Circuit Malfunction
s: The ECU detects a general electrical fault in the intake air temperature (IAT) sensor circuit. Signal absent, shorted or out of plausible range. Generic catch-all when no more specific IAT code (low/high/range) is set.

t: Intake Air Temperature Sensor Range/Performance
s: The intake air temperature (IAT) sensor reading does not change plausibly with engine warm-up or after driving begins. Many vehicles share the IAT into the MAF housing; on those, MAF replacement also replaces IAT.

t: Intake Air Temperature Sensor Low Input
s: The IAT signal voltage is below threshold, which the ECU interprets as an implausibly hot intake (low NTC resistance). Usually a wiring short to ground or a failed sensor.

t: Intake Air Temperature Sensor High Input
s: The IAT signal voltage is above threshold, which the ECU interprets as an implausibly cold intake (open-circuit-like behaviour). Usually open wiring or a sensor that has failed open.

t: Intake Air Temperature Sensor Intermittent
s: The IAT signal shows intermittent dropouts or jumps that fall outside slope expectations but do not stay above or below threshold long enough to trigger P0112/P0113. Usually a wiring or connector issue.

t: Engine Coolant Temperature Sensor Circuit Malfunction
s: A general fault has been detected in the engine coolant temperature (ECT) sensor circuit. The ECU uses ECT for fuel enrichment during cold start, fan control, and closed-loop entry timing.

t: Engine Coolant Temperature Sensor Range/Performance
s: The coolant temperature sensor reading does not change plausibly during warm-up, or its value disagrees with the intake air temperature when the engine has been off long enough that they should match. Often caused by a sensor that reports near a fixed value despite engine warm-up.

t: Engine Coolant Temperature Sensor Low Input
s: The ECT signal voltage is below threshold, which the ECU interprets as an implausibly hot coolant temperature (typical NTC sensor characteristic — low resistance at high temperature).

t: Engine Coolant Temperature Sensor High Input
s: The ECT signal voltage is above threshold, which the ECU interprets as an implausibly cold temperature (open-circuit-like behaviour). When this code is active, the ECU runs a default cold-start enrichment that wastes fuel and may cause black smoke.

t: Engine Coolant Temperature Sensor Circuit Intermittent
s: The ECU detects intermittent signal dropouts on the engine coolant temperature (ECT) sensor circuit. Fault not constantly present, typically due to loose connector, chafed wiring or a sensor failing thermally.

t: Engine Coolant Temperature Sensor 1/2 Correlation
s: ECT sensors 1 and 2 readings disagree beyond plausible limits — typically near startup both should match within tolerance.

t: Engine Coolant Temperature/Intake Air Temperature Correlation
s: ECT and IAT readings disagree at long soak (engine off for hours) when both should match ambient. The intake air temperature sensor feeds the ECM's density calculation — a drifted reading miscalculates fuel mass and pushes mixture lean (cold reading) or rich (hot reading).

t: Charge Air Cooler Temperature/Intake Air Temperature Correlation Bank 1
s: CAC temperature and IAT on bank 1 disagree at long soak. The intake air temperature sensor feeds the ECM's density calculation — a drifted reading miscalculates fuel mass and pushes mixture lean (cold reading) or rich (hot reading).

t: Charge Air Cooler Temperature/Intake Air Temperature Correlation Bank 2
s: CAC temperature and IAT on bank 2 disagree at long soak. The intake air temperature sensor feeds the ECM's density calculation — a drifted reading miscalculates fuel mass and pushes mixture lean (cold reading) or rich (hot reading).

t: Engine Coolant Temperature 1 / Ambient Air Temperature Correlation
s: ECT 1 and ambient air temperature disagree after long soak — both should match within tolerance. The engine coolant temperature sensor feeds warm-up enrichment, fan control and closed-loop entry — a stuck-cold reading keeps the engine in open loop and rich forever.

t: Engine Coolant Temperature 2 / Ambient Air Temperature Correlation
s: ECT 1 and ambient air temperature disagree after long soak — both should match within tolerance. The engine coolant temperature sensor feeds warm-up enrichment, fan control and closed-loop entry — a stuck-cold reading keeps the engine in open loop and rich forever.

t: Throttle Position Sensor / Switch A Circuit Malfunction
s: The ECU detects a general electrical fault on the throttle position sensor/switch A circuit. Signal missing, implausible, or wiring broken. Generic catch-all when no more specific TPS-A code applies.

t: Throttle/Pedal Position Sensor "A" Range/Performance
s: The throttle position sensor (TPS) signal does not match the expected range for the commanded pedal position. On drive-by-wire vehicles, two redundant sensors are compared; a divergence between them or implausible motion triggers this code.

t: Throttle/Pedal Position Sensor "A" Low Input
s: The TPS signal voltage is below the lower acceptable threshold. The ECU usually enters limp-home mode and limits engine torque while the fault is active.

t: Throttle/Pedal Position Sensor "A" High Input
s: The TPS signal voltage exceeds the upper acceptable threshold. As with P0122, limp-home mode is typical until the fault clears.

t: Throttle/Pedal Position Sensor "A" Circuit Intermittent
s: The TPS signal shows intermittent dropouts that do not stay outside threshold long enough to trigger P0122/P0123. Drive-by-wire systems are sensitive to this and may briefly enter limp-home mode each time.

t: Insufficient Coolant Temperature for Closed Loop Fuel Control
s: The engine has run long enough that closed-loop fuel control should be active, but the coolant temperature has not risen high enough. Often caused by a stuck-open thermostat or a failed coolant temperature sensor.

t: Insufficient Coolant Temperature for Stable Operation
s: Coolant temperature stays below the value expected for stable engine operation. Almost always points to a thermostat stuck open or a sensor reading lower than the actual engine temperature.

t: Intake Air Temperature Too High
s: Intake air temperature exceeds the safe maximum. Risk of preignition or knock; ECU may reduce power.

t: Coolant Thermostat Below Regulating Temperature
s: The engine has been running long enough to reach operating temperature, but coolant temperature stayed below the thermostat's regulating value. Almost always caused by a thermostat stuck open. Costs fuel economy and increases cold-engine emissions.

t: Barometric Pressure Too Low
s: Barometric pressure reading below the plausible minimum. Either operating at extreme altitude or sensor fault.

t: Turbocharger/Supercharger Inlet Pressure Sensor A Circuit
s: Generic electrical fault on turbo/supercharger inlet pressure sensor A. Boost control tracks commanded vs. actual manifold pressure; persistent deviation means wastegate, vanes, hose or actuator are not delivering the requested pressure.

t: Turbocharger/Supercharger Inlet Pressure Sensor A Circuit Range/Performance
s: Turbo inlet pressure sensor A reading implausible. Boost control tracks commanded vs. actual manifold pressure; persistent deviation means wastegate, vanes, hose or actuator are not delivering the requested pressure.

t: Turbocharger/Supercharger Inlet Pressure Sensor A Circuit Low
s: Turbo inlet pressure sensor A signal voltage below expected range. Boost control tracks commanded vs. actual manifold pressure; persistent deviation means wastegate, vanes, hose or actuator are not delivering the requested pressure.

t: Turbocharger/Supercharger Inlet Pressure Sensor A Circuit High
s: Turbo inlet pressure sensor A signal voltage above expected range. Boost control tracks commanded vs. actual manifold pressure; persistent deviation means wastegate, vanes, hose or actuator are not delivering the requested pressure.

t: Turbocharger/Supercharger Inlet Pressure Sensor A Circuit Intermittent/Erratic
s: Intermittent signal from turbo inlet pressure sensor A. Boost control tracks commanded vs. actual manifold pressure; persistent deviation means wastegate, vanes, hose or actuator are not delivering the requested pressure.

t: Engine Coolant Temperature / Engine Oil Temperature Correlation
s: Engine coolant and oil temperature readings disagree beyond plausible limits. The engine coolant temperature sensor feeds warm-up enrichment, fan control and closed-loop entry — a stuck-cold reading keeps the engine in open loop and rich forever.

t: O2 Sensor Circuit Malfunction (Bank 1, Sensor 1)
s: The upstream oxygen sensor on bank 1 reports an implausible signal — typically stuck mid-range, no switching between rich and lean, or no signal at all. Closed-loop fuel control cannot run while this fault is active.

t: O2 Sensor Circuit Low Voltage (Bank 1, Sensor 1)
s: The upstream oxygen sensor signal is fixed at a low voltage, indicating the ECU sees a continuously lean exhaust on bank 1 — or the sensor signal path is shorted to ground.

t: O2 Sensor Circuit High Voltage (Bank 1, Sensor 1)
s: The upstream oxygen sensor signal is fixed at a high voltage, indicating either a continuously rich exhaust on bank 1 or a short to supply voltage in the signal circuit.

t: O2 Sensor Slow Response (Bank 1, Sensor 1)
s: The upstream oxygen sensor switches between rich and lean too slowly. Aged sensors lose response speed before they fail outright; this code typically appears before P0130/P0131/P0132.

t: O2 Sensor Circuit No Activity / Slow Response (Bank 1 Sensor 1)
s: The ECU detects that the upstream oxygen sensor on bank 1 shows no switching activity or responds too slowly to changing exhaust gas composition. Often the sensor is aged, contaminated by silicone or fuel additives, or the heater circuit is degraded.

t: O2 Sensor Heater Circuit Malfunction (Bank 1, Sensor 1)
s: The heating element inside the upstream oxygen sensor draws no current, draws too much current, or is open-circuit. The heater brings the sensor to operating temperature so closed-loop control can start within seconds rather than minutes.

t: O2 Sensor Circuit Malfunction (Bank 1, Sensor 2)
