s: A general fault has been detected in the downstream (post-catalyst) oxygen sensor on bank 1. The downstream sensor monitors catalyst efficiency rather than fuel mixture; with no signal, the catalyst monitor cannot run.

t: O2 Sensor Circuit Low Voltage (Bank 1, Sensor 2)
s: The downstream oxygen sensor signal is fixed at a low voltage. Either the catalyst is letting unburned oxygen through (sensor sees lean), the sensor itself is shorted to ground, or the catalyst is so degraded that the downstream signal mirrors a lean upstream condition.

t: O2 Sensor Circuit High Voltage (Bank 1, Sensor 2)
s: The downstream oxygen sensor signal is fixed at a high voltage, indicating either a continuously rich downstream reading (less common, since post-cat exhaust should be more stable) or a short to supply voltage in the signal circuit.

t: O2 Sensor Circuit High Voltage (Bank 1 Sensor 2)
s: The ECU detects that the downstream oxygen sensor on bank 1 reports an abnormally high voltage signal, suggesting a persistently rich exhaust condition past the catalyst, a shorted sensor, or wiring shorted to a voltage source.

t: O2 Sensor Slow Response - Rich to Lean Bank 1 Sensor 2
s: Post-cat O2 sensor on bank 1 responds too slowly when mixture transitions from rich to lean. Sensor aging or contamination.

t: O2 Sensor Slow Response - Lean to Rich Bank 1 Sensor 2
s: Post-cat O2 sensor on bank 1 responds too slowly when mixture transitions from lean to rich. A drifted or sluggish lambda sensor lets the mixture run lean or rich without correction — fuel economy and emissions suffer immediately.

t: O2 Sensor Slow Response - Rich to Lean Bank 2 Sensor 2
s: Post-cat O2 sensor on bank 2 responds too slowly when mixture transitions from rich to lean. Sensor aging or contamination.

t: O2 Sensor Slow Response - Lean to Rich Bank 2 Sensor 2
s: Post-cat O2 sensor on bank 2 responds too slowly when mixture transitions from lean to rich. A drifted or sluggish lambda sensor lets the mixture run lean or rich without correction — fuel economy and emissions suffer immediately.

t: O2 Sensor Delayed Response - Rich to Lean Bank 1 Sensor 2
s: Post-cat O2 sensor on bank 1 reacts only after delay when mixture switches rich to lean. A drifted or sluggish lambda sensor lets the mixture run lean or rich without correction — fuel economy and emissions suffer immediately.

t: O2 Sensor Delayed Response - Lean to Rich Bank 1 Sensor 2
s: Post-cat O2 sensor on bank 1 reacts only after delay when mixture switches lean to rich. A drifted or sluggish lambda sensor lets the mixture run lean or rich without correction — fuel economy and emissions suffer immediately.

t: O2 Sensor Circuit No Activity / Slow Response (Bank 1 Sensor 2)
s: The ECU detects no switching or excessively slow response from the downstream oxygen sensor on bank 1. Typical for an aged or poisoned sensor; downstream sensors should show a relatively flat signal but must respond to fueling changes during diagnostic monitor runs.

t: O2 Sensor Heater Circuit Malfunction (Bank 1, Sensor 2)
s: The heating element inside the downstream oxygen sensor on bank 1 is open-circuit, drawing no current, or drawing too much. Without heater, the sensor takes much longer to reach operating temperature, delaying catalyst diagnosis.

t: O2 Sensor Heater Circuit Malfunction (Bank 1 Sensor 2)
s: The ECU detects an electrical fault in the heater circuit of the downstream oxygen sensor on bank 1. The heater current is out of range, suggesting an open or shorted heater element, blown fuse or wiring fault.

t: O2 Sensor Circuit Malfunction (Bank 1 Sensor 3)
s: The ECU detects a general electrical fault on the third oxygen sensor in the bank 1 exhaust path (typically located after a second catalyst on diesel or large engines with multi-cat exhausts).

t: O2 Sensor Circuit Low Voltage (Bank 1 Sensor 3)
s: The ECU detects an abnormally low voltage from the third O2 sensor on bank 1. Indicates persistently lean exhaust, an open sensor or wiring shorted to ground.

t: O2 Sensor Circuit High Voltage (Bank 1 Sensor 3)
s: The ECU detects an abnormally high voltage from the third O2 sensor on bank 1. Indicates persistently rich exhaust, a shorted sensor or wiring shorted to a voltage source.

t: O2 Sensor Circuit No Activity / Slow Response (Bank 1 Sensor 3)
s: The ECU detects no switching activity or too slow a response from the third O2 sensor on bank 1. Typical for aged or poisoned sensor.

t: O2 Sensor Heater Circuit Malfunction (Bank 1 Sensor 3)
s: The ECU detects an electrical fault in the heater circuit of the third O2 sensor on bank 1. Heater current out of range, indicating open/shorted heater element, blown fuse or wiring fault.

t: Fuel Delivery Error
s: Fuel quantity actually delivered does not match the commanded value over a long observation window. Mechanical issue in the fuel system.

t: Fuel Timing Error
s: Injection timing deviates from target. Often caused by chain wear, sensor offset, or injector response drift.

t: O2 Sensor Delayed Response - Rich to Lean Bank 2 Sensor 2
s: Post-cat O2 sensor on bank 2 reacts only after delay when mixture switches rich to lean. A drifted or sluggish lambda sensor lets the mixture run lean or rich without correction — fuel economy and emissions suffer immediately.

t: O2 Sensor Delayed Response - Lean to Rich Bank 2 Sensor 2
s: Post-cat O2 sensor on bank 2 reacts only after delay when mixture switches lean to rich. A drifted or sluggish lambda sensor lets the mixture run lean or rich without correction — fuel economy and emissions suffer immediately.

t: O2 Sensor Slow Response - Rich to Lean Bank 1 Sensor 1
s: Pre-cat O2 sensor on bank 1 responds too slowly when mixture transitions from rich to lean. A drifted or sluggish lambda sensor lets the mixture run lean or rich without correction — fuel economy and emissions suffer immediately.

t: O2 Sensor Slow Response - Lean to Rich Bank 1 Sensor 1
s: Pre-cat O2 sensor on bank 1 responds too slowly when mixture transitions from lean to rich. A drifted or sluggish lambda sensor lets the mixture run lean or rich without correction — fuel economy and emissions suffer immediately.

t: O2 Sensor Slow Response - Rich to Lean Bank 2 Sensor 1
s: Pre-cat O2 sensor on bank 2 responds too slowly when mixture transitions from rich to lean. A drifted or sluggish lambda sensor lets the mixture run lean or rich without correction — fuel economy and emissions suffer immediately.

t: O2 Sensor Slow Response - Lean to Rich Bank 2 Sensor 1
s: Pre-cat O2 sensor on bank 2 responds too slowly when mixture transitions from lean to rich. A drifted or sluggish lambda sensor lets the mixture run lean or rich without correction — fuel economy and emissions suffer immediately.

t: O2 Sensor Circuit Malfunction (Bank 2, Sensor 1)
s: Same condition as P0130 but on bank 2 of a multi-bank engine. Closed-loop fuel control on bank 2 is disabled while this fault is active.

t: O2 Sensor Circuit Low Voltage (Bank 2, Sensor 1)
s: Same condition as P0131 but on bank 2. The upstream sensor sees a continuously lean exhaust on bank 2 or has a signal short to ground.

t: O2 Sensor Circuit High Voltage (Bank 2, Sensor 1)
s: Same condition as P0132 but on bank 2. The upstream sensor reports a continuously rich condition or has a short to supply voltage.

t: O2 Sensor Circuit High Voltage (Bank 2 Sensor 1)
s: The ECU detects an abnormally high voltage from the upstream O2 sensor on bank 2. Indicates persistently rich exhaust, shorted sensor or wiring shorted to voltage.

t: O2 Sensor Circuit No Activity / Slow Response (Bank 2 Sensor 1)
s: The ECU detects no switching activity or too slow a response from the upstream O2 sensor on bank 2. Typically aged or poisoned sensor, weak heater, or upstream exhaust leak.

t: O2 Sensor Heater Circuit Malfunction (Bank 2, Sensor 1)
s: Same condition as P0135 but on the upstream sensor of bank 2. The sensor heater warms the element to operating temperature (~600 °C); without it the lambda sensor stays inactive in closed loop and the ECM cannot trim mixture.

t: O2 Sensor Circuit Malfunction (Bank 2, Sensor 2)
s: Same condition as P0136 but on the downstream sensor of bank 2. The catalyst efficiency monitor for bank 2 cannot run while this fault is active.

t: O2 Sensor Circuit Low Voltage (Bank 2 Sensor 2)
s: The ECU detects abnormally low voltage from the downstream O2 sensor on bank 2. Indicates persistently lean exhaust past the catalyst, open sensor or wiring shorted to ground.

t: O2 Sensor Circuit Low Voltage (Bank 2 Sensor 2)
s: The ECU detects abnormally low voltage from the downstream O2 sensor on bank 2 (alternate definition mapping in some manufacturers' interpretation). Indicates persistent lean reading, open sensor or wiring shorted to ground.

t: O2 Sensor Circuit High Voltage (Bank 2 Sensor 2)
s: The ECU detects an abnormally high voltage from the downstream O2 sensor on bank 2. Indicates persistently rich exhaust past the cat, shorted sensor or wiring shorted to voltage.

t: O2 Sensor Delayed Response - Rich to Lean Bank 1 Sensor 1
s: Pre-cat O2 sensor on bank 1 reacts only after delay when mixture switches rich to lean. A drifted or sluggish lambda sensor lets the mixture run lean or rich without correction — fuel economy and emissions suffer immediately.

t: O2 Sensor Delayed Response - Lean to Rich Bank 1 Sensor 1
s: Pre-cat O2 sensor on bank 1 reacts only after delay when mixture switches lean to rich. A drifted or sluggish lambda sensor lets the mixture run lean or rich without correction — fuel economy and emissions suffer immediately.

t: O2 Sensor Delayed Response - Rich to Lean Bank 2 Sensor 1
s: Pre-cat O2 sensor on bank 2 reacts only after delay when mixture switches rich to lean. A drifted or sluggish lambda sensor lets the mixture run lean or rich without correction — fuel economy and emissions suffer immediately.

t: O2 Sensor Delayed Response - Lean to Rich Bank 2 Sensor 1
s: Pre-cat O2 sensor on bank 2 reacts only after delay when mixture switches lean to rich. A drifted or sluggish lambda sensor lets the mixture run lean or rich without correction — fuel economy and emissions suffer immediately.

t: Excessive Time to Enter Closed Loop Fuel Timing Control
s: ECU took longer than allowed to enter closed-loop control of fuel injection timing. Often slow sensor warm-up.

t: Excessive Time to Enter Closed Loop Cylinder Balance Control
s: ECU took longer than allowed to enter closed-loop cylinder balance control.

t: O2 Sensor Circuit No Activity / Slow Response (Bank 2 Sensor 2)
s: The ECU detects no switching or too slow a response from the downstream O2 sensor on bank 2. Often aged or poisoned sensor; downstream sensors usually flat but must respond on diagnostic monitor runs.

t: O2 Sensor Heater Circuit Malfunction (Bank 2, Sensor 2)
s: Same condition as P0141 but on the downstream sensor of bank 2. The sensor heater warms the element to operating temperature (~600 °C); without it the lambda sensor stays inactive in closed loop and the ECM cannot trim mixture.

t: O2 Sensor Heater Circuit Malfunction (Bank 2 Sensor 2)
s: The ECU detects an electrical fault in the heater circuit of the downstream O2 sensor on bank 2. Heater current out of range — open or shorted heater element, blown fuse, or wiring fault.

t: O2 Sensor Circuit Malfunction (Bank 2 Sensor 3)
s: The ECU detects a general electrical fault on the third O2 sensor in the bank 2 exhaust path (typically post-second-cat in multi-cat exhausts).

t: O2 Sensor Circuit Low Voltage (Bank 2 Sensor 3)
s: The ECU detects abnormally low voltage from the third O2 sensor on bank 2. Indicates lean exhaust, open sensor or wiring shorted to ground.

t: O2 Sensor Circuit High Voltage (Bank 2 Sensor 3)
s: The ECU detects abnormally high voltage from the third O2 sensor on bank 2. Indicates rich exhaust or wiring shorted to voltage.

t: O2 Sensor Circuit No Activity / Slow Response (Bank 2 Sensor 3)
s: The ECU detects no switching or too slow a response from the third O2 sensor on bank 2. Typically aged or poisoned sensor or weak heater.

t: O2 Sensor Heater Circuit Malfunction (Bank 2 Sensor 3)
s: The ECU detects an electrical fault in the heater circuit of the third O2 sensor on bank 2. Heater current out of range — open or shorted heater, blown fuse, wiring fault.

t: Fuel Temperature Too High
s: Fuel temperature exceeds the safe maximum. Risk of fuel pump damage and injection imprecision.

t: Incorrect Fuel Composition
s: Fuel composition (e.g. ethanol fraction on flex-fuel vehicles) outside expected range. Wrong fuel filled or sensor fault.

t: Excessive Time To Enter Closed Loop Air/Fuel Ratio Control
s: ECU took longer than allowed to enter closed-loop air/fuel ratio control. Most often O2 sensor heater aging or coolant temperature reading wrong.

t: Closed Loop Air/Fuel Ratio Control At Limit - System Too Rich
s: Closed-loop fuel trim hit its lean correction limit because mixture is structurally too rich. Likely fuel pressure too high, leaking injector, or O2 sensor reporting too lean.

t: Closed Loop Air/Fuel Ratio Control At Limit - System Too Lean
s: Closed-loop fuel trim hit its rich correction limit because mixture is structurally too lean. Likely vacuum leak, low fuel pressure, or contaminated MAF.

t: Excessive Time To Enter Closed Loop Fuel Pressure Control
s: ECU took longer than allowed to close the loop on fuel pressure control. Pressure rise too slow at start.

t: Closed Loop Fuel Pressure Control At Limit - Pressure Too High
s: Closed-loop fuel pressure control hit its lower limit because rail pressure is structurally too high.

t: Closed Loop Fuel Pressure Control At Limit - Pressure Too Low
s: Closed-loop fuel pressure control hit its upper limit because rail pressure is structurally too low.

t: Fuel Trim Malfunction (Bank 1)
s: The ECU detects that short-term and long-term fuel trim values on bank 1 are outside acceptable limits. Engine cannot maintain stoichiometric mixture — typically a vacuum leak, fuel pressure issue, or failing MAF/O2 sensor.

t: System Too Lean (Bank 1)
s: Long-term fuel trim on bank 1 is at the upper limit — the ECU is adding more fuel than baseline to maintain stoichiometric mixture but cannot reach target. Indicates unmetered air entering the intake or insufficient fuel delivery.

t: System Too Rich (Bank 1)
s: Long-term fuel trim on bank 1 is at the lower limit — the ECU is removing fuel from baseline but mixture remains too rich. Indicates excess fuel delivery, restricted air flow, or a faulty oxygen sensor reporting falsely lean.

t: Fuel Trim Malfunction Bank 2
s: Fuel trims on bank 2 exceed adaptation limits in either lean or rich direction. The ECU cannot maintain stoichiometric mixture within calibrated boundaries.

t: System Too Lean (Bank 2)
s: Same condition as P0171 but on bank 2 of a multi-bank engine (V6, V8, flat-six, etc.). When P0171 and P0174 appear together, the cause is usually a shared component such as the MAF sensor or a central vacuum leak rather than a bank-specific fault.

t: System Too Rich (Bank 2)
s: Same condition as P0172 but on bank 2. When paired with P0172, suspect shared causes (fuel pressure, blocked air filter); when alone, suspect a bank-specific injector or oxygen sensor.

t: Fuel Composition Sensor Circuit Malfunction
s: The ECU detects an electrical fault in the flex-fuel composition sensor circuit. The sensor measures ethanol content (E0–E85) in the fuel; signal missing, shorted or out of plausible range.

t: Fuel Composition Sensor Circuit Range / Performance
s: The flex-fuel composition sensor signal is within electrical limits but does not match expected values for the current fuel mix or rate-of-change is implausible.

t: Fuel Composition Sensor Circuit Low Input
s: The flex-fuel composition sensor signal is below the minimum threshold. Likely shorted to ground, open circuit or failed sensor.

t: Fuel Composition Sensor Circuit High Input
s: The flex-fuel composition sensor signal exceeds the maximum threshold. Likely shorted to voltage, open ground or failed sensor.

t: Cylinder Head Temperature Sensor A Circuit
s: Generic electrical fault on cylinder head temperature sensor A (CHT sensor — used on engines without conventional ECT).

t: Cylinder Head Temperature Sensor A Range/Performance
s: CHT sensor A reading implausible.

t: Cylinder Head Temperature Sensor A Circuit Low
s: CHT sensor A signal voltage below expected range. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Cylinder Head Temperature Sensor A Circuit High

t: EVAP System Leak Detection Pump Module Air Filter Restricted
s: Air filter on the EVAP leak detection pump module is restricted. Leak detection cannot establish reference pressure.

t: Fuel Temperature Sensor A Circuit Malfunction
s: The ECU detects an electrical fault in the fuel temperature sensor A circuit. Fuel temperature input is missing or out of plausible range — typical for sensor failure or wiring open/short.

t: Fuel Temperature Sensor A Circuit Range / Performance
s: The fuel temperature sensor A signal is within electrical limits but inconsistent with plausibility checks (e.g. doesn't change with engine warmup, disagrees with ambient temperature).

t: Fuel Temperature Sensor A Circuit Low Input
s: The fuel temperature sensor A signal is below the minimum threshold. Likely shorted to ground or open circuit.

t: Fuel Temperature Sensor A Circuit High Input
s: The fuel temperature sensor A signal exceeds the maximum threshold. Likely shorted to voltage, open ground or sensor open-circuit.

t: Fuel Temperature Sensor A Circuit Intermittent
s: The ECU detects intermittent dropouts on the fuel temperature sensor A circuit. Loose connector, chafed wiring or sensor failing thermally.

t: Fuel Temperature Sensor B Circuit Malfunction
s: The ECU detects an electrical fault in the fuel temperature sensor B circuit (second sensor used on systems with separate supply/return temperature monitoring or twin-tank arrangements).

t: Fuel Temperature Sensor B Circuit Range / Performance

t: Fuel Temperature Sensor B Circuit Low Input

t: Fuel Temperature Sensor B Circuit High Input

t: Fuel Temperature Sensor B Circuit Intermittent

t: Fuel System Over Pressure Relief Valve Frequent Activation
s: The mechanical pressure relief valve has activated repeatedly within a short window — pressure regulation is failing more than once.

t: Fuel Rail Pressure Sensor Circuit
s: The ECU detects an out-of-range signal from the fuel rail pressure sensor. The voltage is outside calibrated min/max boundaries during normal operation.

t: Fuel Rail Pressure Sensor Circuit Range / Performance
s: The fuel rail pressure sensor signal is within electrical limits but doesn't match commanded rail pressure under various engine loads. Common in common-rail diesel and GDI petrol engines.

t: Fuel Rail Pressure Sensor Circuit Low Input
s: The ECU reads a voltage from the fuel rail pressure sensor below the calibrated minimum, indicating short to ground or open supply.

t: Fuel Rail Pressure Sensor Circuit High Input
s: The ECU reads a voltage from the fuel rail pressure sensor above the calibrated maximum, indicating short to power or open ground reference.

t: Fuel Rail Pressure Sensor Circuit Intermittent
s: The ECU detects intermittent signal dropouts on the fuel rail pressure sensor. Loose connector, chafed wiring or sensor failing under vibration/heat.

t: Engine Oil Temperature Sensor Malfunction
s: The ECU detects a general electrical fault in the engine oil temperature sensor circuit. Signal missing, shorted or out of plausible range.

t: Engine Oil Temperature Sensor Range / Performance
s: Engine oil temperature sensor signal is within electrical limits but implausible (no warmup change, contradicts coolant temperature).

t: Engine Oil Temperature Sensor Low
s: Engine oil temperature sensor signal below minimum. Shorted to ground or open.

t: Engine Oil Temperature Sensor High
s: Engine oil temperature sensor signal exceeds maximum. Shorted to voltage, open ground or open sensor.

t: Engine Oil Temperature Sensor Intermittent
s: The ECU detects intermittent dropouts on the engine oil temperature sensor circuit. Loose connector, chafed wiring or sensor failing thermally.

t: O2 Sensor Delayed Response - Rich to Lean Bank 1 Sensor 3
s: Bank 1 sensor 3 O2 sensor reacts only after delay during rich-to-lean transitions. A drifted or sluggish lambda sensor lets the mixture run lean or rich without correction — fuel economy and emissions suffer immediately.

t: Engine Coolant Temperature Sensor 3 Circuit
s: Generic electrical fault on engine coolant temperature sensor 3 (third measurement location, e.g. radiator outlet or heater core).

t: Engine Coolant Temperature Sensor 3 Circuit Range/Performance
s: ECT sensor 3 reading implausible. The engine coolant temperature sensor feeds warm-up enrichment, fan control and closed-loop entry — a stuck-cold reading keeps the engine in open loop and rich forever.

t: Engine Coolant Temperature Sensor 3 Circuit Low
s: ECT sensor 3 signal voltage below expected range. The engine coolant temperature sensor feeds warm-up enrichment, fan control and closed-loop entry — a stuck-cold reading keeps the engine in open loop and rich forever.

t: Engine Coolant Temperature Sensor 3 Circuit High
s: ECT sensor 3 signal voltage above expected range. The engine coolant temperature sensor feeds warm-up enrichment, fan control and closed-loop entry — a stuck-cold reading keeps the engine in open loop and rich forever.

t: Engine Coolant Temperature Sensor 3 Circuit Intermittent/Erratic
s: Intermittent signal from ECT sensor 3. The engine coolant temperature sensor feeds warm-up enrichment, fan control and closed-loop entry — a stuck-cold reading keeps the engine in open loop and rich forever.

t: Engine Coolant Heater A Control Circuit/Open
s: Open circuit at engine coolant heater A (electric heater used for fast warm-up or hybrid coolant management).

t: Engine Coolant Heater A Control Circuit Low
s: Coolant heater A control voltage below expected range. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Engine Coolant Heater A Control Circuit High
s: Coolant heater A control voltage above expected range. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Engine Coolant Heater A Control Circuit Performance
s: Coolant heater A does not heat the coolant as expected when commanded.

t: Engine Coolant Heater B Control Circuit/Open
s: Open circuit at engine coolant heater A (electric heater used for fast warm-up or hybrid coolant management).

t: Engine Coolant Heater B Control Circuit Low
s: Coolant heater A control voltage below expected range. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Engine Coolant Heater B Control Circuit High
s: Coolant heater A control voltage above expected range. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Engine Coolant Heater B Control Circuit Performance
s: Coolant heater A does not heat the coolant as expected when commanded.

t: Coolant Temperature Relapsed Below Diagnostic Monitoring Temperature
s: Coolant temperature dropped below the diagnostic monitoring threshold after warm-up. Often thermostat stuck open or stuck-open coolant heater bypass.

t: O2 Sensor Circuit Bank 1 Sensor 4
s: Generic electrical fault on the bank 1, sensor 4 oxygen sensor (fourth sensor downstream — used on systems with multiple cat stages or NOx-trap diagnosis).

t: O2 Sensor Circuit Low Voltage Bank 1 Sensor 4
s: Bank 1 sensor 4 O2 sensor signal voltage below expected range — short to ground or sensor stuck low.

t: O2 Sensor Circuit High Voltage Bank 1 Sensor 4
s: Bank 1 sensor 4 O2 sensor signal voltage above expected range — short to power or sensor stuck high. A drifted or sluggish lambda sensor lets the mixture run lean or rich without correction — fuel economy and emissions suffer immediately.

t: O2 Sensor Circuit Slow Response Bank 1 Sensor 4
s: Bank 1 sensor 4 O2 sensor responds too slowly to mixture changes. Sensor aged. A drifted or sluggish lambda sensor lets the mixture run lean or rich without correction — fuel economy and emissions suffer immediately.

t: O2 Sensor Circuit No Activity Detected Bank 1 Sensor 4
s: Bank 1 sensor 4 O2 sensor shows no signal change despite operating conditions that should produce activity. A drifted or sluggish lambda sensor lets the mixture run lean or rich without correction — fuel economy and emissions suffer immediately.

t: O2 Sensor Signal Biased/Stuck Lean Bank 1 Sensor 4
s: Bank 1 sensor 4 O2 sensor signal is biased low — sensor stuck reading lean regardless of true mixture. A drifted or sluggish lambda sensor lets the mixture run lean or rich without correction — fuel economy and emissions suffer immediately.

t: O2 Sensor Signal Biased/Stuck Rich Bank 1 Sensor 4
s: Bank 1 sensor 4 O2 sensor signal is biased high — sensor stuck reading rich regardless of true mixture. A drifted or sluggish lambda sensor lets the mixture run lean or rich without correction — fuel economy and emissions suffer immediately.

t: Engine Coolant Heater A Control Circuit Driver Current/Temperature Too High
s: Driver stage of coolant heater A reports overcurrent or overtemperature. Likely short or thermal shutdown.

t: Engine Coolant Heater B Control Circuit Driver Current/Temperature Too High
s: Driver stage of coolant heater A reports overcurrent or overtemperature. Likely short or thermal shutdown.

t: Turbocharger Outlet Pressure Sensor B Range/Performance
s: Turbo outlet pressure sensor B reading implausible. Boost control tracks commanded vs. actual manifold pressure; persistent deviation means wastegate, vanes, hose or actuator are not delivering the requested pressure.

t: Turbocharger Outlet Pressure Sensor B Circuit Low
s: Turbo outlet pressure sensor B signal voltage below expected range. Boost control tracks commanded vs. actual manifold pressure; persistent deviation means wastegate, vanes, hose or actuator are not delivering the requested pressure.

t: Turbocharger Outlet Pressure Sensor B Circuit High
s: Turbo outlet pressure sensor B signal voltage above expected range. Boost control tracks commanded vs. actual manifold pressure; persistent deviation means wastegate, vanes, hose or actuator are not delivering the requested pressure.

t: Turbocharger Outlet Pressure Sensor B Intermittent/Erratic
s: Intermittent signal from turbo outlet pressure sensor B. Boost control tracks commanded vs. actual manifold pressure; persistent deviation means wastegate, vanes, hose or actuator are not delivering the requested pressure.

t: Turbocharger Outlet Pressure Sensor A/B Correlation
s: Turbocharger outlet pressure sensors A and B disagree beyond plausible deviation. Boost control tracks commanded vs. actual manifold pressure; persistent deviation means wastegate, vanes, hose or actuator are not delivering the requested pressure.

t: Injector Circuit/Open
s: Generic open circuit on the injector circuit (no specific cylinder reported). Often a wiring issue affecting multiple injectors.

t: Injector Circuit Malfunction — Cylinder 1
s: An electrical fault has been detected in the cylinder 1 injector circuit — open winding, short to ground or short to supply. This typically disables that injector to protect the driver stage in the ECU; the cylinder runs without fuel and triggers a misfire code as well.

t: Cold Start Injector 1
s: Fault detected on cold start injector 1 (auxiliary injector for cold engine starting on some older systems). A flow-deviating injector throws off cylinder balance — adjacent cylinders run leaner/richer to compensate, often masking the root cause until the trim limit is hit.

t: Cold Start Injector 2
s: Fault detected on cold start injector 2. A flow-deviating injector throws off cylinder balance — adjacent cylinders run leaner/richer to compensate, often masking the root cause until the trim limit is hit.

t: Engine Shutoff Solenoid
s: Fault detected on the engine shutoff solenoid (used on diesels and some petrol engines to stop fuel flow at engine off).

t: Injector/Injection Timing Control Circuit
s: Fault on the injection timing control circuit (used on mechanical injection pumps with electronic timing control).

t: Engine Coolant Over Temperature Condition
s: Coolant temperature exceeded the safe maximum. Engine overheating event detected — risk of head gasket damage or seizure.

t: Transmission Fluid Over Temperature
s: The TCM/PCM detected that automatic transmission fluid temperature exceeded the safe operating threshold. Continued operation accelerates wear on clutches and friction surfaces; many vehicles will engage a fail-safe mode and limit gear selection.

t: Engine Overspeed Condition
s: The ECU recorded an engine speed above the manufacturer's redline limit. Typical causes are missed downshifts, mis-shifts on a manual gearbox, or runaway diesel events. The code is informational but may trigger fuel cut-off and protective derate.

t: Cylinder 7 Injection Timing
s: Generic injection timing fault on cylinder 7.

t: Cylinder 8 Injection Timing
s: Generic injection timing fault on cylinder 8.

t: Cylinder 9 Injection Timing
s: Generic injection timing fault on cylinder 9.

t: Cylinder 10 Injection Timing
s: Generic injection timing fault on cylinder 10.

t: Cylinder 11 Injection Timing
s: Generic injection timing fault on cylinder 11.

t: Cylinder 12 Injection Timing
s: Generic injection timing fault on cylinder 12.

t: Throttle/Pedal Position Sensor/Switch "B" Circuit
s: A general electrical fault was detected in the secondary (B) throttle or pedal position sensor circuit. Modern drive-by-wire systems use redundant TPS tracks; the ECU compares them and flags this code on the B channel when out of range.

t: Throttle/Pedal Position Sensor/Switch "B" Range/Performance
s: The B channel of the throttle or pedal position sensor reports values that do not correlate with the A channel or with engine load. Often caused by mechanical wear in the resistive track or by a sticking pedal/throttle plate.

t: Throttle/Pedal Position Sensor/Switch "B" Circuit Low Input
s: The B channel voltage is below the lower diagnostic threshold, suggesting an open circuit, short to ground, or sensor element failure on track B.

t: Throttle/Pedal Position Sensor/Switch "B" Circuit High Input
s: The B channel voltage is above the upper diagnostic threshold, typically caused by a short to supply voltage or a defective sensor.

t: Throttle/Pedal Position Sensor/Switch B Circuit Intermittent
s: Intermittent signal from throttle position sensor B (electronic throttle systems use redundant sensors).

t: Throttle/Pedal Position Sensor/Switch C Circuit
s: Generic electrical fault on throttle/pedal position sensor C (third sensor in some redundant designs).

t: Throttle/Pedal Position Sensor C Circuit Range/Performance
s: Throttle position sensor C signal is not tracking the other channels of the redundant TPS within tolerance. The ECU compares all three channels and detects rationality failure.

t: Throttle/Pedal Position Sensor/Switch C Circuit Low
s: Throttle position sensor C signal voltage below expected range. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Throttle/Pedal Position Sensor/Switch C Circuit High
s: Throttle position sensor C signal voltage above expected range. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Throttle/Pedal Position Sensor/Switch C Circuit Intermittent
s: Intermittent signal from throttle position sensor C. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Charge Air Cooler Bypass Control A Circuit/Open
s: Open circuit at charge air cooler bypass control A — bypass valve cannot be commanded.

t: Charge Air Cooler Bypass Control A Circuit Low
s: CAC bypass control A voltage below expected range. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Charge Air Cooler Bypass Control A Circuit High
s: CAC bypass control A voltage above expected range. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Charge Air Cooler Bypass Control B Circuit/Open
s: Open circuit at charge air cooler bypass control A — bypass valve cannot be commanded.

t: Charge Air Cooler Bypass Control B Circuit Low
s: CAC bypass control A voltage below expected range. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Charge Air Cooler Bypass Control B Circuit High
s: CAC bypass control A voltage above expected range. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Fuel Pump Primary Circuit Malfunction
s: An electrical fault in the fuel pump primary control circuit (relay or driver). The ECU detects an open, short, or out-of-range condition on the relay/driver output.

t: Fuel Pump Secondary Circuit Low
s: The voltage on the secondary side of the fuel pump circuit is below the expected level when the pump is commanded on. Often caused by a failed pump motor, corroded ground, or wiring fault.

t: Fuel Pump Secondary Circuit High
s: The voltage on the secondary side of the fuel pump circuit is higher than expected. Typical of a stuck relay or short to supply voltage that keeps the pump powered when it should be off.

t: Fuel Pump Secondary Circuit Intermittent
s: Intermittent fault on the secondary fuel pump circuit (transfer/lift pump or low-pressure stage). Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Turbocharger/Supercharger "A" Overboost Condition
s: Boost pressure exceeded the upper limit during commanded full load. The ECU usually drops fuel and ignition or commands the wastegate fully open to protect the engine; persistent overboost can crack pistons or rings.

t: Turbocharger/Supercharger Boost Sensor "A" Circuit
s: The ECU detected an electrical fault in the primary (A) boost pressure sensor circuit. The signal does not match plausibility checks against MAP, RPM, and load. With no boost feedback, the ECU typically limits boost via the wastegate.

t: Turbocharger/Supercharger Boost Sensor "A" Circuit Range/Performance
s: The boost pressure A signal stays within electrical limits but does not match expected values for the current operating point. Typical causes are leaking charge piping, a stuck wastegate, or sensor drift.

t: Turbocharger/Supercharger Boost Sensor "A" Circuit Low
s: The boost pressure A signal voltage is below the lower diagnostic threshold — open circuit, short to ground, or sensor element failure.

t: Turbocharger/Supercharger Boost Sensor "A" Circuit High
s: The boost pressure A signal voltage is above the upper diagnostic threshold — short to supply voltage or defective sensor.

t: Turbocharger/Supercharger Boost Sensor B Circuit
s: Generic electrical fault on boost pressure sensor B (twin-turbo or two-stage system). Boost control tracks commanded vs. actual manifold pressure; persistent deviation means wastegate, vanes, hose or actuator are not delivering the requested pressure.

t: Charge Air Cooler Coolant Pump A Control Circuit/Open
s: Open circuit at charge air cooler coolant pump A (water-cooled CAC system).

t: Charge Air Cooler Coolant Pump A Control Circuit Low
s: CAC coolant pump A control voltage below expected range. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Charge Air Cooler Coolant Pump A Control Circuit High
s: CAC coolant pump A control voltage above expected range. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Manifold Absolute Pressure - Turbocharger/Supercharger Boost Sensor A Correlation
s: Manifold absolute pressure and boost sensor A readings disagree beyond plausible deviation. Boost control tracks commanded vs. actual manifold pressure; persistent deviation means wastegate, vanes, hose or actuator are not delivering the requested pressure.

t: Manifold Absolute Pressure - Turbocharger/Supercharger Boost Sensor B Correlation
s: Manifold absolute pressure and boost sensor B readings disagree beyond plausible deviation. Boost control tracks commanded vs. actual manifold pressure; persistent deviation means wastegate, vanes, hose or actuator are not delivering the requested pressure.

t: Fuel Pump Secondary Circuit/Open
s: Open circuit on the secondary fuel pump (transfer/lift pump). Pump cannot be driven.

t: Turbocharger/Supercharger Boost Sensor B Circuit Range/Performance
s: Boost pressure sensor B reading implausible. Boost control tracks commanded vs. actual manifold pressure; persistent deviation means wastegate, vanes, hose or actuator are not delivering the requested pressure.

t: Turbocharger/Supercharger Boost Sensor B Circuit Low
s: Boost pressure sensor B signal voltage below expected range. Boost control tracks commanded vs. actual manifold pressure; persistent deviation means wastegate, vanes, hose or actuator are not delivering the requested pressure.

t: Turbocharger/Supercharger Boost Sensor B Circuit High
s: Boost pressure sensor B signal voltage above expected range. Boost control tracks commanded vs. actual manifold pressure; persistent deviation means wastegate, vanes, hose or actuator are not delivering the requested pressure.

t: Turbocharger/Supercharger Wastegate Actuator A
s: Generic fault on wastegate actuator A. Boost control tracks commanded vs. actual manifold pressure; persistent deviation means wastegate, vanes, hose or actuator are not delivering the requested pressure.

t: Turbocharger/Supercharger Wastegate Actuator A Range/Performance
s: Wastegate actuator A does not respond mechanically as expected. Linkage wear or carbon binding. Boost control tracks commanded vs. actual manifold pressure; persistent deviation means wastegate, vanes, hose or actuator are not delivering the requested pressure.

t: Turbocharger/Supercharger Wastegate Solenoid "A" Low
s: The ECU detected the wastegate control solenoid A circuit voltage below the lower threshold — typically open winding or short to ground. With the solenoid inactive, the wastegate either stays open (no boost) or fully closed (overboost), depending on system layout.

t: Turbocharger/Supercharger Wastegate Solenoid "A" High
s: The ECU detected the wastegate control solenoid A circuit voltage below the lower threshold — typically open winding or short to ground. With the solenoid inactive, the wastegate either stays open (no boost) or fully closed (overboost), depending on system layout.

t: Turbocharger Wastegate Solenoid B Low
s: The control circuit for wastegate solenoid B reports a voltage below the expected range. Likely a short to ground, open winding or wiring fault.

t: Turbocharger Wastegate Solenoid B High
s: The control circuit for wastegate solenoid B reports a voltage above the expected range. Typical causes are a short to power or an open ground.

t: Turbocharger/Supercharger Wastegate Actuator B Low
s: Wastegate actuator B control voltage below expected range. Boost control tracks commanded vs. actual manifold pressure; persistent deviation means wastegate, vanes, hose or actuator are not delivering the requested pressure.

t: Charge Air Cooler Bypass Control A Range/Performance
s: CAC bypass A does not respond mechanically as expected — bypass valve binding or actuator weak.

t: Charge Air Cooler Bypass Control A Stuck
s: CAC bypass A is mechanically stuck and does not move when commanded.

t: Charge Air Cooler Bypass Position Sensor A Circuit
s: Generic electrical fault on CAC bypass position sensor A (feedback sensor for bypass valve).

t: Charge Air Cooler Bypass Position Sensor A Circuit Range/Performance
s: CAC bypass position sensor A reading implausible. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Charge Air Cooler Bypass Position Sensor A Circuit Low
s: CAC bypass position sensor A signal voltage below expected range. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Charge Air Cooler Bypass Position Sensor A Circuit High
s: CAC bypass position sensor A signal voltage above expected range. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Turbocharger/Supercharger Wastegate Actuator B High
s: Wastegate actuator B control voltage above expected range. Boost control tracks commanded vs. actual manifold pressure; persistent deviation means wastegate, vanes, hose or actuator are not delivering the requested pressure.

t: Injection Pump Fuel Metering Control A (Cam/Rotor/Injector)
s: Generic fault on the fuel metering control A of the injection pump (mechanical or rotary distribution pumps).

t: Injection Pump Fuel Metering Control A Range/Performance (Cam/Rotor/Injector)
s: Injection pump metering control A does not respond as expected. A flow-deviating injector throws off cylinder balance — adjacent cylinders run leaner/richer to compensate, often masking the root cause until the trim limit is hit.

t: Injection Pump Fuel Metering Control A Low (Cam/Rotor/Injector)
s: Injection pump metering control A signal voltage below expected range. A flow-deviating injector throws off cylinder balance — adjacent cylinders run leaner/richer to compensate, often masking the root cause until the trim limit is hit.

t: Injection Pump Fuel Metering Control A High (Cam/Rotor/Injector)
s: Injection pump metering control A signal voltage above expected range. A flow-deviating injector throws off cylinder balance — adjacent cylinders run leaner/richer to compensate, often masking the root cause until the trim limit is hit.

t: Injection Pump Fuel Metering Control A Intermittent (Cam/Rotor/Injector)
s: Intermittent signal on injection pump metering control A. A flow-deviating injector throws off cylinder balance — adjacent cylinders run leaner/richer to compensate, often masking the root cause until the trim limit is hit.

t: Injection Pump Fuel Metering Control B (Cam/Rotor/Injector)
s: Generic fault on the fuel metering control A of the injection pump (mechanical or rotary distribution pumps).

t: Injection Pump Fuel Metering Control B Range/Performance (Cam/Rotor/Injector)
s: Injection pump metering control A does not respond as expected. A flow-deviating injector throws off cylinder balance — adjacent cylinders run leaner/richer to compensate, often masking the root cause until the trim limit is hit.

t: Injection Pump Fuel Metering Control B Low (Cam/Rotor/Injector)
s: Injection pump metering control A signal voltage below expected range. A flow-deviating injector throws off cylinder balance — adjacent cylinders run leaner/richer to compensate, often masking the root cause until the trim limit is hit.

t: Injection Pump Fuel Metering Control B High (Cam/Rotor/Injector)
s: Injection pump metering control A signal voltage above expected range. A flow-deviating injector throws off cylinder balance — adjacent cylinders run leaner/richer to compensate, often masking the root cause until the trim limit is hit.

t: Fuel Pump Module A Control Circuit/Open
s: Open circuit at fuel pump module A control. Pump control unit cannot be commanded.

t: Fuel Pump Module A Control Circuit Range/Performance
s: Fuel pump module A does not respond as expected. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Fuel Pump Module A Control Circuit Low
s: Fuel pump module A control voltage below expected range. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Fuel Pump Module A Control Circuit High
s: Fuel pump module A control voltage above expected range. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Turbocharger/Supercharger Boost Sensor A Intermittent/Erratic
s: Intermittent signal from boost pressure sensor A. Boost control tracks commanded vs. actual manifold pressure; persistent deviation means wastegate, vanes, hose or actuator are not delivering the requested pressure.

t: Turbocharger/Supercharger Boost Sensor B Intermittent/Erratic
s: Intermittent signal from boost pressure sensor B. Boost control tracks commanded vs. actual manifold pressure; persistent deviation means wastegate, vanes, hose or actuator are not delivering the requested pressure.

t: Cylinder 1 Injector Circuit Performance
s: The injector drive circuit for cylinder 1 does not respond as expected. Pulse width or current feedback differs from the commanded value, indicating a wiring, driver or solenoid problem.

t: Cylinder 1 Injector Circuit Low
s: The cylinder 1 injector control circuit voltage is below the lower diagnostic threshold — open winding, short to ground, or wiring fault. The ECU disables the injector to protect the driver stage.

t: Cylinder 1 Injector Circuit High
s: The cylinder 1 injector control circuit voltage is below the lower diagnostic threshold — open winding, short to ground, or wiring fault. The ECU disables the injector to protect the driver stage.

t: Cylinder 1 Contribution/Balance Fault
s: The ECU compares each cylinder's contribution to crankshaft speed (rough run analysis) and determined cylinder 1 contributes less or more than the average. This points to a mechanical or fuel delivery problem rather than a pure ignition fault.

t: Cylinder 2 Injector Circuit Low
s: The cylinder 2 injector control circuit voltage is below the lower diagnostic threshold — open winding, short to ground, or wiring fault. The ECU disables the injector to protect the driver stage.

t: Cylinder 2 Injector Circuit High
s: The cylinder 2 injector control circuit voltage is below the lower diagnostic threshold — open winding, short to ground, or wiring fault. The ECU disables the injector to protect the driver stage.

t: Cylinder 2 Contribution/Balance Fault
s: The ECU compares each cylinder's contribution to crankshaft speed (rough run analysis) and determined cylinder 2 contributes less or more than the average. This points to a mechanical or fuel delivery problem rather than a pure ignition fault.

t: Cylinder 3 Injector Circuit Low
s: The cylinder 3 injector control circuit voltage is below the lower diagnostic threshold — open winding, short to ground, or wiring fault. The ECU disables the injector to protect the driver stage.

t: Cylinder 3 Injector Circuit High
s: The cylinder 3 injector control circuit voltage is below the lower diagnostic threshold — open winding, short to ground, or wiring fault. The ECU disables the injector to protect the driver stage.

t: Cylinder 3 Contribution/Balance Fault
s: The ECU compares each cylinder's contribution to crankshaft speed (rough run analysis) and determined cylinder 3 contributes less or more than the average. This points to a mechanical or fuel delivery problem rather than a pure ignition fault.

t: Charge Air Cooler Efficiency Below Threshold
s: Charge air cooler efficiency dropped below diagnostic threshold — intake air not cooled enough after compression. Risk of knock and loss of power.

t: Injection Timing Performance
s: Generic injection timing performance fault — actual timing deviates from target.

t: Fuel Injection Quantity A Lower Than Expected
s: Fuel injection quantity A is lower than the commanded amount. Often clogged or worn injector.

t: Fuel Injection Quantity A Higher Than Expected
s: Fuel injection quantity A is higher than commanded — often a leaking injector or stuck-open nozzle.

t: Charge Air Cooler Coolant Pump A Performance
s: CAC coolant pump A does not deliver expected flow when commanded.

t: Coolant Thermostat Performance
s: Thermostat does not regulate coolant temperature in the expected range. Often stuck open (slow warmup) or stuck closed (overheat).

t: Cylinder 4 Injector Circuit Low
s: The cylinder 4 injector control circuit voltage is below the lower diagnostic threshold — open winding, short to ground, or wiring fault. The ECU disables the injector to protect the driver stage.

t: Cylinder 4 Injector Circuit High
s: The cylinder 4 injector control circuit voltage is below the lower diagnostic threshold — open winding, short to ground, or wiring fault. The ECU disables the injector to protect the driver stage.

t: Cylinder 4 Contribution/Balance Fault
s: The ECU compares each cylinder's contribution to crankshaft speed (rough run analysis) and determined cylinder 4 contributes less or more than the average. This points to a mechanical or fuel delivery problem rather than a pure ignition fault.

t: Cylinder 2 Contribution/Balance
s: The ECU compares torque contribution of cylinder 2 against other cylinders by analyzing crankshaft angular velocity. Persistent low contribution triggers this code.

t: Cylinder 5 Injector A Circuit High
s: Cylinder 5 injector A control voltage above expected range — short to power or open ground. A flow-deviating injector throws off cylinder balance — adjacent cylinders run leaner/richer to compensate, often masking the root cause until the trim limit is hit.

t: Cylinder 5 Contribution/Balance
s: Cylinder 5 contributes less power to the engine than the other cylinders. Compression, ignition, or injection issue.

t: Cylinder 6 Injector A Circuit Low
s: Cylinder 6 injector A control voltage below expected range — short to ground. A flow-deviating injector throws off cylinder balance — adjacent cylinders run leaner/richer to compensate, often masking the root cause until the trim limit is hit.

t: Cylinder 6 Injector A Circuit High
s: Cylinder 6 injector A control voltage above expected range. A flow-deviating injector throws off cylinder balance — adjacent cylinders run leaner/richer to compensate, often masking the root cause until the trim limit is hit.

t: Cylinder 6 Contribution/Balance
s: Cylinder 6 contributes less power than expected.

t: Cylinder 7 Injector A Circuit Low
s: Cylinder 7 injector A control voltage below expected range. A flow-deviating injector throws off cylinder balance — adjacent cylinders run leaner/richer to compensate, often masking the root cause until the trim limit is hit.

t: Fuel Pump Module B Control Circuit/Open
s: Open circuit at fuel pump module B (second pump module on dual-tank or high-output fuel systems).

t: Fuel Pump Module B Control Circuit Range/Performance
s: Fuel pump module B does not respond as expected. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Fuel Pump Module B Control Circuit Low
s: Fuel pump module B control voltage below expected range. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Fuel Pump Module B Control Circuit High
s: Fuel pump module B control voltage above expected range. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Cold Start Fuel Injection Quantity A Lower Than Expected
s: During cold start the injection quantity A is below the commanded amount. Hard starting in cold conditions.

t: Cold Start Fuel Injection Quantity A Higher Than Expected
s: During cold start injection quantity A is above commanded — over-fueling, possible flooding.

s: Cylinder 7 injector A control voltage above expected range. A flow-deviating injector throws off cylinder balance — adjacent cylinders run leaner/richer to compensate, often masking the root cause until the trim limit is hit.

s: Cylinder 7 contributes less power than expected.

t: Charge Air Cooler Cooling Fan Control Circuit/Open
s: Open circuit at the charge air cooler cooling fan (electric fan helping to cool the intercooler).

t: Charge Air Cooler Cooling Fan Performance/Stuck Off
s: CAC cooling fan does not run when commanded.

t: Charge Air Cooler Cooling Fan Stuck On
s: CAC cooling fan runs continuously regardless of commanded state.

t: Charge Air Cooler Cooling Fan Control Circuit Low

s: Driver stage of CAC cooling fan control reports performance fault.

t: Vehicle Overspeed Condition
s: Vehicle speed exceeded a defined safety threshold. ECU may trigger fuel cut or speed limiter.

t: Engine Oil Over Temperature
s: Engine oil temperature exceeded the safe maximum. Risk of oil breakdown and bearing damage.

t: Turbocharger/Supercharger "A" Underboost Condition
s: Boost pressure under load is below the commanded value. Very common on turbodiesel and direct-injection turbo gasoline engines as the turbo system ages — typically a leaking boost pipe, a sticky variable-geometry vane assembly, or a wastegate that no longer holds.

t: Cylinder 1 - Fuel Trim at Max Limit
s: Per-cylinder fuel trim on cylinder 1 has reached its maximum correction limit. Cylinder running too lean structurally.

t: Cylinder 1 - Fuel Trim at Min Limit
s: Per-cylinder fuel trim on cylinder 1 has reached its minimum correction limit. Cylinder running too rich structurally.

t: Cylinder 1 - Injector Restricted
s: Cylinder 1 injector flow restricted — partial blockage detected. A flow-deviating injector throws off cylinder balance — adjacent cylinders run leaner/richer to compensate, often masking the root cause until the trim limit is hit.

t: Cylinder 1 - Injector Leaking
s: Cylinder 1 injector leaking fuel between commanded openings. A flow-deviating injector throws off cylinder balance — adjacent cylinders run leaner/richer to compensate, often masking the root cause until the trim limit is hit.

s: Per-cylinder fuel trim on cylinder 5 has reached its minimum correction limit. Cylinder running too rich structurally.

t: Cylinder 5 - Injector Restricted
s: Cylinder 5 injector flow restricted — partial blockage detected. A flow-deviating injector throws off cylinder balance — adjacent cylinders run leaner/richer to compensate, often masking the root cause until the trim limit is hit.

t: Cylinder 5 - Injector Leaking
s: Cylinder 5 injector leaking fuel between commanded openings. A flow-deviating injector throws off cylinder balance — adjacent cylinders run leaner/richer to compensate, often masking the root cause until the trim limit is hit.

t: Turbocharger/Supercharger B Overboost Condition
s: Boost pressure on the secondary turbocharger/supercharger (B) on a sequential or twin-charged setup is above the upper threshold. Wastegate stuck closed, VGT vanes stuck, or boost pressure sensor reads high.

t: Turbocharger/Supercharger B Underboost Condition
s: Boost pressure on the secondary turbocharger/supercharger (B) is below the lower threshold. Wastegate stuck open, charge-air leak, or VGT vanes stuck open.

t: Cylinder 1 Fuel Injector A Offset Learning At Min Limit
s: The PCM applies a per-cylinder injector offset (Mengenausgleich) to compensate for production-tolerance differences in injector flow rate. On cylinder 1 the learned correction has reached its minimum value — the injector is delivering significantly more fuel than the calibrated reference and the controller cannot trim it any further.

t: Cylinder 1 Fuel Injector A Offset Learning At Max Limit
s: The per-cylinder injector offset on cylinder 1 has reached its maximum value — the injector is delivering significantly less fuel than the calibrated reference, and the controller cannot trim it any further.

t: Diesel Intake Air Flow A Control Circuit/Open
s: The drive circuit for the diesel intake air-flow control valve A is open. This valve (also called intake throttle, swirl flap actuator, or anti-shudder valve) closes the diesel intake to control airflow during regeneration and shutdown.

t: Diesel Intake Air Flow A Control Performance
s: Diesel intake air-flow control A is electrically OK but does not move the throttle plate as commanded — typically carbon buildup or seized actuator.

t: Diesel Intake Air Flow A Control Circuit Low
s: Diesel intake air-flow control A drive line stuck low — short to ground. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Diesel Intake Air Flow A Control Circuit High
s: Diesel intake air-flow control A drive line stuck high — short to B+. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Diesel Intake Air Flow A Control Stuck Open
s: Diesel intake throttle A is mechanically stuck open — anti-shudder function on shutdown will not work and DPF regen airflow control is lost.

t: Diesel Intake Air Flow A Control Stuck Closed
s: Diesel intake throttle A is mechanically stuck closed — engine starves of intake air.

t: Diesel Intake Air Flow A Position Sensor Circuit
s: A general electrical fault is reported on diesel intake throttle A position sensor — open, shorted, or out of plausibility.

t: Diesel Intake Air Flow A Position Sensor Circuit Range/Performance
s: Position sensor reads in range but plausibility check fails — does not match commanded throttle position.

t: Diesel Intake Air Flow A Position Sensor Circuit Low
s: Position sensor below lower plausibility — short to ground. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Diesel Intake Air Flow A Position Sensor Circuit High
s: Position sensor above upper plausibility — open signal or short to B+. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Diesel Intake Air Flow A Position Sensor Circuit Intermittent/Erratic
s: Position sensor signal jumps or drops out intermittently — connector or harness issue. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Diesel Intake Air Flow A Control Motor Current Range/Performance
s: Motor current draw of the diesel intake throttle A actuator does not match the calibrated map for the commanded position — high friction, partial short, or stiction from carbon.

t: Diesel Intake Air Flow A Control System - High Air Flow Detected
s: Air flow through diesel intake throttle A is higher than the commanded set point — flap leaking past the seat or stuck partly open.

t: Diesel Intake Air Flow A Control System - Low Air Flow Detected
s: Air flow through diesel intake throttle A is lower than the commanded set point — flap stuck partly closed.

t: Cylinder 1 Injector Circuit Range/Performance
s: The cylinder 1 injector drive circuit is electrically OK but the response (current ramp, dwell time, or peak voltage) does not match the calibrated profile — typically a high-resistance solenoid winding or wiring corrosion.

t: Cylinder 2 Injector Circuit Range/Performance
s: The cylinder 2 injector drive circuit is electrically OK but the response (current ramp, dwell time, or peak voltage) does not match the calibrated profile — typically a high-resistance solenoid winding or wiring corrosion.

t: Cylinder 3 Injector Circuit Range/Performance
s: The cylinder 3 injector drive circuit is electrically OK but the response (current ramp, dwell time, or peak voltage) does not match the calibrated profile — typically a high-resistance solenoid winding or wiring corrosion.

s: The cylinder 4 injector drive circuit is electrically OK but the response (current ramp, dwell time, or peak voltage) does not match the calibrated profile — typically a high-resistance solenoid winding or wiring corrosion.

s: The cylinder 9 injector drive circuit is electrically OK but the response (current ramp, dwell time, or peak voltage) does not match the calibrated profile — typically a high-resistance solenoid winding or wiring corrosion.

t: Cylinder 10 Injector Circuit Range/Performance
s: The cylinder 10 injector drive circuit is electrically OK but the response (current ramp, dwell time, or peak voltage) does not match the calibrated profile — typically a high-resistance solenoid winding or wiring corrosion.

s: The cylinder 11 injector drive circuit is electrically OK but the response (current ramp, dwell time, or peak voltage) does not match the calibrated profile — typically a high-resistance solenoid winding or wiring corrosion.
