s: The cylinder 12 injector drive circuit is electrically OK but the response (current ramp, dwell time, or peak voltage) does not match the calibrated profile — typically a high-resistance solenoid winding or wiring corrosion.

t: Diesel Intake Air Flow Position Sensor Minimum/Maximum Stop Performance
s: The diesel intake throttle position sensor does not reach the calibrated minimum or maximum stop value during a self-test sweep — typically sensor wear, mechanical stop misalignment, or a sticky throttle plate.

t: Cold Start Fuel Injection Control Circuit/Open
s: The drive circuit for the cold-start auxiliary fuel injector or the cold-start ECU output is open. Some diesels use an extra cold-start injector to inject fuel into the intake manifold during very cold starts.

t: Cold Start Fuel Injection Control Circuit Low
s: Cold-start injector drive line stuck low — short to ground. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Cold Start Fuel Injection Control Circuit High
s: Cold-start injector drive line stuck high — short to B+. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Cold Start Fuel Injection Control Circuit Range/Performance
s: Cold-start injector drive is electrically OK but does not deliver the calibrated injection quantity — partial blockage or weak coil.

t: Propulsion System Inactive Timer Performance
s: The timer that monitors how long the propulsion system has been inactive (used by hybrid and stop-start strategies to schedule rebalancing tasks) is not behaving plausibly — typically a clock or PCM-state fault.

t: Random/Multiple Cylinder Misfire Detected
s: The ECU has detected misfires that are not concentrated on a single cylinder. The misfire monitor measures crankshaft acceleration after each combustion event; missing acceleration on multiple cylinders, or randomly across cylinders, triggers this code.

t: Cylinder 1 Misfire Detected
s: Misfires concentrated on cylinder 1. A cylinder-specific code points away from shared causes (fuel pressure, MAF) toward components dedicated to that cylinder — coil, spark plug, injector, compression.

t: Cylinder 2 Misfire Detected
s: Misfires concentrated on cylinder 2. Diagnostic approach is identical to P0301 — swap the suspect coil or plug with a known-good cylinder and see if the misfire follows.

t: Cylinder 3 Misfire Detected
s: Misfires concentrated on cylinder 3. A cylinder-specific code points away from shared causes (fuel pressure, MAF) toward components dedicated to that cylinder — coil, spark plug, injector, compression. Verify by swapping the suspected coil/plug to a different cylinder; if the code follows, the part is bad.

t: Cylinder 4 Misfire Detected
s: Misfires concentrated on cylinder 4. A cylinder-specific code points away from shared causes (fuel pressure, MAF) toward components dedicated to that cylinder — coil, spark plug, injector, compression. Verify by swapping the suspected coil/plug to a different cylinder; if the code follows, the part is bad.

t: Cylinder 5 Misfire Detected
s: Misfires concentrated on cylinder 5 (only present on engines with 5+ cylinders). Same diagnostic approach as P0301 — swap the suspect coil/plug with another cylinder.

t: Cylinder 6 Misfire Detected
s: Misfires concentrated on cylinder 6. A cylinder-specific code points away from shared causes (fuel pressure, MAF) toward components dedicated to that cylinder — coil, spark plug, injector, compression. Verify by swapping the suspected coil/plug to a different cylinder; if the code follows, the part is bad.

t: Cylinder 7 Misfire Detected
s: Misfires concentrated on cylinder 7. A cylinder-specific code points away from shared causes (fuel pressure, MAF) toward components dedicated to that cylinder — coil, spark plug, injector, compression.

t: Cylinder 8 Misfire Detected
s: Misfires concentrated on cylinder 8. A cylinder-specific code points away from shared causes (fuel pressure, MAF) toward components dedicated to that cylinder — coil, spark plug, injector, compression.

t: Cylinder 9 Misfire Detected
s: Misfires concentrated on cylinder 9. A cylinder-specific code points away from shared causes (fuel pressure, MAF) toward components dedicated to that cylinder — coil, spark plug, injector, compression.

t: Ignition A Control Signal Circuit/Open
s: The ignition A control signal circuit is open — typically the PCM drive line for the first ignition coil group has lost continuity.

t: Ignition A Control Signal Circuit Low
s: Ignition A control signal stuck low — short to ground. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Ignition A Control Signal Circuit High
s: Ignition A control signal stuck high — short to B+. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Ignition B Control Signal Circuit/Open
s: The ignition B control signal circuit is open — typically the PCM drive line for the first ignition coil group has lost continuity.

t: Ignition B Control Signal Circuit Low
s: Ignition B control signal stuck low — short to ground. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Ignition B Control Signal Circuit High
s: Ignition B control signal stuck high — short to B+. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Cylinder 10 Misfire Detected
s: Misfires concentrated on cylinder 10. A cylinder-specific code points away from shared causes (fuel pressure, MAF) toward components dedicated to that cylinder — coil, spark plug, injector, compression.

t: Cylinder 11 Misfire Detected
s: Misfires concentrated on cylinder 11. A cylinder-specific code points away from shared causes (fuel pressure, MAF) toward components dedicated to that cylinder — coil, spark plug, injector, compression.

t: Cylinder 12 Misfire Detected
s: Misfires concentrated on cylinder 12. A cylinder-specific code points away from shared causes (fuel pressure, MAF) toward components dedicated to that cylinder — coil, spark plug, injector, compression.

t: Misfire Detected with Low Fuel
s: Misfires are detected while the fuel level is low. The ECM disables further misfire reporting under this condition because intermittent fuel starvation can cause false positives in catalyst-damaging logic.

t: Single Cylinder Misfire — Cylinder Not Specified
s: A persistent misfire is detected on one cylinder, but the engine controller cannot identify which cylinder is affected. Often seen on engines without per-cylinder feedback or when crank position correlation fails.

t: Crankshaft Position System Variation Not Learned
s: The PCM has not yet learned the crankshaft position variation table required for accurate misfire detection. After flywheel/sensor replacement or PCM relearn, a procedure must be performed (typically deceleration from a defined RPM with closed throttle).

t: Engine Misfire Detected on Startup (First 1000 Revolutions)
s: The misfire monitor detected misfires during the first 1000 revolutions after startup but the engine then settled. Often caused by fouled spark plugs that warm up clean, weak ignition coils, or a borderline cold-start fuel mixture.

t: Rough Road Hardware Not Present
s: The PCM has been configured to use rough-road sensor input for misfire detection (to mask false positives on bumpy surfaces) but the expected hardware is not detected on the network. Typically a configuration mismatch after replacement.

t: Rough Road Sensor A Signal Circuit
s: A general electrical fault is reported on rough-road sensor A — used to gate misfire detection so that engine vibration from rough roads is not misclassified as misfire.

t: Rough Road Sensor B Signal Circuit
s: A general electrical fault is reported on rough-road sensor B — used to gate misfire detection so that engine vibration from rough roads is not misclassified as misfire.

t: Ignition C Control Signal Circuit/Open
s: The ignition C control signal circuit is open — typically the PCM drive line for the first ignition coil group has lost continuity.

t: Ignition C Control Signal Circuit Low
s: Ignition C control signal stuck low — short to ground. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Ignition C Control Signal Circuit High
s: Ignition C control signal stuck high — short to B+. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Ignition D Control Signal Circuit/Open
s: The ignition D control signal circuit is open — typically the PCM drive line for the first ignition coil group has lost continuity.

t: Ignition D Control Signal Circuit Low
s: Ignition D control signal stuck low — short to ground. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Ignition D Control Signal Circuit High
s: Ignition D control signal stuck high — short to B+. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Ignition/Distributor Engine Speed Input Circuit
s: A general electrical fault is reported on the engine speed input from the ignition/distributor — open, shorted, or out of plausibility. On older systems this is the distributor pickup; on newer ones it is the crank/cam-derived speed signal feeding the ignition driver.

t: Ignition/Distributor Engine Speed Input Circuit Range/Performance
s: Engine speed input from the ignition/distributor is in electrical range but plausibility check fails — slow response, drift, or noise.

t: Ignition/Distributor Engine Speed Input Circuit No Signal
s: No engine speed signal from the ignition/distributor — open circuit, dead pickup, or sensor air gap too wide.

t: Ignition/Distributor Engine Speed Input Circuit Intermittent
s: Engine speed input signal jumps or drops out intermittently — connector or wiring issue. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Knock/Combustion Vibration Control System Error
s: The knock-control subsystem reports an internal error — typically a self-test fault or a noise floor that cannot be calibrated. The system protects against detonation by retarding ignition; with this fault knock control is disabled.

t: Knock Sensor 1 Circuit Malfunction (Bank 1)
s: A general fault has been detected in the bank 1 knock sensor circuit. The ECU uses the knock sensor to detect pre-ignition and retards ignition timing when knock occurs; with no signal, the ECU runs conservative timing and loses power.

t: Knock/Combustion Vibration Sensor A Circuit Range/Performance
s: Knock sensor A signal is in electrical range but plausibility check fails — typically a loose mounting, wrong torque, or sensor element drift.

t: Knock Sensor 1 Circuit Low Input (Bank 1)
s: The knock sensor signal voltage is below the expected range. Usually points to a piezoelectric sensor that has lost its output, or a short to ground in the signal line.

t: Knock Sensor 1 Circuit High Input (Bank 1)
s: The knock sensor signal voltage is above the expected range. Often caused by a sensor producing excess output due to mechanical noise (loose mounting, accessory drive vibration) rather than a sensor fault.

t: Knock/Combustion Vibration Sensor A Circuit Intermittent
s: Knock sensor A signal jumps or drops out intermittently — usually a corroded connector or chafed wire near the cylinder head.

t: Knock/Combustion Vibration Sensor C Circuit
s: A general electrical fault on knock sensor C — used on engines with multiple knock sensors (one per bank or per cylinder pair).

t: Knock/Combustion Vibration Sensor C Circuit Range/Performance
s: Knock sensor C signal is in electrical range but plausibility check fails — typically a loose mounting, wrong torque, or sensor element drift.

t: Knock/Combustion Vibration Sensor C Circuit Low
s: Knock sensor C signal below lower plausibility — short to ground. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Knock/Combustion Vibration Sensor C Circuit High
s: Knock sensor C signal above upper plausibility — open signal or short to B+. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Knock/Combustion Vibration Sensor C Circuit Intermittent
s: Knock sensor C signal jumps or drops out intermittently — usually a corroded connector or chafed wire near the cylinder head.

t: Combustion Engine Producing Insufficient Torque
s: The engine torque measured (or modelled from intake/exhaust signals) is below the calibrated set point for the commanded throttle position. Causes range from low compression, intake leak, fuel pressure issue, ignition fault, to high mechanical drag.

t: Knock Sensor 2 Circuit (Bank 2)
s: A general electrical fault in the bank 2 knock sensor circuit. The ECU monitors a piezoelectric sensor for engine vibration patterns associated with detonation; an open, shorted, or out-of-range circuit triggers this code.

t: Knock/Combustion Vibration Sensor B Circuit Range/Performance
s: Knock sensor B signal is in electrical range but plausibility check fails — typically a loose mounting, wrong torque, or sensor element drift.

t: Knock Sensor 2 Circuit Low Input (Bank 2)
s: Knock sensor 2 (bank 2) reports a signal level below the diagnostic threshold. The sensor is a piezo element bolted to the engine block; output should be a small AC voltage on each combustion event. A cold or open sensor reads 0 V; a loose mounting bolt damps the signal. Without knock detection, the ECM retards spark globally as a safety measure — fuel economy and power suffer.

t: Knock Sensor 2 Circuit High Input (Bank 2)
s: The bank 2 knock sensor signal is above the expected voltage range. Typically caused by a short to supply voltage or an internal sensor fault.

t: Knock/Combustion Vibration Sensor B Circuit Intermittent
s: Knock sensor B signal jumps or drops out intermittently — usually a corroded connector or chafed wire near the cylinder head.

t: Crankshaft Position Sensor "A" Circuit Malfunction
s: The crankshaft position (CKP) sensor signal is missing or implausible. The CKP is essential — without it the ECU cannot synchronise injection or ignition, and the engine will not start or will stall.

t: Crankshaft Position Sensor "A" Circuit Range/Performance
s: The CKP signal is present but inconsistent with engine RPM, or pulse counts deviate from the reluctor's expected pattern. Often points to a damaged tooth on the reluctor or an air gap that has changed.

t: Crankshaft Position Sensor "A" Circuit Low Input
s: The CKP signal voltage is below the lower threshold. Usually a sensor whose internal coil has degraded, or a wiring short to ground.

t: Crankshaft Position Sensor "A" Circuit High Input
s: The CKP signal voltage is above the upper threshold. Either a sensor producing excess output or a short to supply voltage.

t: Crankshaft Position Sensor A Circuit Intermittent
s: The crankshaft position sensor A signal jumps or drops out intermittently — connector, sensor air gap, or damaged tone wheel. The PCM may detect a "false" stall or misfire spike when the signal drops.

t: Knock/Combustion Vibration Sensor D Circuit
s: A general electrical fault on knock sensor D — used on engines with multiple knock sensors (one per bank or per cylinder pair).

t: Knock/Combustion Vibration Sensor D Circuit Range/Performance
s: Knock sensor D signal is in electrical range but plausibility check fails — typically a loose mounting, wrong torque, or sensor element drift.

t: Knock/Combustion Vibration Sensor D Circuit Low
s: Knock sensor D signal below lower plausibility — short to ground. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Knock/Combustion Vibration Sensor D Circuit High
s: Knock sensor D signal above upper plausibility — open signal or short to B+. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Knock/Combustion Vibration Sensor D Circuit Intermittent
s: Knock sensor D signal jumps or drops out intermittently — usually a corroded connector or chafed wire near the cylinder head.

t: Crankshaft/Camshaft Loss Of Synchronization
s: The PCM has lost crank-to-cam synchronization — the relative phase between crankshaft and camshaft no longer matches the calibrated reference. Causes include a stretched or jumped timing chain, broken cam timing tooth, or sensor signal corruption.

t: Camshaft Position Sensor "A" Circuit (Bank 1 or Single Sensor)
s: The camshaft position (CMP) sensor signal is missing or implausible. The ECU uses CMP together with CKP to identify cylinder phase for sequential injection and coil-on-plug ignition; without CMP the engine may run on group injection or fail to start.

t: Camshaft Position Sensor "A" Circuit Range/Performance (Bank 1)
s: The CMP signal is present but its phase relative to the crankshaft is outside tolerance. On engines with variable valve timing, this code often appears together with VVT solenoid faults.

t: Camshaft Position Sensor "A" Circuit Low Input (Bank 1)
s: The CMP signal voltage is below the lower threshold. Most often a sensor with degraded internal output or a short to ground.

t: Camshaft Position Sensor "A" Circuit High Input (Bank 1)
s: The CMP signal voltage is above the upper threshold. Either excessive sensor output or a short to supply voltage.

t: Camshaft Position Sensor A Circuit Intermittent (Bank 1 or Single Sensor)
s: Camshaft position sensor A signal jumps or drops out intermittently on bank 1 (or on engines with a single cam sensor).

t: Camshaft Position Sensor "A" Circuit (Bank 2)
s: General electrical fault in the bank 2 camshaft position sensor circuit. The ECU uses this signal together with the crank sensor for sequential injection and ignition timing.

t: Camshaft Position Sensor "A" Circuit Range/Performance (Bank 2)
s: The bank 2 camshaft position sensor signal is present but does not match the crank reference correctly, or its pulse pattern is implausible.

t: Camshaft Position Sensor A Circuit Low (Bank 2)
s: Camshaft position sensor A on bank 2 reads below lower plausibility — short to ground. The camshaft position sensor identifies which cylinder is on the compression stroke — without it the ECM cannot run sequential injection or coil-on-plug ignition correctly.

t: Camshaft Position Sensor A Circuit High (Bank 2)
s: Camshaft position sensor A on bank 2 reads above upper plausibility — open signal or short to B+. The camshaft position sensor identifies which cylinder is on the compression stroke — without it the ECM cannot run sequential injection or coil-on-plug ignition correctly.

t: Camshaft Position Sensor A Circuit Intermittent (Bank 2)
s: Camshaft position sensor A signal jumps or drops out intermittently on bank 2 (or on engines with a single cam sensor).

t: Crankshaft Position Sensor - Crankshaft Start Position Incorrect
s: At engine start the crankshaft position reported by the sensor does not match the calibrated start window — typically a wrong tone-wheel installation, sensor air gap, or PCM not synchronised after a relearn.

t: Crankshaft Position Sensor - Crankshaft Direction Incorrect
s: The crankshaft is rotating in the wrong direction relative to the expected reference — typically only seen on stop-start systems where the engine briefly counter-rotates after shutdown.

t: Ion Sense System Performance
s: Some engines (notably Saab and some Mazda direct-injection units) use the spark plug as an ion-current sensor after spark to detect knock and misfire. This code reports a performance issue with the ion-sense input — typically a worn plug, loose secondary connection, or aged ion-sense module.

t: Ignition Coil Primary/Secondary Circuit/Open
s: A general open-circuit fault has been detected on an ignition coil's primary or secondary winding (no specific cylinder identified). This code is set when the PCM cannot localise the fault to a single coil.

t: Ignition Coil "A" Primary/Secondary Circuit Malfunction (Cylinder 1)
s: An electrical fault has been detected in the ignition coil circuit for cylinder 1 — open primary winding, short to ground, or short to supply. The ECU disables the coil to protect its driver and the cylinder misfires.

t: Ignition Coil "B" Primary/Secondary Circuit Malfunction (Cylinder 2)
s: An electrical fault has been detected in the ignition coil circuit for cylinder 2 — open primary winding, short to ground, or short to supply. The ECU disables the coil to protect its driver and the cylinder misfires.

t: Ignition Coil "C" Primary/Secondary Circuit Malfunction (Cylinder 3)
s: An electrical fault has been detected in the ignition coil circuit for cylinder 3 — open primary winding, short to ground, or short to supply. The ECU disables the coil to protect its driver and the cylinder misfires.

t: Ignition Coil "D" Primary/Secondary Circuit Malfunction (Cylinder 4)
s: An electrical fault has been detected in the ignition coil circuit for cylinder 4 — open primary winding, short to ground, or short to supply. The ECU disables the coil to protect its driver and the cylinder misfires.

t: Ignition Coil "E" Primary/Secondary Circuit
s: An electrical fault has been detected in the ignition coil circuit for cylinder 1 — open primary winding, short to ground, or short to supply. The ECU disables the coil to protect its driver and the cylinder misfires.

t: Ignition Coil "F" Primary/Secondary Circuit
s: An electrical fault has been detected in the ignition coil circuit for cylinder 1 — open primary winding, short to ground, or short to supply. The ECU disables the coil to protect its driver and the cylinder misfires.

t: Ignition Coil "G" Primary/Secondary Circuit
s: An electrical fault has been detected in the ignition coil circuit for cylinder 1 — open primary winding, short to ground, or short to supply. The ECU disables the coil to protect its driver and the cylinder misfires.

t: Ignition Coil "H" Primary/Secondary Circuit
s: An electrical fault has been detected in the ignition coil circuit for cylinder 1 — open primary winding, short to ground, or short to supply. The ECU disables the coil to protect its driver and the cylinder misfires.

t: Ignition Coil I Primary Control Circuit/Open
s: The primary control circuit for ignition coil I is open. Coil designators A through L cover up to 12 individual coils on a coil-on-plug configuration.

t: Ignition E Control Signal Circuit/Open
s: The ignition E control signal circuit is open — typically the PCM drive line for the first ignition coil group has lost continuity.

t: Ignition E Control Signal Circuit Low
s: Ignition E control signal stuck low — short to ground. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Ignition E Control Signal Circuit High
s: Ignition E control signal stuck high — short to B+. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Ignition F Control Signal Circuit/Open
s: The ignition F control signal circuit is open — typically the PCM drive line for the first ignition coil group has lost continuity.

t: Ignition F Control Signal Circuit Low
s: Ignition F control signal stuck low — short to ground. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Ignition F Control Signal Circuit High
s: Ignition F control signal stuck high — short to B+. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Ignition Coil J Primary Control Circuit/Open
s: The primary control circuit for ignition coil I is open. Coil designators A through L cover up to 12 individual coils on a coil-on-plug configuration.

t: Ignition Coil K Primary Control Circuit/Open
s: The primary control circuit for ignition coil I is open. Coil designators A through L cover up to 12 individual coils on a coil-on-plug configuration.

t: Ignition Coil L Primary Control Circuit/Open
s: The primary control circuit for ignition coil I is open. Coil designators A through L cover up to 12 individual coils on a coil-on-plug configuration.

t: Misfire Detected - Fueling Disabled
s: Severe misfire detected — the PCM has disabled fuel injection on the affected cylinder(s) to protect the catalyst from raw fuel. Common protective response on heavy or sustained misfire conditions.

t: DC/AC Converter Control Module Control Circuit Range/Performance
s: The control circuit for the DC/AC converter (used in plug-in hybrid and EV traction systems to convert DC battery voltage to 3-phase AC for the motor) reports a performance issue. This is part of the high-voltage drivetrain.

t: Camshaft Position Sensor B Circuit (Bank 1)
s: A general electrical fault on camshaft position sensor B (typically the exhaust cam) on bank 1 — open, shorted, or out of plausibility. Required for variable valve timing on the exhaust cam.

t: Camshaft Position Sensor B Circuit Range/Performance (Bank 1)
s: Cam sensor B on bank 1 reads in range but plausibility check fails — slow response, drift, or signal does not match expected cam profile.

t: Camshaft Position Sensor B Circuit Low (Bank 1)
s: Cam sensor B on bank 1 below lower plausibility — short to ground. The camshaft position sensor identifies which cylinder is on the compression stroke — without it the ECM cannot run sequential injection or coil-on-plug ignition correctly.

t: Camshaft Position Sensor B Circuit High (Bank 1)
s: Cam sensor B on bank 1 above upper plausibility — open signal or short to B+. The camshaft position sensor identifies which cylinder is on the compression stroke — without it the ECM cannot run sequential injection or coil-on-plug ignition correctly.

t: Camshaft Position Sensor B Circuit Intermittent (Bank 1)
s: Camshaft position sensor B signal jumps or drops out intermittently on bank 1 (or on engines with a single cam sensor).

t: Ignition G Control Signal Circuit/Open
s: The ignition G control signal circuit is open — typically the PCM drive line for the first ignition coil group has lost continuity.

t: Ignition G Control Signal Circuit Low
s: Ignition G control signal stuck low — short to ground. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Ignition G Control Signal Circuit High
s: Ignition G control signal stuck high — short to B+. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Ignition H Control Signal Circuit/Open
s: The ignition H control signal circuit is open — typically the PCM drive line for the first ignition coil group has lost continuity.

t: Ignition H Control Signal Circuit Low
s: Ignition H control signal stuck low — short to ground. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Ignition H Control Signal Circuit High
s: Ignition H control signal stuck high — short to B+. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Timing Reference High Resolution Signal A
s: A general electrical fault on the high-resolution timing reference signal A — used by ignition and injection systems for precise crank angle measurement (typically a 60-2 or 36-1 tone wheel signal).

t: Timing Reference High Resolution Signal A Too Many Pulses
s: Timing reference signal A delivers more pulses per crank revolution than expected — typically electrical noise, missing reluctor gap, or wrong tone wheel.

t: Timing Reference High Resolution Signal A Too Few Pulses
s: Timing reference signal A delivers fewer pulses per crank revolution than expected — broken reluctor teeth, sensor air gap too wide, or tone wheel chipped.

t: Timing Reference High Resolution Signal A Intermittent/Erratic Pulses
s: Timing reference signal A pulses are erratic — connector or wiring intermittent, contamination on tone wheel, or sensor partially failed.

t: Timing Reference High Resolution Signal A No Pulses
s: No pulses detected on timing reference signal A — open circuit, dead sensor, or air gap too wide.

t: Timing Reference High Resolution Signal B
s: A general electrical fault on the high-resolution timing reference signal B — used by ignition and injection systems for precise crank angle measurement (typically a 60-2 or 36-1 tone wheel signal).

t: Timing Reference High Resolution Signal B Too Many Pulses
s: Timing reference signal B delivers more pulses per crank revolution than expected — typically electrical noise, missing reluctor gap, or wrong tone wheel.

t: Timing Reference High Resolution Signal B Too Few Pulses
s: Timing reference signal B delivers fewer pulses per crank revolution than expected — broken reluctor teeth, sensor air gap too wide, or tone wheel chipped.

t: Timing Reference High Resolution Signal B Intermittent/Erratic Pulses
s: Timing reference signal B pulses are erratic — connector or wiring intermittent, contamination on tone wheel, or sensor partially failed.

t: Timing Reference High Resolution Signal B No Pulses
s: No pulses detected on timing reference signal B — open circuit, dead sensor, or air gap too wide.

t: Glow Plug/Heater Indicator Control Circuit Low
s: The driver circuit for the glow plug warning indicator (the dashboard glow lamp) is stuck low — short to ground or stuck-low driver.

t: Glow Plug/Heater Indicator Control Circuit High
s: Glow plug indicator drive line stuck high — short to B+. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Cold Start Camshaft Profile System Performance
s: On engines with multi-profile (cold-start) cam lobes (e.g. some Honda i-VTEC variants and BMW Valvetronic), the cold-start cam profile actuator is not switching as expected — typically a stuck oil control valve or worn locking pin.

t: Glow Plug Sense Circuit
s: The diagnostic feedback line that monitors glow plug current draw reports a general electrical fault — open, shorted, or out of plausibility. Used to verify each plug actually heats up.

t: Glow Plug Sense Circuit Low
s: Glow plug sense line below lower plausibility — short to ground. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Glow Plug Sense Circuit High
s: Glow plug sense line above upper plausibility — open signal or short to B+. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Glow Plug/Heater Circuit "A" Malfunction
s: The glow plug control module reports an electrical fault on circuit A — typically a single glow plug open-circuit, the relay failing, or wiring to the harness damaged. Diesel-specific; affects cold-start behaviour.

t: Glow Plug/Heater Indicator Circuit Malfunction
s: Electrical fault in the glow plug indicator lamp or relay circuit. The ECU monitors driver feedback to ensure the dashboard indicator can be commanded; an open or shorted circuit triggers this code.

t: Glow Plug/Heater Circuit B
s: A general electrical fault on glow-plug heater circuit B — typically the second bank of glow plugs on an inline-6 or V-engine.

t: Glow Plug Control Module 1 Control Circuit Low
s: The drive line from the PCM to glow plug control module 1 is stuck low — short to ground. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Glow Plug Control Module 1 Control Circuit High
s: The drive line from the PCM to glow plug control module 1 is stuck high — short to B+. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Crankshaft Position Sensor B Circuit
s: A general electrical fault on crankshaft position sensor B — second crank sensor used as backup or for advanced timing models on some engines.

t: Crankshaft Position Sensor B Circuit Range/Performance
s: Crank sensor B reads in range but plausibility check fails. The crankshaft position sensor provides RPM and crank angle — without a valid signal, no ignition and no injection. Engine will not start or stalls instantly.

t: Crankshaft Position Sensor B Circuit Low
s: Crank sensor B below lower plausibility — short to ground. The crankshaft position sensor provides RPM and crank angle — without a valid signal, no ignition and no injection. Engine will not start or stalls instantly.

t: Crankshaft Position Sensor B Circuit High
s: Crank sensor B above upper plausibility — open signal or short to B+. The crankshaft position sensor provides RPM and crank angle — without a valid signal, no ignition and no injection. Engine will not start or stalls instantly.

t: Crankshaft Position Sensor B Circuit Intermittent
s: Crank sensor B signal jumps or drops out intermittently — connector or wiring issue. The crankshaft position sensor provides RPM and crank angle — without a valid signal, no ignition and no injection. Engine will not start or stalls instantly.

t: Ignition I Control Signal Circuit/Open
s: The ignition I control signal circuit is open — typically the PCM drive line for the first ignition coil group has lost continuity.

t: Ignition I Control Signal Circuit Low
s: Ignition I control signal stuck low — short to ground. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Ignition I Control Signal Circuit High
s: Ignition I control signal stuck high — short to B+. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Ignition J Control Signal Circuit/Open
s: The ignition J control signal circuit is open — typically the PCM drive line for the first ignition coil group has lost continuity.

t: Ignition J Control Signal Circuit Low
s: Ignition J control signal stuck low — short to ground. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Ignition J Control Signal Circuit High
s: Ignition J control signal stuck high — short to B+. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Camshaft Position Sensor B Circuit (Bank 2)
s: A general electrical fault on camshaft position sensor B (typically the exhaust cam) on bank 2 — open, shorted, or out of plausibility. Required for variable valve timing on the exhaust cam.

t: Camshaft Position Sensor B Circuit Range/Performance (Bank 2)
s: Cam sensor B on bank 2 reads in range but plausibility check fails — slow response, drift, or signal does not match expected cam profile.

t: Camshaft Position Sensor B Circuit Low (Bank 2)
s: Cam sensor B on bank 2 below lower plausibility — short to ground. The camshaft position sensor identifies which cylinder is on the compression stroke — without it the ECM cannot run sequential injection or coil-on-plug ignition correctly.

t: Camshaft Position Sensor B Circuit High (Bank 2)
s: Cam sensor B on bank 2 above upper plausibility — open signal or short to B+. The camshaft position sensor identifies which cylinder is on the compression stroke — without it the ECM cannot run sequential injection or coil-on-plug ignition correctly.

t: Camshaft Position Sensor B Circuit Intermittent (Bank 2)
s: Camshaft position sensor B signal jumps or drops out intermittently on bank 2 (or on engines with a single cam sensor).

t: Cylinder 1 Pressure Sensor Circuit
s: A general electrical fault on the cylinder 1 combustion pressure sensor — used on engines with closed-loop combustion control (e.g. some Volvo and BMW direct-injection diesels) to measure cylinder pressure directly via a sensor at the glow plug or injector position.

t: Cylinder 1 Pressure Sensor Circuit Range/Performance
s: Cylinder 1 pressure sensor reads in range but plausibility check fails. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Cylinder 1 Pressure Sensor Circuit Low
s: Cylinder 1 pressure sensor below lower plausibility — short to ground. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Cylinder 1 Pressure Sensor Circuit High
s: Cylinder 1 pressure sensor above upper plausibility — open signal or short to B+. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Cylinder 1 Pressure Sensor Circuit Intermittent/Erratic
s: Cylinder 1 pressure sensor signal jumps or drops out intermittently — connector or wiring issue. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Cylinder 1 Pressure Too Low
s: Cylinder 1 measured peak combustion pressure is below the calibrated minimum — typically low compression, weak injection, or stuck-open exhaust valve.

t: Cylinder 1 Pressure Too High
s: Cylinder 1 measured peak combustion pressure is above the calibrated maximum — typically pre-ignition, knock, over-fuelling, or runaway combustion.

t: Cylinder 1 Pressure Variation Low
s: Cylinder-to-cylinder pressure variation on cylinder 1 is below the calibrated minimum — pressure stays unusually constant cycle-to-cycle, often a sign that the closed-loop combustion controller has lost authority or the sensor signal is filtered.

t: Cylinder 1 Pressure Variation High
s: Cylinder-to-cylinder pressure variation on cylinder 1 is above the calibrated maximum — combustion is unstable cycle-to-cycle, typical of partial misfire or injector seat erosion.

t: Cylinder 1 Combustion Performance
s: The closed-loop combustion controller reports that combustion performance on cylinder 1 is below the calibrated quality metric. Computed from the cylinder pressure trace — IMEP (indicated mean effective pressure), CA50 (50% mass burnt angle) or peak pressure deviates from the model.

t: Cylinder 2 Pressure Sensor Circuit
s: A general electrical fault on the cylinder 2 combustion pressure sensor — used on engines with closed-loop combustion control (e.g. some Volvo and BMW direct-injection diesels) to measure cylinder pressure directly via a sensor at the glow plug or injector position.

t: Cylinder 2 Pressure Sensor Circuit Range/Performance
s: Cylinder 2 pressure sensor reads in range but plausibility check fails. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

s: Cylinder 2 pressure sensor below lower plausibility — short to ground. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Cylinder 2 Pressure Sensor Circuit High
s: Cylinder 2 pressure sensor above upper plausibility — open signal or short to B+. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

s: Cylinder 2 pressure sensor signal jumps or drops out intermittently — connector or wiring issue. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Cylinder 2 Pressure Too High
s: Cylinder 2 measured peak combustion pressure is above the calibrated maximum — typically pre-ignition, knock, over-fuelling, or runaway combustion.

s: A general electrical fault on the cylinder 3 combustion pressure sensor — used on engines with closed-loop combustion control (e.g. some Volvo and BMW direct-injection diesels) to measure cylinder pressure directly via a sensor at the glow plug or injector position.

t: Cylinder 4 Pressure Sensor Circuit
s: A general electrical fault on the cylinder 4 combustion pressure sensor — used on engines with closed-loop combustion control (e.g. some Volvo and BMW direct-injection diesels) to measure cylinder pressure directly via a sensor at the glow plug or injector position.

t: Cylinder 4 Pressure Sensor Circuit Range/Performance
s: Cylinder 4 pressure sensor reads in range but plausibility check fails. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Cylinder 4 Pressure Sensor Circuit Low

t: Cylinder 4 Pressure Sensor Circuit Intermittent/Erratic

t: Cylinder 4 Pressure Too Low
s: Cylinder 4 measured peak combustion pressure is below the calibrated minimum — typically low compression, weak injection, or stuck-open exhaust valve.

t: Cylinder 4 Pressure Too High
s: Cylinder 4 measured peak combustion pressure is above the calibrated maximum — typically pre-ignition, knock, over-fuelling, or runaway combustion.

t: Cylinder 4 Pressure Variation Low
s: Cylinder-to-cylinder pressure variation on cylinder 4 is below the calibrated minimum — pressure stays unusually constant cycle-to-cycle, often a sign that the closed-loop combustion controller has lost authority or the sensor signal is filtered.

t: Cylinder 4 Pressure Variation High
s: Cylinder-to-cylinder pressure variation on cylinder 4 is above the calibrated maximum — combustion is unstable cycle-to-cycle, typical of partial misfire or injector seat erosion.

t: Cylinder 4 Combustion Performance
s: The closed-loop combustion controller reports that combustion performance on cylinder 4 is below the calibrated quality metric. Computed from the cylinder pressure trace — IMEP (indicated mean effective pressure), CA50 (50% mass burnt angle) or peak pressure deviates from the model.

t: A Camshaft Position Actuator Position Sensor C Circuit (Bank 1)
s: A general electrical fault on the position sensor C of the intake (A) camshaft phaser actuator on bank 1. Used as additional feedback on engines with multiple cam profiles or three-position cam phasing.

t: A Camshaft Position Actuator Position Sensor C Range/Performance (Bank 1)
s: Position sensor C reads in range but plausibility check fails — slow response or stuck reading. The camshaft position sensor identifies which cylinder is on the compression stroke — without it the ECM cannot run sequential injection or coil-on-plug ignition correctly.

t: A Camshaft Position Actuator Position Sensor C Circuit Low (Bank 1)
s: Position sensor C below lower plausibility — short to ground. The camshaft position sensor identifies which cylinder is on the compression stroke — without it the ECM cannot run sequential injection or coil-on-plug ignition correctly.

t: A Camshaft Position Actuator Position Sensor C Circuit High (Bank 1)
s: Position sensor C above upper plausibility — open signal or short to B+. The camshaft position sensor identifies which cylinder is on the compression stroke — without it the ECM cannot run sequential injection or coil-on-plug ignition correctly.

t: A Camshaft Profile Actuator Position Sensor A Exceeded Learning Limit
s: The adaptive learning offset for camshaft profile actuator A position sensor A has reached its learning limit. Long-term mechanical wear of the cam profile lock pin or oil control valve.

t: A Camshaft Profile Actuator Position Sensor B Exceeded Learning Limit
s: The adaptive learning offset for camshaft profile actuator A position sensor B has reached its learning limit. Long-term mechanical wear of the cam profile lock pin or oil control valve.

t: A Camshaft Profile Actuator Position Sensor C Exceeded Learning Limit
s: The adaptive learning offset for camshaft profile actuator A position sensor C has reached its learning limit. Long-term mechanical wear of the cam profile lock pin or oil control valve.

t: A Camshaft Profile Actuator C Control Circuit/Open (Bank 1)
s: The drive circuit for cam profile actuator A in position C on bank 1 is open. Three-stage cam profile systems use a separate control valve per profile position.

t: A Camshaft Profile Actuator C Control Circuit Low (Bank 1)
s: Cam profile actuator A position C drive line stuck low — short to ground. The cam phaser actuator adjusts intake/exhaust timing under load — a stuck or slow phaser kills mid-rpm torque and can throw correlation codes between cam and crank.

t: A Camshaft Profile Actuator C Control Circuit High (Bank 1)
s: Cam profile actuator A position C drive line stuck high — short to B+. The cam phaser actuator adjusts intake/exhaust timing under load — a stuck or slow phaser kills mid-rpm torque and can throw correlation codes between cam and crank.

t: A Camshaft Profile Actuator C Control Performance/Stuck Off (Bank 1)
s: Cam profile actuator A position C is electrically OK but does not engage when commanded. The cam phaser actuator adjusts intake/exhaust timing under load — a stuck or slow phaser kills mid-rpm torque and can throw correlation codes between cam and crank.

t: A Camshaft Profile Actuator C Control Stuck On (Bank 1)
s: Cam profile actuator A position C is stuck engaged when commanded off — typically a stuck oil control valve.

t: B Camshaft Profile Actuator C Control Circuit/Open (Bank 1)
s: The drive circuit for cam profile actuator B in position C on bank 1 is open. Three-stage cam profile systems use a separate control valve per profile position.

t: B Camshaft Profile Actuator C Control Circuit Low (Bank 1)
s: Cam profile actuator A position C drive line stuck low — short to ground. The cam phaser actuator adjusts intake/exhaust timing under load — a stuck or slow phaser kills mid-rpm torque and can throw correlation codes between cam and crank.

t: B Camshaft Profile Actuator C Control Circuit High (Bank 1)
s: Cam profile actuator A position C drive line stuck high — short to B+. The cam phaser actuator adjusts intake/exhaust timing under load — a stuck or slow phaser kills mid-rpm torque and can throw correlation codes between cam and crank.

t: B Camshaft Profile Actuator C Control Performance/Stuck Off (Bank 1)
s: Cam profile actuator B position C is electrically OK but does not engage when commanded. The cam phaser actuator adjusts intake/exhaust timing under load — a stuck or slow phaser kills mid-rpm torque and can throw correlation codes between cam and crank.

t: B Camshaft Profile Actuator C Control Stuck On (Bank 1)
s: Cam profile actuator B position C is stuck engaged when commanded off — typically a stuck oil control valve.

t: B Camshaft Position Actuator Position Sensor C Circuit (Bank 1)
s: A general electrical fault on the position sensor C of the intake (A) camshaft phaser actuator on bank 1. Used as additional feedback on engines with multiple cam profiles or three-position cam phasing.

t: B Camshaft Position Actuator Position Sensor C Range/Performance (Bank 1)
s: Position sensor C reads in range but plausibility check fails — slow response or stuck reading. The camshaft position sensor identifies which cylinder is on the compression stroke — without it the ECM cannot run sequential injection or coil-on-plug ignition correctly.

t: B Camshaft Position Actuator Position Sensor C Circuit Low (Bank 1)
s: Position sensor C below lower plausibility — short to ground. The camshaft position sensor identifies which cylinder is on the compression stroke — without it the ECM cannot run sequential injection or coil-on-plug ignition correctly.

t: B Camshaft Position Actuator Position Sensor C Circuit High (Bank 1)
s: Position sensor C above upper plausibility — open signal or short to B+. The camshaft position sensor identifies which cylinder is on the compression stroke — without it the ECM cannot run sequential injection or coil-on-plug ignition correctly.

t: Ignition K Control Signal Circuit/Open
s: The ignition A control signal circuit is open — typically the PCM drive line for the first ignition coil group has lost continuity.

t: Ignition K Control Signal Circuit Low
s: Ignition A control signal stuck low — short to ground. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Ignition K Control Signal Circuit High
s: Ignition A control signal stuck high — short to B+. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Ignition L Control Signal Circuit/Open
s: The ignition A control signal circuit is open — typically the PCM drive line for the first ignition coil group has lost continuity.

t: Ignition L Control Signal Circuit Low
s: Ignition A control signal stuck low — short to ground. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Ignition L Control Signal Circuit High
s: Ignition A control signal stuck high — short to B+. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Exhaust Gas Recirculation Flow Malfunction
s: A general fault has been detected in the exhaust gas recirculation (EGR) system. EGR routes a portion of exhaust back into the intake to reduce combustion temperature and NOx emissions. Most EGR codes today are caused by carbon buildup blocking the valve.

t: Exhaust Gas Recirculation Flow Insufficient Detected
s: When the ECU commands EGR open, the actual flow measured by the DPFE sensor or the resulting MAP/MAF change is below threshold. By far the most common cause is a carboned-up EGR valve or blocked intake passages.

t: Exhaust Gas Recirculation Flow Excessive Detected
s: EGR flow is higher than expected when the valve should be closed, or higher than commanded when partially open. Typically a valve that has stuck open due to carbon, causing rough idle and stalling.

t: EGR Control Circuit
s: The control circuit for the EGR valve solenoid is open or shorted. The ECM cannot drive the valve to the commanded position.

t: EGR Range/Performance
s: The EGR valve does not move through its expected range or its position feedback does not track command. The valve may be sticking, the position sensor may be drifting or carbon buildup is preventing full travel.

t: Exhaust Gas Recirculation Sensor "A" Circuit Low
s: The EGR position sensor A signal voltage is below the lower diagnostic threshold — open circuit, short to ground, or sensor fault. With no valid feedback, the ECU disables EGR and may set additional EGR codes.

t: Exhaust Gas Recirculation Sensor "A" Circuit High
s: The EGR position sensor A signal voltage is below the lower diagnostic threshold — open circuit, short to ground, or sensor fault. With no valid feedback, the ECU disables EGR and may set additional EGR codes.

t: EGR Sensor B Circuit Low
s: The signal from EGR position or pressure sensor B is below the lower diagnostic threshold. Indicates a short to ground, open signal wire or sensor fault.

t: Exhaust Gas Recirculation Sensor "B" Circuit Low
s: The EGR position sensor A signal voltage is below the lower diagnostic threshold — open circuit, short to ground, or sensor fault. With no valid feedback, the ECU disables EGR and may set additional EGR codes.

t: Exhaust Gas Recirculation Sensor "B" Circuit High
s: The EGR position sensor A signal voltage is below the lower diagnostic threshold — open circuit, short to ground, or sensor fault. With no valid feedback, the ECU disables EGR and may set additional EGR codes.

t: EGR Temperature Sensor A Circuit
s: A general electrical fault on EGR temperature sensor A — open, shorted, or out of plausibility. The sensor monitors recirculated exhaust gas temperature for EGR cooler protection and dosing.

t: EGR Temperature Sensor A Circuit Range/Performance
s: EGR temperature sensor A reads in range but plausibility check fails — slow response or stuck reading.

t: EGR Temperature Sensor A Circuit Low
s: EGR temperature sensor A below lower plausibility — short to ground. EGR mixes inert exhaust into intake to lower combustion peak temperature — too little raises NOx, too much causes misfires and rough running.

t: EGR Temperature Sensor A Circuit High
s: EGR temperature sensor A above upper plausibility — open signal or short to B+. EGR mixes inert exhaust into intake to lower combustion peak temperature — too little raises NOx, too much causes misfires and rough running.

t: EGR Temperature Sensor A Circuit Intermittent/Erratic
s: EGR temperature sensor A signal jumps or drops out intermittently. EGR mixes inert exhaust into intake to lower combustion peak temperature — too little raises NOx, too much causes misfires and rough running.

t: EGR Temperature Sensor A/B Correlation
s: EGR temperature sensors A and B disagree by more than the allowed tolerance over a drive cycle. EGR mixes inert exhaust into intake to lower combustion peak temperature — too little raises NOx, too much causes misfires and rough running.

t: Secondary Air Injection System Malfunction
s: A general fault in the secondary air injection system. The system pumps fresh air into the exhaust during cold start to accelerate catalyst light-off; common faults are a seized pump, blocked passages or a stuck valve.

t: Secondary Air Injection System Incorrect Flow
s: When the ECU commands secondary air injection, the actual flow measured by the front oxygen sensor (or a dedicated mass flow meter) does not match the expected value. Usually a partially blocked passage or a leaking combi valve.

t: Secondary Air Injection System Switching Valve A Circuit
s: A general electrical fault on AIR system switching valve A — open, shorted, or out of plausibility. The valve diverts secondary air between manifolds during cold start to speed up catalyst light-off.

t: Secondary Air Injection System Switching Valve A Circuit Open
s: AIR system switching valve A drive circuit open — solenoid winding or harness break. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Secondary Air Injection System Switching Valve "A" Circuit Shorted
s: The ECU detected a short circuit in the secondary air injection switching valve A control circuit. With the valve unable to switch correctly, the cold-start emissions strategy fails and the catalyst takes longer to reach light-off.

t: Secondary Air Injection System Switching Valve B Circuit
s: A general electrical fault on AIR system switching valve A — open, shorted, or out of plausibility. The valve diverts secondary air between manifolds during cold start to speed up catalyst light-off.

t: Secondary Air Injection System Switching Valve B Circuit Open
s: AIR system switching valve A drive circuit open — solenoid winding or harness break. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Secondary Air Injection System Switching Valve B Circuit Shorted
s: AIR switching valve B drive circuit shorted to ground or B+.

t: Secondary Air Injection System Relay "A" Circuit Malfunction
s: General electrical fault in the secondary air injection pump relay A circuit. Without the relay, the air pump cannot be commanded and the system cannot complete its cold-start function.

t: Secondary Air Injection System Control B Circuit
s: A general electrical fault on AIR system control circuit B (typically the AIR pump driver line on the second pump or relay).

t: EGR Temperature Sensor B Circuit
s: A general electrical fault on EGR temperature sensor B — open, shorted, or out of plausibility. The sensor monitors recirculated exhaust gas temperature for EGR cooler protection and dosing.

t: EGR Temperature Sensor B Circuit Range/Performance
s: EGR temperature sensor B reads in range but plausibility check fails — slow response or stuck reading.

t: EGR Temperature Sensor B Circuit Low
s: EGR temperature sensor B below lower plausibility — short to ground. EGR mixes inert exhaust into intake to lower combustion peak temperature — too little raises NOx, too much causes misfires and rough running.

t: EGR Temperature Sensor B Circuit High
s: EGR temperature sensor B above upper plausibility — open signal or short to B+. EGR mixes inert exhaust into intake to lower combustion peak temperature — too little raises NOx, too much causes misfires and rough running.

t: EGR Temperature Sensor B Circuit Intermittent/Erratic
s: EGR temperature sensor B signal jumps or drops out intermittently. EGR mixes inert exhaust into intake to lower combustion peak temperature — too little raises NOx, too much causes misfires and rough running.

t: Secondary Air Injection System Switching Valve A Circuit Low
s: AIR switching valve A drive line stuck low — short to ground. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Catalyst System Efficiency Below Threshold (Bank 1)
s: The oxygen storage capacity of the main catalytic converter on bank 1 has degraded below the OBD threshold. The downstream oxygen sensor signal increasingly mirrors the upstream sensor, indicating reduced conversion efficiency.

t: Warm Up Catalyst Efficiency Below Threshold (Bank 1)
s: The pre-cat (warm-up) catalyst on bank 1 has lost too much oxygen storage capacity. Its primary purpose — quickly reaching light-off after a cold start — is no longer met within the OBD limits.

t: Main Catalyst Efficiency Below Threshold (Bank 1)
s: Generic main catalyst efficiency code on bank 1 — used by some manufacturers in addition to or instead of P0420. Indicates the same root cause — degraded oxygen storage in the main catalyst.

t: Heated Catalyst Efficiency Below Threshold (Bank 1)
s: Heated (electrically warmed) catalyst on bank 1 fails efficiency monitoring. In addition to standard catalyst aging, faults in the catalyst heater control or supply can cause this code.

t: Heated Catalyst Temperature Below Threshold (Bank 1)
s: The heated catalyst on bank 1 does not reach its target temperature within the expected time. Common causes are a defective heater element, supply fault, or a damaged temperature sensor.

t: Catalyst Temperature Sensor (Bank 1, Sensor 1)
s: General electrical fault detected in the catalyst temperature sensor on bank 1 sensor position 1. The ECU uses this signal to manage heater control and protect the catalyst from overtemperature.

t: Catalyst Temperature Sensor Circuit Range/Performance (Bank 1 Sensor 1)
s: Catalyst temperature sensor at bank 1 sensor 1 reads in range but plausibility check fails — slow response or stuck reading.

t: Catalyst Temperature Sensor Circuit Low (Bank 1 Sensor 1)
s: Catalyst temperature sensor at bank 1 sensor 1 below lower plausibility — short to ground. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Catalyst Temperature Sensor Circuit High (Bank 1 Sensor 1)
s: Catalyst temperature sensor at bank 1 sensor 1 above upper plausibility — open signal or short to B+.

t: Catalyst Heater Control Circuit/Open (Bank 1)
s: The drive circuit for the heated catalyst on bank 1 is open. Heated catalysts use an electrical heating element to reach light-off temperature within seconds of cold start.

t: Catalyst Temperature Sensor Circuit (Bank 1 Sensor 2)
s: A general electrical fault on the catalyst temperature sensor at bank 1 sensor 2.

t: Catalyst Temperature Sensor Circuit Range/Performance (Bank 1 Sensor 2)
s: Catalyst temperature sensor at bank 1 sensor 2 reads in range but plausibility check fails — slow response or stuck reading.

t: Catalyst Temperature Sensor Circuit Low (Bank 1 Sensor 2)
s: Catalyst temperature sensor at bank 1 sensor 2 below lower plausibility — short to ground. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Catalyst Temperature Sensor Circuit High (Bank 1 Sensor 2)
s: Catalyst temperature sensor at bank 1 sensor 2 above upper plausibility — open signal or short to B+.

t: EGR A Control Stuck Open
s: EGR valve A is mechanically stuck open — exhaust gas continues to flow into the intake at all times, causing rough idle and lean condition during acceleration.

t: EGR A Control Stuck Closed
s: EGR valve A is mechanically stuck closed — no exhaust gas recirculation, NOx emissions rise and EGR-dependent strategies fail.

t: Catalyst System Efficiency Below Threshold (Bank 2)
s: Same condition as P0420 but on bank 2. On engines with one shared catalyst per bank, P0420 and P0430 appearing together usually point to age rather than damage.

t: Warm Up Catalyst Efficiency Below Threshold (Bank 2)
s: The pre-cat (warm-up) catalyst on bank 2 has lost too much oxygen storage capacity. Its primary purpose — quickly reaching light-off after a cold start — is no longer met within the OBD limits.

t: Main Catalyst Efficiency Below Threshold (Bank 2)
s: Generic main catalyst efficiency code on bank 2 — used by some manufacturers in addition to or instead of P0420. Indicates the same root cause — degraded oxygen storage in the main catalyst.

t: Heated Catalyst Efficiency Below Threshold (Bank 2)
s: Heated (electrically warmed) catalyst on bank 2 fails efficiency monitoring. In addition to standard catalyst aging, faults in the catalyst heater control or supply can cause this code.

t: Heated Catalyst Temperature Below Threshold (Bank 2)
s: The heated catalyst on bank 2 has not reached its calibrated light-off temperature within the expected time after engine start. Heater element weak or insufficient supply.

t: Catalyst Temperature Sensor Circuit (Bank 2 Sensor 1)
s: A general electrical fault on the catalyst temperature sensor at bank 2 sensor 1.

t: Catalyst Temperature Sensor Circuit Range/Performance (Bank 2 Sensor 1)
s: Catalyst temperature sensor at bank 2 sensor 1 reads in range but plausibility check fails — slow response or stuck reading.

t: Catalyst Temperature Sensor Circuit Low (Bank 2 Sensor 1)
s: Catalyst temperature sensor at bank 2 sensor 1 below lower plausibility — short to ground. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Catalyst Temperature Sensor Circuit High (Bank 2 Sensor 1)
s: Catalyst temperature sensor at bank 2 sensor 1 above upper plausibility — open signal or short to B+.

t: Catalyst Heater Control Circuit/Open (Bank 2)
s: The drive circuit for the heated catalyst on bank 2 is open. Heated catalysts use an electrical heating element to reach light-off temperature within seconds of cold start.

t: Catalyst Temperature Sensor Circuit (Bank 2 Sensor 2)
s: A general electrical fault on the catalyst temperature sensor at bank 2 sensor 2.

t: Catalyst Temperature Sensor Circuit Range/Performance (Bank 2 Sensor 2)
s: Catalyst temperature sensor at bank 2 sensor 2 reads in range but plausibility check fails — slow response or stuck reading.

t: Catalyst Temperature Sensor Circuit Low (Bank 2 Sensor 2)
s: Catalyst temperature sensor at bank 2 sensor 2 below lower plausibility — short to ground. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Catalyst Temperature Sensor Circuit High (Bank 2 Sensor 2)
s: Catalyst temperature sensor at bank 2 sensor 2 above upper plausibility — open signal or short to B+.

t: EVAP System Leak Detection Reference Orifice Low Flow
s: During the EVAP leak-detection self-test, the calibration airflow through the reference orifice is below the expected rate. The orifice may be partially blocked.

t: EVAP System Leak Detection Reference Orifice High Flow
s: During the leak-detection self-test, airflow through the reference orifice is above the expected rate — typically a leak around the orifice or a damaged seal.

t: Evaporative Emission Control System Malfunction
s: A general fault in the evaporative emission (EVAP) system has been detected. The system seals the fuel tank and captures fuel vapour in a charcoal canister; this code indicates a failed leak test or sensor without identifying the specific failure.

t: Evaporative Emission System Incorrect Purge Flow
s: The actual purge flow does not match the commanded value. Usually a purge valve stuck closed (no flow) or stuck open (too much flow at idle). Distinct from a leak code.

t: Evaporative Emission System Leak Detected (Small Leak)
s: A small leak (typically equivalent to a 0.040 inch / 1.0 mm orifice) has been detected in the EVAP system. By far the most common cause is a fuel cap that is loose, missing, or has a worn seal.

t: Evaporative Emission System Purge Control Valve Circuit
s: Electrical fault in the EVAP purge solenoid circuit. The ECU detects an open, shorted, or out-of-range condition on the purge valve driver.

t: Evaporative Emission Purge Control Valve Circuit Open
s: The ECU has detected an open circuit in the purge solenoid control line — usually a broken wire, unplugged connector, or open coil winding.

t: Evaporative Emission System Purge Control Valve Circuit Shorted
s: The ECU detected a short in the EVAP purge solenoid control circuit. With the valve permanently energized or shorted, the system can either over-purge causing rich running, or stay closed.

t: Evaporative Emission Vent Control Circuit Malfunction
s: A fault in the EVAP vent valve circuit. The vent valve seals the canister against atmosphere during the leak test; without it, the test cannot pull a vacuum and may set false leak codes alongside.

t: Evaporative Emission System Vent Control Circuit Open
s: The ECU detected an open circuit on the EVAP vent valve control. The vent valve is needed to seal the system for leak diagnostics; with the circuit open the leak test cannot complete.

t: EVAP System Vent Control Circuit Shorted
s: EVAP vent valve drive circuit shorted to ground or B+. The vent valve seals the canister during pressure-test diagnostics.

t: Evaporative Emission System Vent Valve/Solenoid Circuit Malfunction
s: A general electrical fault was detected in the EVAP vent valve/solenoid control circuit. Without a working vent valve the EVAP leak test cannot complete and the system may remain not-ready.

t: EGR Sensor C Circuit
s: A general electrical fault on EGR sensor C — used as a third feedback (e.g. EGR delta-pressure or third position sensor) on advanced EGR systems.

t: EGR Sensor C Circuit Range/Performance
s: A general electrical fault on EGR sensor C — used as a third feedback (e.g. EGR delta-pressure or third position sensor) on advanced EGR systems.

t: EGR Sensor C Circuit Low
s: EGR sensor C below lower plausibility — short to ground. EGR mixes inert exhaust into intake to lower combustion peak temperature — too little raises NOx, too much causes misfires and rough running.

t: EGR Sensor C Circuit High
s: EGR sensor C above upper plausibility — open signal or short to B+. EGR mixes inert exhaust into intake to lower combustion peak temperature — too little raises NOx, too much causes misfires and rough running.

t: EGR Sensor C Circuit Intermittent/Erratic
s: EGR sensor C signal jumps or drops out intermittently. EGR mixes inert exhaust into intake to lower combustion peak temperature — too little raises NOx, too much causes misfires and rough running.

t: Secondary Air Injection System Switching Valve A Circuit High
s: AIR switching valve A drive line stuck high — short to B+. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: EVAP System Pressure Sensor/Switch A Circuit
s: A general electrical fault on EVAP pressure sensor/switch A — used to monitor canister pressure during leak-detection diagnostics.

t: Evaporative Emission System Pressure Sensor Range/Performance
s: The fuel tank pressure sensor reading is implausible — for example, fluctuating signal, noise, or values that do not respond to known pressure events such as a key-on tank refill.

t: Evaporative Emission System Pressure Sensor Low Input
s: The fuel tank pressure sensor signal is below the expected minimum voltage. Often caused by a failed sensor or a short to ground in the signal line.

t: Evaporative Emission System Pressure Sensor High Input
s: The fuel tank pressure sensor signal is above the expected maximum voltage, typical of a short to supply or a sensor that has failed high.

t: EVAP System Pressure Sensor/Switch A Circuit Intermittent
s: EVAP pressure sensor A signal jumps or drops out intermittently. The evaporative system captures fuel vapor from the tank and purges it into intake; leaks or stuck purge valves leak hydrocarbons and cause emissions failures.

t: Evaporative Emission System Leak Detected (Large Leak)
s: A large leak has been detected during the EVAP system pressure or vacuum test — large enough that the system cannot hold pressure for the diagnostic interval. Typically a missing fuel cap, disconnected hose, or split canister.

t: Evaporative Emission System Leak Detected (Very Small Leak)
s: A very small leak (typically equivalent to a 0.020 inch / 0.5 mm orifice) has been detected. Smaller threshold than P0442; intermittent and often weather-dependent. Modern OBD-II requires this monitor on most vehicles since model year 2000.

t: Evaporative Emission System Leak Detected (Fuel Cap Loose/Off)
s: The EVAP system detected a large leak that strongly correlates with a missing or improperly closed fuel filler cap. After re-tightening the cap, the code clears once the readiness monitor completes.

t: EVAP System Purge Control Valve A Circuit Low
s: EVAP purge control valve A drive line stuck low — short to ground. The evaporative system captures fuel vapor from the tank and purges it into intake; leaks or stuck purge valves leak hydrocarbons and cause emissions failures.

t: EVAP System Purge Control Valve A Circuit High
s: EVAP purge control valve A drive line stuck high — short to B+. The evaporative system captures fuel vapor from the tank and purges it into intake; leaks or stuck purge valves leak hydrocarbons and cause emissions failures.

t: EGR B Control Circuit
s: A general electrical fault on EGR control circuit B — second EGR valve on engines with high-pressure and low-pressure EGR loops.

t: EGR B Control Circuit Range/Performance
s: EGR valve B is electrically OK but does not respond as commanded — typically carbon buildup or sticking actuator.

t: EGR B Control Circuit Low
s: EGR B drive line stuck low — short to ground. EGR mixes inert exhaust into intake to lower combustion peak temperature — too little raises NOx, too much causes misfires and rough running.

t: EGR B Control Circuit High
s: EGR B drive line stuck high — short to B+. EGR mixes inert exhaust into intake to lower combustion peak temperature — too little raises NOx, too much causes misfires and rough running.

t: EGR B Control Stuck Open
s: EGR valve A is mechanically stuck open — exhaust gas continues to flow into the intake at all times, causing rough idle and lean condition during acceleration.

t: EGR B Control Stuck Closed
s: EGR valve A is mechanically stuck closed — no exhaust gas recirculation, NOx emissions rise and EGR-dependent strategies fail.

t: Fuel Level Sensor A Circuit
s: A general electrical fault on the primary fuel-level sensor (A) — open, shorted, or out of plausibility.

t: Fuel Level Sensor A Circuit Range/Performance
s: Fuel-level sensor A reads in range but plausibility check fails — value sticks, jumps, or does not match consumption.

t: Fuel Level Sensor A Circuit Low
s: Fuel-level sensor A below lower plausibility — short to ground. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Fuel Level Sensor A Circuit High
s: Fuel-level sensor A above upper plausibility — open signal or short to B+. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Fuel Level Sensor A Circuit Intermittent
s: Fuel-level sensor A signal jumps or drops out intermittently. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: EVAP Purge Flow Sensor Circuit
s: A general electrical fault on the EVAP purge flow sensor — measures actual flow through the purge valve to verify the canister is being purged.

t: EVAP Purge Flow Sensor Circuit Range/Performance
s: EVAP purge flow sensor reads in range but plausibility check fails. The evaporative system captures fuel vapor from the tank and purges it into intake; leaks or stuck purge valves leak hydrocarbons and cause emissions failures.

t: EVAP Purge Flow Sensor Circuit Low
s: EVAP purge flow sensor below lower plausibility — short to ground. The evaporative system captures fuel vapor from the tank and purges it into intake; leaks or stuck purge valves leak hydrocarbons and cause emissions failures.

t: EVAP Purge Flow Sensor Circuit High
s: EVAP purge flow sensor above upper plausibility — open signal or short to B+. The evaporative system captures fuel vapor from the tank and purges it into intake; leaks or stuck purge valves leak hydrocarbons and cause emissions failures.

t: EVAP Purge Flow Sensor Circuit Intermittent
s: EVAP purge flow sensor signal jumps or drops out intermittently. The evaporative system captures fuel vapor from the tank and purges it into intake; leaks or stuck purge valves leak hydrocarbons and cause emissions failures.

t: Catalyst Temperature Sensor 1/2 Correlation (Bank 1)
s: Catalyst temperature sensors 1 and 2 on bank 1 disagree by more than the allowed tolerance over a drive cycle.

t: Catalyst Temperature Sensor 1/2 Correlation (Bank 2)
s: Catalyst temperature sensors 1 and 2 on bank 2 disagree by more than the allowed tolerance over a drive cycle.

t: EGR Sensor A Circuit Range/Performance
s: EGR sensor A reads in range but plausibility check fails — slow response or stuck reading. EGR mixes inert exhaust into intake to lower combustion peak temperature — too little raises NOx, too much causes misfires and rough running.

t: EGR Sensor A Circuit Intermittent/Erratic
s: EGR sensor A signal jumps or drops out intermittently. EGR mixes inert exhaust into intake to lower combustion peak temperature — too little raises NOx, too much causes misfires and rough running.

t: EGR Sensor B Circuit Range/Performance
s: EGR sensor B reads in range but plausibility check fails — slow response or stuck reading. EGR mixes inert exhaust into intake to lower combustion peak temperature — too little raises NOx, too much causes misfires and rough running.

t: EGR Sensor B Circuit Intermittent/Erratic
s: EGR sensor B signal jumps or drops out intermittently. EGR mixes inert exhaust into intake to lower combustion peak temperature — too little raises NOx, too much causes misfires and rough running.

t: Exhaust Pressure Sensor A Circuit
s: A general electrical fault on exhaust pressure sensor A — used to monitor backpressure across the DPF or to control variable-geometry turbo.

t: Exhaust Pressure Sensor A Circuit Range/Performance
