s: Exhaust pressure sensor A reads in range but plausibility check fails. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Exhaust Pressure Sensor A Circuit Low
s: Exhaust pressure sensor A below lower plausibility — short to ground. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Exhaust Pressure Sensor A Circuit High
s: Exhaust pressure sensor A above upper plausibility — open signal or short to B+. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Exhaust Pressure Sensor A Circuit Intermittent/Erratic
s: Exhaust pressure sensor A signal jumps or drops out intermittently. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Exhaust Pressure Control Valve A
s: A general fault on exhaust pressure control valve A — used as a backpressure brake on heavy-duty diesels and to assist DPF regeneration by raising exhaust temperature.

t: Exhaust Pressure Control Valve A Range/Performance
s: Exhaust pressure control valve A is electrically OK but does not move as commanded.

t: Exhaust Pressure Control Valve A Low
s: Exhaust pressure control valve A drive line stuck low — short to ground.

t: Exhaust Pressure Control Valve A High
s: Exhaust pressure control valve A drive line stuck high — short to B+.

t: Exhaust Pressure Control Valve A Intermittent
s: Exhaust pressure control valve A drive signal is unstable.

t: Exhaust Pressure Sensor B Circuit
s: A general electrical fault on exhaust pressure sensor B — used to monitor backpressure across the DPF or to control variable-geometry turbo.

t: Exhaust Pressure Sensor B Circuit Range/Performance
s: Exhaust pressure sensor B reads in range but plausibility check fails. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Exhaust Pressure Sensor B Circuit Low
s: Exhaust pressure sensor B below lower plausibility — short to ground. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Exhaust Pressure Sensor B Circuit High
s: Exhaust pressure sensor B above upper plausibility — open signal or short to B+. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Exhaust Pressure Sensor B Circuit Intermittent/Erratic
s: Exhaust pressure sensor B signal jumps or drops out intermittently. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Exhaust Pressure Control Valve A Stuck Open
s: Exhaust pressure control valve A is mechanically stuck open — backpressure cannot be raised, exhaust brake and DPF regen support fail.

t: Cooling Fan 1 Control Circuit
s: An electrical fault in the cooling fan 1 control circuit (relay or PWM driver). May be set when the ECU detects an open, short, or unexpected current draw.

t: Cooling Fan 2 Control Circuit
s: An electrical fault in the cooling fan 1 control circuit (relay or PWM driver). May be set when the ECU detects an open, short, or unexpected current draw.

t: Cooling Fan 3 Control Circuit Malfunction
s: Electrical fault detected in the third cooling fan control circuit. With one stage of cooling lost, engine and A/C condenser cooling capacity is reduced, particularly at low vehicle speeds.

t: Cooling Fan Rationality Check
s: The ECM detects that the cooling fan response does not match command. Either the fan rotates without command, fails to rotate when commanded or its current draw is implausible.

t: Cooling Fan Current Too High
s: The current draw of the cooling fan exceeds the permitted maximum. Caused by a seized or worn fan motor or wiring shorted to ground at high load.

t: Cooling Fan Power/Ground Circuit
s: A fault is detected in the supply or ground path of the cooling fan circuit. Voltage drops or open ground prevent reliable fan operation.

t: EGR Sensor B Circuit
s: A general electrical fault on EGR sensor B — second EGR sensor on dual-loop or HP/LP EGR systems. EGR mixes inert exhaust into intake to lower combustion peak temperature — too little raises NOx, too much causes misfires and rough running.

t: EGR Throttle Control Circuit A/Open
s: The drive circuit of EGR throttle A is open. EGR throttles partially close intake to increase exhaust gas recirculation flow at low loads.

t: EGR Throttle Control Circuit A Range/Performance
s: EGR throttle A is electrically OK but does not move as commanded. EGR mixes inert exhaust into intake to lower combustion peak temperature — too little raises NOx, too much causes misfires and rough running.

t: EGR A Control Circuit Low
s: EGR A drive line stuck low — short to ground. EGR mixes inert exhaust into intake to lower combustion peak temperature — too little raises NOx, too much causes misfires and rough running.

t: Exhaust Pressure Control Valve A Stuck Closed
s: Exhaust pressure control valve A is mechanically stuck closed — backpressure runs away, can stall the engine.

t: Exhaust Pressure Control Valve A Position Sensor/Switch Circuit
s: A general electrical fault on the position sensor of exhaust pressure control valve A.

t: Exhaust Pressure Control Valve A Position Sensor/Switch Circuit Range/Performance
s: Position sensor reads in range but plausibility check fails. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Exhaust Pressure Control Valve A Position Sensor/Switch Circuit Low
s: Position sensor below lower plausibility — short to ground. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Exhaust Pressure Control Valve A Position Sensor/Switch Circuit High
s: Position sensor above upper plausibility — open signal or short to B+. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Exhaust Pressure Control Valve A Position Sensor/Switch Circuit Intermittent/Erratic
s: Position sensor signal jumps or drops out intermittently. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: EGR A Control Circuit High
s: EGR A drive line stuck high — short to B+. EGR mixes inert exhaust into intake to lower combustion peak temperature — too little raises NOx, too much causes misfires and rough running.

t: Secondary Air Injection System Insufficient Flow (Bank 1)
s: AIR system flow on bank 1 is below threshold during the cold-start self-test — typically a clogged check valve, leaking hose, or weak air pump.

t: Secondary Air Injection System Insufficient Flow (Bank 2)
s: AIR system flow on bank 2 is below threshold during the cold-start self-test — typically a clogged check valve, leaking hose, or weak air pump.

t: Fan Overspeed
s: The cooling fan is rotating faster than the commanded speed. The control unit measures the fan tachometer feedback against the requested duty cycle and finds it well above the expected band - typically a stuck-open relay, a shorted PWM line driving the fan to full power, or a feedback line that misreports speed.

t: Fan 1 Underspeed
s: Cooling fan 1 is rotating slower than commanded. The ECU sees the tachometer feedback below the expected window for the requested duty cycle - usually a worn fan motor, a high-resistance harness or feed, or a partially seized impeller.

t: Fan 1 Overspeed
s: The cooling fan is rotating faster than the commanded speed. The control unit measures the fan tachometer feedback against the requested duty cycle and finds it well above the expected band - typically a stuck-open relay, a shorted PWM line driving the fan to full power, or a feedback line that misreports speed.

t: Evaporative Emission System High Purge Flow
s: The ECU detected EVAP purge flow during a non-purge condition, or higher than commanded purge flow. Typical causes are a stuck-open purge valve or a damaged vent system that draws unmetered air.

t: EVAP System Low Purge Flow
s: The evaporative emissions system measures purge flow either via a dedicated purge flow sensor or via short-term fuel trim response when the purge valve opens. The flow is significantly lower than expected for the commanded purge rate - typically a clogged purge line, a stuck purge valve, or a leaking vapor canister vent.

t: EVAP System Vent Valve Control Circuit Low
s: The vent valve closes the EVAP canister to atmosphere during leak tests. The control line for that valve is shorted to ground - the ECU drives the valve and measures voltage below the expected level, indicating either a harness short or an internal solenoid short.

t: EVAP System Vent Valve Control Circuit High
s: Same as P0498 with the control line open or shorted to supply. The evaporative system captures fuel vapor from the tank and purges it into intake; leaks or stuck purge valves leak hydrocarbons and cause emissions failures.

t: EGR B Flow
s: The exhaust-gas recirculation system has a second EGR loop (B) - typically used on engines with a dedicated low-pressure EGR for emissions or on twin-bank V engines. The ECU detects an EGR B flow fault - the measured or modelled flow does not match the requested rate, without yet diagnosing it as too low or too high.

t: EGR B Flow Insufficient Detected
s: Same as P049A but specifically with measured flow below the required rate - typically EGR valve sticking closed or cooler heavily clogged.

t: EGR B Flow Excessive Detected
s: Same as P049A but with measured flow above the required rate - typically EGR valve sticking open or position sensor offset low.

t: EGR A Control Position Exceeded Learning Limit
s: The ECU continuously learns offsets for the EGR A actuator to compensate for soot buildup and ageing. The learned correction has reached its software-side maximum (or minimum) and cannot trim further - the underlying mechanical drift exceeds the design margin.

t: EGR B Control Position Exceeded Learning Limit
s: The ECU continuously learns offsets for the EGR A actuator to compensate for soot buildup and ageing. The learned correction has reached its software-side maximum (or minimum) and cannot trim further - the underlying mechanical drift exceeds the design margin.

t: Exhaust Pressure Control Valve B
s: A general circuit fault is detected on the second exhaust pressure control valve (B) - used for example on dual exhaust brakes, twin-bank diesels with separate back-pressure flaps, or for DPF regeneration backpressure assist on the second bank. The ECU loses control of this stage.

t: Exhaust Pressure Control Valve B Range or Performance
s: Same as P049F with feedback in plausible range yet not matching the engine model - actuator slow, slipping or position sensor offset.

t: Exhaust Pressure Control Valve B Low
s: Same as P049F with the control line shorted to ground.

t: Exhaust Pressure Control Valve B High
s: Same as P049F with the control line shorted to supply.

t: Exhaust Pressure Control Valve B Intermittent
s: Same as P049F with intermittent or erratic feedback - typically a connector or harness fault.

t: Exhaust Pressure Control Valve B Stuck Open
s: The exhaust pressure control valve B is mechanically stuck in the open position. The ECU commands closing for back-pressure assist or exhaust braking, but the position feedback shows it remains open - typically heavy soot, broken linkage, or a sticky valve shaft.

t: Exhaust Pressure Control Valve B Stuck Closed
s: Same as P04A4 but the valve B is stuck closed - back-pressure stays high even when the engine should run with low restriction; risk of overheating or excessive fuel consumption.

t: Exhaust Pressure Control Valve B Position Sensor Circuit
s: A circuit fault is detected on the position sensor of exhaust pressure control valve B. Without valid position feedback the ECU cannot run closed-loop control of the valve and falls back to a default substitute.

t: Exhaust Pressure Control Valve B Position Sensor Circuit Range or Performance
s: Same as P04A6 with the signal in plausible range but not matching the actual valve angle.

t: Exhaust Pressure Control Valve B Position Sensor Circuit Low
s: Same as P04A6 with the signal voltage below the plausible range. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Exhaust Pressure Control Valve B Position Sensor Circuit High
s: Same as P04A6 with the signal voltage above the plausible range. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Exhaust Pressure Control Valve B Position Sensor Circuit Intermittent or Erratic
s: Same as P04A6 with intermittent or erratic feedback - typically a connector or harness fault. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: EVAP System Purge Control Valve B Circuit Open
s: The second EVAP purge control valve (B) - used on systems with a divided purge path or on twin-bank engines - shows an open control circuit. The ECU cannot drive the valve, so vapor cannot be purged through this stage; tank pressure may rise and emissions diagnostics fail.

t: EVAP System Purge Control Valve B Circuit Low
s: Same as P04AB with the control line shorted to ground. The evaporative system captures fuel vapor from the tank and purges it into intake; leaks or stuck purge valves leak hydrocarbons and cause emissions failures.

t: EVAP System Purge Control Valve B Circuit High
s: Same as P04AB with the control line shorted to supply. The evaporative system captures fuel vapor from the tank and purges it into intake; leaks or stuck purge valves leak hydrocarbons and cause emissions failures.

t: EVAP System Purge Control Valve B Performance Stuck Open
s: The EVAP purge valve B is mechanically stuck open or its feedback indicates flow even when commanded closed - vapor flows uncontrolled into the intake, disturbing fuel trim and idle quality.

t: EVAP System Purge Control Valve B Stuck Closed
s: Same as P04AE but the valve B is stuck closed - no vapor purge possible through this stage, leading to elevated tank pressure and possible canister saturation.

t: Refueling Vapor Control Valve Circuit Open
s: The refueling vapor control valve - typically part of the ORVR or capless fueling system - has an open control circuit. The valve directs fuel vapor from the tank into the canister during refueling and must open when the fuel filler is opened. Without it, vapor escapes during refueling and the system fails leak diagnostics.

t: Refueling Vapor Control Valve Circuit Low
s: Same as P04B0 with the control line shorted to ground. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Refueling Vapor Control Valve Circuit High
s: Same as P04B0 with the control line shorted to supply. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Refueling Vapor Control Valve Performance Stuck Open
s: The refueling vapor control valve is stuck open. Vapor pathways meant to be closed except during refueling stay permanently open, distorting EVAP leak diagnostics and allowing vapor flow even with the fuel cap closed.

t: Refueling Vapor Control Valve Stuck Closed
s: Same as P04B3 with the valve stuck closed - vapor cannot reach the canister during refueling, vapor escape from the filler is possible.

t: Fuel Fill Door Stuck Open
s: On vehicles with a motorised fuel fill door (no manual lever) the actuator and position switch report that the door is mechanically stuck open. The motor cannot retract it, typically due to a frozen or dirty hinge, broken cable, or jammed actuator.

t: Fuel Fill Door Stuck Closed
s: Same as P04B5 with the door stuck closed - the user cannot refuel until the fault is cleared.

t: Fuel Fill Door Position Sensor Circuit Intermittent or Erratic
s: The position sensor or switch on the fuel fill door reports an intermittent signal - opens and closes randomly without matching the actual door position. Typically a connector or chafed harness fault.

t: Fuel Fill Door Position Sensor Circuit
s: A general circuit fault is detected on the fuel fill door position sensor or switch - the ECU cannot determine whether the door is open or closed. Usually an open in supply, signal or ground.

t: Fuel Fill Door Position Sensor Circuit Low
s: Same as P04B8 with the signal voltage below the plausible range. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Fuel Fill Door Position Sensor Circuit High
s: Same as P04B8 with the signal voltage above the plausible range. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Fuel Fill Door Lock Control Circuit Open
s: The fuel fill door has a powered lock that secures it against tampering and against opening at high speed. The lock control line is open - the ECU cannot drive the lock solenoid, leaving it in its mechanical default state.

t: Fuel Fill Door Lock Control Range or Performance
s: The fuel fill door lock does not respond as commanded - lock or unlock requests do not match the position feedback within the expected time.

t: Fuel Fill Door Lock Control Circuit Low
s: Same as P04BB with the control line shorted to ground. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Fuel Fill Door Lock Control Circuit High
s: Same as P04BB with the control line shorted to supply. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Fuel Fill Door Unlock Control Circuit Open
s: The fuel fill door has a powered lock that secures it against tampering and against opening at high speed. The lock control line is open - the ECU cannot drive the lock solenoid, leaving it in its mechanical default state.

t: Fuel Fill Door Unlock Control Range or Performance
s: The fuel fill door lock does not respond as commanded - lock or unlock requests do not match the position feedback within the expected time.

t: Fuel Fill Door Unlock Control Circuit Low
s: Same as P04BD on the unlock side. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Fuel Fill Door Unlock Control Circuit High
s: Same as P04BE on the unlock side. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Fuel Fill Door Lock Position Sensor Circuit
s: A general circuit fault is detected on the fuel fill door lock position sensor or switch. Without it the ECU cannot determine whether the lock has actually engaged or released.

t: Fuel Fill Door Lock Position Sensor Circuit Range or Performance
s: Same as P04C3 with feedback in plausible range yet not matching the commanded lock state.

t: Fuel Fill Door Lock Position Sensor Circuit Low
s: Same as P04C3 with the signal voltage below the plausible range. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Fuel Fill Door Lock Position Sensor Circuit High
s: Same as P04C3 with the signal voltage above the plausible range. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Fuel Fill Door Lock Position Sensor Circuit Intermittent or Erratic
s: Same as P04C3 with intermittent feedback. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Fuel Fill Door Open Request Sensor or Switch Circuit
s: A general circuit fault is detected on the open-request input - the button or switch the driver presses to release the fuel fill door. The ECU loses the request signal, so motorised release becomes unavailable.

t: Fuel Fill Door Open Request Performance Stuck Off
s: The open-request switch never registers an active state - either the contact stays open mechanically or the wiring is permanently broken. The driver cannot trigger door release.

t: Fuel Fill Door Open Request Sensor or Switch Circuit Low
s: Same as P04C8 with the signal voltage shorted to ground or constantly low. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Fuel Fill Door Open Request Sensor or Switch Circuit High
s: Same as P04C8 with the signal voltage shorted to supply or constantly high. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Fuel Fill Door Open Request Sensor or Switch Circuit Intermittent or Erratic
s: Same as P04C8 with intermittent or erratic signal - typically a connector or harness fault. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Fuel Fill Door Open Request Sensor or Switch Stuck On
s: The open-request switch is stuck in the active state - the ECU continuously reads a request to open the fuel fill door. Either the switch is mechanically held closed or the line is shorted to its active level.

t: EGR Temperature Sensor C Circuit
s: A general circuit fault is detected on the third EGR temperature sensor (C) - typically used on systems with multiple EGR loops or to monitor cooler outlet temperature on the secondary loop. The ECU falls back to a substitute value, EGR cooler diagnostics on this stage are limited.

t: EGR Temperature Sensor C Circuit Range or Performance
s: Same as P04CE with the signal in plausible range yet not matching the engine model - typically sensor drift or a slow response after engine warm-up.

t: EGR Temperature Sensor C Circuit Low
s: Same as P04CE with the signal voltage below the plausible range. EGR mixes inert exhaust into intake to lower combustion peak temperature — too little raises NOx, too much causes misfires and rough running.

t: EGR Temperature Sensor C Circuit High
s: Same as P04CE with the signal voltage above the plausible range. EGR mixes inert exhaust into intake to lower combustion peak temperature — too little raises NOx, too much causes misfires and rough running.

t: EGR Temperature Sensor C Circuit Intermittent or Erratic
s: Same as P04CE with intermittent or erratic signal. EGR mixes inert exhaust into intake to lower combustion peak temperature — too little raises NOx, too much causes misfires and rough running.

t: Exhaust Brake Input Circuit
s: The exhaust brake activation input - typically from a stalk lever, brake pedal switch, or vehicle bus message - shows a circuit fault. The ECU cannot accept a request to engage the exhaust brake, which limits engine braking on heavy vehicles.

t: Exhaust Brake Input Circuit Range or Performance
s: Same as P04D3 with the signal in plausible range yet not matching expected behaviour - e.g. activation request without driver input or no response after a request.

t: Exhaust Brake Input Circuit Low
s: Same as P04D3 with the signal voltage below the plausible range. The exhaust control valve modulates exhaust backpressure or routing (EGR feed, sound, regen support); a malfunction shifts torque mapping and may trigger limp mode.

t: Exhaust Brake Input Circuit High
s: Same as P04D3 with the signal voltage above the plausible range. The exhaust control valve modulates exhaust backpressure or routing (EGR feed, sound, regen support); a malfunction shifts torque mapping and may trigger limp mode.

t: Exhaust Brake Input Circuit Intermittent or Erratic
s: Same as P04D3 with intermittent or erratic signal. The exhaust control valve modulates exhaust backpressure or routing (EGR feed, sound, regen support); a malfunction shifts torque mapping and may trigger limp mode.

t: Excessive Time To Enter Closed Loop EGR Control
s: After cold start the ECU operates EGR open-loop until preconditions (cooler temperature, sensor warm-up, engine load) are met, then transitions into closed-loop control. The transition is taking too long - typically due to a slow EGR temperature sensor, a stuck EGR valve still warming up, or a long-trip drive cycle that does not meet load conditions.

t: Closed Loop EGR Control At Limit Flow Too Low
s: With EGR running in closed loop the controller has driven the actuator to its maximum opening but cannot reach the required EGR flow. The valve, cooler, or pipework is restricted enough that even full open does not deliver the modelled rate.

t: Closed Loop EGR Control At Limit Flow Too High
s: Same as P04D9 with the controller fully closing the actuator yet still measuring more EGR flow than required. The valve does not seat fully, or position feedback under-reads.

t: Crankcase Ventilation System Disconnected
s: A pressure or flow sensor in the crankcase ventilation (PCV) circuit reports atmospheric conditions where it should see crankcase or intake vacuum - the hose between crankcase and intake is disconnected, cracked, or open. PCV no longer routes blow-by gases into the intake, increasing oil mist emissions and risking oil leaks.

t: Fuel Fill Door Position Sensor Circuit Range or Performance
s: Same as P04B8 with the signal in plausible range yet not matching the actuator command - door reports closed when motor commanded open, or vice versa.

t: Cold Start EGR A Flow Insufficient Detected
s: During the cold-start strategy the ECU briefly opens EGR A to slow combustion temperatures and reduce engine-out NOx during catalyst light-off. The measured EGR flow stays below the calibrated target despite the requested opening - typically a stuck-closed valve, restricted cold-start path, or sensor offset that under-reports flow.

t: Cold Start EGR A Flow Excessive Detected
s: Same as P04DD with EGR flow above the cold-start target - typically valve sticking open during the cold phase or position sensor offset low.

t: EVAP System Purge Control Valve A Performance Stuck Open
s: The EVAP purge valve B is mechanically stuck open or its feedback indicates flow even when commanded closed - vapor flows uncontrolled into the intake, disturbing fuel trim and idle quality.

t: EVAP System Purge Control Valve A Stuck Closed
s: Same as P04AF on EVAP purge valve A. The evaporative system captures fuel vapor from the tank and purges it into intake; leaks or stuck purge valves leak hydrocarbons and cause emissions failures.

t: Crankcase Ventilation Hose Connection Sensor Circuit
s: A dedicated sensor monitors whether the PCV hose is fitted to its connector at the engine. The ECU detects a circuit fault on this sensor - cannot determine whether the hose is connected. Required by some emissions regulations to flag a disconnected PCV hose immediately.

t: Crankcase Ventilation Hose Connection Sensor Circuit Low
s: Same as P04E1 with the signal voltage below the plausible range. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Crankcase Ventilation Hose Connection Sensor Circuit High
s: Same as P04E1 with the signal voltage above the plausible range. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Crankcase Ventilation Hose Connection Sensor Circuit Intermittent or Erratic
s: Same as P04E1 with intermittent or erratic signal. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: EGR Temperature Sensor D Circuit
s: A general circuit fault is detected on the third EGR temperature sensor (C) - typically used on systems with multiple EGR loops or to monitor cooler outlet temperature on the secondary loop. The ECU falls back to a substitute value, EGR cooler diagnostics on this stage are limited.

t: EGR Temperature Sensor D Circuit Range or Performance
s: Same as P04CF on EGR temperature sensor D. EGR mixes inert exhaust into intake to lower combustion peak temperature — too little raises NOx, too much causes misfires and rough running.

t: EGR Temperature Sensor D Circuit Low
s: Same as P04D0 on EGR temperature sensor D. EGR mixes inert exhaust into intake to lower combustion peak temperature — too little raises NOx, too much causes misfires and rough running.

t: EGR Temperature Sensor D Circuit High
s: Same as P04D1 on EGR temperature sensor D. EGR mixes inert exhaust into intake to lower combustion peak temperature — too little raises NOx, too much causes misfires and rough running.

t: EGR Temperature Sensor D Circuit Intermittent or Erratic
s: Same as P04D2 on EGR temperature sensor D. EGR mixes inert exhaust into intake to lower combustion peak temperature — too little raises NOx, too much causes misfires and rough running.

t: EVAP System Small Leak Detected Fuel Tank Side
s: The EVAP leak diagnostic has detected a small leak on the fuel tank side of the system - typically a hose, gasket, fuel cap, or fuel sender flange that does not seal completely. Vapor escapes during pressure or vacuum tests.

t: EVAP System Small Leak Detected Fresh Air Side
s: Same as P04EA but on the fresh-air side - the path between vent valve and atmosphere is not sealing completely during the active test phase.

t: EVAP System Large Leak Detected Fuel Tank Side
s: Same as P04EA but the leak is large enough that the system cannot build the test pressure or vacuum at all - typically a missing fuel cap, an unseated hose or a torn vapor line on the tank side.

t: EVAP System Large Leak Detected Fresh Air Side
s: Same as P04EB on the fresh-air side, but with a leak large enough to prevent test build-up. The evaporative system captures fuel vapor from the tank and purges it into intake; leaks or stuck purge valves leak hydrocarbons and cause emissions failures.

t: EVAP System Very Small Leak Detected Fuel Tank Side
s: Same as P04EA but at the most sensitive leak threshold - typically the OBD-II 0.020-inch (~0.5 mm) test. The system holds pressure for some time but loses it slower than the diagnostic limit allows; usually fuel cap O-ring fatigue or microcracks in a hose.

t: EVAP System Very Small Leak Detected Fresh Air Side
s: Same as P04EE on the fresh-air side - microcrack in a hose or imperfect vent valve seal at the most sensitive test threshold.

t: EVAP System High Load Purge Line A Performance
s: On engines that have a separate high-load purge line - to use boost pressure to pull vapor on turbo applications - the ECU monitors flow when this path is commanded. The flow is below the expected level - the venturi, line, or check valve is restricted, or the line is incorrectly routed.

t: EVAP System Purge Line Performance
s: A general performance fault on the EVAP purge line - flow does not match the requested rate, without specifying which segment. Usually a partially blocked line, a stuck purge valve, or a leak that decouples flow from valve commands.

t: Catalyst Heater Supply Voltage Bank 1
s: The electric catalyst heater on bank 1 - used on hybrids and modern petrol cars to bring the catalyst above light-off temperature within seconds of cold start - reports that its supply voltage is outside the expected window. The heater cannot deliver the rated power, so cold-start emissions rise until the catalyst reaches light-off conventionally.

t: Catalyst Heater Supply Voltage Bank 2
s: The electric catalyst heater on bank 2 - used on hybrids and modern petrol cars to bring the catalyst above light-off temperature within seconds of cold start - reports that its supply voltage is outside the expected window. The heater cannot deliver the rated power, so cold-start emissions rise until the catalyst reaches light-off conventionally.

t: Catalyst Heater Circuit Low Bank 1
s: The control or supply line to the electric catalyst heater on bank 1 is shorted to ground or measures below the expected voltage when the heater is commanded on.

t: Catalyst Heater Circuit Low Bank 2
s: The control or supply line to the electric catalyst heater on bank 2 is shorted to ground or measures below the expected voltage when the heater is commanded on.

t: Catalyst Heater Circuit High Bank 1
s: Same as P04F4 with the line shorted to supply or open - the ECU measures higher voltage than the commanded duty cycle allows.

t: Catalyst Heater Circuit High Bank 2
s: Same as P04F6 on bank 2. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Catalyst Heater Circuit Intermittent Bank 1
s: Same as P04F4 with intermittent or erratic signal - typically a connector or harness fault on the heater feed.

t: Catalyst Heater Circuit Intermittent Bank 2
s: Same as P04F8 on bank 2. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: EGR A Control Temperature Too High
s: The ECU monitors the operating temperature of the EGR A actuator (or its control electronics). The reading has exceeded the safe upper threshold and the control unit derates or disables EGR A to protect the actuator from thermal damage.

t: Crankcase Ventilation Hose Connection Sensor Range or Performance
s: The sensor that monitors PCV hose connection reports a value within plausible range yet inconsistent with the engine model - signal does not switch when the hose is moved during diagnosis, or shows offset against actual hose state.

t: Crankcase Ventilation System Performance
s: A general performance fault of the crankcase ventilation system - PCV pressure or flow does not match the engine model. Possible causes are a clogged PCV valve, blocked separator, oil-filler cap with a damaged seal, or excessive blow-by from worn rings.

t: EGR Actuator A Control Processor Performance
s: Modern smart EGR actuators contain their own microcontroller that handles position control internally and reports back over LIN or PWM. The ECU detects implausible behaviour from the actuator processor - missed messages, incorrect responses, or watchdog resets.

t: Catalyst Heater Circuit Performance Bank 1
s: The catalyst heater on bank 1 is electrically intact but does not deliver the expected thermal effect - measured catalyst light-off time is longer than calibrated. Heater element ageing, increased contact resistance, or a partial winding failure are typical causes.

t: Catalyst Heater Circuit Performance Bank 2
s: The catalyst heater on bank 2 is electrically intact but does not deliver the expected thermal effect - measured catalyst light-off time is longer than calibrated. Heater element ageing, increased contact resistance, or a partial winding failure are typical causes.

t: Vehicle Speed Sensor "A" Malfunction
s: The vehicle speed sensor (VSS) signal is missing or implausible. On automatic transmissions this often disables cruise control and shift adaptation; ABS-equipped cars typically derive speed from wheel speed sensors as a fallback.

t: Vehicle Speed Sensor "A" Range/Performance
s: The VSS signal is present but inconsistent — either values jump implausibly or the sensor reports a speed inconsistent with engine RPM in gear. Often a damaged reluctor ring or a sensor with degraded magnetic response.

t: Vehicle Speed Sensor A Circuit Low
s: The primary vehicle speed sensor (VSS A) reports a signal voltage below the plausible lower limit. With no usable speed signal the ECU loses cruise control, gear-shift logic and certain anti-stall functions.

t: Vehicle Speed Sensor A Circuit Intermittent Erratic or High
s: Same as P0502 with the signal noisy, erratic or above the plausible upper limit - typical for a chafed harness, missing reluctor tooth or a sensor with a weakened magnet.

t: Brake Switch "A"/"B" Correlation
s: Modern vehicles use two redundant brake switch signals (often one normally-open and one normally-closed). When the two signals disagree, this code is set; common causes include a misadjusted switch, internal switch failure, or wiring fault.

t: Idle Air Control System Malfunction
s: A general fault has been detected in the idle air control (IAC) system. On older vehicles with a separate IAC valve, this means the valve cannot maintain commanded idle RPM; on drive-by-wire systems, the equivalent fault is in the throttle body's idle adaptation.

t: Idle Air Control System RPM Lower Than Expected
s: The engine idles below the target RPM despite the IAC opening fully. Indicates restricted air path past the IAC — typically a heavily fouled throttle body or stuck IAC valve.

t: Idle Air Control System RPM Higher Than Expected
s: Idle RPM exceeds the target despite the IAC commanded fully closed. Almost always caused by a vacuum leak that lets air bypass the IAC — torn intake hose, leaking PCV, brake booster, or intake manifold gasket.

t: Idle Air Control System Circuit Low
s: The control line to the idle air control valve - which opens a small bypass around the throttle to set idle airflow - is shorted to ground or measures a voltage below the plausible level.

t: Idle Air Control System Circuit High
s: Same as P0508 with the line shorted to supply or open. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Cold Start Idle Control System Performance
s: During the cold-start phase the ECU runs an elevated idle speed and a richer mixture to bring the catalyst above light-off temperature quickly. The cold-start idle does not behave as commanded - speed sags, hunts, or fails to come up. Possible causes are intake leaks active when cold, an IAC valve sticking, or implausible coolant temperature.

t: Cold Start Ignition Timing Performance
s: During cold-start the ECU retards ignition timing to raise exhaust temperature for catalyst light-off. The strategy does not deliver the expected timing - feedback from knock sensor or combustion model shows ignition too far advanced or retarded for the demanded calibration.

t: Cold Start Engine Coolant Temperature Performance
s: During cold-start the ECU expects coolant temperature to rise within a calibrated time window. Either the engine warms up too slowly (stuck-open thermostat, weak combustion) or the temperature signal does not reflect the actual warm-up.

t: Cold Start Rough Idle
s: The ECU detects rough idle behaviour during the cold-start phase only. Once the engine has warmed up the issue disappears. Typically caused by injector deposits, low compression on one cylinder when cold, or sticky valve guides.

t: Cold Start Engine Exhaust Temperature Too Low
s: During the cold-start strategy the ECU monitors exhaust temperature to ensure catalyst light-off. The measured EGT remains below the target threshold within the allowed time - heat-up strategy is not effective. Causes include excessive heat loss, very rich mixture cooling the exhaust, or a faulty EGT sensor.

t: Brake Assist Vacuum Too Low
s: The ECU monitors the vacuum supplied to the brake booster (either via a dedicated sensor or via intake manifold pressure). The level has dropped below the calibrated threshold; brake-pedal feel will be hard, and an electric vacuum pump may be commanded to restore vacuum.

t: Closed Throttle Position Switch
s: The dedicated closed-throttle switch (or closed-throttle bit on the TPS) does not behave as expected - either it never activates, never deactivates, or shows incoherent state against TPS voltage and engine load. Used by older systems for fuel-cutoff and idle recognition.

t: Idle Air Control Circuit
s: A general circuit fault is detected on the idle air control valve - open or short on supply, signal or ground. The ECU loses control of idle bypass airflow.

t: Starter Request Circuit
s: The starter request input - the signal that tells the ECU the driver wants to crank the engine - shows a circuit fault. Without it the engine cannot be started under software-managed start strategies (stop-start, push-button, immobiliser-coordinated start).

t: Incorrect Immobilizer Key
s: The immobiliser system has detected a key that is not authorised for this vehicle. Engine start is blocked. Possible causes are an unprogrammed replacement key, a transponder fault, or a deliberate attempt to start with the wrong key.

t: Battery Temperature Sensor Circuit Range or Performance
s: The battery temperature sensor reports a value within plausible range yet inconsistent with engine bay or coolant temperature. Modern alternator regulation uses battery temperature to adjust charging voltage, so an offset here causes over- or undercharging.

t: Battery Temperature Sensor Circuit
s: A general circuit fault is detected on the battery temperature sensor - open or short on supply, signal or ground.

t: Battery Temperature Sensor Circuit Low
s: Same as P0515 with the signal voltage below the plausible range. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Battery Temperature Sensor Circuit High
s: Same as P0515 with the signal voltage above the plausible range. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Idle Air Control Circuit Intermittent
s: Same as P0511 with intermittent or erratic signal. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Idle Air Control System Performance
s: The idle air control system is electrically intact, but the actual idle airflow does not match the commanded value - typical for a sticky valve, intake leak, or carbon build-up around the throttle bore.

t: Crankcase Pressure Sensor A Circuit
s: A general circuit fault is detected on the crankcase pressure sensor A - typically used to monitor PCV operation and detect a clogged separator or disconnected hose.

t: Crankcase Pressure Sensor A Circuit Range or Performance
s: Same as P051A with the signal in plausible range yet not matching the engine model.

t: Crankcase Pressure Sensor A Circuit Low
s: Same as P051A with the signal voltage below the plausible range. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Crankcase Pressure Sensor A Circuit High
s: Same as P051A with the signal voltage above the plausible range. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Crankcase Pressure Sensor A Circuit Intermittent or Erratic
s: Same as P051A with intermittent or erratic signal. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: PCV Filter Restriction
s: The pressure or flow sensor in the PCV path detects increased restriction across the oil-mist filter element. Once saturated with oil mist, the filter no longer passes blow-by gas, raising crankcase pressure and pushing oil past seals.

t: Engine Oil Pressure Sensor or Switch A Circuit
s: A general circuit fault on the engine oil pressure sensor or switch A. Without a valid oil pressure signal the ECU cannot warn the driver about loss of oil pressure - a critical engine-protection function.

t: Engine Oil Pressure Sensor/Switch Range/Performance
s: The engine oil pressure sensor or switch reports values that are not plausible for current RPM, oil temperature, and engine load. Could indicate a sensor fault or genuinely abnormal pressure.

t: Engine Oil Pressure Sensor/Switch Low Voltage
s: The oil pressure sensor signal is below the expected minimum. Often points to an electrical fault — short to ground or failed sensor — but can also indicate genuinely low oil pressure.

t: Engine Oil Pressure Sensor or Switch A High
s: Same as P0520 with the signal voltage above the plausible range - usually a short to supply or sensor failure that pegs the output high.

t: Engine Oil Pressure Too Low
s: The ECU has determined that engine oil pressure is below the safe operating threshold. Caused by low oil level, worn oil pump, severe engine wear, or a stuck pressure relief valve. Continued operation can rapidly destroy the engine.

t: Cruise Control Servo Control Circuit Range or Performance
s: The control circuit to the cruise control servo (vacuum or electric actuator that holds throttle position) shows feedback in plausible range yet inconsistent with the commanded action - servo slow, sticking, or supply weak.

t: Fan Speed Sensor Circuit
s: A general circuit fault on the cooling fan tachometer feedback - the ECU loses fan speed information needed to verify cooling capacity and detect a stalled or runaway fan.

t: Fan Speed Sensor Circuit Range or Performance
s: Same as P0526 with the signal in plausible range yet not matching the commanded fan speed - typically sensor drift, weak magnet, or partial reluctor damage.

t: Fan Speed Sensor Circuit No Signal
s: Same as P0526 with no detectable signal at all - sensor not producing pulses despite the fan being commanded on.

t: Fan Speed Sensor Circuit Intermittent
s: Same as P0526 with intermittent or erratic signal. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Cold Start A Camshaft Position Timing Over-Advanced Bank 1
s: During cold-start cam phasing the ECU detects that the A camshaft on bank 1 is more advanced than the cold-start target by more than the diagnostic limit. Possible causes are an oil-pressure spike, a stuck phaser, or oil aeration that hydraulically locks the phaser advanced.

t: Cold Start A Camshaft Position Timing Over-Retarded Bank 1
s: Same as P052A with the phaser stuck retarded - cold-start strategy cannot pull the cam to the advanced position needed for fast catalyst light-off.

t: Cold Start A Camshaft Position Timing Over-Advanced Bank 2
s: During cold-start cam phasing the ECU detects that the A camshaft on bank 2 is more advanced than the cold-start target by more than the diagnostic limit. Possible causes are an oil-pressure spike, a stuck phaser, or oil aeration that hydraulically locks the phaser advanced.

t: Cold Start A Camshaft Position Timing Over-Retarded Bank 2
s: Same as P052B on bank 2. The camshaft position sensor identifies which cylinder is on the compression stroke — without it the ECM cannot run sequential injection or coil-on-plug ignition correctly.

t: PCV Regulator Valve Performance
s: The crankcase ventilation regulator valve - which throttles blow-by gas flow toward the intake based on engine load - is electrically intact but does not deliver the modelled pressure regulation. Either the valve diaphragm is fatigued or the spring is weak.

t: Glow Plug Control Module 1 System Voltage
s: The first glow plug control module reports its supply voltage outside the calibrated window - either too low to drive the plugs to full temperature or too high, risking damage. Affects diesel cold-start time and white-smoke emissions.

t: A/C Refrigerant Pressure Sensor "A" Circuit
s: General electrical fault on the A/C refrigerant pressure sensor circuit. The ECU uses this signal to enable the compressor and cooling fan; without it the A/C is typically disabled.

t: A/C Refrigerant Pressure Sensor Range/Performance
s: The A/C refrigerant pressure sensor signal is implausible against operating conditions. Reading does not change as expected when the compressor cycles or ambient conditions change.

t: A/C Refrigerant Pressure Sensor "A" Circuit Low
s: A/C pressure sensor signal voltage below the lower threshold — open, short to ground, sensor fault, or a refrigerant charge so low that the pressure is genuinely low.

t: A/C Refrigerant Pressure Sensor "A" Circuit High
s: A/C pressure sensor signal voltage above the upper threshold — short to supply, defective sensor, or a system overcharge/blocked condenser causing genuine high pressure.

t: A/C Refrigerant Charge Loss
s: The system detects an abnormally low refrigerant charge. Compressor operation is typically inhibited to prevent damage.

t: A/C Evaporator Temperature Sensor Circuit
s: The evaporator temperature sensor signal is missing or out of range. Used to prevent icing of the evaporator core.

t: A/C Evaporator Temperature Sensor Circuit Range or Performance
s: The evaporator temperature sensor used to prevent evaporator icing reports a value within plausible range yet not matching the engine model or cabin conditions.

t: A/C Evaporator Temperature Sensor Circuit Low
s: The evaporator temperature sensor signal voltage is below the lower diagnostic threshold. Indicates a short to ground or sensor fault.

t: A/C Evaporator Temperature Sensor Circuit High
s: The evaporator temperature sensor signal voltage is above the upper diagnostic threshold. Typically open circuit or short to power.

t: A/C Evaporator Temperature Sensor Circuit Intermittent
s: Same as P0536 with intermittent or erratic signal. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: PCV Heater A Control Circuit Open
s: The heater on PCV path A - used to prevent ice plugs in the crankcase ventilation hose during cold weather - has an open control circuit. Without it, condensation can freeze and block PCV flow, raising crankcase pressure dangerously.

t: PCV Heater A Control Circuit Low
s: Same as P053A with the control line shorted to ground. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: PCV Heater A Control Circuit High
s: Same as P053A with the control line shorted to supply. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: PCV Heater A Performance
s: The PCV heater on path A is electrically intact but does not deliver the expected thermal effect - hose temperature does not rise as commanded. Heater element aged or contact resistance increased.

t: Crankcase Pressure Too High
s: The crankcase pressure sensor reports a value above the safe upper threshold. Excessive crankcase pressure pushes oil past seals and contaminates the PCV path with mist - causes are clogged PCV system, frozen breather hose, or excessive blow-by from worn rings.

t: Cold Start Fuel Pressure Performance Bank 1
s: During cold-start the ECU runs an elevated rail pressure on direct-injection systems for better cold spray atomisation. The pressure target on bank 1 is not reached within the calibrated time window - typical for a high-pressure pump that loses output when cold or a pressure regulator with cold-stickiness.

t: Intake Air Heater A Circuit
s: The intake air heater control circuit reports a generic fault. Heater is used on diesel and some gasoline engines for cold-start emissions and starting reliability.

t: Intake Air Heater A Circuit Low
s: The intake air heater control circuit reports a generic fault. Heater is used on diesel and some gasoline engines for cold-start emissions and starting reliability.

t: Intake Air Heater A Circuit High
s: The intake air heater control circuit reports a generic fault. Heater is used on diesel and some gasoline engines for cold-start emissions and starting reliability.

t: Intake Air Heater A Circuit Open
s: The intake air heater (grid heater) on path A - used on cold-start of diesel engines to warm intake air for reliable combustion - has an open control circuit. Without it, cold-start emissions and idle quality on cold days suffer.

t: Exhaust Gas Temperature Sensor Circuit Bank 1 Sensor 1
s: A general circuit fault is detected on the EGT sensor at bank 1 sensor 1 (typically just downstream of the turbo or before the catalyst). The ECU loses thermal protection feedback for the catalyst and DPF.

t: Exhaust Gas Temperature Sensor Bank 1 Sensor 1 Low
s: The exhaust gas temperature sensor signal at bank 1, sensor 1 is below the lower diagnostic threshold. Indicates short to ground or sensor fault.

t: Exhaust Gas Temperature Sensor Bank 1 Sensor 1 High
s: The exhaust gas temperature sensor signal at bank 1, sensor 1 is above the upper diagnostic threshold. Typically open circuit or short to power.

t: Exhaust Gas Temperature Sensor Circuit Bank 2 Sensor 1
s: A general circuit fault is detected on the EGT sensor at bank 2 sensor 1 (typically just downstream of the turbo or before the catalyst). The ECU loses thermal protection feedback for the catalyst and DPF.

t: Exhaust Gas Temperature Sensor Circuit Low Bank 2 Sensor 1
s: A general circuit fault is detected on the EGT sensor at bank 2 sensor 1 (typically just downstream of the turbo or before the catalyst). The ECU loses thermal protection feedback for the catalyst and DPF.

t: Exhaust Gas Temperature Sensor Circuit High Bank 2 Sensor 1
s: A general circuit fault is detected on the EGT sensor at bank 2 sensor 1 (typically just downstream of the turbo or before the catalyst). The ECU loses thermal protection feedback for the catalyst and DPF.

t: Cold Start B Camshaft Position Timing Over-Advanced Bank 1
s: During cold-start cam phasing the ECU detects that the A camshaft on bank 1 is more advanced than the cold-start target by more than the diagnostic limit. Possible causes are an oil-pressure spike, a stuck phaser, or oil aeration that hydraulically locks the phaser advanced.

t: Cold Start B Camshaft Position Timing Over-Retarded Bank 1
s: Same as P052B on the B camshaft of bank 1. The camshaft position sensor identifies which cylinder is on the compression stroke — without it the ECM cannot run sequential injection or coil-on-plug ignition correctly.

t: Cold Start B Camshaft Position Timing Over-Advanced Bank 2
s: During cold-start cam phasing the ECU detects that the A camshaft on bank 2 is more advanced than the cold-start target by more than the diagnostic limit. Possible causes are an oil-pressure spike, a stuck phaser, or oil aeration that hydraulically locks the phaser advanced.

t: Cold Start B Camshaft Position Timing Over-Retarded Bank 2
s: Same as P052B on the B camshaft of bank 2. The camshaft position sensor identifies which cylinder is on the compression stroke — without it the ECM cannot run sequential injection or coil-on-plug ignition correctly.

t: Idle Control System Fuel Quantity Lower Than Expected
s: At idle the ECU expects a calibrated fuel quantity to maintain set-speed. The actual quantity is significantly less than modelled - typical for a tight engine, very low friction, dragging accessory load missing, or implausible idle airflow.

t: Idle Control System Fuel Quantity Higher Than Expected
s: Same as P054E with actual fuel quantity above the model - high friction, sticky throttle, or accessory drag.

t: Power Steering Pressure Sensor Circuit
s: General electrical fault on the power steering pressure sensor. The ECU uses this signal to compensate idle speed when the steering load increases; without it idle stability can suffer during parking maneuvers.

t: Power Steering Pressure Sensor Range/Performance
s: The PS pressure sensor signal stays in electrical range but is implausible for the operating state, e.g. high pressure reported with the wheels straight.

t: Power Steering Pressure Sensor Circuit Low Input
s: PS pressure sensor signal voltage below threshold — open, short to ground, or sensor failure. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Power Steering Pressure Sensor Circuit High Input
s: PS pressure sensor signal voltage above threshold — short to supply or sensor failure. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Power Steering Pressure Sensor Intermittent
s: The power steering pressure sensor signal is intermittent. The ECM cannot consistently raise idle to compensate for steering load.

t: Brake Booster Pressure Sensor Circuit
s: A general circuit fault is detected on the brake booster pressure sensor - the ECU cannot determine vacuum reserve in the booster, which limits the safe operation of stop-start, regenerative-braking blending, and electric vacuum pump control.

t: Brake Booster Pressure Sensor Circuit Range or Performance
s: Same as P0555 with the signal in plausible range yet not matching the engine model - drift, partial blockage of the reference port.

t: Brake Booster Pressure Sensor Circuit Low
s: Same as P0555 with the signal voltage below the plausible range. Boost control tracks commanded vs. actual manifold pressure; persistent deviation means wastegate, vanes, hose or actuator are not delivering the requested pressure.

t: Brake Booster Pressure Sensor Circuit High
s: Same as P0555 with the signal voltage above the plausible range. Boost control tracks commanded vs. actual manifold pressure; persistent deviation means wastegate, vanes, hose or actuator are not delivering the requested pressure.

t: Brake Booster Pressure Sensor Circuit Intermittent
s: Same as P0555 with intermittent or erratic signal. Boost control tracks commanded vs. actual manifold pressure; persistent deviation means wastegate, vanes, hose or actuator are not delivering the requested pressure.

t: Engine Oil Pressure Sensor or Switch B Circuit
s: A general circuit fault on the engine oil pressure sensor or switch A. Without a valid oil pressure signal the ECU cannot warn the driver about loss of oil pressure - a critical engine-protection function.

t: Engine Oil Pressure Sensor or Switch B Range or Performance
s: Same as P055A with the signal in plausible range yet not matching the engine model.

t: Engine Oil Pressure Sensor or Switch B Low
s: Same as P055A with the signal voltage below the plausible range.

t: Engine Oil Pressure Sensor or Switch B High
s: Same as P055A with the signal voltage above the plausible range.

t: Oxygen Sensor Control Module System Voltage
s: The dedicated O2 sensor control module - found on systems with a wide-band sensor needing its own pumping current driver - reports its supply voltage outside the calibrated window.

t: Engine Oil Pressure Out of Range
s: The measured oil pressure is far outside the expected range for current engine speed and oil temperature - either too low (oil pump weak, oil level low, bearing wear) or anomalously high (relief valve stuck closed, blocked oil cooler).

t: System Voltage Malfunction
s: A general fault in the system voltage seen by the ECU — value implausible, missing, or unstable but without crossing the specific thresholds of P0562 (low) or P0563 (high). Often appears intermittently with a marginal alternator or weak battery.

t: System Voltage Unstable
s: The ECU monitors battery voltage and detects unstable behaviour - rapid fluctuations beyond what current load changes explain. Typical causes are a failing alternator, loose battery terminals, weak battery, or a heavy load (radiator fan, glow plugs, heated screen) drawing voltage down briefly.

t: System Voltage Low
s: Battery / charging system voltage seen by the ECU has dropped below the lower threshold (typically around 10.5 V). Modern ECUs are sensitive to under-voltage and may set this code briefly during cranking on a weak battery.

t: System Voltage High
s: Battery / charging system voltage seen by the ECU exceeds the upper threshold (typically around 16 V). Caused by a failing voltage regulator inside the alternator. Sustained over-voltage can damage other electronics.

t: Cruise Control Multi-Function Input A Circuit
s: A general circuit fault is detected on the multi-function cruise control input A - the multiplexed input that carries multiple stalk button states (set, resume, cancel, accelerate, coast) on a single analogue line.

t: Cruise Control On Signal
s: The ECU detects an implausible state of the cruise control on/off signal - either the master switch is stuck on, intermittent, or shows a logic level that does not match other dash signals.

t: Cruise Control Cancel Input Circuit
s: The cancel input from the cruise control switch reports an electrical fault or implausible signal.

t: Cruise Control Resume Input Circuit
s: The cancel input from the cruise control switch reports an electrical fault or implausible signal.

t: Cruise Control Set Input Circuit
s: The cancel input from the cruise control switch reports an electrical fault or implausible signal.

t: Cruise Control Coast Signal
s: The coast (decelerate) signal of the cruise control reports an implausible state - typically a stuck or shorted contact.

t: Cruise Control Increase Distance Signal
s: The increase-distance command signal (used on adaptive cruise to lengthen the gap) is implausible - stuck, missing, or shorted.

t: Cruise Control Decrease Distance Signal
s: The increase-distance command signal (used on adaptive cruise to lengthen the gap) is implausible - stuck, missing, or shorted.

t: Cruise Control Cancel Signal
s: The cancel signal (driver-initiated cruise off) reports an implausible state - stuck, missing, or shorted.

t: Reductant Control Module System Voltage
s: The reductant (AdBlue) control module reports its supply voltage outside the calibrated window. Reductant dosing is suspended until voltage returns to range, which can drive NOx emissions out of compliance.

t: Cold Start Turbocharger Boost Control A Performance
s: During cold-start the ECU runs an elevated boost target on the A turbo to support catalyst heat-up and cold-running combustion stability. The actual boost does not reach the cold-start target - typically a wastegate or VGT actuator sticking when cold.

t: Cold Start Turbocharger Boost Control B Performance
s: During cold-start the ECU runs an elevated boost target on the A turbo to support catalyst heat-up and cold-running combustion stability. The actual boost does not reach the cold-start target - typically a wastegate or VGT actuator sticking when cold.

t: Cruise Control Accel Signal Malfunction
s: The cruise control accelerate input signal is implausible — switch contacts welded, wiring fault, or stalk defect. The ECU disables cruise control to prevent unintended acceleration.

t: Brake Switch "A" Circuit Malfunction
s: The brake light switch signal seen by the ECU is implausible — for example, both contacts active simultaneously, or no signal change despite the brake pedal being pressed (cruise control engages and disengages on this signal). On many cars also disables shift interlock.

t: Cruise Control/Brake Switch B Circuit Low
s: The brake switch B input used by the cruise control system reads below the lower diagnostic threshold.

t: Cruise Control/Brake Switch B Circuit High
s: The brake switch B input used by the cruise control system reads below the lower diagnostic threshold.

t: Cruise Control System Vehicle Speed Too High
s: The cruise control sees actual vehicle speed exceed the set-speed by more than the diagnostic margin (typically going downhill or with throttle override). Persistent oversize means the system cannot bring speed down - either an actuator fault, brake-blending failure, or driver kick-down stuck.

t: Cruise Control Input Circuit
s: A general circuit fault is detected on a cruise control input that is not specifically multiplexed - typically the dedicated set/resume button input.

t: Cruise Control Input Circuit Low
s: Same as P0575 with the input voltage below the plausible range. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Cruise Control Input Circuit High
s: Same as P0575 with the input voltage above the plausible range. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Cruise Control Multi-Function Input A Circuit Stuck
s: The multi-function cruise input A has stayed in one of its discrete levels longer than any plausible button hold - the input is stuck.

t: Cruise Control Multi-Function Input A Circuit Range or Performance
s: Same as P0564 with the analogue level inside the plausible range yet not matching any of the calibrated button levels - resistor ladder drifted, contact resistance increased, or wrong stalk module fitted.

t: Brake Pedal Position Sensor A Circuit Open
s: The brake pedal position sensor A has an open control circuit. The ECU loses analogue feedback about pedal travel - used by stop-start, regenerative braking and adaptive cruise to coordinate engine, motor and friction brakes.

t: Brake Pedal Position Sensor A Circuit Range or Performance
s: Same as P057A with the signal in plausible range yet not matching the brake-light switch state - typical for a sensor offset against pedal travel or a worn potentiometer track.

t: Brake Pedal Position Sensor A Circuit Low
s: Same as P057A with the signal voltage below the plausible range. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Brake Pedal Position Sensor A Circuit High
s: Same as P057A with the signal voltage above the plausible range. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Brake Pedal Position Sensor A Circuit Intermittent or Erratic
s: Same as P057A with intermittent or erratic signal. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Battery A State of Charge Performance
s: The battery monitor reports a State of Charge (SoC) value that does not match the rest-voltage and current pattern - typically a battery near end of life, sulfation, or a deep discharge that the SoC algorithm has not converged on.

t: Cruise Control Multi-Function Input A Circuit Low
s: Same as P0564 with the analogue input voltage below the plausible range. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Cruise Control Multi-Function Input A Circuit High
s: Same as P0564 with the analogue input voltage above the plausible range. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Cruise Control Vacuum Control Circuit Open
s: The vacuum-control solenoid of a vacuum-driven cruise servo (older systems) has an open control circuit - the servo cannot pull the throttle.

t: Cruise Control Vacuum Control Circuit Low
s: Same as P0582 with the control line shorted to ground. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Cruise Control Vacuum Control Circuit High
s: Same as P0582 with the control line shorted to supply. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Cruise Control Multi-Function Input A and B Correlation
s: Cruise control uses two redundant multiplexed inputs (A and B) for safety. The two inputs do not agree - one reports a button press the other does not, or both report incompatible levels at the same time.

t: Cruise Control Vent Control Circuit Open
s: The vent solenoid of a vacuum-driven cruise servo - which releases vacuum to drop throttle - has an open control circuit. The servo cannot release vacuum, so cruise speed cannot be reduced.

t: Cruise Control Vent Control Circuit Low
s: Same as P0586 with the control line shorted to ground. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Cruise Control Vent Control Circuit High
s: Same as P0586 with the control line shorted to supply. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Cruise Control Multi-Function Input B Circuit
s: A general circuit fault is detected on the multi-function cruise control input A - the multiplexed input that carries multiple stalk button states (set, resume, cancel, accelerate, coast) on a single analogue line.

t: Battery Monitor Module Performance
s: The battery monitor module is electrically intact but does not deliver plausible measurements - typical for a sensor offset, watchdog event in the module, or a wiring loop that introduces noise on the current measurement.

t: Battery Monitor Module Current Monitoring Performance
s: The battery monitor module is electrically intact but does not deliver plausible measurements - typical for a sensor offset, watchdog event in the module, or a wiring loop that introduces noise on the current measurement.

t: Battery Monitor Module Temperature Monitoring Performance
s: The battery monitor module is electrically intact but does not deliver plausible measurements - typical for a sensor offset, watchdog event in the module, or a wiring loop that introduces noise on the current measurement.

t: Battery Monitor Module Voltage Monitoring Performance
s: The battery monitor module is electrically intact but does not deliver plausible measurements - typical for a sensor offset, watchdog event in the module, or a wiring loop that introduces noise on the current measurement.

t: Battery Monitor Module Temperature Too High
s: The battery monitor module measures battery temperature above the safe upper threshold - charging is derated or stopped to prevent thermal runaway. Causes are heat soak from a hot engine bay, hard charging on a depleted battery, or a faulty sensor.

t: Battery Monitor Module Temperature Too Low
s: Same as P058E with the temperature below the safe lower threshold - charging current is reduced to prevent damage to a frozen battery.

t: Cruise Control Multi-Function Input B Circuit Stuck
s: The multi-function cruise input A has stayed in one of its discrete levels longer than any plausible button hold - the input is stuck.

t: Cruise Control Multi-Function Input B Circuit Range or Performance
s: Same as P0579 on input B.

t: Cruise Control Multi-Function Input B Circuit Low
s: Same as P0580 on input B. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Cruise Control Multi-Function Input B Circuit High
s: Same as P0581 on input B. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Cruise Control Servo Control Circuit Open
s: The servo motor that drives the cruise control throttle actuator has an open control circuit. The ECU cannot drive the servo - cruise control is unavailable.

t: Cruise Control Servo Control Circuit Low
s: Same as P0594 with the control line shorted to ground. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Cruise Control Servo Control Circuit High
s: Same as P0594 with the control line shorted to supply. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Thermostat Heater Control Circuit Open
s: The map-controlled thermostat - electrically heated to lower its opening temperature on demand for emissions or torque mapping - has an open control circuit. The ECU loses the ability to override coolant flow timing.

t: Thermostat Heater Control Circuit Low
s: Same as P0597 with the control line shorted to ground. The sensor heater warms the element to operating temperature (~600 °C); without it the lambda sensor stays inactive in closed loop and the ECM cannot trim mixture.

t: Thermostat Heater Control Circuit High
s: Same as P0597 with the control line shorted to supply. The sensor heater warms the element to operating temperature (~600 °C); without it the lambda sensor stays inactive in closed loop and the ECM cannot trim mixture.

t: Active Grille Air Shutter A Position Sensor Circuit
s: A general circuit fault is detected on the position sensor of active grille air shutter A. The shutter (electrically actuated grille flap) opens or closes the radiator inlet for aerodynamics and warm-up control; without position feedback the ECU cannot control its angle.

t: Active Grille Air Shutter A Position Sensor Range or Performance
s: Same as P059A with feedback in plausible range yet not matching the commanded angle.

t: Active Grille Air Shutter A Position Sensor Circuit High
s: Same as P059A with the signal voltage above the plausible range. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Active Grille Air Shutter A Position Sensor Circuit Low
s: Same as P059A with the signal voltage below the plausible range. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Active Grille Air Shutter A Position Sensor Circuit Intermittent
s: Same as P059A with intermittent signal. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Active Grille Air Shutter A Performance Stuck Off
s: The active grille shutter A does not move when commanded - typically jammed by debris, ice, broken linkage, or a seized actuator.

t: Active Grille Air Shutter A Stuck On
s: Same as P059F with the shutter A stuck in the open or active position - typically broken return spring or seized linkage.

t: Active Grille Air Shutter A Position Sensor Minimum or Maximum Stop Performance
s: During the calibration drive the position sensor of shutter A does not reach one of its mechanical end stops as expected - actuator slipping, linkage worn, or stop sensor offset.

t: Active Grille Air Shutter A Control Circuit Open
s: The control circuit driving the actuator of shutter A is open - the ECU cannot move the shutter. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Active Grille Air Shutter A Control Circuit Range or Performance
s: Same as P05A2 with electrical drive intact but the shutter response not matching the commanded movement - actuator slow or weak.

t: Active Grille Air Shutter A Control Circuit High
s: Same as P05A2 with the control line shorted to supply. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Active Grille Air Shutter A Control Circuit Low
