s: Same as P05A2 with the control line shorted to ground. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Active Grille Air Shutter A Supply Voltage Circuit Open
s: The supply line that powers the shutter A actuator is open - typically a blown fuse, a failed supply relay, or a broken supply wire.

t: Active Grille Air Shutter A Supply Voltage Circuit Low
s: Same as P05A6 with supply voltage below the plausible range. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Active Grille Air Shutter A Supply Voltage Circuit High
s: Same as P05A6 with supply voltage above the plausible range. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Active Grille Air Shutter B Position Sensor Circuit
s: A general circuit fault is detected on the position sensor of active grille air shutter A. The shutter (electrically actuated grille flap) opens or closes the radiator inlet for aerodynamics and warm-up control; without position feedback the ECU cannot control its angle.

t: Active Grille Air Shutter B Position Sensor Range or Performance
s: Same as P059B on shutter B.

t: Active Grille Air Shutter B Position Sensor Circuit High
s: Same as P059C on shutter B. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Active Grille Air Shutter B Position Sensor Circuit Low
s: Same as P059D on shutter B. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Active Grille Air Shutter B Position Sensor Circuit Intermittent
s: Same as P059E on shutter B. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Active Grille Air Shutter B Performance Stuck Off
s: The active grille shutter A does not move when commanded - typically jammed by debris, ice, broken linkage, or a seized actuator.

t: Active Grille Air Shutter B Stuck On
s: Same as P05A0 on shutter B.

t: Active Grille Air Shutter B Position Sensor Minimum or Maximum Stop Performance
s: During the calibration drive the position sensor of shutter A does not reach one of its mechanical end stops as expected - actuator slipping, linkage worn, or stop sensor offset.

t: Active Grille Air Shutter B Control Circuit Open
s: The control circuit driving the actuator of shutter A is open - the ECU cannot move the shutter. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Active Grille Air Shutter B Control Circuit Range or Performance
s: Same as P05A3 on shutter B.

t: Active Grille Air Shutter B Control Circuit High
s: Same as P05A4 on shutter B. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Active Grille Air Shutter B Control Circuit Low
s: Same as P05A5 on shutter B. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Active Grille Air Shutter B Supply Voltage Circuit Open
s: The supply line that powers the shutter A actuator is open - typically a blown fuse, a failed supply relay, or a broken supply wire.

t: Active Grille Air Shutter B Supply Voltage Circuit Low
s: Same as P05A7 on shutter B. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Active Grille Air Shutter B Supply Voltage Circuit High
s: Same as P05A8 on shutter B. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Restraint Deployment Signal Circuit Open
s: The signal line that the restraint control module uses to inform the engine ECU about a deployment event has an open circuit. The ECU cannot run its post-crash strategy (fuel cutoff, hazard lights, unlock) automatically on impact.

t: Restraint Deployment Signal Circuit Low
s: Same as P05B8 with the signal shorted to ground. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Restraint Deployment Signal Circuit High
s: Same as P05B8 with the signal shorted to supply. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Restraints Deployed
s: The restraint control module reports that one or more airbag or pretensioner stages have been deployed. The engine ECU latches this and runs the crash strategy - fuel cutoff, hazard lights, unlock central locking, disable cruise. The code can only be cleared after the restraint module is reset (typically requires module replacement).

t: Stop-Start Mode Switch Circuit Open
s: The driver-controlled switch that disables stop-start has an open control circuit - the ECU cannot read the driver preference and falls back to the default mode.

t: Stop-Start Mode Switch Circuit Range or Performance
s: Same as P05BC with the input in plausible range yet not matching the expected toggle behaviour - typically a worn switch contact.

t: Stop-Start Mode Switch Circuit Low
s: Same as P05BC with the signal shorted to ground. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Stop-Start Mode Switch Circuit High
s: Same as P05BC with the signal shorted to supply. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Active Grille Air Shutter Module A Over Temperature
s: The control electronics in the shutter A module report excessive operating temperature - typically heat soak from the engine bay or a stuck shutter that draws excessive motor current.

t: Active Grille Air Shutter Module B Over Temperature
s: The control electronics in the shutter A module report excessive operating temperature - typically heat soak from the engine bay or a stuck shutter that draws excessive motor current.

t: Engine Coolant Pressure Sensor Circuit
s: A general circuit fault is detected on the engine coolant pressure sensor - used to detect a pressurised coolant system that has lost pressure (cap, hose, head gasket leak) or is over-pressurised (combustion gases entering coolant).

t: Engine Coolant Pressure Sensor Circuit Range or Performance
s: Same as P05C2 with the signal in plausible range yet not matching the expected pressure profile.

t: Engine Coolant Pressure Sensor Circuit Low
s: Same as P05C2 with the signal voltage below the plausible range. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Engine Coolant Pressure Sensor Circuit High
s: Same as P05C2 with the signal voltage above the plausible range. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Engine Coolant Pressure Sensor Circuit Intermittent or Erratic
s: Same as P05C2 with intermittent or erratic signal. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Economy Mode Switch Circuit Open
s: The economy-mode (ECO) switch input has an open circuit. The ECU cannot read whether the driver requested fuel-saving mode (changes throttle map, transmission shift points, climate strategy).

t: Economy Mode Switch Circuit Range or Performance
s: Same as P05C7 with the input in plausible range yet not matching expected behaviour.

t: Economy Mode Switch Circuit Low
s: Same as P05C7 with the signal shorted to ground. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Economy Mode Switch Circuit High
s: Same as P05C7 with the signal shorted to supply. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Vehicle Hardware Incompatibility
s: The ECU detects that one or more connected modules report a hardware variant or part number that is not compatible with the current ECU calibration. Typically appears after replacing a part with the wrong revision or after a software update without matching hardware.

t: Cold Start A Camshaft Position Timing Performance Bank 1
s: General cold-start performance fault on the A camshaft phaser of bank 1 - the phaser is not delivering the calibrated cold-start position within the allowed time.

t: Cold Start A Camshaft Position Timing Performance Bank 2
s: General cold-start performance fault on the A camshaft phaser of bank 2 - the phaser is not delivering the calibrated cold-start position within the allowed time.

t: Cold Start B Camshaft Position Timing Performance Bank 1
s: General cold-start performance fault on the A camshaft phaser of bank 1 - the phaser is not delivering the calibrated cold-start position within the allowed time.

t: Cold Start B Camshaft Position Timing Performance Bank 2
s: General cold-start performance fault on the A camshaft phaser of bank 2 - the phaser is not delivering the calibrated cold-start position within the allowed time.

t: Driver Mode Select Switch A Circuit
s: A general circuit fault on the driver mode select switch A - the switch with which the driver picks Sport, Comfort, Sand, Snow, etc. The ECU cannot read which mode the driver wants.

t: Driver Mode Select Switch A Circuit Low
s: Same as P05D0 with the signal shorted to ground. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Driver Mode Select Switch A Circuit High
s: Same as P05D0 with the signal shorted to supply. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Driver Mode Select Switch A Range or Performance
s: Same as P05D0 with the input in plausible range yet not matching the expected mode pattern - typically a worn switch or analog encoder drift.

t: Driver Mode Select Switch B Circuit
s: A general circuit fault on the driver mode select switch A - the switch with which the driver picks Sport, Comfort, Sand, Snow, etc. The ECU cannot read which mode the driver wants.

t: Driver Mode Select Switch B Circuit Low
s: Same as P05D1 on switch B. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Driver Mode Select Switch B Circuit High
s: Same as P05D2 on switch B. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Driver Mode Select Switch B Range or Performance
s: Same as P05D3 on switch B.

t: PCV Regulator Valve Control Circuit Open
s: The control circuit of the electrically actuated PCV regulator valve has an open circuit. The ECU cannot adjust crankcase ventilation flow against engine load - typically affects emissions and oil consumption under high load.

t: PCV Regulator Valve Control Circuit Low
s: Same as P05D8 with the control line shorted to ground. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: PCV Regulator Valve Control Circuit High
s: Same as P05D8 with the control line shorted to supply. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Brake Pedal Position Sensor B Circuit Open
s: The brake pedal position sensor B has an open control circuit. The ECU loses analogue feedback about pedal travel - used by stop-start, regenerative braking and adaptive cruise to coordinate engine, motor and friction brakes.

t: Brake Pedal Position Sensor B Circuit Range or Performance
s: Same as P057B on sensor B.

t: Brake Pedal Position Sensor B Circuit Low
s: Same as P057C on sensor B. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Brake Pedal Position Sensor B Circuit High
s: Same as P057D on sensor B. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Brake Pedal Position Sensor B Circuit Intermittent or Erratic
s: Same as P057E on sensor B. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Brake Pedal Position Sensor A and B Correlation
s: Brake-by-wire and electronic brake systems use two redundant pedal position sensors. Their readings disagree by more than the diagnostic tolerance - one or both has drifted, or wiring picks up noise on one channel.

t: A/C Refrigerant Pressure Sensor A Circuit Intermittent or Erratic
s: The high-side refrigerant pressure sensor A reports intermittent or erratic signal - typically a connector or harness fault on the sensor that lives close to the compressor.

t: A/C Refrigerant Pressure Sensor B Circuit Intermittent or Erratic
s: The high-side refrigerant pressure sensor B reports intermittent or erratic signal - typically a connector or harness fault on the sensor that lives close to the compressor.

t: Park Brake Sensor or Switch Circuit
s: The park brake apply switch or position sensor reports a circuit fault. The ECU loses the indication whether the parking brake is engaged - relevant for stop-start, hill-hold, and certain shift-interlock strategies.

t: Park Brake Sensor or Switch Circuit Low
s: Same as P05E3 with the signal shorted to ground. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Park Brake Sensor or Switch Circuit High
s: Same as P05E3 with the signal shorted to supply. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Park Brake Sensor or Switch Circuit Range or Performance
s: Same as P05E3 with input in plausible range yet not matching expected behaviour.

t: Park Brake Sensor or Switch Circuit Intermittent or Erratic
s: Same as P05E3 with intermittent or erratic signal. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Engine Oil Bypass Valve Control Circuit Open
s: The oil bypass valve - which routes oil around the cooler to speed up warm-up - has an open control circuit. Without it, oil temperature rise during cold-start is slower than calibrated.

t: Engine Oil Bypass Valve Control Circuit Low
s: Same as P05E8 with the control line shorted to ground. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Engine Oil Bypass Valve Control Circuit High
s: Same as P05E8 with the control line shorted to supply. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Cold Start SCR NOx Catalyst Inlet Temperature Too Low
s: Same as P22FF but specifically during the cold-start window - the SCR catalyst inlet temperature does not reach the threshold needed for NOx reduction in the cold-start phase, so reductant injection cannot be enabled.

t: Cold Start Injection Timing Performance
s: During cold-start the ECU adjusts injection timing (post-injection on diesels, intake-stroke timing on direct-injection petrols) to support catalyst light-off. The strategy does not deliver the expected timing - injector response slow or sensor offset.

t: Reductant Heater Control Module Supply Voltage
s: The reductant (AdBlue) heater control module - which warms tank, lines and injector to keep AdBlue from freezing - reports its supply voltage outside the calibrated window.

t: Reductant Pump Supply Voltage Circuit Open
s: The supply line that powers the AdBlue dosing pump is open. Without supply the pump cannot deliver reductant pressure - dosing is suspended and NOx emissions can drift out of compliance.

t: Reductant Pump Supply Voltage Circuit Low
s: Same as P05EE with the supply voltage below the plausible range. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Reductant Pump Supply Voltage Circuit High
s: Same as P05EE with the supply voltage above the plausible range. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Reductant Pump Supply Voltage Circuit Stuck On
s: The supply relay or driver for the reductant pump is welded or stuck active - the pump runs continuously, even with the engine off, draining the battery and over-pressurising the dosing circuit.

t: Reductant Pump Supply Voltage Circuit Stuck Off
s: Same as P05EE with the supply stuck off - relay does not energise on command.

t: Exhaust Aftertreatment Power Relay Control Circuit Open
s: The power relay that supplies the entire exhaust aftertreatment system (DOC, DPF, SCR sensors and heaters) reports an open control circuit. Aftertreatment effectively goes offline.

t: Exhaust Aftertreatment Power Relay Control Circuit Low
s: Same as P05F3 with the control line shorted to ground. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Exhaust Aftertreatment Power Relay Control Circuit High
s: Same as P05F3 with the control line shorted to supply. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Exhaust Aftertreatment Power Relay Control Circuit Stuck On
s: The aftertreatment power relay is stuck on - aftertreatment electronics stay powered after key-off, draining the battery.

t: Exhaust Aftertreatment Power Relay Control Circuit Stuck Off
s: Same as P05F3 with the relay not engaging on command.

t: Reductant Heater Control Module Performance
s: The reductant heater module is electrically intact but does not deliver the expected thermal effect - heater elements aged, supply line resistance, or thermostat sensor offset.

t: Stop-Start Status Signal Circuit
s: A general circuit fault on the stop-start status signal - the bus or hardwire that tells other modules whether the engine is auto-stopped or running.

t: Stop-Start Status Signal Circuit Low
s: Same as P05F9 with the signal shorted to ground. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Stop-Start Status Signal Circuit High
s: Same as P05F9 with the signal shorted to supply. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Cold Start Fuel Pressure Performance Bank 2
s: During cold-start the ECU runs an elevated rail pressure on direct-injection systems for better cold spray atomisation. The pressure target on bank 2 is not reached within the calibrated time window - typical for a high-pressure pump that loses output when cold or a pressure regulator with cold-stickiness.

t: Turbocharger Boost Control A Temperature Too High
s: The boost control actuator A reports excessive operating temperature - typically heat soak from the turbo housing, prolonged high-load operation, or insufficient cooling. Boost is derated to protect the actuator electronics.

t: Active Grille Air Shutter C Performance Stuck Off
s: The active grille shutter A does not move when commanded - typically jammed by debris, ice, broken linkage, or a seized actuator.

t: Brake Pressure Sensor and Brake Pedal Position Sensor Correlation
s: The brake pressure sensor and pedal position sensor disagree by more than the diagnostic tolerance - one indicates pedal action without the other showing pressure, or vice versa.

t: Serial Communication Link
s: A general fault on a serial communication link - typically used by older single-wire diagnostic protocols (KWP2000, ISO 9141) before CAN became universal. ECU has lost or corrupted communication with another module.

t: Internal Control Module Memory Check Sum Error
s: The ECU's internal memory checksum does not match the expected value at startup — the firmware/calibration in flash has become corrupt. Often appears after a failed flash, low battery during programming, or genuine module hardware failure.

t: Control Module Programming Error
s: The ECU is reporting that it is missing required calibration data — typically appears after a virgin module is installed but never coded/programmed for the vehicle, or after a flash that did not write all blocks.

t: Internal Control Module Keep Alive Memory (KAM) Error
s: The ECU detected a fault in its KAM (battery-backed memory used to store adaptations and DTCs). Often appears after battery disconnect or low battery; persistent codes indicate an internal ECU fault.

t: Internal Control Module Random Access Memory (RAM) Error
s: ECU self-test detected a RAM fault during runtime checks. This is an internal ECU defect; replacement or reflash with manufacturer support is generally required.

t: Internal Control Module Read Only Memory (ROM) Error
s: ECU self-test detected a ROM/checksum failure on the program memory. The control software cannot be trusted; vehicle typically defaults to limp-home with reduced function.

t: ECM/PCM Processor Fault
s: The ECU's internal processor self-test has failed. This is a hard module fault — the ECU usually triggers limp-home mode and may not respond to scan tools fully until replaced or repaired.

t: Control Module Performance
s: The ECM performs an internal self-test and detects a processor or memory performance fault that does not fit the more specific P0601 to P0606 codes.

t: Control Module VSS Output A
s: The vehicle speed signal output from the control module to other systems is faulty. Other modules expect a speed signal that the ECM no longer provides correctly.

t: Control Module VSS Output B
s: A fault is detected on the secondary vehicle speed signal output (VSS B) that the ECU sends to other modules (instrument cluster, ABS, transmission). Output level inconsistent with the internal speed value.

t: Internal Control Module Monitoring Processor Performance
s: Modern ECUs have a separate watchdog or monitoring processor that supervises the main processor. This processor reports a self-diagnosis fault - typically a watchdog event, ROM/RAM mismatch, or check-sum error in its monitor section. The ECU runs in a safety-fallback mode until repair.

t: Internal Control Module A and D Processing Performance
s: The ECU's analog-to-digital converter does not deliver consistent results - typically reference voltage drift, multiplexer fault, or noise on the analog ground.

t: Internal Control Module Main Processor Performance
s: The ECU main processor self-diagnosis flagged a performance fault - watchdog, ROM check, or instruction-set self-test failed. Engine runs on safety strategy.

t: Internal Control Module Accelerator Pedal Position Performance
s: The ECU has detected an internal performance fault in the processing of accelerator pedal position - the redundant signals are tracked internally and one of them shows a software-side anomaly.

t: Internal Control Module Throttle Position Performance
s: The ECU has detected an internal performance fault in the processing of accelerator pedal position - the redundant signals are tracked internally and one of them shows a software-side anomaly.

t: Internal Control Module Coolant Temperature Performance
s: The ECU has detected an internal performance fault in the processing of accelerator pedal position - the redundant signals are tracked internally and one of them shows a software-side anomaly.

t: Control Module Vehicle Options Error
s: The ECU's stored vehicle option configuration is invalid or does not match the connected hardware. Common after replacing or swapping a control module without proper coding/configuration.

t: Fuel Injector Control Module Performance
s: Modern direct-injection systems use a dedicated injector driver module that generates the high-voltage charge pulses needed to open piezo or solenoid injectors. The module reports an internal performance fault.

t: Fuel Injector Control Module Relay Control
s: A circuit fault on the relay that powers the injector driver module - either control line or contact integrity issue.

t: TCM Processor
s: The transmission control module performs an internal self-test and detects a processor performance fault.

t: ECM and TCM Incompatible
s: The ECM and TCM exchange identification or calibration data over the powertrain bus and detect that they are not a valid pair - typically appears after replacing one of the modules with a unit from a different vehicle variant.

t: Starter Relay Circuit
s: General electrical fault on the starter relay control circuit driven by the ECU/PCM. Typical symptom is a no-crank or intermittent crank condition.

t: Starter Relay A Circuit Low
s: The control line for starter relay A is shorted to ground - the ECU drives the relay and measures voltage too low.

t: Starter Relay Circuit High
s: The starter relay control circuit voltage is above the upper threshold — short to supply or stuck driver.

t: Alternative Fuel Control Module KAM Error
s: The control module for alternative fuel (CNG, LPG, hydrogen) reports a Keep-Alive-Memory error - persistent calibration values are corrupt or unreadable. Adaptive learning is reset.

t: Alternative Fuel Control Module RAM and ROM Error
s: The alternative fuel control module reports an internal RAM or ROM check failure - module no longer trustworthy.

t: Internal Control Module Torque Performance
s: The ECU has detected an internal performance fault in the processing of accelerator pedal position - the redundant signals are tracked internally and one of them shows a software-side anomaly.

t: Internal Control Module Torque Calculation Performance
s: The ECU has detected an internal performance fault in the processing of accelerator pedal position - the redundant signals are tracked internally and one of them shows a software-side anomaly.

t: Internal Control Module Engine RPM Performance
s: The ECU has detected an internal performance fault in the processing of accelerator pedal position - the redundant signals are tracked internally and one of them shows a software-side anomaly.

t: Internal Control Module Engine Air Mass Performance
s: The ECU has detected an internal performance fault in the processing of accelerator pedal position - the redundant signals are tracked internally and one of them shows a software-side anomaly.

t: Internal Control Module Brake Signal Performance
s: The ECU has detected an internal performance fault in the processing of accelerator pedal position - the redundant signals are tracked internally and one of them shows a software-side anomaly.

t: Internal Control Module Throttle Actuator Controller Performance
s: The ECU has detected an internal performance fault in the processing of accelerator pedal position - the redundant signals are tracked internally and one of them shows a software-side anomaly.

t: Generator Control Circuit
s: General electrical fault detected in the alternator/generator control circuit. The ECU regulates field excitation on most modern alternators; a fault here causes charging issues and may set additional battery voltage codes.

t: Generator Lamp L Control Circuit
s: The control circuit for the generator warning lamp output (L terminal) reports a fault.

t: Generator Field F Control Circuit
s: The control circuit for the generator field terminal (F) reports a fault. The ECM cannot regulate field current as expected.

t: Generator Lamp Control Circuit
s: Generic generator warning lamp control circuit fault, often used where the lamp terminal designation is not L or where multiple lamps share a single output.

t: Fuel Cap Lamp Control Circuit
s: The control circuit for the fuel cap warning lamp reports a fault. The ECM cannot drive the indicator that warns of an open or loose fuel cap.

t: Generator Field Terminal Circuit Low
s: Voltage on the alternator F terminal (field excitation) is below threshold — open or short to ground in the excitation path.

t: Generator Field/F Terminal Circuit Performance
s: The generator field circuit response does not match command. Field current ramps slowly, drifts or fails to reach commanded values during regulation.

t: Fuel Pump "A" Control Circuit Open
s: The ECU detected an open circuit in the fuel pump A control output. With no pump operation the engine cannot run; this code is often paired with no-start and no fuel pressure.

t: Fuel Pump A Control Circuit Low
s: The control circuit for fuel pump A reports voltage below the lower diagnostic threshold. Indicates short to ground or open winding.

t: Fuel Pump A Control Circuit High
s: The control circuit for fuel pump A reports voltage below the lower diagnostic threshold. Indicates short to ground or open winding.

t: Fuel Pump A Control Circuit Range or Performance
s: The control output to the primary fuel pump A is electrically intact yet response and fuel pressure response do not match - typically a worn pump, a clogged strainer, or a partially shorted PWM driver.

t: Internal Control Module Fuel Injector Control Performance
s: The ECU has detected an internal performance fault in the processing of accelerator pedal position - the redundant signals are tracked internally and one of them shows a software-side anomaly.

t: Internal Control Module Vehicle Speed Performance
s: The ECU has detected an internal performance fault in the processing of accelerator pedal position - the redundant signals are tracked internally and one of them shows a software-side anomaly.

t: Fuel Injector Driver Circuit Performance Bank 1
s: The injector driver stage on bank 1 - the high-side or low-side switching transistor that fires the injectors - reports a performance fault. Typical for a partial driver short, drift in the boost capacitor, or supply ripple under load.

t: Fuel Injector Driver Circuit Performance Bank 2
s: The injector driver stage on bank 2 - the high-side or low-side switching transistor that fires the injectors - reports a performance fault. Typical for a partial driver short, drift in the boost capacitor, or supply ripple under load.

t: Internal Control Module EEPROM Error
s: The ECU's EEPROM (persistent calibration storage) reports a check-sum or write error. Calibration values may be corrupted - the ECU runs on safe defaults until repair.

t: VIN Not Programmed or Incompatible ECM or PCM
s: The Vehicle Identification Number stored in the ECM or PCM is empty or does not match the VIN reported by other modules - typically appears after replacing a control module without programming the VIN.

t: VIN Not Programmed or Incompatible TCM
s: The Vehicle Identification Number stored in the ECM or PCM is empty or does not match the VIN reported by other modules - typically appears after replacing a control module without programming the VIN.

t: Odometer Not Programmed ECM or PCM
s: The odometer value stored in the ECM/PCM is missing or implausible - typical after a module replacement without odometer programming, or after persistent storage corruption.

t: Immobiliser Key Not Programmed
s: The immobiliser system rejects the current key because it is not programmed to this vehicle. Engine start is inhibited.

t: Control Module Internal Temperature A Too High
s: The ECU measures its own internal temperature and reports the value above the safe upper threshold - typical for a heat-soaked engine bay, blocked airflow around the ECU, or a failing internal temperature sensor.

t: Power Steering Control Circuit
s: A general circuit fault is detected on the power steering control output - typically the line that the ECU uses to request raised idle when steering load is high, or to enable an electric pump.

t: Power Steering Control Circuit Low
s: Same as P0635 with the control line shorted to ground. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Power Steering Control Circuit High
s: Same as P0635 with the control line shorted to supply. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Throttle Actuator Control Range or Performance Bank 1
s: The drive-by-wire throttle actuator on bank 1 does not respond as commanded - actuator slow, gearing slipping, or position feedback offset against actual blade angle.

t: Throttle Actuator Control Range or Performance Bank 2
s: The drive-by-wire throttle actuator on bank 2 does not respond as commanded - actuator slow, gearing slipping, or position feedback offset against actual blade angle.

t: Generator Voltage Sense Circuit A
s: A general circuit fault is detected on the alternator voltage sense line A - the line over which the regulator measures battery-side voltage to set charging output.

t: Generator Voltage Sense Circuit A Range or Performance
s: Same as P063A with the sense voltage in plausible range yet not matching the alternator output - high contact resistance or wrong calibration.

t: Generator Voltage Sense Circuit A Low
s: Same as P063A with the sense voltage below the plausible range.

t: Generator Voltage Sense Circuit A High
s: Same as P063A with the sense voltage above the plausible range.

t: Auto Configuration Throttle Input Not Present Bank 1
s: At ECU power-up the auto-configuration routine cannot detect the bank 1 throttle input - typically a wiring fault, missing module, or a calibration that expects a sensor not fitted.

t: Auto Configuration Engine Coolant Temperature Input Not Present
s: At ECU power-up the auto-configuration routine cannot detect the bank 1 throttle input - typically a wiring fault, missing module, or a calibration that expects a sensor not fitted.

t: Intake Air Heater Control Circuit
s: A general circuit fault on the intake air heater control - the ECU cannot drive the grid heater at cold-start.

t: Sensor Reference Voltage "A" Circuit Open
s: The 5 V reference voltage that the ECU supplies to multiple sensors (TPS, MAP, accelerator pedal, etc.) has dropped out or is open-circuit. Symptoms cascade — many sensor codes appear at once, all related to the same reference rail.

t: Sensor Reference Voltage "A" Circuit Low
s: The 5 V reference voltage on the A circuit (used by multiple sensors) is below threshold — short to ground or excessive current draw by a single sensor pulling the rail down.

t: Sensor Reference Voltage "A" Circuit High
s: 5 V reference rail A above threshold — short to a higher voltage source. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Driver Display Serial Communication Circuit
s: The serial communication line to the driver display (instrument cluster, MIL/check-engine warning) shows a circuit fault. Driver may not see warning lights as expected.

t: A and C Clutch Relay Control Circuit
s: A general circuit fault on the A/C clutch relay control - the line over which the ECU engages the magnetic clutch to drive the A/C compressor.

t: A and C Clutch Relay Control Circuit Low
s: Same as P0645 with the control line shorted to ground. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: A and C Clutch Relay Control Circuit High
s: Same as P0645 with the control line shorted to supply. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Immobilizer Lamp Control Circuit
s: A circuit fault on the control output to the immobiliser indicator lamp - the lamp cannot be turned on or off as expected.

t: Speed Control Lamp Control Circuit
s: A circuit fault on the control output to the immobiliser indicator lamp - the lamp cannot be turned on or off as expected.

t: Fuel Pump Control Module A
s: A general fault is detected on the dedicated fuel pump control module A. Used on PWM-controlled returnless fuel systems to vary pump speed against demand. The module reports an internal performance issue.

t: PTO Control Module
s: A general fault on the power take-off (PTO) control module - reports internal performance issue or communication trouble. Affects vehicles with auxiliary mechanical drives (cranes, snow ploughs, hydraulic pumps).

t: Glow Plug Control Module 1
s: A general fault is detected on the first glow plug control module - typically a self-diagnosis flag for an internal driver, supply, or watchdog issue.

t: Internal Control Module O2 Sensor Processor Performance Bank 1
s: The ECU has detected an internal performance fault in the processing of accelerator pedal position - the redundant signals are tracked internally and one of them shows a software-side anomaly.

t: Internal Control Module O2 Sensor Processor Performance Bank 2
s: The ECU has detected an internal performance fault in the processing of accelerator pedal position - the redundant signals are tracked internally and one of them shows a software-side anomaly.

t: Unauthorized Software or Calibration Detected
s: The ECU's secure boot or signature check has detected calibration data that is not signed by the manufacturer - typically appears after chip-tuning or unauthorised flash programming. The ECU may run on safe defaults or refuse to start.

t: Malfunction Indicator Lamp Control Circuit Malfunction
s: A fault in the wiring or control circuit between the ECU and the malfunction indicator lamp (MIL). The MIL may stay on permanently, fail to light at key-on, or flicker — the OBD-II self-check on the bulb itself failed.

t: Sensor Reference Voltage B Circuit Open
s: Modern ECUs supply 5 V reference rails to multiple sensors. Reference rail B is open - the ECU loses supply on every sensor connected to it (typically TPS B, MAP, or pressure sensors).

t: Sensor Reference Voltage B Circuit Low
s: Same as P0651 with the reference rail shorted to ground. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Sensor Reference Voltage B Circuit High
s: Same as P0651 with the reference rail shorted to supply or above the design voltage. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Engine RPM Output Circuit Open
s: The engine RPM signal output that the ECU sends to other modules (tachometer, transmission, ABS) has an open circuit.

t: Engine Hot Lamp Output Control Circuit
s: A circuit fault on the engine-hot warning lamp output - the high-coolant-temperature warning may not light when needed.

t: Fuel Level Output Circuit
s: A circuit fault on the fuel level output that the ECU sends to other modules (instrument cluster, range estimator).

t: Actuator Supply Voltage A Circuit Open
s: The supply rail that powers a group of actuators (typically injectors, EGR, throttle motor) is open. Multiple downstream actuators stop working.

t: Actuator Supply Voltage A Circuit Low
s: Same as P0657 with the supply voltage below the plausible range. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Actuator Supply Voltage A Circuit High
s: Same as P0657 with the supply voltage above the plausible range. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Generator System Performance
s: A general performance fault in the alternator system - charging output too low or too high under normal load conditions, often caused by a worn brush, a failing regulator, or harness resistance.

t: Generator A Control Circuit Range or Performance
s: The PWM control line to the alternator A regulator does not deliver the expected charging response - high contact resistance, regulator drift, or wrong calibration.

t: Generator A Mechanical Performance
s: The alternator A reports a mechanical performance issue - typically a slipping belt, worn pulley clutch, or seizing rotor that causes the output to lag behind speed.

t: Reductant System Malfunction Lamp Control Circuit
s: A circuit fault on the AdBlue malfunction warning lamp output - regulatory in many regions to alert the driver of NOx aftertreatment problems.

t: Intake Manifold Tuning Valve Performance Bank 1
s: The intake manifold tuning valve (IMT) on bank 1 - which switches the intake runners between long and short paths for torque optimisation - is electrically intact yet does not move as commanded. Carbon build-up, broken linkage, or seized actuator.

t: Intake Manifold Tuning Valve Performance Bank 2
s: The intake manifold tuning valve (IMT) on bank 2 - which switches the intake runners between long and short paths for torque optimisation - is electrically intact yet does not move as commanded. Carbon build-up, broken linkage, or seized actuator.

t: Intake Manifold Tuning Valve Control Circuit Open Bank 1
s: The control line of the IMT valve on bank 1 is open - the ECU cannot drive the valve. The intake manifold runner control (IMRC) flaps tune intake geometry for filling efficiency across the rpm range; stuck flaps cost part-load torque and can set lean codes.

t: Intake Manifold Tuning Valve Control Circuit Low Bank 1
s: Same as P0660 with the control line shorted to ground. The intake manifold runner control (IMRC) flaps tune intake geometry for filling efficiency across the rpm range; stuck flaps cost part-load torque and can set lean codes.

t: Intake Manifold Tuning Valve Control Circuit High Bank 1
s: Same as P0660 with the control line shorted to supply. The intake manifold runner control (IMRC) flaps tune intake geometry for filling efficiency across the rpm range; stuck flaps cost part-load torque and can set lean codes.

t: Intake Manifold Tuning Valve Control Circuit Open Bank 2
s: The control line of the IMT valve on bank 2 is open - the ECU cannot drive the valve. The intake manifold runner control (IMRC) flaps tune intake geometry for filling efficiency across the rpm range; stuck flaps cost part-load torque and can set lean codes.

t: Intake Manifold Tuning Valve Control Circuit Low Bank 2
s: Same as P0661 on bank 2. The intake manifold runner control (IMRC) flaps tune intake geometry for filling efficiency across the rpm range; stuck flaps cost part-load torque and can set lean codes.

t: Intake Manifold Tuning Valve Control Circuit High Bank 2
s: Same as P0662 on bank 2. The intake manifold runner control (IMRC) flaps tune intake geometry for filling efficiency across the rpm range; stuck flaps cost part-load torque and can set lean codes.

t: Control Module Internal Temperature Sensor A Circuit

t: Control Module Internal Temperature Sensor A Range or Performance
s: Same as P0666 with the signal in plausible range yet not matching the engine bay environment.

t: Control Module Internal Temperature Sensor A Circuit Low
s: Same as P0666 with the signal voltage below the plausible range. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Control Module Internal Temperature Sensor A Circuit High
s: Same as P0666 with the signal voltage above the plausible range. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: ECM or PCM Power Relay Control Circuit Open
s: The control circuit of the main ECM/PCM power relay is open - the ECU can lose power on shutdown sequence or fail to wake up at next start.

t: ECM/PCM Power Relay Control Circuit Open
s: The control circuit for the ECM main power relay is open. Without this relay, the controller cannot maintain its keep-alive supply.

t: ECM/PCM Power Relay Sense Circuit Open
s: The ECM cannot detect that the main power relay has switched on. The sense input remains low when commanded high.

t: ECM/PCM Power Relay Sense Circuit High
s: The ECM cannot detect that the main power relay has switched on. The sense input remains low when commanded high.

t: ECM or PCM Power Relay Sense Circuit Low
s: The voltage sense line that confirms the main ECU power relay has actually closed reads low - either the relay is not closing or the sense line is shorted to ground.

t: ECM or PCM Power Relay De-Energized Performance Too Early
s: The main power relay drops out earlier than the calibrated post-run window allows. The ECU loses power before completing housekeeping (e.g. saving adaptions, parking the throttle), which causes adaptive learning to reset.

t: ECM or PCM Power Relay De-Energized Performance Too Late
s: Same as P068A with the relay dropping out later than the calibrated window allows - keeps the ECU powered too long after key-off, drains the battery.

t: Cooling Fan 1 Control Circuit Low

t: Fan 3 Control Circuit Low

t: Fan 3 Control Circuit High
s: Same as P0695 with the line shorted to supply or open. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

s: Modern ECUs supply 5 V reference rails to multiple sensors. Reference rail B is open - the ECU loses supply on every sensor connected to it (typically TPS B, MAP, or pressure sensors).

t: Sensor Reference Voltage C Circuit Low

t: Engine Oil Pressure Control Circuit Open
s: Modern engines use an electrically controlled variable-displacement oil pump or a pressure regulator solenoid. The control circuit on this regulator has an open circuit - the ECU cannot vary oil pressure with load and the pump runs at default mechanical pressure.

t: Engine Oil Pressure Control Circuit Low
s: Same as P06DA with the control line shorted to ground. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

s: Same as P06DA with the control line shorted to supply. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Engine Oil Pressure Control Circuit Performance Stuck Off
s: Same as P06DA with the regulator stuck in low-pressure (off) state - oil pressure cannot be raised on demand.

t: Engine Oil Pressure Control Circuit Stuck On
s: Same as P06DA with the regulator stuck on - oil pressure remains at the high-output level even at idle, increasing pump losses.

t: Control Module Wake-up Circuit Performance

t: Glow Plug Control Module 1 Performance
s: Glow plug control module 1 is electrically intact but does not deliver the expected heating profile - aged drivers, increased contact resistance, or watchdog event.

t: Engine Starter Performance
s: The ECU detects unusual cranking behaviour - excessive crank time, slow crank speed, or starter draw outside the normal envelope. Could be a worn starter, weak battery, high engine drag, or excess starter solenoid resistance.

s: After a stop-start auto-stop the engine takes longer than calibrated to restart, or restart is missed. Possible causes are weak starter, low battery state of charge, weak crank position signal at low RPM, or a fuel rail that lost prime.
