s: The control circuit driving glow plug control module 2 is open. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Glow Plug Control Module 2 Control Circuit Low
s: Same as P06F6 with the control line shorted to ground. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Glow Plug Control Module 2 Control Circuit High
s: Same as P06F6 with the control line shorted to supply. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Glow Plug Control Module 2 System Voltage
s: The first glow plug control module reports its supply voltage outside the calibrated window - either too low to drive the plugs to full temperature or too high, risking damage. Affects diesel cold-start time and white-smoke emissions.

t: Glow Plug Control Module 2 Performance
s: Glow plug control module 1 is electrically intact but does not deliver the expected heating profile - aged drivers, increased contact resistance, or watchdog event.

t: Glow Plug Control Module 2 Memory Checksum Error
s: Glow plug control module 1 reports a check-sum failure on its internal memory - calibration or configuration data may be corrupted.

t: Glow Plug Control Module 2 to PCM Communication Circuit
s: A circuit fault is detected on the communication line between glow plug control module 1 and the PCM. The PCM cannot read plug status and cannot command preheat.

t: Glow Plug Control Module 2 to PCM Communication Range or Performance
s: Same as P0684 on module 2.

t: Cold Start Diesel Intake Air Flow Control Performance
s: During cold-start of a diesel engine the ECU controls intake airflow (e.g. anti-shudder valve, swirl flaps) to support smooth ignition. The control loop does not deliver the expected airflow - flap stuck or position offset.

t: Economy Mode Lamp Control Circuit
s: A circuit fault on the stop-start mode indicator lamp output.

t: Transmission Control System Malfunction
s: A request from the transmission control module (TCM) to illuminate the MIL. P0700 itself carries no specific information — it is a wrapper that tells the engine ECU "the TCM has a code". Read the TCM directly to find the actual transmission DTC.

t: Transmission Control System Range/Performance
s: The TCM has detected a transmission performance issue without identifying a specific component fault — typically slip outside expected range, shift timing outside spec, or a hydraulic issue not yet diagnosed by a more specific code.

t: Transmission Control System Electrical
s: A general electrical fault is detected in the transmission control system. The TCM has flagged a circuit problem (open, short, or supply issue) without specifying which component. Look up additional codes from the TCM for detail.

t: Brake Switch B Circuit
s: A general circuit fault on the secondary brake switch B - typically used as redundant input for safety-critical functions like cruise control cancellation and shift-interlock.

t: Clutch Switch Input Circuit
s: A circuit fault on the clutch pedal position switch input - used by the ECU to inhibit cruise control, allow cranking, and time gear-shift events on manual transmissions.

t: Transmission Range Sensor Circuit Malfunction
s: The transmission range sensor (PRNDL switch / inhibitor switch) signal is implausible — multiple positions active simultaneously, or no position recognized. Engine may refuse to start unless P or N is detected.

t: Transmission Range Sensor Circuit Range/Performance
s: The transmission range sensor reports a position that is implausible against vehicle speed and engine state. Often caused by sensor misadjustment or worn contacts.

t: Transmission Range Sensor A Circuit Low
s: The primary transmission range sensor A (PRNDL switch position) reports voltage below the plausible range - signal shorted to ground or sensor fault.

t: Transmission Range Sensor A Circuit High
s: Same as P0707 with the signal voltage above the plausible range. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Transmission Range Sensor A Circuit Intermittent
s: Same as P0707 with intermittent or erratic signal. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Transmission Fluid Level Sensor Circuit
s: A general circuit fault on the transmission fluid level sensor.

t: Transmission Fluid Level Sensor Circuit Range or Performance
s: Same as P070A with the signal in plausible range yet not matching the engine model.

t: Transmission Fluid Level Sensor Circuit Low
s: Same as P070A with the signal voltage below the plausible range. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Transmission Fluid Level Sensor Circuit High
s: Same as P070A with the signal voltage above the plausible range. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Transmission Fluid Level Sensor Circuit Intermittent or Erratic
s: Same as P070A with intermittent or erratic signal. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Transmission Fluid Level Too Low
s: The fluid level sensor reports level below the safe minimum - risk of pump cavitation, hydraulic pressure loss and gearbox damage. Drive carefully or stop and refill.

t: Transmission Fluid Temperature Sensor A Circuit
s: The transmission fluid temperature sensor A signal is missing or out of range. Used by shift logic to adapt timings to fluid viscosity.

t: Transmission Fluid Temperature Sensor A Circuit Range or Performance
s: The transmission fluid temperature sensor A reports a value within plausible range yet not matching the engine model - sensor drift or slow response.

t: Transmission Fluid Temperature Sensor Circuit Low
s: ATF temperature sensor signal voltage below the lower threshold — open, short to ground, or sensor fault.

t: Transmission Fluid Temperature Sensor Circuit High
s: ATF temperature sensor signal voltage above the upper threshold — short to supply or sensor fault. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Transmission Fluid Temperature Sensor A Circuit Intermittent
s: Same as P0711 with intermittent or erratic signal. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Input/Turbine Speed Sensor "A" Circuit Malfunction
s: The transmission input speed (turbine) sensor signal is missing or implausible. Without this signal, the TCM cannot detect torque converter slip or calculate gear ratio in real time, and shifts become harsh.

t: Input/Turbine Speed Sensor "A" Circuit Range/Performance
s: The transmission input/turbine speed sensor signal is implausible compared to engine RPM and current gear ratio. Often caused by a failing sensor, debris on the reluctor, or wiring noise.

t: Input/Turbine Speed Sensor Circuit No Signal
s: TCM detects no signal from the input/turbine speed sensor while the engine is running and the transmission is in gear. Without this signal the TCM cannot compute slip, and the transmission usually enters limp mode.

t: Input or Turbine Shaft Speed Sensor A Circuit Intermittent
s: The input/turbine shaft speed sensor A reports intermittent signal - typical for damaged reluctor teeth, weak magnet, or harness fault.

t: Brake Switch B Circuit Low
s: The transmission control system sees the brake switch B input below the lower threshold. Used to release torque converter lockup on braking.

t: Transmission Mode Switch A Circuit
s: A circuit fault on the transmission mode switch A - the driver-controlled switch that selects modes like Sport, Manual, Tow on automatic transmissions.

t: Transmission Mode Switch A Circuit Low
s: Same as P071A with the signal shorted to ground. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Transmission Mode Switch A Circuit High
s: Same as P071A with the signal shorted to supply. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Transmission Mode Switch B Circuit
s: A circuit fault on the transmission mode switch A - the driver-controlled switch that selects modes like Sport, Manual, Tow on automatic transmissions.

t: Transmission Mode Switch B Circuit Low
s: Same as P071B on switch B. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Transmission Mode Switch B Circuit High
s: Same as P071C on switch B. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Output Speed Sensor Circuit Malfunction
s: The transmission output speed sensor signal is missing or implausible. The TCM uses this signal together with input speed to determine actual gear ratio; many vehicles also use it as a backup vehicle speed signal for the cluster and ECU.

t: Output Speed Sensor Circuit Range/Performance
s: The transmission output speed sensor signal is implausible compared to vehicle speed and gear ratio. Sometimes set together with VSS or input-speed-sensor codes.

t: Output Speed Sensor Circuit No Signal
s: TCM detects no output shaft speed signal while the vehicle is moving. Triggers shift abnormalities, speedometer issues, and limp-home mode.

t: Output Shaft Speed Sensor Circuit Intermittent
s: The input/turbine shaft speed sensor A reports intermittent signal - typical for damaged reluctor teeth, weak magnet, or harness fault.

t: Brake Switch B Circuit High
s: The transmission control system sees the brake switch B input below the lower threshold. Used to release torque converter lockup on braking.

t: Engine Speed Input Circuit
s: A general circuit fault on the engine speed signal that the TCM receives from the ECM. Without this signal the TCM cannot synchronise gear shifts to engine load.

t: Engine Speed Input Circuit Range or Performance
s: Same as P0725 with the signal in plausible range yet not matching the engine model.

t: Engine Speed Input Circuit No Signal
s: The transmission control module receives no engine speed signal. Required to coordinate shifts and torque converter lockup with engine state.

t: Engine Speed Input Circuit Intermittent
s: Same as P0725 with intermittent or erratic signal. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Gear 6 Incorrect Ratio
s: When 1st gear is commanded, the actual ratio between input and output speed sensors does not match the expected value. Indicates internal slip — typically worn clutches or low line pressure.

t: Stuck in Neutral
s: The TCM detects that the transmission is mechanically stuck in neutral - shift solenoids do not engage a gear despite commanded driving range. Driveline produces no torque.

t: Stuck In Reverse
s: Same as P072A with the transmission stuck in reverse - cannot select forward driving range.

t: Stuck in Gear 1
s: The transmission is stuck in gear 1 - upshifts to higher gears are blocked by a stuck shift solenoid or hydraulic valve.

t: Stuck in Gear 2
s: The transmission is stuck in gear 1 - upshifts to higher gears are blocked by a stuck shift solenoid or hydraulic valve.

t: Stuck in Gear 3
s: The transmission is stuck in gear 1 - upshifts to higher gears are blocked by a stuck shift solenoid or hydraulic valve.

t: Stuck in Gear 4
s: The transmission is stuck in gear 1 - upshifts to higher gears are blocked by a stuck shift solenoid or hydraulic valve.

t: Incorrect Gear Ratio
s: The ratio between input and output speed does not match any expected gear. Indicates slippage, valve body fault or sensor inaccuracy without identifying a specific gear.

t: Gear 1 Incorrect Ratio
s: When 1st gear is commanded, the actual ratio between input and output speed sensors does not match the expected value. Indicates internal slip — typically worn clutches or low line pressure.

t: Gear 2 Incorrect Ratio
s: When 1st gear is commanded, the actual ratio between input and output speed sensors does not match the expected value. Indicates internal slip — typically worn clutches or low line pressure.

t: Gear 3 Incorrect Ratio
s: When 1st gear is commanded, the actual ratio between input and output speed sensors does not match the expected value. Indicates internal slip — typically worn clutches or low line pressure.

t: Gear 4 Incorrect Ratio
s: When 1st gear is commanded, the actual ratio between input and output speed sensors does not match the expected value. Indicates internal slip — typically worn clutches or low line pressure.

t: Gear 5 Incorrect Ratio
s: When 1st gear is commanded, the actual ratio between input and output speed sensors does not match the expected value. Indicates internal slip — typically worn clutches or low line pressure.

t: Reverse Incorrect Ratio
s: The TCM detected a ratio mismatch in reverse — likely a stuck solenoid, slipping clutch, or worn band.

t: TCM Engine Speed Output Circuit
s: A circuit fault on the engine speed value the TCM sends to other modules (rev counter, hybrid module).

t: TCM Engine Speed Output Circuit Low
s: Same as P0737 with the output shorted to ground. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: TCM Engine Speed Output Circuit High
s: Same as P0737 with the output shorted to supply. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Stuck in Gear 5
s: The transmission is stuck in gear 1 - upshifts to higher gears are blocked by a stuck shift solenoid or hydraulic valve.

t: Stuck in Gear 6
s: The transmission is stuck in gear 1 - upshifts to higher gears are blocked by a stuck shift solenoid or hydraulic valve.

t: Stuck in Gear 7
s: The transmission is stuck in gear 1 - upshifts to higher gears are blocked by a stuck shift solenoid or hydraulic valve.

t: Unable to Engage Neutral
s: The TCM cannot disengage all gears to neutral - typically a stuck clutch pack or hydraulic pressure issue keeping a gear engaged.

t: Unable to Engage Reverse
s: Reverse cannot be engaged when commanded - reverse-band, clutch pack or hydraulic pressure on the reverse circuit has failed.

t: Unable to Engage Gear 1
s: The transmission cannot engage gear 1 - typically a failed shift solenoid, clutch pack, or hydraulic supply problem on the gear-1 circuit.

t: Torque Converter Clutch Circuit Malfunction
s: A general fault has been detected in the torque converter clutch (TCC) lockup circuit. The TCC mechanically links the engine to the transmission input shaft above a certain speed to eliminate slip and improve fuel economy.

t: Torque Converter Clutch Circuit Performance/Stuck Off
s: When the TCM commands torque converter lockup, the actual slip stays above threshold — the clutch is not engaging. Often signals worn TCC friction material; transmission fluid analysis usually shows debris.

t: Torque Converter Clutch Circuit Stuck On
s: The torque converter clutch (TCC) cannot disengage. Symptom is engine stalling when coming to a stop, because the engine is mechanically locked to the transmission output.

t: Torque Converter Clutch Circuit Electrical
s: An electrical fault in the torque converter clutch solenoid circuit — open, shorted, or out-of-range driver current. The transmission usually inhibits TCC apply when this code is active.

t: Torque Converter Clutch Circuit Intermittent
s: The TCC circuit drops in and out — intermittent open or short. Symptoms include erratic shudder at lock-up speeds and intermittent engine stalls when stopping.

t: Pressure Control Solenoid A
s: A general fault on transmission pressure control solenoid A - the solenoid that meters main line pressure for the entire valve body.

t: Pressure Control Solenoid A Performance
s: Pressure control solenoid A response does not match command. Line pressure does not track expected values, leading to harsh, slipping or delayed shifts.

t: Pressure Control Solenoid A Stuck On
s: Pressure control solenoid A appears electrically commanded off but pressure feedback indicates it is stuck on. Line pressure stays high.

t: Pressure Control Solenoid A Electrical
s: An electrical fault on the pressure control solenoid A circuit - typically open or short to ground or supply.

t: Pressure Control Solenoid A Intermittent
s: Same as P0748 with intermittent or erratic signal.

t: Unable to Engage Gear 2
s: The transmission cannot engage gear 1 - typically a failed shift solenoid, clutch pack, or hydraulic supply problem on the gear-1 circuit.

t: Unable to Engage Gear 3
s: The transmission cannot engage gear 1 - typically a failed shift solenoid, clutch pack, or hydraulic supply problem on the gear-1 circuit.

t: Unable to Engage Gear 4
s: The transmission cannot engage gear 1 - typically a failed shift solenoid, clutch pack, or hydraulic supply problem on the gear-1 circuit.

t: Unable to Engage Gear 5
s: The transmission cannot engage gear 1 - typically a failed shift solenoid, clutch pack, or hydraulic supply problem on the gear-1 circuit.

t: Unable to Engage Gear 6
s: The transmission cannot engage gear 1 - typically a failed shift solenoid, clutch pack, or hydraulic supply problem on the gear-1 circuit.

t: Unable to Engage Gear 7
s: The transmission cannot engage gear 1 - typically a failed shift solenoid, clutch pack, or hydraulic supply problem on the gear-1 circuit.

t: Shift Solenoid "A" Malfunction
s: An electrical or hydraulic fault in shift solenoid A. Shift solenoids open or close hydraulic passages inside the valve body to engage the right clutch pack for each gear; a stuck or shorted solenoid causes harsh shifts, no-shift, or limp-home.

t: Shift Solenoid A Performance
s: Shift solenoid A is electrically intact but its mechanical response does not match command. Often caused by mechanical sticking or contamination.

t: Shift Solenoid A Stuck On
s: Shift solenoid A is stuck on - hydraulic pressure to its gear circuit stays applied even when the TCM commands deactivation.

t: Shift Solenoid A Electrical
s: An electrical fault on shift solenoid A - open or short on the control circuit.

t: Shift Solenoid A Intermittent
s: Same as P0753 with intermittent signal.

t: Shift Solenoid "B" Malfunction
s: An electrical or hydraulic fault in shift solenoid A. Shift solenoids open or close hydraulic passages inside the valve body to engage the right clutch pack for each gear; a stuck or shorted solenoid causes harsh shifts, no-shift, or limp-home.

t: Shift Solenoid B Performance
s: Shift solenoid A is electrically intact but its mechanical response does not match command. Often caused by mechanical sticking or contamination.

t: Shift Solenoid B Stuck On
s: Shift solenoid A is stuck on - hydraulic pressure to its gear circuit stays applied even when the TCM commands deactivation.

t: Shift Solenoid "B" Electrical
s: Electrical fault on shift solenoid B circuit — open winding, short to ground, or short to supply.

t: Shift Solenoid B Intermittent
s: Same as P0754 on shift solenoid B.

t: Shift Solenoid G
s: A general fault on shift solenoid G - typically the seventh shift solenoid in transmissions with 7 or more gears.

t: Shift Solenoid G Performance/Stuck Off
s: Shift solenoid G is electrically intact, but its hydraulic effect is missing — when commanded on, the gear it should engage does not lock up. Either the solenoid is mechanically stuck closed, the valve body bore is worn, or hydraulic pressure does not reach the clutch pack.

t: Shift Solenoid G Stuck On
s: Shift solenoid G remains hydraulically active even when the TCM commands it off. The corresponding clutch or band stays applied, blocking shifts that need it released — typical symptoms are harsh engagement, stuck in a single gear, or refusal to shift cleanly.

t: Shift Solenoid G Electrical
s: General electrical fault on the shift solenoid G control circuit — open coil, short to ground, short to battery voltage, or driver feedback outside the expected range. The TCM disables this solenoid and falls back to limp-home gear logic to protect the transmission.

t: Shift Solenoid G Intermittent
s: Shift solenoid G shows intermittent electrical or hydraulic dropouts — vibration on the harness, loose connector, contaminated ATF that occasionally jams the spool, or thermal sensitivity. Difficult to catch with a static check; usually documented by snapshot/freeze-frame data when the fault occurs.

t: Transmission Fluid Level Too High
s: The transmission fluid level monitor reports overfill above the safe maximum. Causes range from incorrect refill at service to coolant intrusion via a failed transmission cooler. Overfilling can cause foaming, accelerated wear and seal damage from raised internal pressure.

t: Shift Solenoid "C" Malfunction
s: An electrical or hydraulic fault in shift solenoid A. Shift solenoids open or close hydraulic passages inside the valve body to engage the right clutch pack for each gear; a stuck or shorted solenoid causes harsh shifts, no-shift, or limp-home.

t: Shift Solenoid C Performance
s: Shift solenoid A is electrically intact but its mechanical response does not match command. Often caused by mechanical sticking or contamination.

t: Shift Solenoid C Stuck On
s: Shift solenoid C remains hydraulically active when commanded off — the corresponding clutch or band cannot release, locking out shifts that depend on it. The TCM detects the missing release via gear-ratio error or output-shaft slip.

t: Shift Solenoid "C" Electrical
s: Electrical fault on shift solenoid B circuit — open winding, short to ground, or short to supply.

t: Shift Solenoid C Intermittent
s: Shift solenoid C shows intermittent electrical or hydraulic dropouts — vibration, loose connector, ATF contamination or thermal sensitivity. Hard to reproduce on a static bench check.

t: Shift Solenoid "D" Malfunction
s: An electrical or hydraulic fault in shift solenoid A. Shift solenoids open or close hydraulic passages inside the valve body to engage the right clutch pack for each gear; a stuck or shorted solenoid causes harsh shifts, no-shift, or limp-home.

t: Shift Solenoid D Performance
s: Shift solenoid A is electrically intact but its mechanical response does not match command. Often caused by mechanical sticking or contamination.

t: Shift Solenoid D Stuck On
s: Shift solenoid D remains hydraulically active when commanded off — corresponding clutch or band cannot release. The TCM detects the missing release via gear-ratio error.

t: Shift Solenoid "D" Electrical
s: Electrical fault on shift solenoid B circuit — open winding, short to ground, or short to supply.

t: Shift Solenoid D Intermittent
s: Shift solenoid D shows intermittent electrical or hydraulic dropouts.

t: Shift Solenoid H
s: General fault on shift solenoid H — used in 8/9/10-speed transmissions where multiple shift solenoids manage the larger gear matrix. The TCM detects an electrical or hydraulic anomaly without yet being able to attribute it to a specific failure mode.

t: Shift Solenoid H Performance/Stuck Off
s: Shift solenoid H is electrically intact but does not engage hydraulically when commanded — corresponding clutch or band does not lock up.

t: Shift Solenoid H Stuck On
s: Shift solenoid H remains hydraulically active when commanded off — corresponding clutch or band cannot release, blocking dependent shifts.

t: Shift Solenoid H Electrical
s: General electrical fault on the shift solenoid H control circuit — open coil, short to ground or battery, driver feedback outside range.

t: Shift Solenoid H Intermittent
s: Shift solenoid H shows intermittent electrical or hydraulic dropouts.

t: Gear 7 Incorrect Ratio
s: While in 7th gear, the ratio measured between input shaft speed and output shaft speed does not match the calibrated value for that gear. Either a clutch or band is slipping, the wrong gear actually engaged, or speed sensor data is wrong.

t: Shift Solenoid E
s: General fault on shift solenoid E. The TCM detects an electrical or hydraulic anomaly that has not yet been pinned to a specific failure mode.

t: Shift Solenoid E Performance/Stuck Off
s: Shift solenoid E is electrically intact but does not engage hydraulically — associated clutch or band does not lock up.

t: Shift Solenoid E Stuck On
s: Shift solenoid E remains hydraulically active when commanded off — associated clutch or band cannot release, blocking dependent shifts.

t: Shift Solenoid "E" Electrical
s: Electrical fault on shift solenoid B circuit — open winding, short to ground, or short to supply.

t: Shift Solenoid E Intermittent
s: Shift solenoid E shows intermittent electrical or hydraulic dropouts — vibration, loose connector or ATF contamination.

t: Pressure Control Solenoid B
s: Generic fault on pressure control solenoid B circuit. Either electrical or response fault depending on manufacturer.

t: Pressure Control Solenoid B Performance
s: Pressure control solenoid A response does not match command. Line pressure does not track expected values, leading to harsh, slipping or delayed shifts.

t: Pressure Control Solenoid B Stuck On
s: Pressure control solenoid B does not release line pressure when commanded — output pressure remains at maximum, leading to harsh shifts and elevated stress on the clutch packs and seals.

t: Pressure Control Solenoid B Electrical
s: General electrical fault on the pressure control solenoid B circuit — open coil, short to ground or battery, or driver feedback outside the expected range.

t: Pressure Control Solenoid B Intermittent
s: Pressure control solenoid B shows intermittent electrical or hydraulic dropouts — vibration, loose connector, or contaminated ATF that occasionally restricts the regulator port.

t: Output Shaft Speed Sensor Circuit - Loss of Direction Signal
s: The output shaft speed sensor uses a quadrature output (two channels phase-shifted by 90°) so the TCM can determine direction of rotation. The direction channel is missing — TCM cannot tell whether the vehicle is rolling forward or backward, blocking neutral-idle and rollback-protection strategies.

t: Output Shaft Speed Sensor Circuit - Direction Error
s: The TCM reads a direction from the output shaft speed sensor that contradicts the commanded gear — for example reverse direction while drive is engaged, or forward direction while reverse is engaged. Either the sensor wiring is swapped, the sensor is wrong, or the gear actually engaged is not the one commanded.

t: Output Shaft Speed Sensor Circuit Low
s: Output shaft speed sensor signal voltage below the lower limit — short to ground or open supply. TCM disables vehicle-speed dependent shift logic and may force limp-home.

t: Output Shaft Speed Sensor Circuit High
s: Output shaft speed sensor signal voltage above the upper limit — short to reference voltage or open signal return.

t: Transmission Fluid Temperature Measurement System - Multiple Sensor Correlation
s: Modern transmissions can have two or more ATF temperature sensors (sump, cooler return, valve body). After a long key-off soak they should all read approximately the same ambient. The TCM detects an implausible spread between sensors — one of them drifts or is wired wrong.

t: Reverse 2 Incorrect Ratio
s: Some transmissions provide a second reverse ratio (typically a slower crawler reverse for trailer manoeuvring or hill descent). The TCM detects that the input/output ratio in this gear does not match its calibrated value — slipping clutch or wrong gear actually engaged.

t: Shift Malfunction
s: The TCM detects that a commanded shift between gears did not occur within the expected time and quality window. Slip rates or speed ratios remain implausible during shift.

t: 1-2 Shift Malfunction
s: The TCM detects a fault during the 1-to-2 upshift. The actual gear ratio after the shift command does not match the expected ratio for second gear.

t: 2-3 Shift Malfunction
s: The TCM detects a fault during the 2-to-3 upshift. Gear ratio after shift command does not match third gear expected ratio.

t: 3-4 Shift Malfunction
s: The TCM detects a fault during the 3-to-4 upshift, where gear ratio after the shift does not match fourth gear expected value.

t: 4-5 Shift Malfunction
s: The TCM detects a fault during the 4-to-5 upshift on transmissions equipped with five or more forward gears.

t: Shift Timing Solenoid A
s: General fault on shift timing solenoid A — controls the rate at which a shift event takes place (smooth ramp vs. quick snap). The TCM detects an electrical or hydraulic anomaly that has not yet been pinned to a specific failure mode.

t: Shift Timing Solenoid A Range/Performance
s: Shift timing solenoid A is electrically intact, but the actual shift time does not match the model — shifts too fast (harsh) or too slow (slipping under load). Often aged solenoid with weakened force or contaminated ATF restricting the regulating port.

t: Shift Timing Solenoid A Low
s: Shift timing solenoid A control circuit reads stuck low — short to ground or low-side driver stuck on.

t: Shift Timing Solenoid A High
s: Shift timing solenoid A control circuit reads stuck high — short to battery voltage or open coil/return.

t: Shift Timing Solenoid A Intermittent
s: Shift timing solenoid A signal jumps or drops out — vibration, loose connector or thermal sensitivity.

t: Shift Timing Solenoid B
s: General fault on shift timing solenoid B — second timing solenoid for shift events that need a different ramp profile (typically downshifts vs. upshifts, or specific gear pairs).

t: Shift Timing Solenoid B Range/Performance
s: Shift timing solenoid B is electrically intact, but actual shift timing does not match the model — same failure modes as solenoid A (aged, dirty ATF), applied to the second timing channel.

t: Shift Timing Solenoid B Low
s: Shift timing solenoid B control circuit reads stuck low — short to ground.

t: Shift Timing Solenoid B High
s: Shift timing solenoid B control circuit reads stuck high — short to battery voltage or open coil.

t: Shift Timing Solenoid B Intermittent
s: Shift timing solenoid B signal jumps or drops out.

t: Transmission Mode Switch C Circuit
s: General electrical fault on the third transmission mode select switch input C — used for additional drive modes (Sport, Snow, Tow/Haul, Eco) on transmissions that offer more than two operator-selectable shift maps. Without a valid input the TCM falls back to the default mode.

t: Normal/Performance Switch Circuit
s: General electrical fault on the Normal/Performance shift-pattern selector switch — lets the driver toggle between a comfort-oriented and a sport-oriented shift map. Without a valid input the TCM defaults to the Normal map.

t: Intermediate Shaft Speed Sensor A Circuit
s: The TCM detects an electrical fault on the intermediate shaft speed sensor A circuit. Used to compute shift slip and torque converter behavior.

t: Intermediate Shaft Speed Sensor A Circuit Range/Performance
s: The intermediate shaft speed sensor (between input and output, used in CVT and dual-clutch boxes for slip detection between gear elements) is electrically intact, but its readings drift away from the model. The TCM cannot reliably detect slip in this stage.

t: Intermediate Shaft Speed Sensor A Circuit No Signal
s: Intermediate shaft speed sensor A is connected and powered, but no rotation pulses arrive while the transmission is in motion. Either the sensor reluctor wheel is damaged, the sensor air gap is wrong, or the element has failed open.

t: Intermediate Shaft Speed Sensor A Circuit Intermittent
s: Intermediate shaft speed sensor A signal jumps or drops out — vibration, loose connector or oil mist on the sensor tip.

t: Pressure Control Solenoid C
s: General fault on pressure control solenoid C — third pressure regulator in transmissions with multiple independently regulated hydraulic circuits (line pressure, clutch C, torque converter clutch). The TCM detects an electrical or hydraulic anomaly that has not yet been pinned to a specific failure mode.

t: Pressure Control Solenoid C Performance
s: Pressure control solenoid A response does not match command. Line pressure does not track expected values, leading to harsh, slipping or delayed shifts.

t: Pressure Control Solenoid C Stuck On
s: The TCM detects that pressure control solenoid C remains energized hydraulically despite commanded de-energization. Line pressure or shift quality deviates from target.

t: Pressure Control Solenoid C Electrical
s: The TCM detects an electrical fault in the pressure control solenoid C circuit, such as open winding, short to ground or short to power.

t: Pressure Control Solenoid C Intermittent
s: Pressure control solenoid C shows intermittent electrical or hydraulic dropouts.

t: Transmission Friction Element A Slip Detected
s: Friction element A (the first clutch or band in the gear matrix) shows measurable slip while it should be fully engaged. Detected by comparing input/output speed sensors against the commanded gear ratio. Slip means the element cannot transmit full torque — risk of glazing the friction surfaces.

t: Transmission Friction Element B Slip Detected
s: Same condition as element A slip, applied to friction element B (the second clutch or band).

t: Transmission Friction Element C Slip Detected
s: Same condition as element A slip, applied to friction element C (third clutch or band).

t: Transmission Friction Element D Slip Detected
s: Same condition as element A slip, applied to friction element D (fourth clutch or band).

t: Transmission Friction Element E Slip Detected
s: Same condition as element A slip, applied to friction element E (fifth clutch or band, used in 8/9/10-speed transmissions).

t: Transmission Friction Element F Slip Detected
s: Same condition as element A slip, applied to friction element F (sixth clutch or band, used in 8/9/10-speed transmissions).

t: Transmission Friction Element G Slip Detected
s: Same condition as element A slip, applied to friction element G (seventh clutch or band, used in 9/10-speed transmissions).

t: Transmission Friction Element H Slip Detected
s: Same condition as element A slip, applied to friction element H (eighth clutch or band, in 10-speed transmissions).

t: Transmission Friction Element A Performance/Stuck Off
s: Friction element A does not lock up when commanded — its hydraulic circuit cannot bring the clutch pack into full engagement. Either the apply piston is stuck, the seal is leaking past the piston, or the supplying pressure regulator is failing.

t: Transmission Friction Element A Stuck On
s: Friction element A does not release when commanded — the apply piston cannot retract, leaving the clutch or band engaged. The transmission cannot complete shifts that need element A released, leading to harsh engagement or stall when stopping.

t: Transmission Friction Element B Performance/Stuck Off
s: Same condition as element A stuck off, applied to friction element B.

t: Transmission Friction Element B Stuck On
s: Same condition as element A stuck on, applied to friction element B.

t: Transmission Friction Element C Performance/Stuck Off
s: Same condition as element A stuck off, applied to element C.

t: Transmission Friction Element C Stuck On
s: Same condition as element A stuck on, applied to element C.

t: Transmission Friction Element D Performance/Stuck Off
s: Same condition as element A stuck off, applied to element D.

t: Transmission Friction Element D Stuck On
s: Same condition as element A stuck on, applied to element D.

t: Transmission Friction Element E Performance/Stuck Off
s: Same condition as element A stuck off, applied to element E.

t: Transmission Friction Element E Stuck On
s: Same condition as element A stuck on, applied to element E.

t: Transmission Friction Element F Performance/Stuck Off
s: Same condition as element A stuck off, applied to element F.

t: Transmission Friction Element F Stuck On
s: Same condition as element A stuck on, applied to element F.

t: Transmission Friction Element G Performance/Stuck Off
s: Same condition as element A stuck off, applied to element G.

t: Transmission Friction Element G Stuck On
s: Same condition as element A stuck on, applied to element G.

t: Transmission Friction Element H Performance/Stuck Off
s: Same condition as element A stuck off, applied to element H.

t: Transmission Friction Element H Stuck On
s: Same condition as element A stuck on, applied to element H.

t: Transmission Park Position Sensor/Switch A Circuit/Open
s: Park position sensor/switch A — confirms that the gear selector lever is in Park, used by shift-by-wire systems and key-interlock logic — is open. The TCM cannot confirm Park engagement, blocking key removal and engine restart logic.

t: Transmission Park Position Sensor/Switch A Circuit Low
s: Park position sensor/switch A reads stuck low — short to ground or stuck-closed contact. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Transmission Park Position Sensor/Switch A Circuit High
s: Park position sensor/switch A reads stuck high — short to battery voltage or open switch return. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Transmission Park Position Sensor/Switch A Circuit Performance/Low
s: Sensor A is electrically intact but its low-end value drifts — for example a value below the calibrated Park threshold while the lever is mechanically out of Park. The TCM cannot fully trust the Park reading.

t: Transmission Park Position Sensor/Switch A Circuit Performance High
s: Sensor A is electrically intact but the high-end value drifts — for example a value above the calibrated non-Park range while the lever actually is in Park.

t: Transmission Park Position Sensor/Switch A Circuit Intermittent/Erratic
s: Park position sensor A signal jumps or drops out — typically a worn switch with contact bounce or a loose connector at the shift lever module.

t: Transmission Park Position Sensor/Switch B Circuit/Open
s: Park position sensor/switch B is the redundant/secondary park-confirmation sensor — used in shift-by-wire systems for safety, so a single sensor failure cannot strand the vehicle in Park or hide a non-Park condition. With B open the TCM falls back to sensor A only.

t: Transmission Park Position Sensor/Switch B Circuit Low
s: Park sensor/switch B reads stuck low — short to ground or stuck-closed contact. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Transmission Park Position Sensor/Switch B Circuit High
s: Park sensor/switch B reads stuck high — short to battery voltage. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Transmission Park Position Sensor/Switch B Circuit Performance/Low
s: Sensor B low-end value drifts away from the calibrated Park threshold.

t: Transmission Park Position Sensor/Switch B Circuit Performance High
s: Sensor B high-end value drifts above the calibrated non-Park range.

t: Transmission Park Position Sensor/Switch B Circuit Intermittent/Erratic
s: Sensor B signal jumps or drops out. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Transmission Park Position Sensor/Switch A/B Correlation
s: The two redundant park position sensors A and B disagree — one reports Park, the other does not. Until the discrepancy is cleared the TCM cannot trust either reading and falls back to a safe state (typically blocking key removal and engine restart).

t: Input/Turbine Shaft Speed Sensor A Circuit Low
s: Input/turbine shaft speed sensor A signal voltage below the lower limit — short to ground or open supply. Without this signal the TCM cannot calculate slip across the torque converter or first gear element.

t: Input/Turbine Shaft Speed Sensor A Circuit High
s: Sensor A signal voltage above the upper limit — short to reference voltage or open signal return. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Input/Turbine Shaft Speed Sensor B Circuit Low
s: Same as sensor A circuit low, applied to sensor B (used in dual-clutch boxes for the second input shaft). Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Input/Turbine Shaft Speed Sensor B Circuit High
s: Same as sensor A circuit high, applied to sensor B. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Input/Turbine Shaft Speed Sensor C Circuit Low
s: Same as sensor A circuit low, applied to sensor C (used in some heavy-duty or hybrid-coupled transmissions with three input speed sensors).

t: Input/Turbine Shaft Speed Sensor C Circuit High
s: Same as sensor A circuit high, applied to sensor C. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Intermediate Shaft Speed Sensor A Circuit Low
s: Intermediate shaft speed sensor A signal voltage below the lower limit. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Intermediate Shaft Speed Sensor A Circuit High
s: Intermediate shaft speed sensor A signal voltage above the upper limit. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Intermediate Shaft Speed Sensor B Circuit Low
s: Intermediate shaft speed sensor B (second intermediate sensor in dual-clutch boxes) signal voltage below the lower limit.

t: Intermediate Shaft Speed Sensor B Circuit High
s: Intermediate shaft speed sensor B signal voltage above the upper limit. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Intermediate Shaft Speed Sensor C Circuit Low
s: Intermediate shaft speed sensor C (third intermediate sensor in heavy-duty or hybrid-coupled transmissions) signal voltage below the lower limit.

t: Intermediate Shaft Speed Sensor C Circuit High
s: Intermediate shaft speed sensor C signal voltage above the upper limit. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Transmission Fluid Thermostat Performance
s: The transmission fluid thermostat (similar in function to a coolant thermostat — opens at a calibrated temperature to route ATF through the cooler) does not regulate temperature within the expected band. Either stuck open (ATF stays cold, friction modifier ineffective) or stuck closed (ATF overheats, oxidising the fluid).

t: Transmission Mode Switch C Circuit Low
s: Transmission mode switch C input reads stuck low — short to ground or stuck-closed contact. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Transmission Mode Switch C Circuit High
s: Transmission mode switch C input reads stuck high — short to battery voltage. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Transmission Mode Switch D Circuit
s: General electrical fault on the fourth transmission mode select switch input D — additional drive mode beyond C (e.g. Manumatic, Off-Road, Trailer).

t: Transmission Mode Switch D Circuit Low
s: Mode switch D input reads stuck low — short to ground or stuck-closed contact. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Transmission Mode Switch D Circuit High
s: Mode switch D input reads stuck high — short to battery voltage. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Transmission Mode Switch E Circuit
s: General electrical fault on the fifth transmission mode select switch input E.

t: Transmission Mode Switch E Circuit Low
s: Mode switch E input reads stuck low. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Transmission Mode Switch E Circuit High
s: Mode switch E input reads stuck high. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Transmission Mode Switch F Circuit
s: General electrical fault on the sixth transmission mode select switch input F.

t: Transmission Mode Switch F Circuit Low
s: Mode switch F input reads stuck low. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Transmission Mode Switch F Circuit High
s: Mode switch F input reads stuck high. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Stuck in Gear 8
s: The transmission has entered 8th gear but cannot leave it on command — typically a stuck shift solenoid or a stuck-on friction element holding the 8th-gear pattern locked. The TCM forces limp-home mode if upshift/downshift attempts fail repeatedly.

t: Unable To Engage Gear 8
s: The TCM commands a shift to 8th gear but the gear-ratio measurement does not confirm engagement — friction element does not lock up, slipping clutch, or shift solenoid does not respond. Falls back to the previously engaged gear.

t: Gear 8 Incorrect Ratio
s: While in 8th gear, the measured input/output ratio does not match the calibrated value — slipping clutch, wrong gear actually engaged, or speed sensor data wrong.

t: 6-7 Shift
s: General fault detected during the 6-to-7 upshift event — slipping during the shift, gear-ratio overshoot, or shift completion timeout. Often a worn friction element on the 7th-gear side or a slow shift solenoid.

t: 7-8 Shift
s: General fault detected during the 7-to-8 upshift event.

t: Incorrect Shift from Gear 1
s: Starting from 1st gear, the TCM commanded a specific upshift but the resulting gear ratio matches a different gear. Wrong shift solenoid pattern actually engaged, or a stuck solenoid blocked the intended pattern.

t: Incorrect Shift from Gear 2
s: Same condition as incorrect shift from gear 1, applied to shifts originating in 2nd gear.

t: Incorrect Shift from Gear 3
s: Same condition, applied to shifts originating in 3rd gear.

t: Incorrect Shift from Gear 4
s: Same condition, applied to shifts originating in 4th gear.

t: Incorrect Shift from Gear 5
s: Same condition, applied to shifts originating in 5th gear.

t: Incorrect Shift from Gear 6
s: Same condition, applied to shifts originating in 6th gear.

t: Incorrect Shift from Gear 7
s: Same condition, applied to shifts originating in 7th gear.

t: Incorrect Shift from Gear 8
s: Same condition, applied to shifts originating in 8th gear.

t: Unable to Engage Park
s: The TCM commands the park pawl to engage but the position sensors do not confirm engagement — typical for shift-by-wire systems where the park actuator (motor or solenoid driving the pawl) is faulty, the cable is broken, or the pawl is jammed against a tooth and cannot drop in.

t: Unable to Engage Drive
s: TCM commands engagement of Drive but the input/output speed relationship does not match — friction element does not lock up, or the shift solenoid pattern for Drive cannot be commanded. Vehicle remains stuck in Park or Neutral.

t: Stuck in Park
s: The vehicle cannot leave Park even though the operator has commanded a different range. Park pawl jammed, park actuator failed, or shift-by-wire input not received by TCM.

t: Stuck in Drive
s: The vehicle cannot leave Drive — typically a stuck shift mechanism, a stuck-on friction element, or a TCM that cannot command the shift to Park or Neutral.

t: Transmission Range Control A Circuit/Open
s: The transmission range control A circuit (used in shift-by-wire to drive the shift actuator that physically positions the manual valve in the valve body) is open. The TCM cannot move the manual valve into the commanded range, blocking shifts between P/R/N/D.

t: Transmission Range Control A Circuit Performance
s: Range control A circuit is electrically intact, but the actuator response does not match the model — for example position feedback that does not move when commanded, or moves slower than expected. Often a sticking actuator or aged motor.

t: Transmission Range Control A Circuit Low
s: Range control A circuit reads stuck low — short to ground or low-side driver stuck on. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Transmission Range Control A Circuit High
s: Range control A circuit reads stuck high — short to battery voltage or open coil/return. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Transmission Range Multi-Function Select Circuit
s: General electrical fault on the multi-function transmission range select circuit — used on shift-by-wire systems with rotary or push-button selectors that encode P/R/N/D as a multi-bit pattern. With the input invalid the TCM cannot decode the operator request.

t: Transmission Range Multi-Function Select Circuit Stuck
s: The multi-function select circuit reports a value that does not change despite operator activity — the rotary encoder is stuck or a push-button is mechanically locked. The TCM ignores further requests until the input becomes plausible.

t: Transmission Range Multi-Function Select Circuit Range/Performance
s: The multi-function select circuit produces values that do not match any valid bit pattern — multiple positions asserted simultaneously, or values outside the calibrated range.

t: Transmission Range Multi-Function Select Circuit Low
s: Multi-function select circuit reads stuck low — short to ground or pegged at minimum. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Transmission Range Multi-Function Select Circuit High
s: Multi-function select circuit reads stuck high — short to battery voltage or pegged at maximum. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Input/Turbine Shaft Speed Sensor C Circuit
s: General electrical fault on input/turbine speed sensor C — third input speed sensor in heavy-duty or hybrid-coupled transmissions.

t: Transmission Range Control Module Communication Circuit
s: General fault on the communication line between the TCM and a separate transmission range control module (used on systems where the rotary/push-button selector lives in its own intelligent module that talks to the TCM via CAN or LIN).

t: Transmission Range Control Module Communication Circuit Low
s: Range control module communication line reads stuck low — bus shorted to ground or one node held the line low continuously.

t: Transmission Range Control Module Communication Circuit High
s: Range control module communication line reads stuck high — bus shorted to battery voltage or open at the bus terminator.

t: Transmission Range Control Module System Voltage Low
s: The range control module reports its supply voltage below the operating threshold — vehicle battery low, charging system underperforming, or supply line resistance excessive. The module may stop responding until voltage recovers.

t: Gear 9 Incorrect Ratio
s: While in 9th gear, the measured input/output ratio does not match the calibrated value — slipping clutch, wrong gear actually engaged, or speed sensor data wrong.

t: Gear 10 Incorrect Ratio
s: While in 10th gear, the measured input/output ratio does not match the calibrated value.

t: Stuck in Gear 9
s: Same condition as stuck in 8th gear, applied to 9th.

t: Unable to Engage Gear 9
s: Same condition as unable to engage 8th gear, applied to 9th.

t: Incorrect Shift From Gear 9
s: Same condition as incorrect shift from 8th, applied to 9th.

t: Stuck in Gear 10
s: Same condition as stuck in 8th, applied to 10th.

t: Unable to Engage Gear 10
s: Same condition as unable to engage 8th, applied to 10th.

t: Incorrect Shift From Gear 10
s: Same condition as incorrect shift from 8th, applied to 10th.

t: Stuck In Gear - Not Specified
s: The TCM detects that the transmission is stuck in some gear but cannot identify which one — typically because multiple input/output ratio cross-checks fail simultaneously, or an early-detection diagnostic fires before the specific-gear detection runs.

t: Cold Start Transmission Performance
s: During the cold-start phase the transmission does not behave to specification — typically very slow shifts, missed shifts or harsh engagement at low ATF temperature. Common causes are degraded ATF (lost cold-flow viscosity), a stuck thermostat or aged solenoid drivers.

t: Transfer Case Control System (MIL Request)
s: The transfer case control module has requested the ECM/PCM to illuminate the MIL because of a fault on its side that is emissions- or driveline-relevant. The original fault is in the transfer case module itself.

t: Reverse Inhibit Control Circuit
s: The TCM detects an electrical fault in the reverse inhibit solenoid circuit, which prevents engagement of reverse at high vehicle speeds.

t: Transmission Control System MIL Request Circuit/Open
s: The dedicated MIL request line from the TCM to the ECM (used in older systems where modules used a hard wire to ask for the MIL instead of CAN messaging) is open. The ECM cannot honour TCM-side MIL requests.

t: Upshift/Skip Shift Solenoid Control Circuit
s: General electrical fault on the upshift/skip-shift solenoid control circuit — used on manual-transmission performance vehicles to force a skip from 1st straight to 4th under low-load cruise (CAFE fuel-economy feature). With the input invalid the lockout cannot be applied.

t: Upshift/Skip Shift Lamp Control Circuit
s: General electrical fault on the upshift/skip-shift indicator lamp control circuit — the dashboard light that prompts the driver to skip from 1st straight to 4th. With the input invalid the lamp cannot be illuminated.

t: Clutch Position Sensor A Circuit
s: General electrical fault on the clutch position sensor A circuit — used in dual-clutch and automated-manual transmissions to monitor clutch fork or release-bearing position. Without a valid signal the TCM cannot close the loop on clutch engagement.

t: Clutch Position Sensor A Circuit Range/Performance
s: Clutch position sensor A is electrically intact, but its readings drift away from the model — for example the position does not move when the actuator is commanded, or the offset shifts after a thermal cycle. Often a misadjusted sensor mount.

t: Clutch Position Sensor A Circuit Low
s: Clutch position sensor A signal voltage below the lower limit — short to ground or open reference voltage.

t: Clutch Position Sensor A Circuit High
s: Clutch position sensor A signal voltage above the upper limit — short to reference voltage or open signal return.

t: Clutch Position Sensor A Circuit Intermittent
s: Clutch position sensor A signal jumps or drops out — vibration, loose connector or oil/heat exposure near the bell housing.

t: Clutch Position Not Learned
s: After a clutch replacement or sensor replacement the TCM has not run a successful clutch-position learn cycle. Without learned end-stops the TCM cannot accurately command engagement points, leading to slipping or harsh take-up. A scan-tool guided learn procedure must be performed.

t: Upshift/Skip Shift Solenoid Control Circuit Range/Performance
s: Skip-shift solenoid is electrically intact, but the lockout effect does not match commands — solenoid sticks or coil aged, gearbox interlock cannot be applied or released within the expected time.

t: Upshift/Skip Shift Solenoid Control Circuit Low
s: Skip-shift solenoid control circuit reads stuck low — short to ground or low-side driver stuck on. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Upshift/Skip Shift Solenoid Control Circuit High
s: Skip-shift solenoid control circuit reads stuck high — short to battery voltage or open coil. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Clutch Position Control Error
s: On automated manual or dual-clutch transmissions, the actual clutch actuator position deviates from commanded position beyond tolerance. Position sensor and actuator do not agree on closed-loop test.

t: Excessive Clutch A Slippage
s: The TCM detects more slip across clutch A (the first clutch in dual-clutch boxes, or the launch clutch in automated-manual systems) than the calibration allows. Heat builds up rapidly — risk of glazing the friction discs or warping the pressure plate.

t: Reverse Input Circuit
s: General electrical fault on the reverse-engagement input — the signal that tells the TCM (and other modules — backup lights, parking sensors) that the gear lever is in Reverse. With the input invalid backup lights and rear sensors do not activate.

t: Reverse Output Circuit
s: General electrical fault on the reverse-output circuit — the line that drives backup lights, reverse alarm or transfer-case reverse signal. With the output broken these consumers cannot be activated.

t: Transmission Range Display Circuit
s: General electrical fault on the transmission range display circuit — drives the PRNDL indicator on the dashboard. With the input invalid the indicator may show the wrong gear range or stay blank.

t: Upshift Switch Circuit
s: The TCM detects an electrical fault on the manual upshift request switch (paddle or +/- lever) circuit.

t: Downshift Switch Circuit
s: The TCM detects an electrical fault on the manual downshift request switch circuit.

t: Starter Disable Circuit/Open
s: The starter-disable circuit (used by transmission, immobiliser or anti-theft to block cranking unless safety conditions are met — e.g. clutch pedal pressed on manual, brake pedal on auto) is open. The starter cannot be disabled, weakening the safety chain.

t: Driveline Disconnect Switch Input Circuit
s: General electrical fault on the driveline disconnect switch input — used on AWD/4WD vehicles to electrically disengage the rear or front axle for fuel economy. With the input invalid the disconnect cannot be commanded.

t: Up and Down Shift Switch to Transmission Range Correlation
s: The up-shift/down-shift paddle or button input is used in manumatic mode to step through gears, but the inputs only make sense when the gear lever is in Drive or the Manual position. The TCM detects an inconsistency — paddle commands while gear lever is in Park or Neutral.

t: Starter Disable Circuit Low
s: Starter disable circuit reads stuck low — short to ground or low-side driver stuck on. The starter is permanently disabled, blocking engine cranking.

t: Starter Disable Circuit High
s: Starter disable circuit reads stuck high — short to battery voltage. The disable cannot be asserted, weakening the safety chain.

t: Park Input Circuit
s: General electrical fault on the Park input circuit — used by ECM/PCM to know that the gear lever is in Park (allows engine start, raises idle for accessory loads). With the input invalid Park-conditional logic falls back to a safe default.

t: Neutral Input Circuit
s: General electrical fault on the Neutral input circuit — used by ECM/PCM to detect Neutral and adjust idle speed and start enable.

t: Excessive Clutch B Slippage
s: Same condition as excessive clutch A slippage, applied to clutch B (second clutch in dual-clutch boxes — the odd-gear clutch typically).

t: Brake Transmission Shift Interlock (BTSI) Signal Correlation
s: The Brake Transmission Shift Interlock prevents shifting out of Park unless the brake pedal is pressed. The TCM compares the brake-pedal input to the BTSI solenoid command and detects an inconsistency — for example shift permitted while brake not pressed, or interlock active while brake is pressed.

t: Gear Lever X-Y Position Sensor Circuit
s: The TCM detects an out-of-range or implausible signal from the gear lever X-Y position sensor used on shift-by-wire transmissions.

t: Gear Lever X Position Sensor 1 Circuit
s: General electrical fault on the X-axis (left/right) position sensor 1 of an electronic gear lever. Used in stick-shift-by-wire systems where the lever is decoded into X/Y coordinates rather than discrete switches.

t: Gear Lever Y Position Sensor 1 Circuit
s: General electrical fault on the Y-axis (forward/backward) position sensor 1 of an electronic gear lever.

t: Gear Lever X Position Sensor 1 Circuit Intermittent/Erratic
s: Gear lever X position sensor 1 signal jumps or drops out — typically a worn rotary contact or vibration on the centre console.

t: Gear Lever Y Position Sensor 1 Circuit Intermittent/Erratic
s: Gear lever Y position sensor 1 signal jumps or drops out. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Gear Lever Push-Pull Switch (Shift Anticipate)
s: General electrical fault on the gear-lever push-pull switch — a low-force capacitive or microswitch sensor in the lever knob that detects the operator gripping the lever before it actually moves. Used for shift-anticipation, where the TCM can pre-charge a friction element so the upcoming shift feels seamless.

t: Up and Down Shift Switch Circuit
s: The TCM detects a fault in the combined up/down shift switch circuit, where both switch states are simultaneously active or both inactive when activity is expected.

t: Up and Down Shift Switch Circuit Low
s: Up/down shift switch input reads stuck low — short to ground or stuck-pressed paddle/button. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Up and Down Shift Switch Circuit High
s: Up/down shift switch input reads stuck high — short to battery voltage. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: 5-6 Shift
s: General fault detected during the 5-to-6 upshift event.

t: Gear Lever X Position Sensor 1 Circuit Range/Performance
s: Gear lever X sensor 1 is electrically intact, but its readings drift outside the calibrated range or do not match the redundant sensor 2.

t: Gear Lever X Position Sensor 1 Circuit Low
s: Gear lever X sensor 1 reads stuck low — short to ground or open reference. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Gear Lever X Position Sensor 1 Circuit High
s: Gear lever X sensor 1 reads stuck high — short to reference voltage or open signal return. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Gear Lever Y Position Sensor 1 Circuit Range/Performance
s: Gear lever Y sensor 1 is electrically intact, but its readings drift outside the calibrated range. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Gear Lever Y Position Sensor 1 Circuit Low
s: Gear lever Y sensor 1 reads stuck low — short to ground. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Gear Lever Y Position Sensor 1 Circuit High
s: Gear lever Y sensor 1 reads stuck high — short to reference voltage. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Clutch Pedal Switch A Circuit
s: The ECU detects an electrical fault on the clutch pedal position switch A used by manual transmission engine control for cruise, start enable and idle correction.

t: Clutch Pedal Switch A Circuit Low
s: Clutch pedal switch A input reads stuck low — short to ground or stuck-closed contact. The ECM may treat clutch as constantly disengaged, blocking cruise control or affecting cranking permission on manual transmissions.

t: Clutch Pedal Switch A Circuit High
s: Clutch pedal switch A input reads stuck high — short to battery voltage. Cannot detect when clutch is pressed.

t: Clutch Pedal Switch B Circuit
s: General electrical fault on the second clutch pedal switch B — provides redundant clutch-state detection (one switch upper end of pedal travel, other lower end) so the ECM can verify both ends of pedal motion. Used for accurate cranking and cruise enable logic.

t: Clutch Pedal Switch B Circuit Low
s: Clutch pedal switch B input reads stuck low. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Clutch Pedal Switch B Circuit High
s: Clutch pedal switch B input reads stuck high. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Four Wheel Drive (4WD) Switch Circuit
s: General electrical fault on the 4WD selector switch input — used by the operator to engage 4WD on demand (4H, 4L, Auto). With the input invalid the transfer case cannot honour operator selection.

t: Four Wheel Drive (4WD) Switch Circuit Range/Performance
s: 4WD switch is electrically intact but its values do not match a valid bit pattern — multiple positions asserted simultaneously, or values outside the calibrated range.

t: Four Wheel Drive (4WD) Switch Circuit Low
s: 4WD switch input reads stuck low — short to ground or stuck-closed contact. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Four Wheel Drive (4WD) Switch Circuit High
s: 4WD switch input reads stuck high — short to battery voltage. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Transmission Fluid Pressure Sensor/Switch G Circuit
s: General electrical fault on the seventh transmission fluid pressure sensor/switch G circuit. Heavy-duty and 8/9/10-speed transmissions can have multiple pressure sensors monitoring different hydraulic circuits — sensor G is the seventh in line.

t: Transmission Fluid Pressure Sensor/Switch G Circuit Range/Performance
s: Pressure sensor G is electrically intact, but the readings do not match the model — drift, jumps or values that do not respond to commanded pressure changes.

t: Transmission Fluid Pressure Sensor/Switch G Circuit Low
s: Pressure sensor G signal voltage below the lower limit — short to ground or open reference. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Transmission Fluid Pressure Sensor/Switch G Circuit High
s: Pressure sensor G signal voltage above the upper limit — short to reference voltage or open signal return.

t: Transmission Fluid Pressure Sensor/Switch G Circuit Intermittent
s: The transmission fluid pressure sensor/switch G reports a circuit intermittent fault. Hydraulic pressure feedback for shift control is unreliable on this channel.

t: Clutch Pedal Switch A/B Correlation

t: TCM Power Input Signal
s: The TCM detects an out-of-range supply voltage at its main power input. Battery voltage is too low or too high during operation.

t: TCM Power Input Signal Range/Performance
s: The power input signal to the Transmission Control Module is outside the calibrated tolerance. Supply or wake-up signal drift.

t: TCM Power Input Signal Low
s: TCM main supply voltage falls below the operating threshold for an extended period, causing reset risk and unreliable shift control.

t: TCM Power Input Signal High
s: TCM main supply voltage exceeds the operating threshold, typically caused by a faulty voltage regulator on the alternator.

t: TCM Power Input Signal Intermittent
s: The TCM detects intermittent loss of main supply voltage above a threshold count. Often caused by loose connector or relay fault.

t: TCM Power Relay Control Circuit/Open
s: The TCM power relay reports a control circuit/open condition. TCM supply control is impaired.

t: TCM Power Relay Control Circuit Low
s: The TCM power relay reports a control circuit low condition. TCM supply control is impaired. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: TCM Power Relay Control Circuit High
s: The TCM power relay reports a control circuit high condition. TCM supply control is impaired. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: TCM Power Relay Sense Circuit

t: Transmission Fluid Filter Deteriorated
s: The TCM detects a deteriorated transmission fluid filter based on differential pressure or flow restriction. Filter change recommended.

t: Transmission Fluid Filter Very Deteriorated
s: The transmission fluid filter is critically restricted. Hydraulic flow is severely impaired - immediate filter change required to prevent transmission damage.

t: Reverse Inhibit Control Circuit Low
s: Voltage on the reverse inhibit control circuit is below the expected window. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Reverse Inhibit Control Circuit High
s: Voltage on the reverse inhibit control circuit is above the expected window. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Planetary Gear Set Failure
s: The TCM detects mechanical failure of a planetary gear set inside the transmission. Sun, ring, or planet gears may be damaged.

t: TCM Power Relay Sense Circuit Low
s: The TCM monitors the output side of its power relay; when the sense voltage is below threshold while relay is commanded closed, this code sets.

t: TCM Power Relay Sense Circuit High
s: The TCM detects voltage on the relay sense line above threshold while relay is commanded open, indicating relay welded closed or short to power.

t: TCM Power Relay Sense Circuit Intermittent
s: The sense feedback from the TCM power relay drops out intermittently. Loose connector or oxidised relay contact.

t: Multiple Gears Engaged
s: The TCM detects that more than one gear element is engaged simultaneously. Risk of transmission lock-up - severe internal fault.

t: Transmission Component Slipping
s: The TCM detects slipping in a clutch or band element. Output speed does not match the calculated value for the engaged gear ratio.

t: Shift Time Too Short
s: The transmission shift time is shorter than the calibrated minimum. Risk of harsh engagement and shock loads.

t: Shift Time Too Long
s: The transmission shift time exceeds the calibrated maximum. Possible hydraulic restriction, weak solenoid, or worn clutch.

t: Transmission Fluid Deteriorated
s: The TCM detects deteriorated transmission fluid based on temperature exposure history or sensor measurement. Fluid change recommended.

t: Transmission Control System MIL Request Circuit High
s: Voltage on the transmission control system MIL request circuit is above the expected window. Wire shorted to B+ or driver open.

t: Motor Electronics Coolant Temperature Sensor Circuit
s: The hybrid control module detects an out-of-range signal from the temperature sensor of the inverter/motor electronics coolant loop. This sensor protects power electronics from overheating.

t: Motor Electronics Coolant Temperature Sensor Range/Performance
s: The motor electronics coolant temperature sensor reading is implausible relative to other thermal models or coolant flow estimates.

t: Motor Electronics Coolant Temperature Sensor Low Input
s: Voltage at motor electronics coolant temperature sensor below threshold. The engine coolant temperature sensor feeds warm-up enrichment, fan control and closed-loop entry — a stuck-cold reading keeps the engine in open loop and rich forever.

t: Motor Electronics Coolant Temperature Sensor High Input
s: Voltage at motor electronics coolant temperature sensor above threshold. The engine coolant temperature sensor feeds warm-up enrichment, fan control and closed-loop entry — a stuck-cold reading keeps the engine in open loop and rich forever.

t: DC/DC Converter Status Circuit

t: High Voltage System Interlock Circuit
s: The hybrid system detects loss of high-voltage interlock continuity. The interlock loop is a safety feature that disables HV power if any service connector is opened.

t: High Voltage System Interlock Circuit A Performance

t: High Voltage System Interlock Circuit Low
s: The HV interlock signal is below threshold, indicating broken loop or short to ground. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: High Voltage System Interlock Circuit High
s: The HV interlock signal voltage is above threshold, indicating short to battery or unexpected continuity loss.

t: High Voltage System Interlock Circuit A Intermittent
s: The high-voltage system interlock circuit A reports a intermittent condition. Safety interlock chain may be compromised.

t: Hybrid Engine Failed to Start
s: The hybrid control system commanded an internal combustion engine start that did not complete within the expected time. Causes range from low 12V supply to fuel, ignition or starter issues.

t: Hybrid Battery Voltage Sense Circuit
s: The battery management system detects a fault in the high-voltage battery pack voltage sense circuit, used to monitor module-level voltages.

t: DC/DC Converter Enable Circuit/Open

t: DC/DC Converter Communication
s: The hybrid control module loses CAN bus communication with the DC/DC converter for longer than the fault threshold.

t: DC/DC Converter Enable Circuit High

t: Battery Energy Control Module
s: A generic fault is reported by the battery energy control module of a hybrid or electric vehicle. The module manages charge balancing, temperature and contactor logic for the high-voltage battery.

t: Motor Torque Sensor Circuit High

t: Drive Motor A Inverter Performance

t: Hybrid Battery Pack Deterioration

t: Hybrid Battery Pack State of Charge Low

t: Motor Electronics Over Temperature
