t: Hybrid Battery Pack Low Voltage
s: HV pack voltage falls below operating minimum during normal load, suggesting deeply discharged or damaged cell modules.

t: Hybrid Battery Pack Overtemperature
s: BMS detects HV pack temperature above the safe operating limit, triggering thermal protection mode and limiting charge/discharge.

t: Hybrid Battery Pack Deterioration
s: The hybrid battery pack capacity or internal resistance has degraded beyond the diagnostic threshold. Performance and fuel economy fall as the pack ages.

t: Replace Hybrid Battery Pack
s: The hybrid battery management system has determined that the battery pack must be replaced. Often set after persistent cell imbalance, capacity loss or repeated thermal events.

s: Voltage on cooling fan 1 control circuit is above threshold, indicating short to battery or open circuit.

t: 14 Volt Power Module Internal Temperature Too High
s: The 14V DC/DC converter module reports internal temperature above the protection threshold. Sustained load or cooling failure.

t: Hybrid/EV Battery System Voltage
s: The hybrid control module detects a generic fault on the HV battery system voltage. Cell-group voltage monitoring is the foundation of HV battery safety — drift here triggers contactor opening, charge inhibit, and reduced power to protect the pack.

t: Hybrid/EV Battery System Voltage Unstable
s: The HV battery system voltage fluctuates beyond the expected window. Loose connection or unstable cell behaviour.

t: Hybrid/EV Battery System Voltage Low
s: The HV battery system voltage is below the expected window. Cell-level depletion, cold conditions, or sense fault. Cell-group voltage monitoring is the foundation of HV battery safety — drift here triggers contactor opening, charge inhibit, and reduced power to protect the pack.

t: Hybrid/EV Battery System Voltage High
s: The HV battery system voltage exceeds the expected window. Charging overvoltage or sense fault. Cell-group voltage monitoring is the foundation of HV battery safety — drift here triggers contactor opening, charge inhibit, and reduced power to protect the pack.

t: Hybrid/EV Battery Pack Sensor Module
s: The HV battery pack sensor module reports an internal fault. Cell-level voltage and temperature monitoring may be impaired.

t: Hybrid/EV Battery Pack Temperature Too Low
s: The HV battery pack temperature is below the safe operating threshold. Power output severely reduced until heating completes.

t: Hybrid/EV Battery System Voltage Too Low for Voltage Step Up Conversion
s: The HV battery system voltage is too low to enable voltage step-up (boost) conversion. DC/DC converter cannot operate.

t: System Voltage Too Low for Voltage Step Down Conversion
s: The system input voltage is too low to enable voltage step-down (buck) conversion to 12V. 12V system support compromised.

t: Electric/Auxiliary Transmission Fluid Pump A Motor Supply Voltage Circuit Low
s: The supply voltage circuit of auxiliary transmission fluid pump A motor reports a low condition. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Electric/Auxiliary Transmission Fluid Pump A Motor Supply Voltage Circuit High

t: Electric/Auxiliary Transmission Fluid Pump Motor Control Module

t: Drive Motor A Current
s: The total current draw of drive motor A reports a current fault. Motor operation may be impaired. The drive motor's position, current and temperature are watched continuously; a fault here downgrades the inverter to a safe operating point or triggers torque reduction.

t: Drive Motor Coolant Pump Control Circuit

t: Drive Motor A Position Sensor Not Learned
s: The hybrid control module has not learned the rotor position offset for drive motor A. Phase commutation cannot be optimised until learning completes.

t: Drive Motor A Torque Delivered Performance
s: The hybrid control module detects that drive motor A torque output does not match the commanded value. Magnet weakness, phase imbalance, or feedback drift.

t: Drive Motor B Torque Delivered Performance
s: The hybrid control module detects that drive motor B torque output does not match the commanded value. Magnet weakness, phase imbalance, or feedback drift.

t: Electric/Auxiliary Transmission Fluid Pump Control Module Internal Temperature Too High
s: The auxiliary transmission fluid pump control module reports internal temperature above the protection threshold.

t: Electric/Auxiliary Transmission Fluid Pump Control Module Internal Temperature Sensor Circuit
s: The internal temperature sensor of the auxiliary transmission fluid pump control module reports a generic fault.

t: Electric/Auxiliary Transmission Fluid Pump Control Module Internal Temperature Sensor Circuit Range/Performance
s: The internal temperature sensor signal is outside the calibrated range. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Electric/Auxiliary Transmission Fluid Pump Control Module Internal Temperature Sensor Circuit Low
s: The internal temperature sensor reports voltage below the expected window. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Electric/Auxiliary Transmission Fluid Pump Control Module Internal Temperature Sensor Circuit High
s: The internal temperature sensor reports voltage above the expected window. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Hybrid/EV Battery Charging Voltage High
s: The HV battery charging voltage exceeds the safe upper limit. Risk of cell damage from overvoltage. Cell-group voltage monitoring is the foundation of HV battery safety — drift here triggers contactor opening, charge inhibit, and reduced power to protect the pack.

t: Battery Charger Coupler Lock Control Circuit/Open

t: Electric/Auxiliary Transmission Fluid Pump A Motor Over Temperature

t: Charge Status Indicator B Control Circuit/Open
s: The charge status indicator B control circuit is open. Indicator cannot illuminate to show charging state.

t: Hybrid/EV Battery Pack Cell Voltage Low
s: The BMS detects at least one cell with voltage below the safe lower limit. Risk of cell damage from deep discharge.

t: Hybrid/EV Battery Pack Cell Voltage High
s: The BMS detects at least one cell with voltage above the safe upper limit. Risk of cell damage from overcharge.

t: Hybrid/EV Battery Pack Coolant Control Valve C Control Circuit/Open

s: The HV battery pack coolant control valve C reports a stuck on condition.

t: Hybrid/EV Battery Pack Coolant Control Valve C Control Circuit Low
s: The HV battery pack coolant control valve C reports a control circuit low condition. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Generator Position Exceeding Learn Limit

t: Hybrid/EV Battery Pack E Voltage Sensor Circuit
s: The ECM detects a circuit fault on the hybrid/ev battery pack e voltage sensor circuit. Reading cannot be used reliably for control.

t: Hybrid/EV Battery Pack E Voltage Sensor Circuit Range/Performance
s: The ECM detects a range/performance fault on the hybrid/ev battery pack e voltage sensor circuit. Reading cannot be used reliably for control.

t: Hybrid/EV Battery Pack E Voltage Sensor Circuit Low
s: The ECM detects a low fault on the hybrid/ev battery pack e voltage sensor circuit. Reading cannot be used reliably for control.

t: Hybrid/EV Battery Pack E Voltage Sensor Circuit High
s: The ECM detects a high fault on the hybrid/ev battery pack e voltage sensor circuit. Reading cannot be used reliably for control.

t: Hybrid/EV Battery Pack E Voltage Sensor Circuit Intermittent/Erratic
s: The ECM detects a intermittent/erratic fault on the hybrid/ev battery pack e voltage sensor circuit. Reading cannot be used reliably for control.

t: Hybrid/EV Battery Pack F Voltage Sensor Circuit
s: The ECM detects a circuit fault on the hybrid/ev battery pack f voltage sensor circuit. Reading cannot be used reliably for control.

t: Hybrid/EV Battery Pack F Voltage Sensor Circuit Range/Performance
s: The ECM detects a range/performance fault on the hybrid/ev battery pack f voltage sensor circuit. Reading cannot be used reliably for control.

t: Hybrid/EV Battery Pack F Voltage Sensor Circuit Low
s: The ECM detects a low fault on the hybrid/ev battery pack f voltage sensor circuit. Reading cannot be used reliably for control.

t: Hybrid/EV Battery Pack F Voltage Sensor Circuit High
s: The ECM detects a high fault on the hybrid/ev battery pack f voltage sensor circuit. Reading cannot be used reliably for control.

t: Hybrid/EV Battery Pack F Voltage Sensor Circuit Intermittent/Erratic
s: The ECM detects a intermittent/erratic fault on the hybrid/ev battery pack f voltage sensor circuit. Reading cannot be used reliably for control.

t: Generator Phase X Current Sensor Circuit
s: The secondary generator phase X current sensor circuit reports a fault.

t: Generator Phase Y Current Sensor Circuit
s: The secondary generator phase Y current sensor circuit reports a fault.

t: Generator Phase Z Current Sensor Circuit
s: The secondary generator phase Z current sensor circuit reports a fault.

t: Hybrid/EV Battery Pack B Deterioration
s: The BMS detects deterioration in HV battery pack section B. Capacity or internal resistance outside service limits.

t: Hybrid/EV Battery Pack C Deterioration
s: The BMS detects deterioration in HV battery pack section C. Capacity or internal resistance outside service limits.

t: Hybrid/EV Battery Pack D Deterioration
s: The BMS detects deterioration in HV battery pack section D. Capacity or internal resistance outside service limits.

t: Hybrid/EV Battery Pack E Deterioration
s: The BMS detects deterioration in HV battery pack section E. Capacity or internal resistance outside service limits.

t: Hybrid/EV Battery Pack F Deterioration
s: The BMS detects deterioration in HV battery pack section F. Capacity or internal resistance outside service limits.

t: A/C Refrigerant Distribution Valve B Control Circuit/Open
s: The A/C refrigerant distribution valve B reports a control circuit/open condition.

t: A/C Refrigerant Distribution Valve B Control Circuit Low
s: The A/C refrigerant distribution valve B reports a control circuit low condition. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: A/C Refrigerant Distribution Valve B Control Circuit High
s: The A/C refrigerant distribution valve B reports a control circuit high condition. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: A/C Refrigerant Distribution Valve B Performance
s: The A/C refrigerant distribution valve B reports a performance condition.

t: Battery Charger Coupler Temperature Sensor B Circuit
s: The ECM detects a circuit fault on the battery charger coupler temperature sensor b circuit. Reading cannot be used reliably for control.

t: Battery Charger Coupler Temperature Sensor B Circuit Range/Performance
s: The ECM detects a range/performance fault on the battery charger coupler temperature sensor b circuit. Reading cannot be used reliably for control.

t: Battery Charger Coupler Temperature Sensor B Circuit Low
s: The ECM detects a low fault on the battery charger coupler temperature sensor b circuit. Reading cannot be used reliably for control.

t: Battery Charger Coupler Temperature Sensor B Circuit High
s: The ECM detects a high fault on the battery charger coupler temperature sensor b circuit. Reading cannot be used reliably for control.

t: Battery Charger Coupler Temperature Sensor B Circuit Intermittent/Erratic
s: The ECM detects a intermittent/erratic fault on the battery charger coupler temperature sensor b circuit. Reading cannot be used reliably for control.

t: Battery Energy Control Module B
s: The battery energy control module B reports a generic internal fault.

t: Hybrid/EV Battery Discharge Circuit Performance
s: The HV battery discharge circuit performance is outside calibration. Discharge resistor or contactor fault.

t: Battery Charger Coupler Temperature B Too High
s: The coupler B temperature exceeds the safety threshold during charging. The on-board charger negotiates voltage, current and safety with the EVSE; a fault interrupts charging or limits it to AC slow-charge despite a DC fast charger being attached.

t: Drive Motor A Control Module Overtemperature
s: The drive motor A control module reports overtemperature. Cooling insufficient or load excessive. The drive motor's position, current and temperature are watched continuously; a fault here downgrades the inverter to a safe operating point or triggers torque reduction.

t: Battery Charger Coupler Unlock Disabled
s: The battery charger coupler unlock is disabled - safety condition prevents unlocking. HV active or charging in progress.

t: Drive Motor C Temperature Sensor Circuit
s: The ECM detects a circuit fault on the drive motor c temperature sensor circuit. Reading cannot be used reliably for control.

t: Drive Motor C Temperature Sensor Circuit Range/Performance
s: The ECM detects a range/performance fault on the drive motor c temperature sensor circuit. Reading cannot be used reliably for control.

t: Drive Motor C Temperature Sensor Circuit Low
s: The ECM detects a low fault on the drive motor c temperature sensor circuit. Reading cannot be used reliably for control.

t: Drive Motor C Temperature Sensor Circuit High
s: The ECM detects a high fault on the drive motor c temperature sensor circuit. Reading cannot be used reliably for control.

t: Drive Motor C Temperature Sensor Circuit Intermittent/Erratic
s: The ECM detects a intermittent/erratic fault on the drive motor c temperature sensor circuit. Reading cannot be used reliably for control.

t: Battery Charger A Input Voltage Sensor B Circuit
s: The ECM detects a circuit fault on the battery charger a input voltage sensor b circuit. Reading cannot be used reliably for control.

t: Battery Charger A Input Voltage Sensor B Circuit Range/Performance
s: The ECM detects a range/performance fault on the battery charger a input voltage sensor b circuit. Reading cannot be used reliably for control.

t: Battery Charger A Input Voltage Sensor B Circuit Low
s: The ECM detects a low fault on the battery charger a input voltage sensor b circuit. Reading cannot be used reliably for control.

t: Battery Charger A Input Voltage Sensor B Circuit High
s: The ECM detects a high fault on the battery charger a input voltage sensor b circuit. Reading cannot be used reliably for control.

t: Battery Charger A Input Voltage Sensor B Circuit Intermittent/Erratic
s: The ECM detects a intermittent/erratic fault on the battery charger a input voltage sensor b circuit. Reading cannot be used reliably for control.

t: Battery Charger A Input Voltage Sensor C Circuit
s: The ECM detects a circuit fault on the battery charger a input voltage sensor c circuit. Reading cannot be used reliably for control.

t: Battery Charger A Input Voltage Sensor C Circuit Range/Performance
s: The ECM detects a range/performance fault on the battery charger a input voltage sensor c circuit. Reading cannot be used reliably for control.

t: Battery Charger A Input Voltage Sensor C Circuit Low
s: The ECM detects a low fault on the battery charger a input voltage sensor c circuit. Reading cannot be used reliably for control.

t: Reserved Powertrain Fault
s: Manufacturer-specific reserved code in the SAE generic range used as catch-all for hybrid system faults that do not map to a more specific defined code. Should be diagnosed using the freeze frame data and any additional manufacturer codes.

t: NOx Adsorber Efficiency Below Threshold (Bank 1)
s: The lean NOx trap (LNT) on bank 1 is no longer storing or releasing NOx within the required efficiency window. Detected by the downstream NOx sensor or model-based monitor — typically caused by sulphur poisoning, thermal aging, or a failed regeneration strategy.

t: NOx Adsorber Efficiency Below Threshold (Bank 2)
s: The lean NOx trap (LNT) on bank 2 is no longer storing or releasing NOx within the required efficiency window. Detected by the downstream NOx sensor or model-based monitor — typically caused by sulphur poisoning, thermal aging, or a failed regeneration strategy.

t: Diesel Particulate Filter Efficiency Below Threshold (Bank 1)
s: The differential pressure sensor or temperature monitor across the diesel particulate filter (DPF) reports that filtration efficiency has dropped below the legal threshold. Common after a damaged filter substrate, blocked passages from failed regenerations, or a leak around the housing.

t: Diesel Particulate Filter Efficiency Below Threshold (Bank 2)
s: The differential pressure sensor or temperature monitor across the diesel particulate filter (DPF) reports that filtration efficiency has dropped below the legal threshold. Common after a damaged filter substrate, blocked passages from failed regenerations, or a leak around the housing.

t: Intake Manifold Runner Control Stuck Open (Bank 1)
s: The intake manifold runner control (IMRC) flap on bank 1 is stuck in the open position. The IMRC swirls or tumbles intake charge at low rpm by closing one runner per cylinder; when stuck open, low-end torque drops and the engine may misfire on cold starts.

t: Intake Manifold Runner Control Stuck Open (Bank 2)
s: The intake manifold runner control (IMRC) flap on bank 2 is stuck in the open position. The IMRC swirls or tumbles intake charge at low rpm by closing one runner per cylinder; when stuck open, low-end torque drops and the engine may misfire on cold starts.

t: Intake Manifold Runner Control Stuck Closed (Bank 1)
s: The IMRC flap on bank 1 is stuck in the closed position and cannot open at higher rpm. Top-end power suffers because the runner is restricted, and the engine may breathe poorly above mid-range.

t: Intake Manifold Runner Control Stuck Closed (Bank 2)
s: Bank 2 counterpart to P2006. The IMRC flap on bank 2 cannot return to the open position and chokes intake flow at higher rpm.

t: Intake Manifold Runner Control Solenoid Circuit Open (Bank 1)
s: The ECU detects an open circuit on the IMRC solenoid for bank 1. No control current flows when the runner is commanded to switch, leaving the flap in its default position.

t: Intake Manifold Runner Control Solenoid Circuit Low (Bank 1)
s: The IMRC solenoid drive line for bank 1 reads below the lower threshold — short to ground or a failed driver stage. The runner cannot be switched on demand.

t: Intake Manifold Runner Performance (Bank 1)
s: The intake manifold runner control (IMRC) on bank 1 reports that flap movement does not match the commanded position within the allowed time window — either too slow, too short a stroke, or position feedback off-target.

t: Intake Manifold Runner Performance (Bank 2)
s: The intake manifold runner control (IMRC) on bank 2 reports that flap movement does not match the commanded position within the allowed time window — either too slow, too short a stroke, or position feedback off-target.

t: Particulate Filter Over Temperature (Bank 1)
s: A temperature sensor at the diesel particulate filter (DPF) on bank 1 has exceeded the safe limit — typically during a runaway regeneration. Continued operation can melt or crack the substrate.

t: Particulate Filter Over Temperature (Bank 2)
s: A temperature sensor at the diesel particulate filter (DPF) on bank 2 has exceeded the safe limit — typically during a runaway regeneration. Continued operation can melt or crack the substrate.

t: Catalyst System Over Temperature (Bank 1)
s: A temperature sensor in the exhaust catalyst on bank 1 reports a value above the safe operating limit. Sustained over-temperature can sinter the precious-metal washcoat and destroy conversion efficiency.

t: Catalyst System Over Temperature (Bank 2)
s: A temperature sensor in the exhaust catalyst on bank 2 reports a value above the safe operating limit. Sustained over-temperature can sinter the precious-metal washcoat and destroy conversion efficiency.

t: Intake Manifold Runner Control Solenoid Circuit High (Bank 1)
s: Drive voltage at the IMRC solenoid on bank 1 is above the upper limit — short to supply or an open ground side. The solenoid is energised constantly or the driver protection trips.

t: Intake Manifold Runner Control Circuit/Open (Bank 2)
s: The IMRC actuator drive circuit on bank 2 is open or has lost continuity — control module no longer detects a closing path through the actuator winding.

t: Intake Manifold Runner Control Circuit Low (Bank 2)
s: The IMRC actuator drive line on bank 2 reads below the expected voltage threshold — typically a short to ground or a stuck-low driver inside the control module.

t: Intake Manifold Runner Control Circuit High (Bank 2)
s: The IMRC actuator drive line on bank 2 reads above the expected voltage threshold — typically a short to battery voltage or an open ground side.

t: Intake Manifold Runner Position Sensor/Switch Circuit (Bank 1)
s: The position feedback signal of the IMRC flap on bank 1 is missing or implausible. Without feedback, the ECU cannot verify that the commanded runner position has been reached.

t: Intake Manifold Runner Position Sensor/Switch Range/Performance (Bank 1)
s: The IMRC position sensor on bank 1 reports a value, but it does not match the commanded flap state — for example, the flap is commanded closed yet the sensor still reads open. Common on engines where the connector pin clip wears out and the sensor reads erratic positions.

t: Intake Manifold Runner Position Sensor/Switch Circuit Low (Bank 1)
s: The IMRC position sensor signal on bank 1 reads below the lower limit. Usually a short to ground on the signal wire or a failed sensor.

t: Intake Manifold Runner Position Sensor/Switch Circuit High (Bank 1)
s: The IMRC position sensor signal on bank 1 reads above the upper limit. Typically a short to supply, an open ground reference, or a sensor that failed high.

t: Intake Manifold Runner Position Sensor/Switch Circuit Open (Bank 2)
s: The ECU detects an open circuit on the IMRC position sensor on bank 2. Without position feedback the ECU cannot confirm runner switching and falls back to a default mode.

t: Intake Manifold Runner Position Sensor/Switch Range/Performance (Bank 2)
s: The IMRC position sensor on bank 2 produces an electrically valid signal that does not match the commanded runner state — typically a sensor drift or sticking flap.

t: Reductant Injection Valve Circuit Range/Performance (Bank 2 Unit 1)
s: The DEF (urea) injection valve on bank 2, unit 1 does not respond to commands within the expected range — flow or response time off-spec, no obvious electrical fault.

t: Intake Manifold Runner Control Actuator Supply Voltage Low (Bank 1)
s: The supply voltage feeding the IMRC actuator on bank 1 is below the lower limit — typically a fuse, relay, or harness fault on the supply rail.

t: Intake Manifold Runner Control Actuator Supply Voltage Low (Bank 2)
s: The supply voltage feeding the IMRC actuator on bank 2 is below the lower limit — typically a fuse, relay, or harness fault on the supply rail.

t: Intake Manifold Runner Control Actuator Internal Performance (Bank 1)
s: Internal self-diagnostics of the IMRC actuator on bank 1 report an off-spec condition — driver stage, position-feedback or thermal limit no longer matches the calibrated profile.

t: Intake Manifold Runner Control Actuator Internal Performance (Bank 2)
s: Internal self-diagnostics of the IMRC actuator on bank 2 report an off-spec condition — driver stage, position-feedback or thermal limit no longer matches the calibrated profile.

t: Reductant Pump A Stuck On
s: The DEF (urea) pump A keeps running when commanded off, or its drive current does not drop to zero. Continuous pumping over-pressurises the dosing line and can crystallise DEF at the injector.

t: Intake Manifold Runner Position Sensor/Switch Circuit Low (Bank 2)
s: The IMRC position sensor on bank 2 reports a voltage below the lower threshold — typically a short to ground or a sensor with very low resistance.

t: Intake Manifold Runner Position Sensor/Switch Circuit High (Bank 2)
s: The IMRC position sensor on bank 2 reports a voltage above the upper threshold — usually a short to positive or an open ground.

t: Intake Manifold Runner Position Sensor/Switch Circuit High (Bank 2)
s: The IMRC position sensor on bank 2 reports a signal above the upper plausibility limit — typically an open signal line, broken sensor, or short to B+.

t: Intake Manifold Runner Position Sensor/Switch Circuit Intermittent (Bank 2)
s: The IMRC position sensor signal on bank 2 jumps or drops out intermittently — usually a loose pin, partially-broken conductor, or moisture in the connector.

t: EVAP Fuel Vapor Temperature Sensor Circuit
s: A general fault is reported on the EVAP fuel-vapor temperature sensor circuit — open, shorted, or out of plausible range. The sensor is part of the evaporative-emission system used to monitor canister loading.

t: EVAP Fuel Vapor Temperature Sensor Performance
s: The EVAP fuel-vapor temperature sensor reading is plausible but does not match expected behaviour over time or temperature — slow response, incorrect offset, or stuck reading.

t: EVAP Fuel Vapor Temperature Sensor Circuit Low Voltage
s: The EVAP fuel-vapor temperature sensor signal sits below the lower plausibility limit — short to ground or short between signal and ground inside the sensor.

t: EVAP Fuel Vapor Temperature Sensor Circuit High Voltage
s: The EVAP fuel-vapor temperature sensor signal sits above the upper plausibility limit — open signal line, broken sensor, or short to B+.

t: EVAP Fuel Vapor Temperature Sensor Circuit Intermittent
s: The EVAP fuel-vapor temperature sensor signal jumps or drops out intermittently — usually a connector or chafed wire.

t: Fuel Fired Heater Disabled
s: The fuel-fired auxiliary heater (used to warm coolant or DEF on cold start) has been disabled by the control module — typically because of repeated start failures, low-voltage supply, or a self-protection condition.

t: Reductant Tank Heater Control Circuit/Open
s: The DEF tank heater control circuit is open or has lost continuity — the PCM cannot drive the resistive heater that prevents the DEF (urea) tank from freezing in winter.

t: Reductant Tank Heater Control Circuit Low
s: The DEF tank heater control line reads below the expected voltage — short to ground or stuck-low driver.

t: Reductant Tank Heater Control Circuit High
s: The DEF tank heater control line reads above the expected voltage — short to B+ or open ground. The sensor heater warms the element to operating temperature (~600 °C); without it the lambda sensor stays inactive in closed loop and the ECM cannot trim mixture.

t: Reductant Leakage
s: A leak has been detected in the DEF (urea) supply system — usually by a pressure-decay test after pump shutdown, or by visible crystallisation around fittings.

t: Reductant Injection Valve Circuit Range/Performance (Bank 1 Unit 1)
s: The DEF (urea) injection valve on bank 2, unit 1 does not respond to commands within the expected range — flow or response time off-spec, no obvious electrical fault.

t: Reductant/Regeneration Supply Control Circuit Range/Performance
s: The control line that gates the DEF supply pump or the regeneration heating circuit does not respond as commanded — duty cycle, current draw, or response time off-spec.

t: Fuel Fired Heater Performance
s: The fuel-fired auxiliary heater is operating but not delivering the expected heat output within the calibrated time — flame sensor reports weak combustion, exhaust temperature does not rise, or coolant heating rate is below threshold.

t: Exhaust Gas Temperature Sensor Circuit (Bank 1 Sensor 2)
s: The exhaust gas temperature (EGT) sensor at position 2 on bank 1 — typically downstream of the catalyst or before the DPF — reports an implausible signal. EGT sensors protect the turbocharger and DPF from over-temperature and time the regeneration cycle.

t: Exhaust Gas Temperature Sensor Circuit Low (Bank 1 Sensor 2)
s: The EGT sensor at bank 1 position 2 reads below the lower limit. Common when the signal is shorted to ground or the sensor element is open.

t: Exhaust Gas Temperature Sensor Circuit High (Bank 1 Sensor 2)
s: The EGT sensor at bank 1 position 2 reads above the upper limit. Usually a short to supply, an open ground or a sensor that drifted high — risk of false DPF over-temperature limp.

t: Exhaust Gas Temperature Sensor Circuit (Bank 2 Sensor 2)
s: A general electrical fault is reported on the exhaust gas temperature (EGT) sensor at bank 2 sensor 2 — open, short, or out of plausibility. EGT sensors monitor catalyst, DPF, and turbocharger inlet/outlet temperatures.

t: Exhaust Gas Temperature Sensor Circuit Low (Bank 2 Sensor 2)
s: The EGT sensor on bank 2 sensor 2 reads below the lower plausibility limit — short to ground or shorted sensor element.

t: Exhaust Gas Temperature Sensor Circuit High (Bank 2 Sensor 2)
s: The EGT sensor on bank 2 sensor 2 reads above the upper plausibility limit — open signal or short to B+.

t: Reductant Injection Air Pressure Sensor A Circuit
s: A general electrical fault is reported on reductant injection air-pressure sensor A — used on air-assisted DEF dosing systems to monitor the atomisation air pressure delivered to the injector.

t: Reductant Injection Air Pressure Sensor A Circuit Range/Performance
s: The reductant injection air-pressure sensor A reads inside the electrical range but does not match expected behaviour over time or load — slow response or wrong offset.

t: Reductant Injection Air Pressure Sensor A Circuit Low
s: The reductant injection air-pressure sensor A reads below its lower plausibility limit — short to ground or sensor internal short.

t: Reductant Level Sensor A Circuit
s: A general electrical fault is reported on DEF level sensor A — open, shorted, or out of plausibility. Some tanks use multiple level senders to cover irregular tank shapes; this is the primary one.

t: Reductant Level Sensor A Circuit Range/Performance
s: DEF level sensor A reading is in electrical range but plausibility check fails — value sticks, jumps, or does not match consumption.

t: Reductant Level Sensor A Circuit Low
s: DEF level sensor A reads below lower plausibility — short to ground. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Reductant Level Sensor A Circuit High
s: DEF level sensor A reads above upper plausibility — open signal or short to B+. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Reductant Level Sensor A Circuit Intermittent/Erratic
s: DEF level sensor A signal jumps or drops out intermittently — typically a corroded sender contact or wiring issue.

t: Reductant Level Too Low
s: The DEF tank level has dropped below the warning threshold — driver action required to refill before the inducement strategy escalates to limp mode or no-restart.

t: Reductant Injection Air Pressure Sensor A Circuit High
s: The reductant injection air-pressure sensor A reads above its upper plausibility limit — open signal line or short to B+.

t: Reductant Injection Air Pressure Sensor A Circuit Intermittent
s: The reductant injection air-pressure sensor A signal jumps or drops out intermittently — connector or harness issue.

t: Reductant Temperature Sensor A Circuit
s: A general electrical fault is reported on reductant (DEF) temperature sensor A — used to compensate dosing for fluid viscosity and to enable tank heating in winter.

t: Reductant Temperature Sensor A Circuit Range/Performance
s: Reductant temperature sensor A reading is within electrical range but plausibility check fails — slow response or sticks at one value through a tank-warming cycle.

t: Reductant Temperature Sensor A Circuit Low
s: Reductant temperature sensor A reads below its lower plausibility limit — short to ground. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Reductant Temperature Sensor A Circuit High
s: Reductant temperature sensor A reads above its upper plausibility limit — open signal or short to B+.

t: Reductant Temperature Sensor A Circuit Intermittent
s: Reductant temperature sensor A signal jumps or drops out intermittently — connector or harness fault.

t: Reductant Injection Valve Circuit/Open (Bank 1 Unit 1)
s: The DEF (urea) injection valve drive circuit at bank 1 unit 1 is open — no continuity through the solenoid winding or harness.

t: Reductant Injection Valve Circuit Low (Bank 1 Unit 1)
s: The DEF injection valve drive line at bank 1 unit 1 reads below the expected voltage — short to ground or stuck-low driver.

t: Reductant Injection Valve Circuit High (Bank 1 Unit 1)
s: The DEF injection valve drive line at bank 1 unit 1 reads above the expected voltage — short to B+ or open ground.

t: Reductant Pressure Sensor Circuit
s: A general electrical fault is reported on the DEF system pressure sensor — used to verify pump priming, line pressure, and to detect leaks via pressure-decay tests.

t: Reductant Pressure Sensor Circuit Range/Performance
s: DEF pressure sensor reads within electrical range but plausibility fails — does not rise during prime, drift over a drive cycle, or stuck at a constant value.

t: Reductant Pressure Sensor Circuit Low
s: DEF pressure sensor reads below the lower plausibility limit — short to ground or sensor element shorted.

t: Reductant Pressure Sensor Circuit High
s: DEF pressure sensor reads above the upper plausibility limit — open signal or short to B+. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Reductant Pressure Sensor Circuit Intermittent/Erratic
s: DEF pressure sensor signal is unstable or jumps — usually a connector or chafed wire. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Reductant System Performance
s: The SCR (selective catalytic reduction) system using AdBlue/DEF is not delivering the expected NOx conversion. Causes range from poor reductant quality, dosing module faults, heater failure on the AdBlue tank in cold weather, or a degraded SCR catalyst.

t: Reductant Injection Valve Circuit/Open (Bank 2 Unit 1)
s: The DEF (urea) injection valve drive circuit at bank 2 unit 1 is open — no continuity through the solenoid winding or harness.

t: Reductant Injection Valve Circuit Low (Bank 2 Unit 1)
s: The DEF injection valve drive line at bank 2 unit 1 reads below the expected voltage — short to ground or stuck-low driver.

t: Reductant Injection Valve Circuit High (Bank 2 Unit 1)
s: The DEF injection valve drive line at bank 2 unit 1 reads above the expected voltage — short to B+ or open ground.

t: Reductant Injection Valve Circuit/Open (Bank 1 Unit 2)
s: The DEF (urea) injection valve drive circuit at bank 1 unit 1 is open — no continuity through the solenoid winding or harness.

t: Reductant Injection Valve Circuit Low (Bank 1 Unit 2)
s: The DEF injection valve drive line at bank 1 unit 1 reads below the expected voltage — short to ground or stuck-low driver.

t: Reductant Injection Valve Circuit High (Bank 1 Unit 2)
s: The DEF injection valve drive line at bank 1 unit 1 reads above the expected voltage — short to B+ or open ground.

t: Reductant Injection Valve Circuit/Open (Bank 2 Unit 2)
s: The DEF (urea) injection valve drive circuit at bank 2 unit 1 is open — no continuity through the solenoid winding or harness.

t: Reductant Injection Valve Circuit Low (Bank 2 Unit 2)
s: The DEF injection valve drive line at bank 2 unit 1 reads below the expected voltage — short to ground or stuck-low driver.

t: Reductant Injection Valve Circuit High (Bank 2 Unit 2)
s: The DEF injection valve drive line at bank 2 unit 1 reads above the expected voltage — short to B+ or open ground.

t: Reductant Injection Air Pump Control Circuit/Open
s: The control circuit driving the air-assist pump for DEF injection is open — atomisation air will not be available, dosing accuracy degrades.

t: Reductant Tank Temperature Sensor A Circuit
s: A general electrical fault is reported on DEF tank temperature sensor A — separate from the metering-unit temperature sensor and used to gate tank-heating.

t: Reductant Tank Temperature Sensor A Circuit Range/Performance
s: DEF tank temperature sensor A reads within electrical range but plausibility fails — sensor sticks during a heating cycle.

t: Reductant Tank Temperature Sensor A Circuit Low
s: DEF tank temperature sensor A reads below lower plausibility — short to ground. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Reductant Tank Temperature Sensor A Circuit High
s: DEF tank temperature sensor A reads above upper plausibility — open signal or short to B+. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Reductant Tank Temperature Sensor A Circuit Intermittent/Erratic
s: DEF tank temperature sensor A signal jumps or drops out intermittently — connector or harness issue.

t: Reductant System Performance (Bank 2)
s: The DEF system as a whole on bank 2 is not delivering the required NOx reduction — NOx-out remains too high relative to commanded dosing across a drive cycle. Causes range from poor DEF quality, restricted dosing line, weak pump, to a degraded SCR catalyst.

t: Reductant Injection Air Pump Control Circuit Low
s: The control circuit driving the air-assist pump for DEF injection is open — atomisation air will not be available, dosing accuracy degrades.

t: Reductant Injection Air Pump Control Circuit High
s: The control circuit driving the air-assist pump for DEF injection is open — atomisation air will not be available, dosing accuracy degrades.

t: Reductant/Regeneration Supply Control Circuit/Open
s: The control line that energises the DEF supply pump or regeneration heating circuit is open — no continuity through the load or harness.

t: Reductant/Regeneration Supply Control Circuit Low
s: Reductant/regeneration supply control line stuck low — short to ground or stuck-low driver. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Reductant/Regeneration Supply Control Circuit High
s: Reductant/regeneration supply control line stuck high — short to B+ or open ground. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Fuel Level Sensor B Circuit
s: A general electrical fault is reported on the secondary fuel-level sensor (B) — vehicles with saddle tanks or auxiliary tanks use a second sender to cover the full tank volume.

t: Fuel Level Sensor B Performance
s: Secondary fuel-level sensor B reads within range but plausibility check fails — value sticks, jumps, or does not match consumption.

t: Fuel Level Sensor B Circuit Low
s: Fuel-level sensor B reads below lower plausibility — short to ground. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Fuel Level Sensor B Circuit High
s: Fuel-level sensor B reads above upper plausibility — open signal or short to B+. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Fuel Level Sensor B Circuit Intermittent
s: Fuel-level sensor B signal jumps or drops out intermittently — usually a corroded sender or wiring. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Reductant Quality Sensor Circuit
s: A general electrical fault is reported on the DEF (urea) quality sensor — measures urea concentration via ultrasonic or refractive method to detect water dilution or wrong fluid.

t: Reductant Quality Sensor Circuit Range/Performance
s: DEF quality sensor reads in range but reports an off-spec urea concentration — typically diluted DEF or non-DEF fluid in the tank.

t: Reductant Quality Sensor Circuit Low
s: DEF quality sensor reads below lower plausibility — short to ground. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Reductant Quality Sensor Circuit High
s: DEF quality sensor reads above upper plausibility — open signal or short to B+. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Intake Manifold Tuning (IMT) Valve Stuck Open (Bank 2)
s: The intake-manifold tuning (IMT) valve on bank 2 is mechanically stuck open — long-runner mode is unavailable, breathing characteristic is wrong at low rpm.

t: Intake Manifold Tuning (IMT) Valve Stuck Closed (Bank 2)
s: The IMT valve on bank 2 is mechanically stuck closed — short-runner mode is unavailable, top-end power suffers.

t: Intake Manifold Tuning (IMT) Valve Stuck Open (Bank 1)
s: The intake-manifold tuning (IMT) valve on bank 2 is mechanically stuck open — long-runner mode is unavailable, breathing characteristic is wrong at low rpm.

t: Intake Manifold Tuning (IMT) Valve Stuck Closed (Bank 1)
s: The IMT valve on bank 2 is mechanically stuck closed — short-runner mode is unavailable, top-end power suffers.

t: Throttle Actuator Control System - Ice Blockage (Bank 1)
s: The electronic throttle on bank 1 is jammed by ice — typical at very low ambient temperatures with high humidity, after a short trip that did not warm the throttle housing. The strategy attempts a self-clearing motion before setting the code.

t: Manifold Absolute Pressure / Mass Air Flow - Throttle Position Correlation at Idle
s: At idle, the relationship between MAP/MAF and throttle position does not match the calibrated map — typically a vacuum leak, dirty MAF, sticking throttle plate, or sensor drift.

t: Manifold Absolute Pressure / Mass Air Flow - Throttle Position Correlation at Higher Load
s: At higher load, the relationship between MAP/MAF and throttle position does not match the calibrated map — typically a charge-air leak, restricted intake, or wide-open MAF underread.

t: Intake Manifold Tuning (IMT) Valve Position Sensor/Switch Circuit (Bank 1)
s: A general electrical fault is reported on the IMT valve position sensor at bank 1 — open, short, or out of plausibility.

t: Intake Manifold Tuning (IMT) Valve Position Sensor/Switch Circuit Range/Performance (Bank 1)
s: IMT valve position sensor at bank 1 reads in range but plausibility check fails — value sticks or does not match commanded position.

t: Intake Manifold Tuning (IMT) Valve Position Sensor/Switch Circuit Low (Bank 1)
s: IMT position sensor at bank 1 reads below lower plausibility — short to ground. The intake manifold runner control (IMRC) flaps tune intake geometry for filling efficiency across the rpm range; stuck flaps cost part-load torque and can set lean codes.

t: Intake Manifold Tuning (IMT) Valve Position Sensor/Switch Circuit High (Bank 1)
s: IMT position sensor at bank 1 reads above upper plausibility — open signal or short to B+. The intake manifold runner control (IMRC) flaps tune intake geometry for filling efficiency across the rpm range; stuck flaps cost part-load torque and can set lean codes.

t: Intake Manifold Tuning (IMT) Valve Position Sensor/Switch Circuit Intermittent (Bank 1)
s: IMT position sensor at bank 1 signal is unstable — connector or harness issue. The intake manifold runner control (IMRC) flaps tune intake geometry for filling efficiency across the rpm range; stuck flaps cost part-load torque and can set lean codes.

t: Intake Manifold Tuning (IMT) Valve Position Sensor/Switch Circuit (Bank 2)
s: A general electrical fault is reported on the IMT valve position sensor at bank 2 — open, short, or out of plausibility.

t: Intake Manifold Tuning (IMT) Valve Position Sensor/Switch Circuit Range/Performance (Bank 2)
s: IMT valve position sensor at bank 2 reads in range but plausibility check fails — value sticks or does not match commanded position.

t: Intake Manifold Tuning (IMT) Valve Position Sensor/Switch Circuit Low (Bank 2)
s: IMT position sensor at bank 2 reads below lower plausibility — short to ground. The intake manifold runner control (IMRC) flaps tune intake geometry for filling efficiency across the rpm range; stuck flaps cost part-load torque and can set lean codes.

t: Intake Manifold Tuning (IMT) Valve Position Sensor/Switch Circuit High (Bank 2)
s: IMT position sensor at bank 2 reads above upper plausibility — open signal or short to B+. The intake manifold runner control (IMRC) flaps tune intake geometry for filling efficiency across the rpm range; stuck flaps cost part-load torque and can set lean codes.

t: Intake Manifold Tuning (IMT) Valve Position Sensor/Switch Circuit Intermittent (Bank 2)
s: IMT position sensor at bank 2 signal is unstable — connector or harness issue. The intake manifold runner control (IMRC) flaps tune intake geometry for filling efficiency across the rpm range; stuck flaps cost part-load torque and can set lean codes.

t: Reductant Quality Performance
s: The ECU has concluded that the AdBlue/DEF in the tank does not meet specification. Diagnosed by comparing expected NOx reduction against the dosed reductant volume — too much dosing for too little NOx drop indicates diluted or wrong fluid.

t: Exhaust Gas Temperature Sensor Range/Performance (Bank 1 Sensor 1)
s: The pre-catalyst EGT sensor on bank 1 returns a value that does not match the expected temperature curve under load. Often slow response time after long service life or a contaminated tip.

t: Exhaust Gas Temperature Sensor Circuit Intermittent (Bank 1 Sensor 1)
s: The EGT sensor at bank 1 sensor 1 signal jumps or drops out intermittently — typically a connector or chafed wire near the exhaust manifold heat zone.

t: Exhaust Gas Temperature Sensor Circuit Range/Performance (Bank 2 Sensor 1)
s: The EGT sensor at bank 2 sensor 1 reads in range but plausibility check fails — slow response, stuck reading, or value does not track other EGT sensors during a regeneration.

t: Exhaust Gas Temperature Sensor Circuit Intermittent (Bank 2 Sensor 1)
s: The EGT sensor at bank 2 sensor 1 signal jumps or drops out intermittently — typically a connector or chafed wire near the exhaust manifold heat zone.

t: Exhaust Gas Temperature Sensor Range/Performance (Bank 1 Sensor 2)
s: The bank 1 sensor 2 EGT reading deviates from the expected ratio against the upstream EGT sensor or modeled exhaust temperature. Confuses DPF regeneration control and may force a forced regen.

t: Exhaust Gas Temperature Sensor Circuit Intermittent (Bank 1 Sensor 2)
s: The EGT sensor at bank 1 sensor 2 signal jumps or drops out intermittently — typically a connector or chafed wire near the exhaust manifold heat zone.

t: Exhaust Gas Temperature Sensor Circuit Range/Performance (Bank 2 Sensor 2)
s: The EGT sensor at bank 2 sensor 2 reads in range but plausibility check fails — slow response, stuck reading, or value does not track other EGT sensors during a regeneration.

t: Exhaust Gas Temperature Sensor Circuit Intermittent (Bank 2 Sensor 2)
s: The EGT sensor at bank 2 sensor 2 signal jumps or drops out intermittently — typically a connector or chafed wire near the exhaust manifold heat zone.

t: Camshaft Position A Actuator Control Circuit Low (Bank 1)
s: Drive voltage to the intake VVT oil control valve on bank 1 reads below the lower limit. Usually a short to ground or a coil shorted internally.

t: Camshaft Position A Actuator Control Circuit High (Bank 1)
s: Drive voltage to the intake VVT oil control valve on bank 1 is above the upper limit — short to supply or open ground side. The solenoid is energised constantly or the driver protection trips.

t: Reductant Pump A Control Circuit/Open
s: The control line driving DEF pump A is open — no continuity through the motor or harness, dosing pressure cannot be built.

t: Reductant Pump A Control Performance/Stuck Off
s: DEF pump A receives a run command but does not start, or shuts off prematurely — pump performance is below threshold.

t: Reductant Pump A Control Circuit Low
s: DEF pump A control line stuck low — short to ground. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Reductant Pump A Control Circuit High
s: DEF pump A control line stuck high — short to B+. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Reductant Injection Valve Stuck Closed (Bank 1 Unit 1)
s: The DEF injection valve at bank 1 unit 1 does not open when commanded — typically crystallised DEF blocks the nozzle, no flow into the SCR.

t: Reductant Injection Valve Stuck Closed (Bank 2 Unit 1)
s: The DEF injection valve at bank 2 unit 1 does not open when commanded — typically crystallised DEF blocks the nozzle, no flow into the SCR.

t: Camshaft Position B Actuator Control Circuit Low (Bank 1)
s: Drive voltage to the exhaust VVT oil control valve on bank 1 is below the lower limit. Same failure pattern as P2088 but for the exhaust cam phaser.

t: Camshaft Position B Actuator Control Circuit High (Bank 1)
s: Drive voltage to the exhaust VVT oil control valve on bank 1 is above the upper limit. Mirror image of P2089 on the exhaust cam side.

t: A Camshaft Position Actuator Control Circuit Low (Bank 2)
s: The intake (A) camshaft phaser solenoid drive line on bank 2 is stuck low — short to ground or stuck-low driver. Variable-valve timing on intake bank 2 cannot be modulated.

t: A Camshaft Position Actuator Control Circuit High (Bank 2)
s: The intake (A) camshaft phaser solenoid drive line on bank 2 is stuck high — short to B+ or open ground.

t: B Camshaft Position Actuator Control Circuit Low (Bank 2)
s: The exhaust (B) camshaft phaser solenoid drive line on bank 2 is stuck low — short to ground or stuck-low driver.

t: B Camshaft Position Actuator Control Circuit High (Bank 2)
s: The exhaust (B) camshaft phaser solenoid drive line on bank 2 is stuck high — short to B+ or open ground.

t: Post Catalyst Fuel Trim System Too Lean (Bank 1)
s: Long-term fuel trim derived from the downstream oxygen sensor on bank 1 has reached the lean limit. The downstream-trim correction is a fine adjustment that runs after the main trim from the upstream sensor; persistent excursion suggests a degraded catalyst, an exhaust leak after the catalyst, or a drifting downstream sensor.

t: Post Catalyst Fuel Trim System Too Rich (Bank 1)
s: Long-term post-catalyst fuel trim on bank 1 has reached its rich limit. The downstream sensor sees more reductant than expected, often pointing to a degraded catalyst that no longer stores oxygen, or a failing downstream sensor biased rich.

t: Post Catalyst Fuel Trim System Too Lean (Bank 2)
s: Bank 2 counterpart of P2096. The post-catalyst fuel trim correction on bank 2 has reached its lean limit, suggesting an exhaust leak after the bank 2 catalyst, a drifting downstream sensor, or a degraded catalyst.

t: Post Catalyst Fuel Trim System Too Rich (Bank 2)
s: Bank 2 counterpart of P2097. Post-catalyst trim on bank 2 has reached the rich limit — usually a depleted catalyst or a downstream sensor biased rich on the bank 2 side.

t: Reductant Injection Air Pressure Sensor B Circuit
s: A general electrical fault is reported on reductant injection air-pressure sensor B — the second air-assist pressure sensor on dual-bank or dual-injector DEF systems.

t: Reductant Injection Air Pressure Sensor B Circuit Range/Performance
s: The reductant injection air-pressure sensor B reads inside the electrical range but does not match expected behaviour over time or load — slow response or wrong offset.

t: Reductant Injection Air Pressure Sensor B Circuit Low
s: The reductant injection air-pressure sensor B reads below its lower plausibility limit — short to ground or sensor internal short.

t: Reductant Injection Air Pressure Sensor B Circuit High
s: The reductant injection air-pressure sensor B reads above its upper plausibility limit — open signal line or short to B+.

t: Reductant Injection Air Pressure Sensor A/B Correlation
s: The two reductant injection air-pressure sensors A and B disagree by more than the allowed tolerance — one is reading off, or there is a real pressure imbalance between the two air-assist branches.

t: Reductant Tank Heater Control Circuit Performance
s: DEF tank heater is electrically OK but does not warm the tank within the expected time — element is partially open, fluid level too low for proper heat transfer, or the heater is undersized for the ambient temperature.

t: Reductant Purge Control Valve A Circuit/Open
s: The reductant purge control valve A drive circuit is open — used to evacuate DEF lines back to the tank when the engine is shut down to prevent freezing damage.

t: Reductant Purge Control Valve A Performance
s: Reductant purge control valve A is electrically OK but does not vent the line within the expected time — partial blockage or weakened solenoid.

t: Reductant Purge Control Valve A Circuit Low
s: Reductant purge control valve A drive line stuck low — short to ground. The evaporative system captures fuel vapor from the tank and purges it into intake; leaks or stuck purge valves leak hydrocarbons and cause emissions failures.

t: Reductant Purge Control Valve A Circuit High
s: Reductant purge control valve A drive line stuck high — short to B+. The evaporative system captures fuel vapor from the tank and purges it into intake; leaks or stuck purge valves leak hydrocarbons and cause emissions failures.

t: Reductant Purge Control Valve A Stuck Open
s: Reductant purge control valve A is mechanically stuck open — system cannot maintain dosing pressure because the line is constantly vented to the tank.

t: Reductant Purge Control Valve A Stuck Closed
s: Reductant purge control valve A is mechanically stuck closed — DEF lines cannot be vented at shutdown, increasing risk of freeze damage in winter.

t: Reductant Injection Air Pressure Control Valve Circuit/Open
s: The drive circuit for the air-assist pressure-control valve in the DEF system is open — atomisation pressure cannot be regulated, dosing accuracy degrades.

t: Reductant Injection Air Pressure Control Valve Performance
s: Reductant air-pressure control valve is electrically OK but cannot hold or modulate pressure within calibration — partial blockage or weak solenoid.

t: Reductant Injection Air Pressure Control Valve Circuit Low
s: Reductant air-pressure control valve drive line stuck low — short to ground. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Reductant Injection Air Pressure Control Valve Circuit High
s: Reductant air-pressure control valve drive line stuck high — short to B+. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Reductant Injection Air Pressure Control Valve Stuck Open
s: Reductant air-pressure control valve mechanically stuck open — air pressure cannot be reduced, dosing atomisation off-spec.

t: Reductant Injection Air Pressure Control Valve Stuck Closed
s: Reductant air-pressure control valve mechanically stuck closed — air supply blocked, dosing fails.

t: Reductant Metering Unit Temperature Sensor Circuit
s: A general electrical fault is reported on the temperature sensor inside the DEF metering unit — used to protect the unit from over-temperature and to compensate for fluid viscosity.

t: Reductant Metering Unit Temperature Sensor Circuit Range/Performance
s: Metering unit temperature sensor in range but fails plausibility — slow response or stuck reading. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Reductant Metering Unit Temperature Sensor Circuit Low
s: Metering unit temperature sensor below lower plausibility — short to ground. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Reductant Metering Unit Temperature Sensor Circuit High
s: Metering unit temperature sensor above upper plausibility — open signal or short to B+. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Reductant Metering Unit Temperature Sensor Circuit Intermittent/Erratic
s: Metering unit temperature sensor signal jumps or drops out — connector or harness issue. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Reductant Heater Coolant Control Valve Circuit/Open
s: The drive circuit for the engine-coolant control valve that warms the DEF tank/lines is open — coolant cannot be routed to the heating loop.

t: Reductant Heater Coolant Control Valve Performance/Stuck Open
s: Reductant coolant control valve is electrically OK but stuck open or does not modulate as commanded — DEF heating loop loses control authority.

t: Reductant Heater Coolant Control Valve Circuit Low
s: Reductant coolant control valve drive line stuck low — short to ground. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Reductant Heater Coolant Control Valve Circuit High
s: Reductant coolant control valve drive line stuck high — short to B+. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Reductant Metering Unit Heater Control Circuit/Open
s: The control circuit for the heater inside the DEF metering unit is open — winter operation cannot keep the dosing block above freezing.

t: Reductant Metering Unit Heater Control Circuit Performance
s: Metering unit heater is electrically OK but does not warm the block within the expected time — partial winding fault or excessive heat loss.

t: Reductant Metering Unit Heater Control Circuit Low
s: Metering unit heater control line stuck low — short to ground. The sensor heater warms the element to operating temperature (~600 °C); without it the lambda sensor stays inactive in closed loop and the ECM cannot trim mixture.

t: Reductant Metering Unit Heater Control Circuit High
s: Metering unit heater control line stuck high — short to B+. The sensor heater warms the element to operating temperature (~600 °C); without it the lambda sensor stays inactive in closed loop and the ECM cannot trim mixture.

t: Reductant Heater A Control Circuit/Open
s: Reductant heater A (typically the supply line heater nearest the tank) control circuit is open — that segment of DEF line will not be kept above freezing in winter.

t: Reductant Heater A Control Performance
s: The reductant (AdBlue/DEF) tank or line heater A does not reach the expected temperature within the calibrated time window. In cold weather frozen DEF blocks the SCR dosing system and the engine enters a no-restart state after ignored warnings.

t: Reductant Heater A Control Circuit Low
s: Reductant heater A control line stuck low — short to ground. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Reductant Heater A Control Circuit High
s: Reductant heater A control line stuck high — short to B+. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Reductant Heater B Control Circuit/Open
s: Reductant heater B (next supply-line segment) control circuit open — that line segment will not be kept above freezing.

t: Reductant Heater B Control Circuit Performance
s: Reductant heater B is electrically OK but does not warm the line in time — partial winding fault or excessive heat loss.

t: Reductant Heater B Control Circuit Low
s: Reductant heater B control line stuck low — short to ground. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Reductant Heater B Control Circuit High
s: Reductant heater B control line stuck high — short to B+. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Reductant Heater C Control Circuit/Open
s: Reductant heater C control circuit open.

t: Reductant Heater C Control Circuit Performance
s: Reductant heater C does not warm in time — partial fault.

t: Reductant Heater C Control Circuit Low
s: Reductant heater C control line stuck low — short to ground. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Reductant Heater C Control Circuit High
s: Reductant heater C control line stuck high — short to B+. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Reductant Heater D Control Circuit/Open
s: Reductant heater D control circuit open.

t: Reductant Heater D Control Circuit Performance
s: Reductant heater D does not warm in time.

t: Reductant Heater D Control Circuit Low
s: Reductant heater D control line stuck low — short to ground. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Reductant Heater D Control Circuit High
s: Reductant heater D control line stuck high — short to B+. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Reductant Control Module Requested MIL Illumination
s: The dedicated reductant/SCR control module has requested that the master PCM illuminate the malfunction indicator lamp — a generic forward of an internal SCR fault. Read the SCR module subcodes for the actual cause.

t: Reductant Injection Air Pressure Leakage
s: A leak has been detected in the air-assist branch of the DEF system — pressure decays faster than allowed when the air pump shuts off.

t: Exhaust Aftertreatment Fuel Injector A Control Circuit/Open
s: The 7th-injector (HC dosing into exhaust upstream of DOC/DPF) for aftertreatment regeneration on circuit A is open — DPF/SCR regeneration cannot raise exhaust temperature.

t: Exhaust Aftertreatment Fuel Injector A Control Performance
s: The aftertreatment HC injector A is electrically OK but does not deliver the commanded fuel mass during regeneration — partial blockage or worn nozzle.

t: Exhaust Aftertreatment Fuel Injector A Control Circuit Low
s: HC injector A drive line stuck low — short to ground. A flow-deviating injector throws off cylinder balance — adjacent cylinders run leaner/richer to compensate, often masking the root cause until the trim limit is hit.

t: Exhaust Aftertreatment Fuel Injector A Control Circuit High
s: HC injector A drive line stuck high — short to B+. A flow-deviating injector throws off cylinder balance — adjacent cylinders run leaner/richer to compensate, often masking the root cause until the trim limit is hit.

t: Exhaust Aftertreatment Fuel Injector A Stuck Open
s: HC injector A is mechanically stuck open — fuel leaks continuously into the exhaust, can ignite uncontrolled regeneration.

t: Exhaust Aftertreatment Fuel Injector A Stuck Closed
s: HC injector A is mechanically stuck closed — DPF regeneration cannot reach burn-off temperature. A flow-deviating injector throws off cylinder balance — adjacent cylinders run leaner/richer to compensate, often masking the root cause until the trim limit is hit.

t: Exhaust Aftertreatment Fuel Injector B Control Circuit/Open
s: The 7th-injector (HC dosing into exhaust upstream of DOC/DPF) for aftertreatment regeneration on circuit A is open — DPF/SCR regeneration cannot raise exhaust temperature.

t: Exhaust Aftertreatment Fuel Injector B Control Performance
s: The aftertreatment HC injector A is electrically OK but does not deliver the commanded fuel mass during regeneration — partial blockage or worn nozzle.

t: Exhaust Aftertreatment Fuel Injector B Control Circuit Low
s: HC injector A drive line stuck low — short to ground. A flow-deviating injector throws off cylinder balance — adjacent cylinders run leaner/richer to compensate, often masking the root cause until the trim limit is hit.

t: Exhaust Aftertreatment Fuel Injector B Control Circuit High
s: HC injector A drive line stuck high — short to B+. A flow-deviating injector throws off cylinder balance — adjacent cylinders run leaner/richer to compensate, often masking the root cause until the trim limit is hit.

t: Exhaust Aftertreatment Fuel Injector B Stuck Open
s: HC injector A is mechanically stuck open — fuel leaks continuously into the exhaust, can ignite uncontrolled regeneration.

t: Exhaust Aftertreatment Fuel Injector B Stuck Closed
s: HC injector A is mechanically stuck closed — DPF regeneration cannot reach burn-off temperature. A flow-deviating injector throws off cylinder balance — adjacent cylinders run leaner/richer to compensate, often masking the root cause until the trim limit is hit.

t: Exhaust Aftertreatment Fuel Supply Control Circuit/Open
s: The control circuit for the dedicated aftertreatment fuel supply pump or solenoid is open — HC dosing into the exhaust will fail.

t: Exhaust Aftertreatment Fuel Supply Control Performance
s: Aftertreatment fuel supply is electrically OK but does not deliver the commanded pressure or flow during regeneration — weak pump or partial blockage.

t: Exhaust Aftertreatment Fuel Supply Control Circuit Low
s: Aftertreatment fuel supply control line stuck low — short to ground. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Exhaust Aftertreatment Fuel Supply Control Circuit High
s: Aftertreatment fuel supply control line stuck high — short to B+. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Exhaust Aftertreatment Fuel Supply Control Stuck Open
s: Aftertreatment fuel supply solenoid stuck open mechanically — fuel pressure cannot be cut off, risk of over-fueling exhaust.

t: Exhaust Aftertreatment Fuel Supply Control Stuck Closed
s: Aftertreatment fuel supply solenoid stuck closed — HC dosing into exhaust fails, regeneration cannot raise temperature.

t: Exhaust Aftertreatment Fuel Pressure Sensor Circuit
s: A general electrical fault is reported on the aftertreatment fuel-pressure sensor — used to verify the dedicated HC-dosing rail is primed.

t: Exhaust Aftertreatment Fuel Pressure Sensor Circuit Range/Performance
s: Aftertreatment fuel-pressure sensor reads in range but plausibility fails — pressure does not rise during prime, drift across a regeneration.

t: Exhaust Aftertreatment Fuel Pressure Sensor Circuit Low
s: Aftertreatment fuel-pressure sensor below lower plausibility — short to ground. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Reductant Pressure Too Low
s: The pressure in the SCR reductant (AdBlue/DEF) line is below the calibrated minimum. Without sufficient pressure the dosing module cannot atomise reagent properly, leading to NOx exceedence.

t: Reductant Pressure Too Low
s: The reductant (AdBlue/DEF) line pressure stays below the demanded value during dosing. The dosing system needs a stable supply pressure to inject metered amounts; without it the system cannot meet NOx targets.

t: Reductant Pressure Too High
s: The reductant line pressure exceeds the upper limit. A blocked return path, a stuck pump or a faulty pressure sensor are common causes; over-pressure can damage the dosing valve and the pump head.

t: Exhaust Gas Temperature Sensor 1/3 Correlation (Bank 1)
s: EGT sensors 1 and 3 on bank 1 disagree by more than the allowed tolerance — one is reading off, or there is a real temperature gradient that exceeds the calibrated map (e.g. from a leaking exhaust joint between the two locations).

t: Exhaust Gas Temperature Sensor 2/3 Correlation (Bank 1)
s: EGT sensors 1 and 3 on bank 1 disagree by more than the allowed tolerance — one is reading off, or there is a real temperature gradient that exceeds the calibrated map (e.g. from a leaking exhaust joint between the two locations).

t: Exhaust Gas Temperature Sensor 1/2 Correlation (Bank 2)
s: EGT sensors 1 and 3 on bank 2 disagree by more than the allowed tolerance — one is reading off, or there is a real temperature gradient that exceeds the calibrated map (e.g. from a leaking exhaust joint between the two locations).

t: Reductant Injection Air Pressure Too Low
s: The DEF system air-assist pressure is below the required threshold — pump weak, line leak, or pressure-control valve stuck open.

t: Reductant Injection Air Pressure Too High
s: The DEF system air-assist pressure exceeds the upper threshold — pressure-control valve stuck closed or air pump runaway.

t: Reductant Pressure Too Low
s: Pressure in the AdBlue/DEF supply line is below the target. Usually a failing reductant pump, a clogged filter, frozen lines, or a leak in the pressurised circuit.

t: Reductant Pressure Too High
s: Manufacturer-specific variant of the reductant over-pressure condition. The pressure exceeds the upper threshold during a defined operating window; on some platforms used in addition to P20E2 to flag a different test path.

t: Reductant Pump Control Circuit Open
s: The control circuit to the reductant (DEF) dosing pump is open — no current flow detected. Without pump pressure no AdBlue reaches the SCR catalyst and NOx reduction stops.

t: Reductant Reservoir Heater "A" Control Circuit
s: A general electrical fault on the reductant tank heater "A" control circuit. AdBlue freezes at -11 C, so the tank heater must work for cold-start dosing.

t: Reductant Heater "A" Control Stuck Off
s: The reductant tank heater "A" does not heat when commanded on. Either a failed element or a stuck-off driver in the dosing module.

t: Reductant Heater "A" Control Stuck On
s: The reductant tank heater "A" stays energised even when not commanded — usually a shorted driver stage. Continuous heating wastes current and can overheat the heater element.

t: NOx Catalyst Efficiency Below Threshold (Bank 1)
s: The SCR catalyst on bank 1 is no longer reducing NOx to the level the ECU expects, even with proper reductant dosing and quality. Often the result of catalyst poisoning by sulphur or hydrocarbons, thermal aging, or a leak between the upstream and downstream NOx sensors.

t: Reductant Injection Valve Performance
s: The SCR reductant injection valve does not deliver the commanded amount of reagent. Causes include clogging by urea crystals, electrical faults or incorrect spray pattern.

t: SCR NOx Catalyst Over Temperature (Bank 2)
s: A temperature sensor at the SCR catalyst on bank 2 reports a value above the safe limit — sustained over-temperature can sinter the SCR washcoat and destroy NOx conversion.

t: SCR NOx Pre-Catalyst Over Temperature (Bank 2)
s: A temperature sensor at the SCR catalyst on bank 2 reports a value above the safe limit — sustained over-temperature can sinter the SCR washcoat and destroy NOx conversion.

t: SCR NOx Catalyst Efficiency Below Threshold (Bank 2)
s: The SCR catalyst on bank 2 is no longer reducing NOx within the required efficiency — downstream NOx remains too high relative to commanded dosing across a drive cycle.

t: SCR NOx Pre-Catalyst Efficiency Below Threshold (Bank 2)
s: The SCR catalyst on bank 2 is no longer reducing NOx within the required efficiency — downstream NOx remains too high relative to commanded dosing across a drive cycle.

t: Reductant Consumption Too Low
s: The DEF consumption observed over the dosing window is below the model expectation — typically a clogged dosing nozzle, weak pump, or leak between pump and injector that recirculates DEF back to the tank.

t: Reductant Consumption Too High
s: The DEF consumption observed over the dosing window is above the model expectation — typically a leaking injector seat, leaking line fitting, or a pressure-control valve that vents constantly to the tank.

t: Reductant Injection Valve Stuck Open (Bank 1 Unit 1)
s: The DEF injection valve at bank 1 unit 1 does not close when commanded — DEF flows continuously into the SCR. Risk of crystallisation in the catalyst and over-dosing.

t: Reductant Injection Valve Stuck Open (Bank 2 Unit 1)
s: The DEF injection valve at bank 2 unit 1 does not close when commanded — DEF flows continuously into the SCR. Risk of crystallisation in the catalyst and over-dosing.

t: Intake Manifold Runner Control Circuit Performance (Bank 1)
s: The IMRC actuator drive circuit on bank 1 is electrically OK but its current draw or duty-cycle response is outside the calibrated profile — typically a high-resistance solenoid winding or a partially shorted driver.

t: Intake Manifold Runner Control Circuit Performance (Bank 2)
s: The IMRC actuator drive circuit on bank 2 is electrically OK but its current draw or duty-cycle response is outside the calibrated profile — typically a high-resistance solenoid winding or a partially shorted driver.

t: Reductant Pump B Control Circuit/Open
s: The control line driving DEF pump B is open. On large engines or dual-tank systems a second pump (B) supplements pump A; with B offline the system loses redundancy.

t: Reductant Pump B Control Performance/Stuck Off
s: DEF pump B receives a run command but does not start, or shuts off prematurely.

t: Reductant Pump B Control Circuit Low
s: DEF pump B control line stuck low — short to ground. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Reductant Pump B Control Circuit High
s: DEF pump B control line stuck high — short to B+. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Reductant Metering Unit 1 Performance
s: The first DEF metering unit (the integrated module that combines pump, filter, level sensor, and dosing valve) is electrically OK but does not deliver the expected pressure or flow during operation.

t: Reductant Control Module Performance
s: The dedicated SCR/reductant control module reports an internal performance fault — failed self-tests, watchdog resets, or calibration mismatch. Frequently a forerunner of total module failure.

t: Throttle Actuator Control Motor Circuit Open
s: The drive-by-wire throttle motor circuit is open — no current flow detected. The ECU loses authority over the throttle plate and forces a limp-home with reduced power.

t: Throttle Actuator Control Motor Circuit Range/Performance
s: The throttle motor responds, but its actual position does not track the commanded position within tolerance. Often a dirty throttle bore where the plate sticks, or a worn motor.

t: Throttle Actuator Control Motor Circuit Low
s: Drive voltage to the throttle motor is below the lower limit. Typically a short to ground or a failed driver stage in the ECU.

t: Throttle Actuator Control Motor Circuit High
s: Drive voltage to the throttle motor is above the upper limit. Usually a short to supply or an open ground.

t: Throttle Actuator Control System — Forced Idle
s: The ECU has detected a fault severe enough that it forces the throttle actuator to the idle stop. The driver loses pedal authority — only idle is available until the underlying TAC fault clears.

t: Throttle Actuator Control System — Forced Engine Shutdown
s: The TAC monitor commanded an engine shutdown because throttle authority can no longer be guaranteed safely. The most aggressive TAC limp mode — used when both throttle position sensors disagree or the motor is uncontrollable.

t: Throttle Actuator Control System — Forced Limited Power
s: The TAC monitor restricts maximum throttle opening as a safety response to a TAC fault. The vehicle can still be driven, but accelerator pedal travel is capped.

t: Throttle Actuator Control Module Internal Processor
