s: The internal processor or watchdog of the throttle actuator control module reports an internal fault. Communication between the TAC monitor core and the main ECU core has failed plausibility — typical for a defective ECU.

t: Throttle Actuator Control Module Performance
s: Self-tests within the TAC module fail their performance criteria — for example RAM/ROM checks or analog reference voltages drift. Indicates an aging ECU rather than a wiring fault.

t: Throttle/Pedal Position Sensor A Minimum Stop Performance
s: The ECU detects that the throttle position sensor A signal at the closed-throttle stop does not match the learned minimum value. The mechanical or electrical zero reference of the throttle has drifted out of the expected range, often after cleaning, replacement or wear.

t: Throttle Actuator B Control Motor Circuit/Open
s: The drive circuit of throttle actuator motor B is open. Throttle B is the secondary throttle on engines with two throttle bodies — typically used for tumble flap or charge-motion control.

t: Throttle Actuator B Control Motor Circuit Range/Performance
s: Throttle actuator B motor responds electrically but does not move the throttle plate as commanded — typically high-resistance brushes, partial winding fault, or a sticky throttle bore.

t: Throttle Actuator B Control Motor Circuit Low
s: Throttle actuator B motor drive line stuck low — short to ground. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Throttle Actuator B Control Motor Circuit High
s: Throttle actuator B motor drive line stuck high — short to B+. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Throttle/Pedal Position Sensor/Switch C/F Voltage Correlation
s: The redundant throttle or accelerator pedal position channels C and F disagree by more than the allowed tolerance. Drive-by-wire systems compare two or three independent channels to detect a faulty potentiometer track.

t: Throttle Actuator B Control System - Forced Limited RPM
s: Throttle B control has detected an unsafe condition and forced an RPM limit. Common protective response when motor current, throttle position feedback, or supply voltage on the B path is out of spec.

t: Throttle Actuator Control System — Forced Limited RPM
s: The TAC monitor caps engine speed at a fixed limp-home rpm because it can no longer trust the throttle response. Driver may notice a hard rev limit well below redline.

t: Throttle Actuator Control System — Stuck Open
s: The throttle plate cannot be commanded back to the closed/idle position. Idle speed runs high or the engine cannot decelerate properly — safety-critical fault that usually triggers immediate limp mode.

t: Throttle Actuator Control System — Stuck Closed
s: The throttle plate cannot be commanded open. The engine stays at idle or stalls under load. Often caused by a seized motor, broken gear, or heavy carbon coking inside the throttle bore.

t: Throttle Actuator Control System Forced Limited RPM
s: The throttle actuator control has detected a fault and limits engine speed to a defined emergency value. The vehicle stays drivable but with severely reduced power until the underlying fault is cleared.

t: Throttle Actuator Control System Forced Below Limit RPM
s: The throttle actuator system has dropped engine speed below the normal limp limit because of a more serious fault. The engine may run only at idle or stall.

t: Throttle/Pedal Position Sensor A Minimum Stop Performance
s: The minimum stop calibration of throttle position sensor A is outside the expected range during the ECU self-check. The signal at fully closed throttle does not match the stored reference, indicating a mechanical or sensor issue.

t: Throttle/Pedal Position Sensor B Minimum Stop Performance
s: The minimum stop calibration of throttle position sensor B does not match the learned reference during ECU self-test. Sensor B is the redundant channel used to cross-check sensor A.

t: Throttle/Pedal Position Sensor C Minimum Stop Performance
s: The minimum stop signal from throttle/pedal position sensor C is out of specification during initialization. Used on systems with three redundant sensor channels for safety-critical drive-by-wire control.

t: Throttle Actuator Control Motor Current Range/Performance
s: The current draw of the throttle motor lies outside the expected range — either much higher (binding plate) or much lower (worn brushes, open winding) than calibrated.

t: Throttle Closed Position Performance
s: When commanded to idle, the throttle plate does not reach the calibrated closed position. Often a coked throttle bore preventing the plate from seating fully.

t: Throttle Actuator B Control System - Stuck Open
s: Throttle B is mechanically stuck open — typically carbon buildup or a broken return spring on the secondary throttle plate.

t: Throttle Actuator B Control System - Stuck Closed
s: Throttle B is mechanically stuck closed — carbon buildup or a seized bearing on the secondary throttle.

t: Throttle Actuator B Control Motor Current Range/Performance
s: Throttle B motor current draw does not match the calibrated map for the commanded position — high friction, partial short, or stiction.

t: Throttle Actuator B Control Throttle Body Range/Performance
s: The throttle B body itself does not respond as commanded — position feedback is plausible but does not track the setpoint within the calibrated time window.

t: Throttle Actuator B Control Module Processor
s: The processor monitoring throttle B inside the throttle/PCM has reported a self-test failure — RAM check, ROM check, or watchdog timeout.

t: Throttle Actuator B Control Module Performance
s: The throttle control module on the B path reports general performance issues — internal fault, communication mismatch, or calibration drift.

t: Throttle/Pedal Position Sensor "C" Circuit
s: A general electrical fault on the "C" channel of the throttle or pedal position sensor. Drive-by-wire pedals carry two or three redundant tracks; "C" is the third channel.

t: Throttle/Pedal Position Sensor "C" Range/Performance
s: The "C" channel of the throttle or pedal sensor produces an electrically valid signal that does not match the other channels or the operating point.

t: Throttle/Pedal Position Sensor "D" Circuit Low
s: Sensor "D" of the redundant accelerator-pedal pair reports a voltage below the valid window. The ECU rejects the signal and falls back on the second sensor or limp-home.

t: Throttle/Pedal Position Sensor "D" Circuit High
s: Sensor "D" of the pedal pair reports a voltage above the valid window. Often a short to supply or an open ground at the connector.

t: Throttle/Pedal Position Sensor "C" Circuit Low
s: The "C" channel signal is below the lower threshold — usually a short to ground or an open signal wire.

t: Throttle/Pedal Position Sensor "C" Circuit High
s: The "C" channel signal is above the upper threshold — typically a short to positive or an open ground.

t: Throttle/Pedal Position Sensor "C" Circuit Intermittent
s: The "C" channel produces an intermittent signal — usually a marginal connector or a worn track inside the pedal sensor.

t: Throttle/Pedal Position Sensor "E" Circuit Low
s: Sensor "D" of the redundant accelerator-pedal pair reports a voltage below the valid window. The ECU rejects the signal and falls back on the second sensor or limp-home.

t: Throttle/Pedal Position Sensor "E" Circuit High
s: Sensor "D" of the pedal pair reports a voltage above the valid window. Often a short to supply or an open ground at the connector.

t: Throttle/Pedal Position Sensor "D" Circuit Intermittent
s: The "D" channel of the throttle or pedal sensor produces an intermittent signal — typically marginal connector contacts.

t: Throttle Position Sensor/Switch G Circuit
s: A general electrical fault is reported on throttle position sensor G — open, shorted, or out of plausibility. Sensor G is part of a multi-channel redundancy on dual-throttle systems.

t: Throttle Position Sensor/Switch G Circuit Range/Performance
s: TPS G reads in range but plausibility check fails — value sticks, lags, or does not match the commanded throttle position.

t: Throttle Position Sensor/Switch G Circuit Low
s: TPS G below lower plausibility — short to ground. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Throttle Position Sensor/Switch G Circuit High
s: TPS G above upper plausibility — open signal or short to B+. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Throttle Position Sensor/Switch G Circuit Intermittent
s: TPS G signal jumps or drops out intermittently — connector or harness issue. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Throttle/Pedal Position Sensor/Switch F/G Voltage Correlation
s: Channels F and G of the throttle/pedal position sensor disagree by more than the allowed tolerance. Drive-by-wire system has lost redundancy and may force limp mode.

t: Throttle/Pedal Position Sensor/Switch F Circuit
s: A general electrical fault is reported on throttle/pedal position sensor F — open, shorted, or out of plausibility.

t: Throttle/Pedal Position Sensor "E" Range/Performance
s: The "E" channel of the pedal/throttle sensor produces electrically valid values that do not match other channels or the operating window.

t: Throttle/Pedal Position Sensor "E" Circuit Low
s: The "E" channel signal is below the lower threshold — typically a short to ground or an open signal wire.

t: Throttle/Pedal Position Sensor "E" Circuit High
s: The "E" channel signal is above the upper threshold — usually a short to positive or an open ground.

t: Throttle/Pedal Position Sensor/Switch F Circuit Intermittent
s: TPS/APP sensor F signal jumps or drops out intermittently. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Throttle/Pedal Position Sensor "A"/"B" Voltage Correlation
s: The two redundant throttle position sensors on the throttle body disagree by more than allowed. Distinct from P2138 which monitors the pedal pair — P2135 monitors the throttle-body pair on drive-by-wire systems.

t: Throttle/Pedal Position Sensor A/C Voltage Correlation
s: The "A" and "C" channels of the throttle or pedal sensor disagree by more than the allowed tolerance. The ECU cannot trust either reading and forces limp-home with reduced torque.

t: Throttle/Pedal Position Sensor B/C Voltage Correlation
s: The "B" and "C" channels of the throttle or pedal sensor disagree beyond tolerance — the ECU cannot trust either signal and reverts to limp-home.

t: Throttle/Pedal Position Sensor "D"/"E" Voltage Correlation
s: The two redundant accelerator pedal position sensors disagree by more than the allowed tolerance. The ECU cannot trust either signal and switches to limp-home with very limited torque. Often appears intermittently with a worn pedal sensor before failing outright.

t: Throttle/Pedal Position Sensor/Switch D/F Voltage Correlation
s: Channels D and F of the throttle/pedal position sensor disagree by more than the allowed tolerance.

t: EGR Throttle Control Circuit B/Open
s: The drive circuit of EGR throttle B is open. EGR throttles partially close intake to increase exhaust gas recirculation flow at low loads.

t: EGR Throttle Control Circuit B Range/Performance
s: EGR throttle B is electrically OK but does not move as commanded — typically carbon buildup or a sticking actuator.

t: EGR Throttle Control Circuit B Low
s: EGR throttle B drive line stuck low — short to ground. EGR mixes inert exhaust into intake to lower combustion peak temperature — too little raises NOx, too much causes misfires and rough running.

t: EGR Throttle Control Circuit B High
s: EGR throttle B drive line stuck high — short to B+. EGR mixes inert exhaust into intake to lower combustion peak temperature — too little raises NOx, too much causes misfires and rough running.

t: Fuel Injection System Fault - Forced Engine Shutdown
s: A safety-critical fuel-injection fault has been detected and the PCM has shut down the engine to prevent damage. Examples are common-rail pressure runaway, multiple stuck-open injectors, or HPFP failure.

t: Fuel Pump System Fault - Forced Engine Shutdown
s: A safety-critical fuel-pump fault has been detected and the engine has been shut down. Typical for HPFP runaway or low-pressure pump that loses control authority.

t: Throttle/Pedal Position Sensor/Switch E/F Voltage Correlation
s: Channels E and F of the throttle/pedal position sensor disagree by more than the allowed tolerance.

t: EGR Throttle Control Circuit A Low
s: EGR throttle A drive line stuck low — short to ground. EGR mixes inert exhaust into intake to lower combustion peak temperature — too little raises NOx, too much causes misfires and rough running.

t: EGR Throttle Control Circuit A High
s: EGR throttle A drive line stuck high — short to B+. EGR mixes inert exhaust into intake to lower combustion peak temperature — too little raises NOx, too much causes misfires and rough running.

t: EGR Vent Control Circuit/Open
s: The drive circuit of the EGR vent solenoid is open — the solenoid releases vacuum from the EGR actuator. Open circuit means the EGR cannot reset its position.

t: EGR Vent Control Circuit Low
s: EGR vent solenoid drive line stuck low — short to ground. EGR mixes inert exhaust into intake to lower combustion peak temperature — too little raises NOx, too much causes misfires and rough running.

t: EGR Vent Control Circuit High
s: EGR vent solenoid drive line stuck high — short to B+. EGR mixes inert exhaust into intake to lower combustion peak temperature — too little raises NOx, too much causes misfires and rough running.

t: Fuel Injector Group A Supply Voltage Circuit/Open
s: The ECU detects an open or missing supply voltage on injector group A. The injectors of this bank cannot be energized; the affected cylinders may not fire and the engine runs rough or stalls.

t: Fuel Injector Group A Supply Voltage Circuit Low
s: The supply voltage measured at injector group A is below the calibrated threshold. Either the supply is shorted to ground or the source voltage has collapsed during cranking or load.

t: Fuel Injector Group A Supply Voltage Circuit High
s: The injector group A supply voltage exceeds the calibrated upper limit. Often a sign of a short to battery voltage on the supply line, an over-charging alternator or an internal driver failure.

t: Fuel Injector Group B Supply Voltage Circuit/Open
s: The ECU detects an open or missing supply voltage on injector group B. The injectors on this bank cannot be energized, leading to misfire on the affected cylinders.

t: SCR NOx Catalyst Inlet Temperature Too High
s: The temperature sensor at the SCR inlet reports a value above the safe limit. The SCR catalyst loses NH3 storage capacity above ~550 °C and can be permanently damaged.

t: SCR NOx Catalyst Inlet Temperature Too High During Particulate Filter Regeneration
s: Specifically during DPF regeneration, the SCR inlet temperature exceeded the safe limit — typically because too much HC was injected upstream or because the regeneration ran too long.

t: SCR NOx Catalyst Outlet Temperature Too High
s: The temperature sensor at the SCR inlet reports a value above the safe limit. The SCR catalyst loses NH3 storage capacity above ~550 °C and can be permanently damaged.

t: SCR NOx Catalyst Outlet Temperature Too High During Particulate Filter Regeneration
s: Specifically during DPF regeneration, the SCR inlet temperature exceeded the safe limit — typically because too much HC was injected upstream or because the regeneration ran too long.

t: Reductant Pump A Current Too High
s: DEF pump A current draw exceeds the upper threshold — typically a seized pump head or partial winding short.

t: Reductant Heater A Current Too High
s: DEF heater A current draw exceeds the upper threshold — typically a partial short in the heating element.

t: Fuel Injector Group B Supply Voltage Circuit Low
s: The supply rail to fuel injector group B is below the lower threshold — fuse, relay, or supply harness fault.

t: Fuel Injector Group B Supply Voltage Circuit High
s: Supply rail to injector group B above the upper threshold — typically a stuck-closed regulator or alternator overshoot.

t: Fuel Injector Group C Supply Voltage Circuit/Open
s: Supply rail to injector group C is open — group C will not fire. A flow-deviating injector throws off cylinder balance — adjacent cylinders run leaner/richer to compensate, often masking the root cause until the trim limit is hit.

t: Fuel Injector Group C Supply Voltage Circuit Low
s: The supply rail to fuel injector group B is below the lower threshold — fuse, relay, or supply harness fault.

t: Fuel Injector Group C Supply Voltage Circuit High
s: Supply rail to injector group B above the upper threshold — typically a stuck-closed regulator or alternator overshoot.

t: Fuel Injector Group D Supply Voltage Circuit/Open
s: Supply rail to injector group C is open — group C will not fire. A flow-deviating injector throws off cylinder balance — adjacent cylinders run leaner/richer to compensate, often masking the root cause until the trim limit is hit.

t: Fuel Injector Group D Supply Voltage Circuit Low
s: The supply rail to fuel injector group B is below the lower threshold — fuse, relay, or supply harness fault.

t: Fuel Injector Group D Supply Voltage Circuit High
s: Supply rail to injector group B above the upper threshold — typically a stuck-closed regulator or alternator overshoot.

t: Vehicle Speed Sensor B Circuit
s: A general electrical fault is reported on vehicle speed sensor B — second VSS used either at the transmission output or as redundancy. Open, short, or implausible signal.

t: Vehicle Speed Sensor B Range/Performance
s: The signal of the secondary vehicle speed sensor is implausible compared to other sensors. The ECU cannot use the value for fuel and ignition control or transmission shifting.

t: Vehicle Speed - Wheel Speed Correlation
s: The vehicle speed signal computed from VSS does not match the wheel-speed signal from ABS within tolerance. One of the two sources is wrong, or there is a tyre-size mismatch.

t: Vehicle Speed - Output Shaft Speed Correlation
s: VSS does not match transmission output-shaft speed within tolerance. Possible transmission slip, sensor fault, or harness issue.

t: Output Shaft Speed - Wheel Speed Correlation
s: Transmission output-shaft speed does not match ABS wheel speed within tolerance — typically transmission slip, driveline fault, or one sensor drift.

t: Reductant Pump B Stuck On
s: The DEF (urea) pump A keeps running when commanded off, or its drive current does not drop to zero. Continuous pumping over-pressurises the dosing line and can crystallise DEF at the injector.

t: Reductant Heater D Current Too Low
s: DEF heater D current draw is below threshold — typically a partially-open winding.

t: Reductant Heater D Current Too High
s: DEF heater D current draw exceeds threshold — element partially shorted.

t: Vehicle Speed Sensor B Circuit Low
s: VSS B reads below lower plausibility — short to ground or sensor not signalling. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Vehicle Speed Sensor B Circuit Intermittent/Erratic/High
s: VSS B signal jumps, drops out, or reads above plausibility — connector or harness issue, or magnetic ring damaged.

t: Vehicle Speed Sensor A/B Correlation
s: VSS A and VSS B disagree by more than the allowed tolerance.

t: Throttle/Pedal Position Sensor A Maximum Stop Performance
s: Throttle/pedal position sensor A does not reach the calibrated maximum-stop value during the wide-open self-test. Sensor wear, mechanical stop misalignment, or a stuck pedal.

t: Throttle/Pedal Position Sensor B Maximum Stop Performance
s: Throttle/pedal position sensor B does not reach the calibrated maximum-stop value during the wide-open self-test. Sensor wear, mechanical stop misalignment, or a stuck pedal.

t: Throttle/Pedal Position Sensor C Maximum Stop Performance
s: Throttle/pedal position sensor C does not reach the calibrated maximum-stop value during the wide-open self-test. Sensor wear, mechanical stop misalignment, or a stuck pedal.

t: Throttle/Pedal Position Sensor D Maximum Stop Performance
s: Throttle/pedal position sensor D does not reach the calibrated maximum-stop value during the wide-open self-test. Sensor wear, mechanical stop misalignment, or a stuck pedal.

t: Throttle/Pedal Position Sensor E Maximum Stop Performance
s: Throttle/pedal position sensor E does not reach the calibrated maximum-stop value during the wide-open self-test. Sensor wear, mechanical stop misalignment, or a stuck pedal.

t: Throttle/Pedal Position Sensor F Maximum Stop Performance
s: Throttle/pedal position sensor F does not reach the calibrated maximum-stop value during the wide-open self-test. Sensor wear, mechanical stop misalignment, or a stuck pedal.

t: Exhaust Pressure Regulator Vent Solenoid Control Circuit/Open
s: The drive circuit of the exhaust pressure regulator vent solenoid is open. The solenoid releases pressure from the exhaust backpressure regulator, which is part of the exhaust brake system on diesel trucks.

t: Fuel Injector Group E Supply Voltage Circuit/Open
s: Supply rail to injector group C is open — group C will not fire. A flow-deviating injector throws off cylinder balance — adjacent cylinders run leaner/richer to compensate, often masking the root cause until the trim limit is hit.

t: Fuel Injector Group E Supply Voltage Circuit Low
s: The supply rail to fuel injector group B is below the lower threshold — fuse, relay, or supply harness fault.

t: Fuel Injector Group E Supply Voltage Circuit High
s: Supply rail to injector group B above the upper threshold — typically a stuck-closed regulator or alternator overshoot.

t: Fuel Injector Group F Supply Voltage Circuit/Open
s: Supply rail to injector group C is open — group C will not fire. A flow-deviating injector throws off cylinder balance — adjacent cylinders run leaner/richer to compensate, often masking the root cause until the trim limit is hit.

t: Fuel Injector Group F Supply Voltage Circuit Low
s: The supply rail to fuel injector group B is below the lower threshold — fuse, relay, or supply harness fault.

t: Fuel Injector Group F Supply Voltage Circuit High
s: Supply rail to injector group B above the upper threshold — typically a stuck-closed regulator or alternator overshoot.

t: Exhaust Pressure Regulator Vent Solenoid Control Circuit Low
s: EPR vent solenoid drive line stuck low — short to ground. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Exhaust Pressure Regulator Vent Solenoid Control Circuit High
s: EPR vent solenoid drive line stuck high — short to B+. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Throttle Actuator Control System - Sudden High Air Flow Detected
s: A sudden, large jump in MAF/MAP signal not matched by a corresponding pedal change has been detected — typically a torn intake boot, dropped MAF sensor, or actively-stuck-open throttle plate.

t: High Airflow/Vacuum Leak Detected (Slow Accumulation)
s: Long-term fuel and air adaptation values indicate a slow but continuous excess of air entering the engine. The ECU concludes that an unmetered air leak is gradually growing in the intake system.

t: Throttle Actuator Control System - Sudden Low Air Flow Detected
s: A sudden, large drop in MAF/MAP signal not matched by a pedal change — typically intake restriction, MAF failure, or actively-stuck-closed throttle plate.

t: Throttle Actuator Control System - Low Air Flow Detected
s: Sustained low air-flow condition detected — slower than P2174 but persistent. Restricted air filter, dirty MAF, or partially-stuck throttle.

t: Throttle Actuator A Control System - Idle Position Not Learned
s: After a battery disconnect or PCM reset, throttle A has not completed the idle-position learn procedure. Adaption is required after throttle body cleaning, replacement, or PCM relearn.

t: System Too Lean Off Idle Bank 1
s: Fuel trim values on bank 1 indicate a lean condition during off-idle operation. The ECU has to add significant fuel correction to maintain stoichiometric mixture above idle.

t: System Too Rich Off Idle Bank 1
s: Bank 1 fuel trim shows a strong rich correction during off-idle operation. The ECU is forced to subtract fuel to keep the mixture near stoichiometric.

t: System Too Lean Off Idle Bank 2
s: Fuel trims on bank 2 show a lean deviation during off-idle operating conditions. Causes are similar to bank 1 but isolated to the second cylinder bank.

t: Fuel Injector Group G Supply Voltage Circuit/Open
s: Supply rail to injector group C is open — group C will not fire. A flow-deviating injector throws off cylinder balance — adjacent cylinders run leaner/richer to compensate, often masking the root cause until the trim limit is hit.

t: Fuel Injector Group G Supply Voltage Circuit Low
s: The supply rail to fuel injector group B is below the lower threshold — fuse, relay, or supply harness fault.

t: Fuel Injector Group G Supply Voltage Circuit High
s: Supply rail to injector group B above the upper threshold — typically a stuck-closed regulator or alternator overshoot.

t: Fuel Injector Group H Supply Voltage Circuit/Open
s: Supply rail to injector group C is open — group C will not fire. A flow-deviating injector throws off cylinder balance — adjacent cylinders run leaner/richer to compensate, often masking the root cause until the trim limit is hit.

t: Fuel Injector Group H Supply Voltage Circuit Low
s: The supply rail to fuel injector group B is below the lower threshold — fuse, relay, or supply harness fault.

t: Fuel Injector Group H Supply Voltage Circuit High
s: Supply rail to injector group B above the upper threshold — typically a stuck-closed regulator or alternator overshoot.

t: System Too Rich Off Idle Bank 2
s: Bank 2 fuel trim shows a rich shift during off-idle conditions. The ECU subtracts fuel to maintain proper combustion. Common causes are stuck injectors or excess fuel pressure.

t: Cooling System Performance
s: The engine cooling system does not reach or maintain the expected temperature within the diagnostic window — usually a stuck-open thermostat, but also a low coolant level or a stuck cooling fan that overcools the engine.

t: Engine Coolant Temperature Sensor 2 Circuit
s: A general electrical fault is reported on engine coolant temperature sensor 2 — open, shorted, or out of plausibility. Sensor 2 is typically at the radiator outlet or as redundancy on the second cylinder bank.

t: Engine Coolant Temperature Sensor 2 Circuit Range/Performance
s: ECT sensor 2 reads in range but plausibility check fails — slow response, stuck reading, or value does not track ECT 1 across a warm-up.

t: Engine Coolant Temperature Sensor 2 Circuit Low
s: ECT sensor 2 reads below lower plausibility — short to ground. The engine coolant temperature sensor feeds warm-up enrichment, fan control and closed-loop entry — a stuck-cold reading keeps the engine in open loop and rich forever.

t: Engine Coolant Temperature Sensor 2 Circuit High
s: The second coolant temperature sensor (often radiator-outlet or cylinder-head bank) reports a voltage above the upper threshold — typically an open signal wire or a sensor with infinite resistance.

t: Engine Coolant Temperature Sensor 2 Circuit Low
s: The second coolant temperature sensor reports a voltage below the lower threshold — typically a short to ground or a sensor with very low resistance.

t: System Too Lean at Idle (Bank 1)
s: Fuel trim on bank 1 hits the lean limit only at idle, while trim returns to normal at higher load. This pattern strongly indicates a vacuum leak — leaks become a larger fraction of total airflow at idle and so dominate the trim, but disappear into background noise above idle.

t: System Too Rich at Idle (Bank 1)
s: Fuel trim on bank 1 hits the rich limit only at idle. Often a leaking injector dribbling fuel at low load, a sticky purge valve dumping fuel vapour, or fuel pressure regulator that fails to relieve pressure when intake vacuum is high.

t: System Too Lean at Idle (Bank 2)
s: Fuel trim on bank 2 hits the lean limit only at idle, while trim returns to normal at higher load. This pattern strongly indicates a vacuum leak — leaks become a larger fraction of total airflow at idle and so dominate the trim, but disappear into background noise above idle.

t: Throttle Actuator B Control System - Idle Position Not Learned
s: After a battery disconnect or PCM reset, throttle A has not completed the idle-position learn procedure. Adaption is required after throttle body cleaning, replacement, or PCM relearn.

t: Throttle/Fuel Inhibit B Circuit
s: A general electrical fault on the throttle/fuel inhibit signal B — used by anti-theft, immobiliser, or transmission to forbid throttle/fuel until released.

t: Throttle/Fuel Inhibit B Circuit Range/Performance
s: Throttle/fuel inhibit B signal reads in range but plausibility fails — does not assert/release as expected.

t: Throttle/Fuel Inhibit B Circuit Low
s: Throttle/fuel inhibit B line stuck low. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Throttle/Fuel Inhibit B Circuit High
s: Throttle/fuel inhibit B line stuck high. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Reductant No Flow Detected
s: The DEF dosing system commands flow but the pressure or flow sensor reports no actual delivery — typically a fully blocked dosing nozzle, a frozen line, or a dead pump.

t: System Too Rich at Idle (Bank 2)
s: Fuel trim on bank 2 hits the rich limit only at idle. Often a leaking injector dribbling fuel at low load, a sticky purge valve dumping fuel vapour, or fuel pressure regulator that fails to relieve pressure when intake vacuum is high.

t: System Too Lean at Higher Load (Bank 1)
s: Bank 1 fuel trim hits the lean limit only at higher engine load. Pattern points to a fuel-supply restriction — failing fuel pump, blocked filter, or weak injectors — rather than a vacuum leak (which would dominate at idle).

t: System Too Rich at Higher Load (Bank 1)
s: Bank 1 fuel trim hits the rich limit only at higher load. Often excessive fuel pressure, a stuck-closed MAF reading low, or coolant temperature signal stuck cold causing over-fuelling.

t: System Too Lean at Higher Load (Bank 2)
s: Bank 2 fuel trim hits the lean limit only at higher engine load. Pattern points to a fuel-supply restriction — failing fuel pump, blocked filter, or weak injectors — rather than a vacuum leak (which would dominate at idle).

t: System Too Rich at Higher Load (Bank 2)
s: Bank 2 fuel trim hits the rich limit only at higher load. Often excessive fuel pressure, a stuck-closed MAF reading low, or coolant temperature signal stuck cold causing over-fuelling.

t: O2 Sensor Signal Stuck Lean (Bank 1, Sensor 1)
s: The upstream oxygen sensor on bank 1 returns a constant lean signal during conditions where rich content is expected (for example after a deceleration fuel cut-off recovery). Usually a sensor that has lost its rich-side response or a slow heater warm-up.

t: O2 Sensor Signal Stuck Rich (Bank 1, Sensor 1)
s: The upstream oxygen sensor on bank 1 returns a constant rich signal under deceleration fuel cut-off where lean is expected. Often a contaminated sensor (silicone, oil, coolant) or short to ground that pulls the signal high in some sensor types.

t: O2 Sensor Signal Stuck Lean (Bank 2, Sensor 1)
s: The upstream oxygen sensor on bank 2 returns a constant lean signal during conditions where rich content is expected (for example after a deceleration fuel cut-off recovery). Usually a sensor that has lost its rich-side response or a slow heater warm-up.

t: O2 Sensor Signal Stuck Rich (Bank 2, Sensor 1)
s: The upstream oxygen sensor on bank 2 returns a constant rich signal under deceleration fuel cut-off where lean is expected. Often a contaminated sensor (silicone, oil, coolant) or short to ground that pulls the signal high in some sensor types.

t: Intake Air Temperature Sensor 1/2 Correlation
s: The two intake air temperature sensors disagree by more than the calibrated correlation threshold during stable conditions. The ECU cannot determine which value is correct.

t: Bank 1 Air-Fuel Ratio Imbalance
s: The fuel-trim values across cylinders on bank 1 differ enough that the bank-average air-fuel ratio is biased — a single cylinder is significantly leaner or richer than the rest. Detected by short-term-fuel-trim variance or by the wide-band O2 sensor harmonic monitor.

t: Bank 2 Air-Fuel Ratio Imbalance
s: The fuel-trim values across cylinders on bank 2 differ enough that the bank-average air-fuel ratio is biased — a single cylinder is significantly leaner or richer than the rest. Detected by short-term-fuel-trim variance or by the wide-band O2 sensor harmonic monitor.

t: Cylinder 1 Air-Fuel Ratio Imbalance
s: Cylinder 1 fuel trim deviates significantly from the bank average — a single-cylinder injector or intake fault.

t: Cylinder 2 Air-Fuel Ratio Imbalance
s: Cylinder 2 fuel trim deviates significantly from the bank average — a single-cylinder injector or intake fault.

t: Cylinder 3 Air-Fuel Ratio Imbalance
s: Cylinder 3 fuel trim deviates significantly from the bank average — a single-cylinder injector or intake fault.

t: Cylinder 4 Air-Fuel Ratio Imbalance
s: Cylinder 4 fuel trim deviates significantly from the bank average — a single-cylinder injector or intake fault.

t: Cylinder 5 Air-Fuel Ratio Imbalance
s: Cylinder 5 fuel trim deviates significantly from the bank average — a single-cylinder injector or intake fault.

t: Cylinder 6 Air-Fuel Ratio Imbalance
s: Cylinder 6 fuel trim deviates significantly from the bank average — a single-cylinder injector or intake fault.

t: Cylinder 7 Air-Fuel Ratio Imbalance
s: Cylinder 7 fuel trim deviates significantly from the bank average — a single-cylinder injector or intake fault.

t: Cylinder 8 Air-Fuel Ratio Imbalance
s: Cylinder 8 fuel trim deviates significantly from the bank average — a single-cylinder injector or intake fault.

t: Cylinder 9 Air-Fuel Ratio Imbalance
s: Cylinder 9 fuel trim deviates significantly from the bank average — a single-cylinder injector or intake fault.

t: Cylinder 10 Air-Fuel Ratio Imbalance
s: Cylinder 10 fuel trim deviates significantly from the bank average — a single-cylinder injector or intake fault.

t: Cylinder 11 Air-Fuel Ratio Imbalance
s: Cylinder 11 fuel trim deviates significantly from the bank average — a single-cylinder injector or intake fault.

t: Cylinder 12 Air-Fuel Ratio Imbalance
s: Cylinder 12 fuel trim deviates significantly from the bank average — a single-cylinder injector or intake fault.

t: Reductant Level Sensor B Circuit
s: A general electrical fault is reported on DEF level sensor B — open, shorted, or out of plausibility. Some tanks use multiple level senders to cover irregular tank shapes; this is the primary one.

t: Reductant Level Sensor B Circuit Range/Performance
s: DEF level sensor B reading is in electrical range but plausibility check fails — value sticks, jumps, or does not match consumption.

t: Reductant Level Sensor B Circuit Low
s: DEF level sensor B reads below lower plausibility — short to ground. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Reductant Level Sensor B Circuit High
s: DEF level sensor B reads above upper plausibility — open signal or short to B+. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Reductant Level Sensor B Circuit Intermittent/Erratic
s: DEF level sensor B signal jumps or drops out intermittently — typically a corroded sender contact or wiring issue.

t: Reductant Level Sensor C Circuit
s: A general electrical fault is reported on DEF level sensor C — open, shorted, or out of plausibility. Some tanks use multiple level senders to cover irregular tank shapes; this is the primary one.

t: Reductant Level Sensor C Circuit Range/Performance
s: DEF level sensor C reading is in electrical range but plausibility check fails — value sticks, jumps, or does not match consumption.

t: Reductant Level Sensor C Circuit Low
s: DEF level sensor C reads below lower plausibility — short to ground. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Reductant Level Sensor C Circuit High
s: DEF level sensor C reads above upper plausibility — open signal or short to B+. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Reductant Level Sensor C Circuit Intermittent/Erratic
s: DEF level sensor C signal jumps or drops out intermittently — typically a corroded sender contact or wiring issue.

t: Excessive Time To Enter Closed Loop NOx Adsorber Control
s: The NOx adsorber control did not enter closed-loop operation within the calibrated time after engine start — typically a slow-warming NOx sensor or pre-heat heater fault.

t: Closed Loop NOx Adsorber Control At Limit - Too High
s: NOx adsorber closed-loop trim has reached the upper authority limit — the controller cannot correct further. Typically a degraded LNT or stuck-rich engine condition.

t: NOx Adsorber Over Temperature (Bank 1)
s: A temperature sensor at the NOx adsorber on bank 1 reports a value above the safe limit. Sustained over-temperature destroys the storage washcoat.

t: NOx Adsorber Over Temperature (Bank 2)
s: A temperature sensor at the NOx adsorber on bank 2 reports a value above the safe limit. Sustained over-temperature destroys the storage washcoat.

t: Cold Start Cylinder 1 Injection Timing
s: Cylinder 1 cold-start injection timing is outside the calibrated window. Diesel cold-start uses adapted injection timing per cylinder to limit white smoke and noise; deviation typically points at a worn injector or faulty crank/cam correlation.

t: Cold Start Cylinder 2 Injection Timing
s: Cylinder 2 cold-start injection timing is outside the calibrated window. Diesel cold-start uses adapted injection timing per cylinder to limit white smoke and noise; deviation typically points at a worn injector or faulty crank/cam correlation.

t: Cold Start Cylinder 3 Injection Timing
s: Cylinder 3 cold-start injection timing is outside the calibrated window. Diesel cold-start uses adapted injection timing per cylinder to limit white smoke and noise; deviation typically points at a worn injector or faulty crank/cam correlation.

t: Cold Start Cylinder 4 Injection Timing
s: Cylinder 4 cold-start injection timing is outside the calibrated window. Diesel cold-start uses adapted injection timing per cylinder to limit white smoke and noise; deviation typically points at a worn injector or faulty crank/cam correlation.

s: Cylinder 5 cold-start injection timing is outside the calibrated window. Diesel cold-start uses adapted injection timing per cylinder to limit white smoke and noise; deviation typically points at a worn injector or faulty crank/cam correlation.

t: Cold Start Cylinder 12 Injection Timing
s: Cylinder 12 cold-start injection timing is outside the calibrated window. Diesel cold-start uses adapted injection timing per cylinder to limit white smoke and noise; deviation typically points at a worn injector or faulty crank/cam correlation.

t: Reductant Heater Relay Control Circuit/Open
s: The control circuit for the relay that powers the DEF heaters is open — main DEF heating supply path interrupted.

t: Reductant Heater Relay Control Circuit Low
s: DEF heater relay control line stuck low — short to ground. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Reductant Heater Relay Control Circuit High
s: DEF heater relay control line stuck high — short to B+. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Reductant Level Sensor A Stuck
s: DEF level sensor A reading does not change over a refill or consumption window — float arm jammed or sender wiper stuck. Distinct from a circuit fault because the electrical signal stays plausible.

t: Reductant Level Sensor B Stuck
s: DEF level sensor B reading does not change over a refill or consumption window — float arm jammed or sender wiper stuck. Distinct from a circuit fault because the electrical signal stays plausible.

t: Reductant Control Module Power Relay Control Circuit/Open
s: The control circuit for the relay that supplies power to the SCR/reductant control module is open — module loses main supply on shutdown or never wakes up.

t: Reductant Control Module Power Relay Control Circuit Low
s: SCR control module power-relay control line stuck low. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Reductant Control Module Power Relay Control Circuit High
s: SCR control module power-relay control line stuck high. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Reductant Control Module Supply Voltage Circuit
s: Internal monitoring of the SCR control module reports a fault on its main supply voltage rail — separate from the relay-control diagnostics, this is the module's own self-diagnosis of its supply pin.

t: Reductant Control Module Supply Voltage Low
s: SCR control module supply voltage measured at the module pin is below the lower threshold.

t: Reductant Control Module Supply Voltage High
s: SCR control module supply voltage above the upper threshold — overvoltage.

t: Reductant Quality Module Supply Voltage Low
s: Supply voltage at the dedicated DEF quality sensor module is below the lower threshold.

t: Reductant Quality Module Performance
s: DEF quality module reports an internal performance fault — self-test failed or measurement loop unable to converge. The module may need replacement.

t: Cylinder 1 Injector B Circuit/Open
s: The drive circuit of cylinder 1 second injector (B) is open. Modern direct-injection systems sometimes use a port-injection backup; injector B refers to that secondary set.

t: Cylinder 2 Injector B Circuit/Open
s: The drive circuit of cylinder 2 second injector (B) is open. Modern direct-injection systems sometimes use a port-injection backup; injector B refers to that secondary set.

t: Cylinder 3 Injector B Circuit/Open
s: The drive circuit of cylinder 3 second injector (B) is open. Modern direct-injection systems sometimes use a port-injection backup; injector B refers to that secondary set.

s: The drive circuit of cylinder 4 second injector (B) is open. Modern direct-injection systems sometimes use a port-injection backup; injector B refers to that secondary set.

s: The drive circuit of cylinder 5 second injector (B) is open. Modern direct-injection systems sometimes use a port-injection backup; injector B refers to that secondary set.

s: The drive circuit of cylinder 6 second injector (B) is open. Modern direct-injection systems sometimes use a port-injection backup; injector B refers to that secondary set.

s: The drive circuit of cylinder 7 second injector (B) is open. Modern direct-injection systems sometimes use a port-injection backup; injector B refers to that secondary set.

s: The drive circuit of cylinder 8 second injector (B) is open. Modern direct-injection systems sometimes use a port-injection backup; injector B refers to that secondary set.

s: The drive circuit of cylinder 9 second injector (B) is open. Modern direct-injection systems sometimes use a port-injection backup; injector B refers to that secondary set.

s: The drive circuit of cylinder 10 second injector (B) is open. Modern direct-injection systems sometimes use a port-injection backup; injector B refers to that secondary set.

t: Cylinder 11 Injector B Circuit/Open
s: The drive circuit of cylinder 11 second injector (B) is open. Modern direct-injection systems sometimes use a port-injection backup; injector B refers to that secondary set.

t: Cylinder 12 Injector B Circuit/Open
s: The drive circuit of cylinder 12 second injector (B) is open. Modern direct-injection systems sometimes use a port-injection backup; injector B refers to that secondary set.

t: Cylinder 1 Injector B Circuit Low
s: Cylinder 1 second-injector (B) drive line stuck low — short to ground. A flow-deviating injector throws off cylinder balance — adjacent cylinders run leaner/richer to compensate, often masking the root cause until the trim limit is hit.

t: Cylinder 1 Injector B Circuit High
s: Cylinder 1 second-injector (B) drive line stuck high — short to B+. A flow-deviating injector throws off cylinder balance — adjacent cylinders run leaner/richer to compensate, often masking the root cause until the trim limit is hit.

t: Reductant Heater A Current Too Low
s: DEF heater A current draw is below threshold — partially-open winding.

t: Cylinder 2 Injector B Circuit Low
s: Cylinder 2 second-injector (B) drive line stuck low — short to ground. A flow-deviating injector throws off cylinder balance — adjacent cylinders run leaner/richer to compensate, often masking the root cause until the trim limit is hit.
