t: Fuel Control System B Too Lean Off Idle (Bank 1)
s: The secondary fuel control loop B on bank 1 reports a lean condition outside idle. On dual-injection systems (port + direct) the secondary loop trims port injection independently from the main loop.

t: Fuel Control System B Too Rich Off Idle (Bank 1)
s: The secondary fuel control loop B on bank 1 reports a rich condition outside idle.

t: Fuel Control System B Too Lean Off Idle (Bank 2)
s: The secondary fuel control loop B on bank 2 reports a lean condition outside idle. On dual-injection systems (port + direct) the secondary loop trims port injection independently from the main loop.

t: Fuel Control System B Too Rich Off Idle (Bank 2)
s: The secondary fuel control loop B on bank 2 reports a rich condition outside idle.

t: Fuel Control System B Too Lean at Idle (Bank 1)
s: Secondary fuel control loop B reports a lean condition at idle on bank 1. Idle is sensitive to vacuum leaks since manifold flow is low.

t: Fuel Control System B Too Rich at Idle (Bank 1)
s: The secondary fuel control loop B on bank 1 reports a rich condition outside idle.

t: Fuel Control System B Too Lean at Idle (Bank 2)
s: Secondary fuel control loop B reports a lean condition at idle on bank 2. Idle is sensitive to vacuum leaks since manifold flow is low.

t: Fuel Control System B Too Rich at Idle (Bank 2)
s: The secondary fuel control loop B reports a rich condition at idle on bank 2. Idle rich often means injectors that leak at low duty cycle.

t: Fuel Control System B Too Lean at Higher Load (Bank 1)
s: Secondary fuel control loop B on bank 1 reports a lean condition at higher load. Lean at high load typically points at fuel pressure shortfall, restricted injectors, or charge-air leak.

t: Fuel Control System B Too Rich at Higher Load (Bank 1)
s: The secondary fuel control loop B on bank 1 reports a rich condition outside idle.

t: Fuel Control System B Too Lean at Higher Load (Bank 2)
s: Secondary fuel control loop B on bank 2 reports a lean condition at higher load. Lean at high load typically points at fuel pressure shortfall, restricted injectors, or charge-air leak.

t: Fuel Control System B Too Rich at Higher Load (Bank 2)
s: The secondary fuel control loop B reports a rich condition at higher load on bank 2. Rich at high load typically points to a stuck-closed return on the fuel pressure regulator or over-delivering injectors.

t: NOx Sensor Circuit (Bank 1)
s: The NOx sensor on bank 1 — typically upstream of the SCR catalyst — does not communicate or sends an invalid signal. NOx sensors are smart sensors with their own controller on the CAN bus; faults often involve heater failure or bus dropout.

t: NOx Sensor Range/Performance (Bank 1)
s: The bank 1 NOx sensor reports values outside the plausibility window against modeled NOx and the downstream sensor. Often slow response from contamination by oil ash or coolant.

t: NOx Sensor Circuit Low (Bank 1)
s: The NOx sensor signal on bank 1 is below the lower limit. Either short to ground on the analog return, sensor failed at low end, or pump cell stuck.

t: NOx Sensor Circuit High (Bank 1)
s: The NOx sensor signal on bank 1 is above the upper limit — short to supply, open ground, or a sensor that failed high. SCR control runs in default mode.

t: NOx Sensor Circuit Intermittent (Bank 1)
s: The bank 1 NOx sensor signal drops out intermittently. Typical cause is a marginal connector, harness chafing near hot exhaust components, or thermal-cycling solder joints inside the smart sensor.

t: NOx Sensor Heater Control Circuit (Bank 1)
s: The heater inside the bank 1 NOx sensor cannot be controlled — open, short, or failed driver. Without proper heater operation the sensor never reaches its operating temperature and reports invalid data.

t: NOx Sensor Circuit (Bank 2)
s: A general electrical fault on the bank 2 NOx sensor circuit. NOx sensors are smart sensors with a CAN/LIN interface; this code typically indicates that the sensor module no longer responds.

t: NOx Sensor Circuit Range/Performance (Bank 2)
s: The bank 2 NOx sensor returns values outside the expected range for the operating point — for example a non-zero reading at engine off or unchanged values during load changes.

t: NOx Sensor Heater Circuit (Bank 2)
s: A general electrical fault on the heater of the bank 2 NOx sensor. NOx sensors must reach roughly 800 C to measure correctly; without heater current the sensor never enters its operating range.

t: NOx Sensor Heater Sense Circuit Range/Performance (Bank 2)
s: The heater feedback (sense) line on the bank 2 NOx sensor reports values outside the expected window — heating is happening but the temperature does not match the model.

t: NOx Sensor Supply Voltage Circuit (Bank 1 Sensor 1)
s: The supply voltage rail to the NOx sensor on bank 1 sensor 1 is faulty — typically a fuse, harness break, or short. NOx sensors need a clean stable supply for the heater and the wide-band measurement cell.

t: NOx Sensor Supply Voltage Circuit (Bank 1 Sensor 2)
s: The supply voltage rail to the NOx sensor on bank 1 sensor 2 is faulty — typically a fuse, harness break, or short. NOx sensors need a clean stable supply for the heater and the wide-band measurement cell.

t: NOx Sensor Supply Voltage Circuit (Bank 2 Sensor 1)
s: The supply voltage rail to the NOx sensor on bank 2 sensor 1 is faulty — typically a fuse, harness break, or short. NOx sensors need a clean stable supply for the heater and the wide-band measurement cell.

t: NOx Sensor Supply Voltage Circuit (Bank 2 Sensor 2)
s: The supply voltage rail to the NOx sensor on bank 2 sensor 2 is faulty — typically a fuse, harness break, or short. NOx sensors need a clean stable supply for the heater and the wide-band measurement cell.

t: NOx Sensor Heater Control Circuit Range/Performance (Bank 1 Sensor 1)
s: The heater control loop for the NOx sensor at bank 1 sensor 1 reports off-spec behaviour — heater current draw or response time outside the calibrated range. NOx sensors only operate above ~600 °C, so heater control is critical.

t: NOx Sensor Heater Control Circuit Range/Performance (Bank 1 Sensor 2)
s: The heater control loop for the NOx sensor at bank 1 sensor 2 reports off-spec behaviour — heater current draw or response time outside the calibrated range. NOx sensors only operate above ~600 °C, so heater control is critical.

t: NOx Sensor Circuit Intermittent (Bank 2)
s: The bank 2 NOx sensor reports correctly some of the time but intermittently drops out. Often a marginal connector, a wiring chafe or a sensor that fails when warm.

t: NOx Sensor Heater Sense Circuit Intermittent (Bank 2)
s: The heater sense line on the bank 2 NOx sensor produces intermittent readings. Often a thermal fault inside the sensor or a connector under temperature cycling.

t: NOx Sensor Heater Sense Circuit Intermittent (Bank 1 Sensor 1)
s: The feedback line that monitors the NOx sensor heater current draw on bank 1 sensor 1 jumps or drops out intermittently — connector or harness issue.

t: NOx Sensor Circuit (Bank 2)
s: A general electrical fault on the NOx sensor on bank 2 — open, shorted, or out of plausibility. Used by SCR control and OBD NOx monitoring.

t: NOx Sensor Circuit Range/Performance (Bank 2)
s: NOx sensor on bank 2 reads in range but plausibility check fails — slow response, drift, or value does not match the model expectation.

t: NOx Sensor Circuit Low (Bank 2)
s: NOx sensor on bank 2 reads below lower plausibility — short to ground. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: NOx Sensor Circuit High (Bank 2)
s: NOx sensor on bank 2 reads above upper plausibility — open signal or short to B+. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: NOx Sensor Circuit Intermittent (Bank 2)
s: NOx sensor on bank 2 signal jumps or drops out intermittently. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: NOx Sensor Heater Control Circuit/Open (Bank 2)
s: The drive circuit for the NOx sensor heater on bank 2 is open. The sensor cannot reach operating temperature.

t: NOx Sensor Heater Control Circuit Low (Bank 2)
s: NOx heater drive line on bank 2 stuck low — short to ground. The sensor heater warms the element to operating temperature (~600 °C); without it the lambda sensor stays inactive in closed loop and the ECM cannot trim mixture.

t: NOx Sensor 1/2 Correlation (Bank 1)
s: NOx sensors 1 and 2 on bank 1 disagree by more than the allowed tolerance over a drive cycle. One sensor drifts, or there is a real NOx gradient that exceeds the calibrated map.

t: NOx Sensor 1/2 Correlation (Bank 2)
s: NOx sensors 1 and 2 on bank 2 disagree by more than the allowed tolerance over a drive cycle. One sensor drifts, or there is a real NOx gradient that exceeds the calibrated map.

t: Reductant Heater B Current Too Low
s: DEF heater B current draw is below threshold — partially-open winding.

t: Reductant Heater B Current Too High
s: DEF heater B current draw exceeds threshold — element partially shorted.

t: Reductant Heater C Current Too Low
s: DEF heater B current draw is below threshold — partially-open winding.

t: Reductant Heater C Current Too High
s: DEF heater B current draw exceeds threshold — element partially shorted.

t: NOx Sensor Heater Control Circuit High (Bank 2)
s: NOx heater drive line on bank 2 stuck high — short to B+. The sensor heater warms the element to operating temperature (~600 °C); without it the lambda sensor stays inactive in closed loop and the ECM cannot trim mixture.

t: NOx Sensor Heater Sense Circuit (Bank 2)
s: The feedback line that monitors NOx sensor heater current draw on bank 2 is faulty — open, shorted, or out of plausibility.

t: NOx Sensor Heater Sense Circuit Range/Performance (Bank 2)
s: NOx sensor heater sense feedback on bank 2 reads in range but plausibility check fails. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: NOx Sensor Heater Sense Circuit Low (Bank 2)
s: NOx heater sense line on bank 2 below lower plausibility — short to ground. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: NOx Sensor Heater Sense Circuit High (Bank 2)
s: NOx heater sense line on bank 2 above upper plausibility — open signal or short to B+. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: NOx Sensor Heater Sense Circuit Intermittent (Bank 2)
s: The feedback line that monitors the NOx sensor heater current draw on bank 2 sensor 1 jumps or drops out intermittently — connector or harness issue.

t: Barometric Pressure Circuit
s: The ECU detects a general fault in the barometric pressure sensor circuit. The atmospheric pressure value used for altitude compensation and fuel/air calculation is not available or implausible.

t: Barometric Pressure Circuit Range/Performance
s: The barometric pressure sensor delivers an electrically valid signal that does not match the operating point — for example pressure jumps that do not match altitude changes, or values stuck near full scale.

t: Barometric Pressure Sensor Circuit Low
s: The barometric (ambient) pressure sensor reports a voltage below the valid window. The ECU uses this signal to compensate fuel and boost calculations for altitude — a stuck-low value tricks the ECU into thinking the car is at high altitude.

t: Barometric Pressure Sensor Circuit High
s: The barometric pressure sensor reports a voltage above the valid window. Usually a short to supply or an open ground.

t: Barometric Pressure Sensor B Circuit
s: A general electrical fault on barometric pressure sensor B — open, shorted, or out of plausibility. The sensor measures atmospheric pressure for altitude compensation; sensor B is a redundant or secondary unit.

t: Barometric Pressure Sensor B Circuit Range/Performance
s: Barometric sensor B reads in range but plausibility check fails — slow response or stuck reading. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Barometric Pressure Sensor B Circuit Low
s: Barometric sensor B below lower plausibility — short to ground. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Barometric Pressure Sensor B Circuit High
s: Barometric sensor B above upper plausibility — open signal or short to B+. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Barometric Pressure Sensor B Circuit Intermittent/Erratic
s: Barometric sensor B signal jumps or drops out intermittently. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Barometric Pressure Sensor A/B Correlation
s: Barometric pressure sensors A and B disagree by more than the allowed tolerance. Used for redundancy on hybrid and emission-critical systems.

t: Barometric Pressure Circuit Low
s: Voltage from the barometric pressure sensor is below the calibrated minimum. The signal is too low for any plausible atmospheric pressure, suggesting a short to ground or sensor fault.

t: Barometric Pressure Circuit High
s: The barometric pressure sensor reports a voltage above the upper limit. The signal cannot represent any realistic atmospheric pressure, indicating an open circuit or short to supply voltage.

t: O2 Sensor Signal Circuit Shorted to Heater Circuit (Bank 1 Sensor 2)
s: The O2 sensor signal wire on bank 1 sensor 2 has shorted to the heater wire. This pulls the signal away from a measurement of exhaust composition and produces erratic fuel-trim behaviour.

t: O2 Sensor Signal Circuit Shorted to Heater Circuit (Bank 1 Sensor 3)
s: The O2 sensor signal wire on bank 1 sensor 3 has shorted to the heater wire. This pulls the signal away from a measurement of exhaust composition and produces erratic fuel-trim behaviour.

t: O2 Sensor Signal Circuit Shorted to Heater Circuit (Bank 2 Sensor 1)
s: The O2 sensor signal wire on bank 2 sensor 1 has shorted to the heater wire. This pulls the signal away from a measurement of exhaust composition and produces erratic fuel-trim behaviour.

t: O2 Sensor Signal Circuit Shorted to Heater Circuit (Bank 2 Sensor 2)
s: The O2 sensor signal wire on bank 2 sensor 2 has shorted to the heater wire. This pulls the signal away from a measurement of exhaust composition and produces erratic fuel-trim behaviour.

t: O2 Sensor Signal Circuit Shorted to Heater Circuit (Bank 2 Sensor 3)
s: The O2 sensor signal wire on bank 2 sensor 3 has shorted to the heater wire. This pulls the signal away from a measurement of exhaust composition and produces erratic fuel-trim behaviour.

t: O2 Sensor Pumping Current Circuit (Bank 1 Sensor 1)
s: The pumping current line of the wide-band lambda sensor on bank 1 sensor 1 is unable to operate. Wide-band sensors regulate exhaust oxygen to a reference cell via this pumping current — without it, AFR cannot be measured.

t: O2 Sensor Pumping Current Circuit Low (Bank 1 Sensor 1)
s: Pumping current at the bank 1 sensor 1 wide-band lambda probe is below the lower limit — short to ground or sensor at end of life.

t: O2 Sensor Pumping Current Circuit High (Bank 1 Sensor 1)
s: Pumping current at the bank 1 sensor 1 wide-band lambda probe is above the upper limit — short to supply or open return path.

t: Reductant Heater E Control Circuit
s: A general electrical fault on the DEF heater E control circuit. On heavy-duty trucks the DEF system can have multiple heaters along the supply path; E is the fifth.

t: Reductant Heater F Control Circuit
s: A general electrical fault on the DEF heater E control circuit. On heavy-duty trucks the DEF system can have multiple heaters along the supply path; E is the fifth.

t: O2 Sensor Pumping Current Range/Performance (Bank 1)
s: The pumping current of the wide-band O2 sensor on bank 1 is in electrical range but plausibility check fails. Wide-band sensors use a pumping cell that adjusts oxygen concentration in a reference chamber; the current required is proportional to lambda.

t: O2 Sensor Pumping Current Range/Performance (Bank 2)
s: The pumping current of the wide-band O2 sensor on bank 2 is in electrical range but plausibility check fails. Wide-band sensors use a pumping cell that adjusts oxygen concentration in a reference chamber; the current required is proportional to lambda.

t: O2 Sensor Reference Resistance Out Of Range (Bank 1)
s: The reference resistance of the wide-band O2 sensor on bank 1 is outside the calibrated range — used by the controller to set the operating point of the pumping cell. Drift here indicates sensor element ageing or contamination.

t: O2 Sensor Reference Resistance Out Of Range (Bank 2)
s: The reference resistance of the wide-band O2 sensor on bank 2 is outside the calibrated range — used by the controller to set the operating point of the pumping cell. Drift here indicates sensor element ageing or contamination.

t: O2 Sensor Pumping Current Circuit (Bank 2 Sensor 1)
s: Bank 2 counterpart of P2237. The wide-band lambda sensor on bank 2 sensor 1 cannot operate its pumping current circuit.

t: O2 Sensor Pumping Current Circuit Low (Bank 2 Sensor 1)
s: The pumping-current line of the wide-band oxygen sensor on bank 2, sensor 1 is below the lower threshold. Wide-band sensors use a small pumping current to keep their measuring cell at lambda 1; a low value usually means an open or shorted pump cell.

t: O2 Sensor Pumping Current Circuit High (Bank 2 Sensor 1)
s: The pumping-current line of the bank 2, sensor 1 wide-band oxygen sensor reads above the upper threshold. Common with a short to a positive supply or a damaged sensor amplifier in the ECU.

t: O2 Sensor Reference Voltage Circuit (Bank 1 Sensor 1)
s: The reference voltage line that powers the wide-band sensor cell on bank 1 sensor 1 is missing or out of tolerance. Without correct reference, the AFR calculation is invalid.

t: O2 Sensor Reference Voltage Circuit Range/Performance (Bank 1 Sensor 1)
s: The reference voltage that the ECU supplies to the wide-band oxygen sensor on bank 1, sensor 1 drifts outside the expected range. The reference is the baseline against which the cell voltages are measured; a drift falsifies every lambda calculation.

t: O2 Sensor Reference Voltage Circuit Low (Bank 1 Sensor 1)
s: The reference voltage feeding the bank 1, sensor 1 wide-band oxygen sensor is below the lower threshold. Often a short to ground in the wiring or a failed reference output stage.

t: O2 Sensor Reference Voltage Circuit High (Bank 1 Sensor 1)
s: The reference voltage on the bank 1, sensor 1 wide-band oxygen sensor reads above the upper threshold. Common with a short to a positive supply or a corroded connector with leakage current.

t: O2 Sensor Reference Voltage Circuit (Bank 2 Sensor 1)
s: Bank 2 counterpart of P2243. Reference voltage on the bank 2 sensor 1 wide-band probe is missing or out of tolerance.

t: O2 Sensor Reference Voltage Circuit Range/Performance (Bank 2 Sensor 1)
s: Bank 2 counterpart to P2244. The reference voltage at the wide-band oxygen sensor on bank 2, sensor 1 is outside its expected range and falsifies the lambda calculation.

t: O2 Sensor Reference Voltage Circuit Low (Bank 2 Sensor 1)
s: Bank 2 counterpart to P2245. The reference voltage on bank 2, sensor 1 is below the lower threshold — typically a short to ground or failed ECU output.

t: NOx Sensor Heater Resistance (Bank 1 Sensor 1)
s: The measured heater element resistance of the NOx sensor on bank 1 sensor 1 is outside the calibrated range. Used as a sensor-element health check.

t: NOx Sensor Heater Resistance (Bank 1 Sensor 2)
s: The measured heater element resistance of the NOx sensor on bank 1 sensor 2 is outside the calibrated range. Used as a sensor-element health check.

t: Alternative Fuel Tank Shutoff Valve A Control Circuit/Open
s: The drive circuit for the shutoff valve at the alternative-fuel tank A is open. Bi-fuel vehicles (CNG, LPG, hydrogen) use a high-pressure shutoff valve at the tank for safety; with this fault the alternative fuel cannot be drawn from the tank.

t: Alternative Fuel Tank Shutoff Valve A Control Circuit Performance/Stuck Off
s: Alternative-fuel shutoff valve A is electrically OK but does not open when commanded. Vehicle remains on petrol/diesel only.

t: Alternative Fuel Tank Shutoff Valve A Control Circuit Low
s: Shutoff valve A drive line stuck low — short to ground. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Alternative Fuel Tank Shutoff Valve A Control Circuit High
s: Shutoff valve A drive line stuck high — short to B+. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: O2 Sensor Reference Voltage Circuit High (Bank 2 Sensor 1)
s: Bank 2 counterpart to P2246. The reference voltage at bank 2, sensor 1 is above the upper threshold, usually due to a short to positive or connector leakage.

t: O2 Sensor Negative Current Control Circuit (Bank 1 Sensor 1)
s: The negative current control line (Vs−) of the wide-band lambda sensor on bank 1 sensor 1 is open or out of range. Used as the virtual ground reference for the Nernst cell.

t: O2 Sensor Negative Current Control Circuit Low (Bank 1 Sensor 1)
s: The negative-current (Vs negative) line on the bank 1, sensor 1 wide-band oxygen sensor is below the lower threshold. This line is the return path for the Nernst cell; loss of the signal blocks the lambda calculation.

t: O2 Sensor Negative Current Control Circuit High (Bank 1 Sensor 1)
s: The Vs negative line on bank 1, sensor 1 reads above the upper threshold. Often a short to positive or a damaged ECU sensor amplifier.

t: O2 Sensor Negative Current Control Circuit Low (Bank 2 Sensor 1)
s: Bank 2 counterpart to P2252. The Vs negative line on the wide-band oxygen sensor at bank 2, sensor 1 is below the lower threshold.

t: O2 Sensor Negative Current Control Circuit High (Bank 2 Sensor 1)
s: Bank 2 counterpart to P2253. The Vs negative line on bank 2, sensor 1 reads above the upper threshold.

t: O2 Sensor Heater Control Circuit Low Bank 1 Sensor 2
s: The ECU detects a low-side voltage below threshold on the heater control of the rear oxygen sensor on bank 1. The heater driver may be stuck on or shorted to ground.

t: Secondary Air Injection System Control A Circuit Low
s: The control circuit of the secondary air injection system A reads a voltage below specification. Either the relay output is shorted to ground or the wiring/load is faulty.

t: Secondary Air Injection System Control A Circuit High
s: The control circuit A of the secondary air injection system shows a voltage above the calibrated upper threshold. Likely a short to battery voltage or an open in the relay coil/output.

t: Secondary Air Injection System Control B Circuit Low
s: The drive line for secondary air injection (AIR) control circuit B is stuck low. Secondary air is pumped into the exhaust manifold during cold start to speed up catalyst light-off.

t: NOx Sensor Calibration Memory (Bank 1 Sensor 1)
s: The factory calibration memory of the NOx sensor on bank 1 sensor 1 cannot be read or contains a checksum error. Each NOx sensor ships with a unique calibration table programmed at the factory.

t: NOx Sensor Calibration Memory (Bank 1 Sensor 2)
s: The factory calibration memory of the NOx sensor on bank 1 sensor 2 cannot be read or contains a checksum error. Each NOx sensor ships with a unique calibration table programmed at the factory.

t: NOx Sensor Performance - Signal Biased/Stuck High (Bank 1 Sensor 1)
s: The NOx sensor on bank 1 sensor 1 reads suspiciously high for too long — biased upward or stuck. Common after sensor poisoning by silicon, sulphur, or oil ash.

t: NOx Sensor Performance - Signal Biased/Stuck Low (Bank 1 Sensor 1)
s: The NOx sensor on bank 1 sensor 1 reads suspiciously high for too long — biased upward or stuck. Common after sensor poisoning by silicon, sulphur, or oil ash.

t: NOx Sensor Performance - Signal Biased/Stuck High (Bank 1 Sensor 2)
s: The NOx sensor on bank 1 sensor 2 reads suspiciously high for too long — biased upward or stuck. Common after sensor poisoning by silicon, sulphur, or oil ash.

t: NOx Sensor Performance - Signal Biased/Stuck Low (Bank 1 Sensor 2)
s: The NOx sensor on bank 1 sensor 2 reads suspiciously low for too long — biased downward or stuck. Common after sensor element ageing, condensate damage from cold start, or wiring fault that pulls the signal down.

t: Secondary Air Injection System Control B Circuit High
s: AIR system control B drive line stuck high — short to B+. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Turbo/Super Charger Bypass Valve Mechanical
s: The ECU detects a mechanical fault in the turbocharger or supercharger bypass valve. The valve does not open or close as commanded, causing boost-pressure errors and reduced engine response.

t: Turbo Boost Pressure Not Detected - Mechanical
s: The ECU does not detect any meaningful boost pressure increase under load even though the turbocharger is commanded. Likely a mechanical defect in the turbo, charge-air piping or wastegate.

t: Turbo/Super Charger Boost System Performance
s: Boost pressure does not match the requested target during normal operation. The deviation between commanded and measured boost exceeds the calibrated tolerance, often due to leaks, sticking actuators or sensor errors.

t: Water in Fuel Sensor Circuit
s: The ECU detects an electrical fault in the diesel water-in-fuel sensor circuit. Until repaired, water contamination in the fuel filter cannot be detected, risking damage to the high-pressure pump and injectors.

t: Water in Fuel Sensor Circuit Range/Performance
s: The ECU detects that the water-in-fuel sensor signal is implausible compared to operating conditions. The reading does not change as expected during fuel filter purge or warm-up.

t: Water in Fuel Sensor A Circuit Low
s: The water-in-fuel sensor at the diesel fuel filter reads below lower plausibility — short to ground. The sensor warns the driver to drain the filter when condensate accumulates.

t: Water in Fuel Sensor A Circuit High
s: Water-in-fuel sensor reads above upper plausibility — open signal or short to B+. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Water in Fuel Sensor A Circuit Intermittent
s: Water-in-fuel sensor signal jumps or drops out intermittently. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Water in Fuel Condition - Sensor A
s: The water-in-fuel sensor at fuel filter A has detected water above the warning threshold. The fuel filter must be drained; running the engine on water-contaminated diesel can damage the high-pressure pump and injectors.

t: Water in Fuel Lamp Control Circuit
s: A general electrical fault on the drive line for the dashboard water-in-fuel warning lamp.

t: Turbocharger/Supercharger A Boost Pressure Too High - Mechanical
s: Boost pressure on turbo A exceeds the upper threshold and the cause is mechanical (wastegate stuck closed, VGT vanes stuck closed) rather than electrical drive fault.

t: Turbocharger Boost Control A Slow Response
s: Turbocharger boost control A reaches the commanded boost pressure too slowly. Common with worn VGT vanes, sticking wastegate, or boost-control solenoid lag.

t: Particulate Filter Deteriorated/Missing Substrate (Bank 1)
s: The DPF substrate on bank 1 has been detected as cracked, melted, or removed. Differential pressure across the filter is too low for the measured exhaust flow — the soot trap no longer works.

t: Particulate Filter Deteriorated/Missing Substrate (Bank 2)
s: The DPF substrate on bank 2 has been detected as cracked, melted, or removed. Differential pressure across the filter is too low for the measured exhaust flow — the soot trap no longer works.

t: Turbocharger Boost Control B Slow Response
s: Turbocharger boost control A reaches the commanded boost pressure too slowly. Common with worn VGT vanes, sticking wastegate, or boost-control solenoid lag.

t: O2 Sensor Signal Stuck Lean (Bank 1, Sensor 2)
s: The downstream oxygen sensor on bank 1 reports a constant lean signal during a forced-rich command from the ECU. Suggests either a sensor that has lost its rich-side response, or a catalyst that is so degraded that no rich exhaust reaches the downstream sensor (cross-check P0420).

t: O2 Sensor Signal Stuck Rich (Bank 1, Sensor 2)
s: The downstream oxygen sensor on bank 1 reports a constant rich signal during a forced-lean command. Suggests a failed downstream sensor or a sensor contaminated by oil/coolant from upstream engine wear.

t: O2 Sensor Signal Stuck Lean (Bank 2, Sensor 2)
s: The downstream oxygen sensor on bank 2 reports a constant lean signal during a forced-rich command from the ECU. Suggests either a sensor that has lost its rich-side response, or a catalyst that is so degraded that no rich exhaust reaches the downstream sensor (cross-check P0420).

t: O2 Sensor Signal Stuck Rich (Bank 2, Sensor 2)
s: The downstream oxygen sensor on bank 2 reports a constant rich signal during a forced-lean command. Suggests a failed downstream sensor or a sensor contaminated by oil/coolant from upstream engine wear.

t: O2 Sensor Signal Biased/Stuck Lean (Bank 1 Sensor 3)
s: O2 sensor at bank 1 sensor 3 reports a lean reading suspiciously stable for too long. Sensor 3 is typically downstream of the second catalyst on systems with three-stage aftertreatment.

t: O2 Sensor Signal Biased/Stuck Rich (Bank 1 Sensor 3)
s: O2 sensor at bank 1 sensor 3 reports a lean reading suspiciously stable for too long. Sensor 3 is typically downstream of the second catalyst on systems with three-stage aftertreatment.

t: O2 Sensor Signal Biased/Stuck Lean (Bank 2 Sensor 3)
s: O2 sensor at bank 2 sensor 3 reports a lean reading suspiciously stable for too long. Sensor 3 is typically downstream of the second catalyst on systems with three-stage aftertreatment.

t: O2 Sensor Signal Biased/Stuck Rich (Bank 2 Sensor 3)
s: O2 sensor at bank 2 sensor 3 reports a rich reading suspiciously stable for too long. Sensor 3 is typically downstream of the second catalyst on systems with three-stage aftertreatment; sensor coated with carbon or fuel residue is the typical cause.

t: O2 Sensor Signals Swapped Bank 1 Sensor 3 / Bank 2 Sensor 3
s: The PCM detects that the O2 sensor signals from bank 1 sensor 3 and bank 2 sensor 3 are swapped — typically a wiring crossover after sensor replacement.

t: Intake Air System Leak
s: The ECU has detected a discrepancy between commanded throttle/intake conditions and the resulting MAP/MAF that points to unmetered air entering after the throttle body. On turbocharged engines this often means a cracked or disconnected boost pipe between turbo and intake; on naturally aspirated engines, a hole in the intake manifold or hose.

t: Barometric Pressure Sensor C Circuit
s: A general electrical fault on barometric pressure sensor C — open, shorted, or out of plausibility. The sensor measures atmospheric pressure for altitude compensation; sensor C is a redundant or secondary unit.

t: Barometric Pressure Sensor C Circuit Range/Performance
s: Barometric sensor C reads in range but plausibility check fails — slow response or stuck reading. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Barometric Pressure Sensor C Circuit Low
s: Barometric sensor C below lower plausibility — short to ground. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Barometric Pressure Sensor C Circuit High
s: Barometric sensor C above upper plausibility — open signal or short to B+. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Barometric Pressure Sensor C Circuit Intermittent/Erratic
s: Barometric sensor C signal jumps or drops out intermittently. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Air Flow Restriction/Air Leak Between Air Filter and MAF (Bank 2)
s: Restriction or leak detected in the intake plumbing between the air filter and the mass-air-flow sensor on bank 2 — typically a cracked intake boot or torn filter element.

t: Air Flow Restriction / Air Filter Restriction
s: The ECU detects an excessive intake restriction. Either the air filter is heavily clogged or an obstruction in the intake duct is reducing airflow below the expected level.

t: Air Leak Between MAF and Throttle Body
s: The ECU detects unmetered air entering between the mass airflow sensor and the throttle body. The actual airflow into the engine is greater than what the MAF measures, leading to lean conditions.

t: Air Leak Between Throttle Body and Intake Valves
s: The ECU detects unmetered air entering downstream of the throttle body. Typical sources are leaking intake-manifold gaskets, vacuum hoses or PCV components.

t: Injector Control Pressure Sensor Circuit
s: A general electrical fault on the injector control pressure (ICP) sensor — used on HEUI (Hydraulically actuated Electronically controlled Unit Injector) systems on heavy-duty diesel engines such as the Ford Powerstroke 6.0/7.3 and Caterpillar engines. ICP measures the high-pressure oil that actuates the injectors.

t: Injector Control Pressure Sensor Circuit Range/Performance
s: The injector control pressure (ICP) sensor on HEUI diesel systems reports a value that does not match expected operating range or commanded pressure. Affects fuel injection timing and quantity.

t: Injector Control Pressure Sensor Circuit Low
s: The voltage of the injector control pressure sensor is below the calibrated minimum. The signal indicates either a short to ground or a sensor that has lost its supply.

t: Injector Control Pressure Sensor Circuit High
s: The voltage of the injector control pressure sensor is above the calibrated maximum. Likely a short to supply voltage or an open ground in the sensor circuit.

t: Injector Control Pressure Sensor Intermittent
s: The injector control pressure sensor signal is intermittently lost or out of range. Often caused by intermittent wiring faults or a failing sensor that drops out under vibration or temperature.

t: Injector Control Pressure Too High
s: ICP is above the upper threshold during operation. Typically the injection pressure regulator (IPR) is stuck closed or the high-pressure oil pump runs above target.

t: Injector Control Pressure Too High - Engine Off
s: ICP remains above threshold after engine shutdown — pressure does not bleed off as expected, indicating a stuck check valve or sensor offset error.

t: Fuel Pressure Regulator A - Forced Engine Shutdown
s: Fuel pressure regulator A has been detected in a state that the PCM considers unsafe and the engine has been shut down. Typical for runaway pressure on the rail or stuck-open regulator.

t: Fuel Pressure Regulator B - Forced Engine Shutdown
s: Fuel pressure regulator A has been detected in a state that the PCM considers unsafe and the engine has been shut down. Typical for runaway pressure on the rail or stuck-open regulator.

t: Fuel Pressure Regulator A Exceeded Control Limits - Pressure Too Low
s: Fuel pressure regulator A cannot maintain target pressure — measured pressure stays below the lower control limit despite full closed-loop authority. Typical for a leaking regulator, weak high-pressure pump, or restricted suction line.

t: Fuel Pressure Regulator A Exceeded Control Limits - Pressure Too High
s: Fuel pressure regulator A cannot reduce pressure below the upper control limit — typically a stuck-closed return path or runaway high-pressure pump.

t: Fuel Pressure Regulator A Exceeded Learning Limits - Too Low
s: The adaptive learning offset for fuel pressure regulator A has reached the lower limit. Long-term drift indicates progressive wear of the regulator or pump.

t: Fuel Pressure Regulator A Exceeded Learning Limits - Too High
s: The adaptive learning offset for fuel pressure regulator A has reached the lower limit. Long-term drift indicates progressive wear of the regulator or pump.

t: Injector Control Pressure Too Low
s: ICP is below the lower threshold — typically IPR stuck open, leaking high-pressure oil rail, or weak HP oil pump.

t: Injector Control Pressure Too Low - Engine Cranking
s: During cranking, ICP does not build up enough to fire injectors. Typical no-start condition on HEUI engines from a stuck-open IPR or weak HP oil pump.

t: Injector Control Pressure Erratic
s: ICP signal is unstable or jumps — connector or sensor issue, or air in the high-pressure oil rail. A flow-deviating injector throws off cylinder balance — adjacent cylinders run leaner/richer to compensate, often masking the root cause until the trim limit is hit.

t: Fuel Pressure Regulator 2 Performance
s: The high-pressure fuel pressure regulator (volume control or pressure control valve on a direct-injection rail) is not maintaining commanded pressure. Often the rail-pressure sensor reading drifts away from the setpoint under load.

t: Fuel Pressure Regulator 2 Control Circuit
s: Electrical fault on the control line to the high-pressure fuel pressure regulator solenoid. Without a working drive signal the rail cannot maintain pressure and the engine may not start or runs in limp-home.

t: Fuel Pressure Regulator 2 Control Circuit Low
s: Drive voltage to the second fuel pressure regulator solenoid is below the lower limit. On common-rail diesels and direct-injection petrol engines this affects high-pressure fuel control directly.

t: Fuel Pressure Regulator 2 Control Circuit High
s: Drive voltage to the second fuel pressure regulator solenoid is above the upper limit — short to supply or open ground side. The driver protection trips, regulator falls back to default.

t: O2 Sensor Out of Range During Deceleration (Bank 1 Sensor 1)
s: During engine deceleration with closed throttle (DFCO), the O2 sensor on bank 1 sensor 1 should swing fully lean as the manifold fills with fresh air. Code is set when the swing does not happen — usually a slow or coated sensor.

t: O2 Sensor Out of Range During Deceleration (Bank 2 Sensor 1)
s: During engine deceleration with closed throttle (DFCO), the O2 sensor on bank 2 sensor 1 should swing fully lean as the manifold fills with fresh air. Code is set when the swing does not happen — usually a slow or coated sensor.

t: Brake Pedal Position / Accelerator Pedal Position Correlation
s: The ECU detects simultaneous brake and accelerator activation beyond a permitted threshold. Modern brake-throttle override safety logic forces idle in this state — usually triggered by a stuck brake light switch or a faulty pedal sensor.

t: Fuel Pressure Regulator B Exceeded Control Limits - Pressure Too Low
s: Fuel pressure regulator A cannot maintain target pressure — measured pressure stays below the lower control limit despite full closed-loop authority. Typical for a leaking regulator, weak high-pressure pump, or restricted suction line.

t: Fuel Pressure Regulator B Exceeded Control Limits - Pressure Too High
s: Fuel pressure regulator A cannot reduce pressure below the upper control limit — typically a stuck-closed return path or runaway high-pressure pump.

t: Fuel Pressure Regulator B Exceeded Learning Limits - Too Low
s: The adaptive learning offset for fuel pressure regulator A has reached the lower limit. Long-term drift indicates progressive wear of the regulator or pump.

t: Fuel Pressure Regulator B Exceeded Learning Limits - Too High
s: The adaptive learning offset for fuel pressure regulator B has reached the upper limit. Long-term drift upward indicates progressive wear of the regulator or a return path that is becoming restricted.

t: NOx Sensor Circuit (Bank 1 Sensor 2)
s: A general electrical fault on the NOx sensor on bank 2 — open, shorted, or out of plausibility. Used by SCR control and OBD NOx monitoring.

t: NOx Sensor Circuit Range/Performance Bank 1 Sensor 2
s: The downstream NOx sensor on bank 1 (after the SCR catalyst) returns implausible NOx readings during operation. Either the sensor is degraded or upstream chemistry is abnormal.

t: NOx Sensor Circuit Low (Bank 1 Sensor 2)
s: NOx sensor on bank 2 reads below lower plausibility — short to ground. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: NOx Sensor Circuit High (Bank 1 Sensor 2)
s: NOx sensor on bank 2 reads above upper plausibility — open signal or short to B+. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: NOx Sensor Circuit Intermittent (Bank 1 Sensor 2)
s: NOx sensor on bank 2 signal jumps or drops out intermittently. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: NOx Sensor Heater Control Circuit/Open (Bank 1 Sensor 2)
s: The drive circuit for the NOx sensor heater on bank 2 is open. The sensor cannot reach operating temperature.

t: NOx Sensor Heater Control Circuit Low (Bank 1 Sensor 2)
s: NOx heater drive line on bank 2 stuck low — short to ground. The sensor heater warms the element to operating temperature (~600 °C); without it the lambda sensor stays inactive in closed loop and the ECM cannot trim mixture.

t: NOx Sensor Heater Control Circuit High (Bank 1 Sensor 2)
s: NOx heater drive line on bank 2 stuck high — short to B+. The sensor heater warms the element to operating temperature (~600 °C); without it the lambda sensor stays inactive in closed loop and the ECM cannot trim mixture.

t: NOx Sensor Heater Sense Circuit (Bank 1 Sensor 2)
s: The feedback line that monitors NOx sensor heater current draw on bank 2 is faulty — open, shorted, or out of plausibility.

t: NOx Sensor Heater Sense Circuit Range/Performance (Bank 1 Sensor 2)
s: NOx sensor heater sense feedback on bank 2 reads in range but plausibility check fails. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: NOx Sensor Heater Sense Circuit Low (Bank 1 Sensor 2)
s: NOx heater sense line on bank 2 below lower plausibility — short to ground. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: NOx Sensor Heater Sense Circuit High (Bank 1 Sensor 2)
s: NOx heater sense line on bank 2 above upper plausibility — open signal or short to B+. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: NOx Sensor Heater Sense Circuit Intermittent (Bank 1 Sensor 2)
s: The feedback line that monitors the NOx sensor heater current draw on bank 1 sensor 2 jumps or drops out intermittently — connector loose or chafed wire near the exhaust heat zone.

t: O2 Sensor Positive Current Control Circuit/Open (Bank 1 Sensor 2)
s: The positive (sourcing) side of the O2 sensor pumping current control circuit on bank 1 sensor 2 is open. Wide-band O2 sensors regulate oxygen concentration in a reference chamber by pumping current; the positive line is open here.

t: O2 Sensor Positive Current Control Circuit Low (Bank 1 Sensor 2)
s: O2 positive current line on bank 1 sensor 2 stuck low — short to ground. A drifted or sluggish lambda sensor lets the mixture run lean or rich without correction — fuel economy and emissions suffer immediately.

t: O2 Sensor Positive Current Control Circuit High (Bank 1 Sensor 2)
s: O2 positive current line on bank 1 sensor 2 stuck high — short to B+. A drifted or sluggish lambda sensor lets the mixture run lean or rich without correction — fuel economy and emissions suffer immediately.

t: O2 Sensor Reference Voltage Circuit/Open (Bank 1 Sensor 2)
s: The reference voltage line for the wide-band O2 sensor on bank 1 sensor 2 is open. The reference is used as the floating mid-point for the pumping cell.

t: O2 Sensor Reference Voltage Performance (Bank 1 Sensor 2)
s: O2 sensor reference voltage on bank 1 sensor 2 is in range but plausibility check fails — drift or instability.

t: O2 Sensor Reference Voltage Circuit Low (Bank 1 Sensor 2)
s: O2 reference voltage on bank 1 sensor 2 below lower plausibility — short to ground. A drifted or sluggish lambda sensor lets the mixture run lean or rich without correction — fuel economy and emissions suffer immediately.

t: O2 Sensor Reference Voltage Circuit High (Bank 1 Sensor 2)
s: O2 reference voltage on bank 1 sensor 2 above upper plausibility — short to B+. A drifted or sluggish lambda sensor lets the mixture run lean or rich without correction — fuel economy and emissions suffer immediately.

t: O2 Sensor Negative Current Control Circuit/Open (Bank 1 Sensor 2)
s: The negative (sinking) side of the O2 sensor pumping current control circuit on bank 1 sensor 2 is open.

t: O2 Sensor Negative Current Control Circuit Low (Bank 1 Sensor 2)
s: O2 negative current line on bank 1 sensor 2 stuck low — short to ground. A drifted or sluggish lambda sensor lets the mixture run lean or rich without correction — fuel economy and emissions suffer immediately.

t: O2 Sensor Negative Current Control Circuit High (Bank 1 Sensor 2)
s: O2 negative current line on bank 1 sensor 2 stuck high — short to B+. A drifted or sluggish lambda sensor lets the mixture run lean or rich without correction — fuel economy and emissions suffer immediately.

t: O2 Sensor Pumping Current Trim Circuit/Open (Bank 1 Sensor 2)
s: The pumping current trim line on bank 1 sensor 2 is open. The trim is the per-sensor calibration code that adjusts pumping current to compensate for production tolerances.

t: O2 Sensor Pumping Current Trim Circuit Low (Bank 1 Sensor 2)
s: O2 trim line on bank 1 sensor 2 stuck low — short to ground. A drifted or sluggish lambda sensor lets the mixture run lean or rich without correction — fuel economy and emissions suffer immediately.

t: O2 Sensor Pumping Current Trim Circuit High (Bank 1 Sensor 2)
s: O2 trim line on bank 1 sensor 2 stuck high — short to B+. A drifted or sluggish lambda sensor lets the mixture run lean or rich without correction — fuel economy and emissions suffer immediately.

t: O2 Sensor Positive Current Control Circuit/Open (Bank 2 Sensor 2)
s: The positive (sourcing) side of the O2 sensor pumping current control circuit on bank 2 sensor 2 is open. Wide-band O2 sensors regulate oxygen concentration in a reference chamber by pumping current; the positive line is open here.

t: O2 Sensor Positive Current Control Circuit Low (Bank 2 Sensor 2)
s: O2 positive current line on bank 2 sensor 2 stuck low — short to ground. A drifted or sluggish lambda sensor lets the mixture run lean or rich without correction — fuel economy and emissions suffer immediately.

t: O2 Sensor Positive Current Control Circuit High (Bank 2 Sensor 2)
s: O2 positive current line on bank 2 sensor 2 stuck high — short to B+. A drifted or sluggish lambda sensor lets the mixture run lean or rich without correction — fuel economy and emissions suffer immediately.

t: O2 Sensor Reference Voltage Circuit/Open (Bank 2 Sensor 2)
s: The reference voltage line for the wide-band O2 sensor on bank 2 sensor 2 is open. The reference is used as the floating mid-point for the pumping cell.

t: O2 Sensor Reference Voltage Performance (Bank 2 Sensor 2)
s: O2 sensor reference voltage on bank 2 sensor 2 is in range but plausibility check fails — drift or instability.

t: O2 Sensor Reference Voltage Circuit Low (Bank 2 Sensor 2)
s: O2 reference voltage on bank 2 sensor 2 below lower plausibility — short to ground. A drifted or sluggish lambda sensor lets the mixture run lean or rich without correction — fuel economy and emissions suffer immediately.

t: O2 Sensor Reference Voltage Circuit High (Bank 2 Sensor 2)
s: O2 reference voltage on bank 2 sensor 2 above upper plausibility — short to B+. A drifted or sluggish lambda sensor lets the mixture run lean or rich without correction — fuel economy and emissions suffer immediately.

t: O2 Sensor Negative Current Control Circuit/Open (Bank 2 Sensor 2)
s: The negative (sinking) side of the O2 sensor pumping current control circuit on bank 2 sensor 2 is open.

t: O2 Sensor Negative Current Control Circuit Low (Bank 2 Sensor 2)
s: O2 negative current line on bank 2 sensor 2 stuck low — short to ground. A drifted or sluggish lambda sensor lets the mixture run lean or rich without correction — fuel economy and emissions suffer immediately.

t: O2 Sensor Negative Current Control Circuit High (Bank 2 Sensor 2)
s: O2 negative current line on bank 2 sensor 2 stuck high — short to B+. A drifted or sluggish lambda sensor lets the mixture run lean or rich without correction — fuel economy and emissions suffer immediately.

t: O2 Sensor Pumping Current Trim Circuit/Open (Bank 2 Sensor 2)
s: The pumping current trim line on bank 2 sensor 2 is open. The trim is the per-sensor calibration code that adjusts pumping current to compensate for production tolerances.

t: O2 Sensor Pumping Current Trim Circuit Low (Bank 2 Sensor 2)
s: O2 trim line on bank 2 sensor 2 stuck low — short to ground. A drifted or sluggish lambda sensor lets the mixture run lean or rich without correction — fuel economy and emissions suffer immediately.

t: O2 Sensor Pumping Current Trim Circuit High (Bank 2 Sensor 2)
s: O2 trim line on bank 2 sensor 2 stuck high — short to B+. A drifted or sluggish lambda sensor lets the mixture run lean or rich without correction — fuel economy and emissions suffer immediately.

t: Turbocharger Compressor Outlet Valve Control Circuit/Open
s: The drive circuit for the turbocharger compressor outlet valve (also called diverter valve or blow-off valve) is open. The valve releases boost pressure when the throttle closes to prevent compressor surge.

t: Turbocharger Compressor Outlet Valve Control Circuit Low
s: Compressor outlet valve drive line stuck low — short to ground. Boost control tracks commanded vs. actual manifold pressure; persistent deviation means wastegate, vanes, hose or actuator are not delivering the requested pressure.

t: Turbocharger Compressor Outlet Valve Control Circuit High
s: Compressor outlet valve drive line stuck high — short to B+. Boost control tracks commanded vs. actual manifold pressure; persistent deviation means wastegate, vanes, hose or actuator are not delivering the requested pressure.

t: Turbocharger Compressor Outlet Valve Stuck Open
s: Compressor outlet valve mechanically stuck open — boost pressure leaks back to intake before reaching the cylinders, causing under-boost.

t: Turbocharger Compressor Outlet Valve Stuck Closed
s: Compressor outlet valve mechanically stuck closed — pressure cannot be released on throttle close, causing audible compressor surge ("flutter") and stress on the turbo bearings.

t: Turbocharger Compressor Outlet Switching Valve Control Circuit/Open
s: The drive circuit for the turbocharger compressor outlet switching valve is open. On twin-turbo or sequential boost setups, this valve routes the compressor outlet between low-pressure and high-pressure stages.

t: Turbocharger Compressor Outlet Switching Valve Control Circuit Low
s: Compressor outlet switching valve drive line stuck low — short to ground. Boost control tracks commanded vs. actual manifold pressure; persistent deviation means wastegate, vanes, hose or actuator are not delivering the requested pressure.

t: Turbocharger Compressor Outlet Switching Valve Control Circuit High
s: Compressor outlet switching valve drive line stuck high — short to B+. Boost control tracks commanded vs. actual manifold pressure; persistent deviation means wastegate, vanes, hose or actuator are not delivering the requested pressure.

t: Turbocharger Compressor Outlet Switching Valve Stuck Open
s: Compressor outlet switching valve mechanically stuck open — sequential boost cannot transition stages cleanly.

t: Turbocharger Compressor Outlet Switching Valve Stuck Closed
s: Compressor outlet switching valve mechanically stuck closed — boost path locked. Boost control tracks commanded vs. actual manifold pressure; persistent deviation means wastegate, vanes, hose or actuator are not delivering the requested pressure.

t: Turbocharger Turbine Inlet Valve Control Circuit/Open
s: The drive circuit for the turbocharger turbine inlet valve is open. On sequential or two-stage charging the turbine inlet valve directs exhaust gas to the relevant turbine.

t: Turbocharger Turbine Inlet Valve Control Circuit Low
s: Turbine inlet valve drive line stuck low — short to ground. Boost control tracks commanded vs. actual manifold pressure; persistent deviation means wastegate, vanes, hose or actuator are not delivering the requested pressure.

t: Turbocharger Turbine Inlet Valve Control Circuit High
s: Turbine inlet valve drive line stuck high — short to B+. Boost control tracks commanded vs. actual manifold pressure; persistent deviation means wastegate, vanes, hose or actuator are not delivering the requested pressure.

t: Turbocharger Turbine Inlet Valve Stuck Open
s: Turbine inlet valve mechanically stuck open — exhaust gas flow cannot be redirected, sequential charging fails.

t: Turbocharger Turbine Inlet Valve Stuck Closed
s: Turbine inlet valve mechanically stuck closed — turbine receives no exhaust gas, no boost. Boost control tracks commanded vs. actual manifold pressure; persistent deviation means wastegate, vanes, hose or actuator are not delivering the requested pressure.

t: Turbocharger Turbine Inlet Valve Position Sensor Circuit
s: A general electrical fault on the turbine inlet valve position sensor — open, shorted, or out of plausibility.

t: Turbocharger Turbine Inlet Valve Position Sensor Circuit Range/Performance
s: Position sensor in range but plausibility fails — does not match commanded position. Boost control tracks commanded vs. actual manifold pressure; persistent deviation means wastegate, vanes, hose or actuator are not delivering the requested pressure.

t: Turbocharger Turbine Inlet Valve Position Sensor Circuit Low
s: Position sensor below lower plausibility — short to ground. Boost control tracks commanded vs. actual manifold pressure; persistent deviation means wastegate, vanes, hose or actuator are not delivering the requested pressure.

t: Turbocharger Turbine Inlet Valve Position Sensor Circuit High
s: Position sensor above upper plausibility — open signal or short to B+. Boost control tracks commanded vs. actual manifold pressure; persistent deviation means wastegate, vanes, hose or actuator are not delivering the requested pressure.

t: Turbocharger Turbine Inlet Valve Position Sensor Circuit Intermittent/Erratic
s: Position sensor signal jumps or drops out intermittently. Boost control tracks commanded vs. actual manifold pressure; persistent deviation means wastegate, vanes, hose or actuator are not delivering the requested pressure.

t: Cylinder 1 Alternative Fuel Injector Control Circuit/Open
s: The drive circuit for the cylinder 1 alternative-fuel injector is open. Bi-fuel vehicles have a second set of injectors for CNG, LPG, or hydrogen — separate from the petrol/diesel injectors.

t: Cylinder 1 Alternative Fuel Injector Control Circuit Low
s: Cylinder 1 alternative-fuel injector drive line stuck low — short to ground. A flow-deviating injector throws off cylinder balance — adjacent cylinders run leaner/richer to compensate, often masking the root cause until the trim limit is hit.

t: Cylinder 1 Alternative Fuel Injector Control Circuit High
s: Cylinder 1 alternative-fuel injector drive line stuck high — short to B+. A flow-deviating injector throws off cylinder balance — adjacent cylinders run leaner/richer to compensate, often masking the root cause until the trim limit is hit.

t: Cylinder 1 Alternative Fuel Injector Control Circuit Range/Performance
s: Cylinder 1 alternative-fuel injector is electrically OK but its response (current ramp, dwell, peak voltage) is off-spec — typically winding ageing.

t: Cylinder 2 Alternative Fuel Injector Control Circuit/Open
s: The drive circuit for the cylinder 2 alternative-fuel injector is open. Bi-fuel vehicles have a second set of injectors for CNG, LPG, or hydrogen — separate from the petrol/diesel injectors.

t: Cylinder 2 Alternative Fuel Injector Control Circuit Low
s: Cylinder 2 alternative-fuel injector drive line stuck low — short to ground. A flow-deviating injector throws off cylinder balance — adjacent cylinders run leaner/richer to compensate, often masking the root cause until the trim limit is hit.

t: Cylinder 2 Alternative Fuel Injector Control Circuit High
s: Cylinder 2 alternative-fuel injector drive line stuck high — short to B+. A flow-deviating injector throws off cylinder balance — adjacent cylinders run leaner/richer to compensate, often masking the root cause until the trim limit is hit.

t: Cylinder 2 Alternative Fuel Injector Control Circuit Range/Performance
s: Cylinder 2 alternative-fuel injector is electrically OK but its response (current ramp, dwell, peak voltage) is off-spec — typically winding ageing.

t: Cylinder 3 Alternative Fuel Injector Control Circuit/Open
s: The drive circuit for the cylinder 3 alternative-fuel injector is open. Bi-fuel vehicles have a second set of injectors for CNG, LPG, or hydrogen — separate from the petrol/diesel injectors.

t: Cylinder 3 Alternative Fuel Injector Control Circuit Low
s: Cylinder 3 alternative-fuel injector drive line stuck low — short to ground. A flow-deviating injector throws off cylinder balance — adjacent cylinders run leaner/richer to compensate, often masking the root cause until the trim limit is hit.

t: Cylinder 3 Alternative Fuel Injector Control Circuit High
s: Cylinder 3 alternative-fuel injector drive line stuck high — short to B+. A flow-deviating injector throws off cylinder balance — adjacent cylinders run leaner/richer to compensate, often masking the root cause until the trim limit is hit.

t: Cylinder 3 Alternative Fuel Injector Control Circuit Range/Performance
s: Cylinder 3 alternative-fuel injector is electrically OK but its response (current ramp, dwell, peak voltage) is off-spec — typically winding ageing.

t: Cylinder 4 Alternative Fuel Injector Control Circuit/Open
s: The drive circuit for the cylinder 4 alternative-fuel injector is open. Bi-fuel vehicles have a second set of injectors for CNG, LPG, or hydrogen — separate from the petrol/diesel injectors.

t: Cylinder 4 Alternative Fuel Injector Control Circuit Low
s: Cylinder 4 alternative-fuel injector drive line stuck low — short to ground. A flow-deviating injector throws off cylinder balance — adjacent cylinders run leaner/richer to compensate, often masking the root cause until the trim limit is hit.

s: Cylinder 4 alternative-fuel injector drive line stuck high — short to B+. A flow-deviating injector throws off cylinder balance — adjacent cylinders run leaner/richer to compensate, often masking the root cause until the trim limit is hit.

s: Cylinder 4 alternative-fuel injector is electrically OK but its response (current ramp, dwell, peak voltage) is off-spec — typically winding ageing.

t: Cylinder 5 Alternative Fuel Injector Control Circuit/Open
s: The drive circuit for the cylinder 5 alternative-fuel injector is open. Bi-fuel vehicles have a second set of injectors for CNG, LPG, or hydrogen — separate from the petrol/diesel injectors.

t: Cylinder 5 Alternative Fuel Injector Control Circuit Low
s: Cylinder 5 alternative-fuel injector drive line stuck low — short to ground. A flow-deviating injector throws off cylinder balance — adjacent cylinders run leaner/richer to compensate, often masking the root cause until the trim limit is hit.

t: Cylinder 5 Alternative Fuel Injector Control Circuit High
s: Cylinder 5 alternative-fuel injector drive line stuck high — short to B+. A flow-deviating injector throws off cylinder balance — adjacent cylinders run leaner/richer to compensate, often masking the root cause until the trim limit is hit.

s: Cylinder 5 alternative-fuel injector is electrically OK but its response (current ramp, dwell, peak voltage) is off-spec — typically winding ageing.

t: Cylinder 6 Alternative Fuel Injector Control Circuit/Open
s: The drive circuit for the cylinder 6 alternative-fuel injector is open. Bi-fuel vehicles have a second set of injectors for CNG, LPG, or hydrogen — separate from the petrol/diesel injectors.

t: Cylinder 6 Alternative Fuel Injector Control Circuit Low
s: Cylinder 6 alternative-fuel injector drive line stuck low — short to ground. A flow-deviating injector throws off cylinder balance — adjacent cylinders run leaner/richer to compensate, often masking the root cause until the trim limit is hit.

t: Cylinder 6 Alternative Fuel Injector Control Circuit High
s: Cylinder 6 alternative-fuel injector drive line stuck high — short to B+. A flow-deviating injector throws off cylinder balance — adjacent cylinders run leaner/richer to compensate, often masking the root cause until the trim limit is hit.

t: Cylinder 6 Alternative Fuel Injector Control Circuit Range/Performance
s: Cylinder 6 alternative-fuel injector is electrically OK but its response (current ramp, dwell, peak voltage) is off-spec — typically winding ageing.

t: Particulate Matter Sensor Heater Performance
s: The particulate matter (PM) sensor heater on diesel after-DPF systems does not reach or hold the calibrated operating temperature within the expected time. Without proper heating the PM sensor cannot be regenerated and read correctly.

t: Cylinder 7 Alternative Fuel Injector Control Circuit Low
s: Cylinder 7 alternative-fuel injector drive line stuck low — short to ground. A flow-deviating injector throws off cylinder balance — adjacent cylinders run leaner/richer to compensate, often masking the root cause until the trim limit is hit.

t: Cylinder 7 Alternative Fuel Injector Control Circuit High
s: Cylinder 7 alternative-fuel injector drive line stuck high — short to B+. A flow-deviating injector throws off cylinder balance — adjacent cylinders run leaner/richer to compensate, often masking the root cause until the trim limit is hit.

t: Cylinder 7 Alternative Fuel Injector Control Circuit Range/Performance
s: Cylinder 7 alternative-fuel injector is electrically OK but its response (current ramp, dwell, peak voltage) is off-spec — typically winding ageing.

t: Cylinder 8 Alternative Fuel Injector Control Circuit/Open
s: The drive circuit for the cylinder 8 alternative-fuel injector is open. Bi-fuel vehicles have a second set of injectors for CNG, LPG, or hydrogen — separate from the petrol/diesel injectors.

t: Cylinder 8 Alternative Fuel Injector Control Circuit Low
s: Cylinder 8 alternative-fuel injector drive line stuck low — short to ground. A flow-deviating injector throws off cylinder balance — adjacent cylinders run leaner/richer to compensate, often masking the root cause until the trim limit is hit.

t: Cylinder 8 Alternative Fuel Injector Control Circuit High
s: Cylinder 8 alternative-fuel injector drive line stuck high — short to B+. A flow-deviating injector throws off cylinder balance — adjacent cylinders run leaner/richer to compensate, often masking the root cause until the trim limit is hit.

t: Cylinder 8 Alternative Fuel Injector Control Circuit Range/Performance
s: Cylinder 8 alternative-fuel injector is electrically OK but its response (current ramp, dwell, peak voltage) is off-spec — typically winding ageing.

t: NOx Sensor Performance - Slow Response Low to High (Bank 1 Sensor 1)
s: NOx sensor on bank 1 sensor 1 responds too slowly when NOx concentration rises (typically observed when SCR dosing is reduced or stopped). The sensor has aged or its protection cap is clogged.

t: NOx Sensor Performance - Slow Response High to Low (Bank 1 Sensor 1)
s: NOx sensor on bank 1 sensor 1 responds too slowly when NOx concentration falls (typically observed when SCR dosing increases). The sensor has aged or its protection cap is clogged.

t: NOx Sensor Performance - Sensing Element (Bank 1 Sensor 1)
s: The wide-band sensing element of the NOx sensor on bank 1 sensor 1 reports an internal performance fault — typically poisoning, condensate damage, or end of service life.

t: NOx Sensor Performance - Slow Response Low to High (Bank 1 Sensor 2)
s: NOx sensor on bank 1 sensor 2 responds too slowly when NOx concentration rises (typically observed when SCR dosing is reduced or stopped). The sensor has aged or its protection cap is clogged.

t: NOx Sensor Performance Slow Response High to Low Bank 1 Sensor 2
s: The downstream NOx sensor on bank 1 (post-SCR) responds too slowly when NOx levels fall after a reductant injection event. The control unit measures the time between commanded reductant dose and the resulting NOx drop and finds it exceeds the diagnostic threshold, indicating an aged sensor element.

t: NOx Sensor Sensing Element Bank 1 Sensor 2
s: The downstream NOx sensor on bank 1 reports that its internal ZrO2 sensing element is no longer producing valid pumping currents - the element has lost its electrochemical response, typically because the active ceramic layer has been poisoned, cracked, or burnt out.

t: SCR NOx Catalyst Inlet Temperature Too Low
s: The temperature sensor at the SCR catalyst inlet reports a value below the threshold needed for efficient ammonia conversion. Below roughly 180-200 °C the SCR cannot reduce NOx and reductant injection is suspended; if this is detected continuously the ECU sets the fault.

t: Ignition Coil A Primary Control Circuit Low
s: The ECU reads a primary-side control voltage of ignition coil A below the expected level. The coil cannot store sufficient energy to fire the spark plug; misfire on cylinder 1 typically follows.

t: Ignition Coil A Primary Control Circuit High
s: Voltage on the primary control of ignition coil A is above the expected limit. Often a short to battery voltage on the primary wire or an open in the coil.

t: Ignition Coil A Secondary Circuit
s: The ECU detects a fault on the secondary side of ignition coil A. The high-voltage spark output is missing or insufficient, leading to misfire on the affected cylinder.

t: Ignition Coil B Primary Control Circuit Low
s: The ECU reads a low primary control voltage on ignition coil B. Misfire is typically observed on the cylinder this coil serves.

t: Ignition Coil B Primary Control Circuit High
s: Voltage on the primary control of ignition coil B exceeds the calibrated upper limit, indicating an electrical fault that prevents proper coil charging.

t: Ignition Coil B Secondary Circuit
s: The secondary side of ignition coil B is faulty. Spark energy at the corresponding spark plug is insufficient, producing misfires.

t: Ignition Coil C Primary Control Circuit Low
s: The primary control of ignition coil C reads below the calibrated minimum. The cylinder served by this coil will misfire.

t: Ignition Coil C Primary Control Circuit High
s: The primary control voltage at ignition coil C exceeds the upper limit, suggesting a short to battery or an open primary winding.

t: Ignition Coil C Secondary Circuit
s: The secondary side of ignition coil C does not produce sufficient spark energy. Misfire is observed on the corresponding cylinder.

t: Ignition Coil D Primary Control Circuit Low
s: Low primary control voltage on ignition coil D. The cylinder served by this coil will misfire if not corrected quickly.

t: Cylinder 1 Air-Fuel Ratio Imbalance Adjustment At Limit During Balance
s: The ECU continuously trims individual cylinders to equalise air-fuel ratio across the bank using ion-current or per-cylinder lambda feedback. For cylinder 1 the per-cylinder fuel correction has hit its software-side maximum or minimum without restoring balance, indicating the underlying mechanical or fuelling deviation is too large to compensate.

t: Cylinder 2 Air-Fuel Ratio Imbalance Adjustment At Limit During Balance
s: The ECU continuously trims individual cylinders to equalise air-fuel ratio across the bank using ion-current or per-cylinder lambda feedback. For cylinder 2 the per-cylinder fuel correction has hit its software-side maximum or minimum without restoring balance, indicating the underlying mechanical or fuelling deviation is too large to compensate.

t: Ignition Coil D Primary Control Circuit High
s: The ECU detects an excessive voltage on the primary control of ignition coil D, normally caused by a short to battery or an open primary winding.

t: Ignition Coil D Secondary Circuit
s: A fault is detected in the secondary side of ignition coil D. Insufficient spark energy results in cylinder misfire.

t: Ignition Coil E Primary Control Circuit Low
s: The ECU reads a primary-side control voltage of ignition coil A below the expected level. The coil cannot store sufficient energy to fire the spark plug; misfire on cylinder 1 typically follows.

t: Ignition Coil E Primary Control Circuit High
s: Voltage on the primary control of ignition coil A is above the expected limit. Often a short to battery voltage on the primary wire or an open in the coil.

t: Ignition Coil E Secondary Circuit
s: The ECU detects a fault on the secondary side of ignition coil A. The high-voltage spark output is missing or insufficient, leading to misfire on the affected cylinder.

s: Voltage on the primary control of ignition coil A is above the expected limit. Often a short to battery voltage on the primary wire or an open in the coil.

t: Ignition Coil F Secondary Circuit
s: The ECU detects a fault on the secondary side of ignition coil A. The high-voltage spark output is missing or insufficient, leading to misfire on the affected cylinder.

t: Ignition Coil G Secondary Circuit
s: The ECU detects a fault on the secondary side of ignition coil A. The high-voltage spark output is missing or insufficient, leading to misfire on the affected cylinder.

s: The ECU reads a primary-side control voltage of ignition coil A below the expected level. The coil cannot store sufficient energy to fire the spark plug; misfire on cylinder 1 typically follows.

t: Ignition Coil I Secondary Circuit
