t: TCM Request Forced Engine Shutdown
s: The transmission control module (TCM) has requested an emergency engine shutdown over the powertrain bus, typically because it has detected a condition that could damage the gearbox or driveline if engine torque were not removed (e.g. severe overheating, loss of pressure or hydraulic control).

t: Cylinder 11 Fuel Injector A Offset Learning At Min Limit
s: The injector-offset learning routine for fuel injector A on cylinder 11 has driven its correction value to the minimum allowed limit and cannot trim further. This indicates the injector flows significantly less than the calibrated reference - typically due to drift, partial blockage or wiring resistance.

t: Cylinder 11 Fuel Injector A Offset Learning At Max Limit
s: The injector-offset learning routine for fuel injector A on cylinder 11 has driven its correction value to the minimum allowed limit and cannot trim further. This indicates the injector flows significantly less than the calibrated reference - typically due to drift, partial blockage or wiring resistance.

t: Cylinder 12 Fuel Injector A Offset Learning At Min Limit
s: The injector-offset learning routine for fuel injector A on cylinder 12 has driven its correction value to the minimum allowed limit and cannot trim further. This indicates the injector flows significantly less than the calibrated reference - typically due to drift, partial blockage or wiring resistance.

t: Cylinder 12 Fuel Injector A Offset Learning At Max Limit
s: The injector-offset learning routine for fuel injector A on cylinder 12 has driven its correction value to the minimum allowed limit and cannot trim further. This indicates the injector flows significantly less than the calibrated reference - typically due to drift, partial blockage or wiring resistance.

t: O2 Sensor Out of Range During Deceleration Bank 1 Sensor 2
s: During fuel cutoff on overrun the post-catalyst lambda sensor on bank 1 should drift toward a fixed lean reference voltage. If it stays outside the expected window the ECU sets this fault, indicating either a slow or stuck downstream sensor or an exhaust leak that draws unburnt gas across it.

t: Ignition Coil K Primary Control Circuit Low
s: The ECU reads a primary-side control voltage of ignition coil A below the expected level. The coil cannot store sufficient energy to fire the spark plug; misfire on cylinder 1 typically follows.

t: Ignition Coil K Primary Control Circuit High
s: Voltage on the primary control of ignition coil A is above the expected limit. Often a short to battery voltage on the primary wire or an open in the coil.

t: Ignition Coil K Secondary Circuit
s: The ECU detects a fault on the secondary side of ignition coil A. The high-voltage spark output is missing or insufficient, leading to misfire on the affected cylinder.

t: Ignition Coil L Primary Control Circuit Low
s: The ECU reads a primary-side control voltage of ignition coil A below the expected level. The coil cannot store sufficient energy to fire the spark plug; misfire on cylinder 1 typically follows.

t: Ignition Coil L Primary Control Circuit High
s: Voltage on the primary control of ignition coil A is above the expected limit. Often a short to battery voltage on the primary wire or an open in the coil.

t: Ignition Coil L Secondary Circuit
s: The ECU detects a fault on the secondary side of ignition coil A. The high-voltage spark output is missing or insufficient, leading to misfire on the affected cylinder.

t: Cylinder 1 Above Knock Threshold
s: The knock sensor channel for cylinder 1 has registered combustion noise above the calibrated knock threshold beyond what the spark retard map can correct. Often associated with low-octane fuel, EGR malfunction or carbon-induced hot spots.

t: Cylinder 2 Above Knock Threshold
s: The knock sensor channel for cylinder 2 has registered combustion noise above the calibrated knock threshold beyond what the spark retard map can correct. Often associated with low-octane fuel, EGR malfunction or carbon-induced hot spots.

t: Cylinder 3 Above Knock Threshold
s: The knock sensor channel for cylinder 3 has registered combustion noise above the calibrated knock threshold beyond what the spark retard map can correct. Often associated with low-octane fuel, EGR malfunction or carbon-induced hot spots.

t: Cylinder 4 Above Knock Threshold
s: The knock sensor channel for cylinder 4 has registered combustion noise above the calibrated knock threshold beyond what the spark retard map can correct. Often associated with low-octane fuel, EGR malfunction or carbon-induced hot spots.

t: Fuel Pressure Regulator C Control Circuit Open
s: The ECU detects an open circuit on the control line for the third (C) fuel pressure regulator. Either the regulator solenoid coil is internally open, the harness is broken, or the connector has lost contact - the ECU cannot drive the regulator and rail pressure cannot be controlled by this stage.

t: Fuel Pressure Regulator C Control Circuit Low
s: Same as P233A but with the control line shorted to ground - the ECU measures a low voltage that does not match the commanded duty cycle.

t: Fuel Pressure Regulator C Control Circuit High
s: Same as P233A but with the control line shorted to supply - the ECU measures a high voltage that does not match the commanded duty cycle.

t: Fuel Pressure Regulator B Solenoid Supply Voltage Control Circuit Open
s: The supply line that powers the solenoid of fuel pressure regulator B is open. Without supply the regulator cannot operate and the ECU cannot maintain rail pressure on this stage; sets when the controller sees no current flow even with the solenoid commanded on.

t: Fuel Pressure Regulator B Solenoid Supply Voltage Control Circuit Low
s: The supply line that powers the solenoid of fuel pressure regulator B is below the expected voltage with the regulator commanded active - typically a high-resistance feed, partial short to ground or weak relay contact.

t: Fuel Pressure Regulator B Solenoid Supply Voltage Control Circuit High
s: Same as P233D but with the supply line shorted to a higher potential than expected, so the ECU measures more voltage on the regulator B feed than the commanded level allows.

t: Cylinder 5 Above Knock Threshold
s: The knock-sensor evaluation reports cylinder 5 above the knock threshold over a sustained window. The ECU retards ignition for that cylinder; persistent knock indicates poor fuel quality, carbon deposits or cooling issues.

t: Cylinder 6 Above Knock Threshold
s: Same condition as P2340 but on cylinder 6 — sustained knock above the threshold with retarded ignition correction.

t: Cylinder 7 Above Knock Threshold
s: Sustained knock above the threshold on cylinder 7 — usual causes are fuel quality, carbon deposits, or cooling issues at this cylinder.

t: Cylinder 8 Above Knock Threshold
s: Sustained knock above the threshold on cylinder 8 — typical on V8 engines under load with poor fuel.

t: Cylinder 9 Above Knock/Combustion Vibration Sensor Threshold
s: The knock or combustion-vibration sensor monitors the structure-borne noise of each cylinder. The signal level for cylinder 9 has stayed above the diagnostic threshold for an extended period, indicating either persistent knock or an electrical fault that biases the channel high.

t: Cylinder 10 Above Knock/Combustion Vibration Sensor Threshold
s: The knock or combustion-vibration sensor monitors the structure-borne noise of each cylinder. The signal level for cylinder 10 has stayed above the diagnostic threshold for an extended period, indicating either persistent knock or an electrical fault that biases the channel high.

t: Cylinder 11 Above Knock/Combustion Vibration Sensor Threshold
s: The knock or combustion-vibration sensor monitors the structure-borne noise of each cylinder. The signal level for cylinder 11 has stayed above the diagnostic threshold for an extended period, indicating either persistent knock or an electrical fault that biases the channel high.

t: Cylinder 12 Above Knock/Combustion Vibration Sensor Threshold
s: The knock or combustion-vibration sensor monitors the structure-borne noise of each cylinder. The signal level for cylinder 12 has stayed above the diagnostic threshold for an extended period, indicating either persistent knock or an electrical fault that biases the channel high.

t: Cylinder 9 Pressure Sensor Circuit
s: A general electrical fault on the cylinder 9 combustion pressure sensor — used on engines with closed-loop combustion control (e.g. some Volvo and BMW direct-injection diesels) to measure cylinder pressure directly via a sensor at the glow plug or injector position.

t: Cylinder 9 Pressure Sensor Circuit Range/Performance
s: Cylinder 9 pressure sensor reads in range but plausibility check fails. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Cylinder 9 Pressure Sensor Circuit Low
s: Cylinder 9 pressure sensor below lower plausibility — short to ground. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Cylinder 9 Pressure Sensor Circuit High
s: Cylinder 9 pressure sensor above upper plausibility — open signal or short to B+. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Cylinder 9 Pressure Sensor Circuit Intermittent or Erratic
s: Cylinder 9 pressure sensor signal jumps or drops out intermittently — connector or wiring issue. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Cylinder 9 Pressure Too Low
s: Cylinder 9 measured peak combustion pressure is below the calibrated minimum — typically low compression, weak injection, or stuck-open exhaust valve.

t: Cylinder 9 Pressure Too High
s: Cylinder 9 measured peak combustion pressure is above the calibrated maximum — typically pre-ignition, knock, over-fuelling, or runaway combustion.

t: Cylinder 9 Pressure Variation Low
s: Cylinder-to-cylinder pressure variation on cylinder 9 is below the calibrated minimum — pressure stays unusually constant cycle-to-cycle, often a sign that the closed-loop combustion controller has lost authority or the sensor signal is filtered.

t: Cylinder 9 Pressure Variation High
s: Cylinder-to-cylinder pressure variation on cylinder 9 is above the calibrated maximum — combustion is unstable cycle-to-cycle, typical of partial misfire or injector seat erosion.

t: Cylinder 9 Combustion Performance
s: The closed-loop combustion controller reports that combustion performance on cylinder 9 is below the calibrated quality metric. Computed from the cylinder pressure trace — IMEP (indicated mean effective pressure), CA50 (50% mass burnt angle) or peak pressure deviates from the model.

t: Cylinder 10 Pressure Sensor Circuit

s: Cylinder 10 pressure sensor signal jumps or drops out intermittently — connector or wiring issue. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

s: Cylinder 10 measured peak combustion pressure is below the calibrated minimum — typically low compression, weak injection, or stuck-open exhaust valve.

t: Cylinder 10 Pressure Too High
s: Cylinder 10 measured peak combustion pressure is above the calibrated maximum — typically pre-ignition, knock, over-fuelling, or runaway combustion.

t: Ignition Coil L Secondary Circuit
s: The ECU detects a secondary-circuit fault on ignition coil L (cylinder 12 on a V12 or another high-numbered cylinder). The cylinder will misfire if the spark output is insufficient.

t: Cylinder 10 Pressure Variation High
s: Cylinder-to-cylinder pressure variation on cylinder 10 is above the calibrated maximum — combustion is unstable cycle-to-cycle, typical of partial misfire or injector seat erosion.

t: Cylinder 10 Combustion Performance

s: Cylinder 11 measured peak combustion pressure is below the calibrated minimum — typically low compression, weak injection, or stuck-open exhaust valve.

t: Glow Plug Module Control Module Power Circuit Low
s: The diesel glow plug control module reports a power-supply voltage below the operating threshold. Glow plug heating may be partially or fully disabled, causing hard cold start.

t: Glow Plug Module Control Module Power Circuit High
s: The voltage at the glow plug control module supply line is above the upper threshold. Indicates wiring or charging-system overvoltage condition.

t: Cylinder 11 Pressure Variation High
s: Cylinder-to-cylinder pressure variation on cylinder 11 is above the calibrated maximum — combustion is unstable cycle-to-cycle, typical of partial misfire or injector seat erosion.

t: Cylinder 11 Combustion Performance
s: The closed-loop combustion controller reports that combustion performance on cylinder 11 is below the calibrated quality metric. Computed from the cylinder pressure trace — IMEP (indicated mean effective pressure), CA50 (50% mass burnt angle) or peak pressure deviates from the model.

s: A general electrical fault on the cylinder 12 combustion pressure sensor — used on engines with closed-loop combustion control (e.g. some Volvo and BMW direct-injection diesels) to measure cylinder pressure directly via a sensor at the glow plug or injector position.

s: Cylinder 12 pressure sensor signal jumps or drops out intermittently — connector or wiring issue. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

s: Cylinder 12 measured peak combustion pressure is below the calibrated minimum — typically low compression, weak injection, or stuck-open exhaust valve.

t: Cylinder 12 Pressure Too High
s: Cylinder 12 measured peak combustion pressure is above the calibrated maximum — typically pre-ignition, knock, over-fuelling, or runaway combustion.

t: Cylinder 12 Pressure Variation Low
s: Cylinder-to-cylinder pressure variation on cylinder 12 is below the calibrated minimum — pressure stays unusually constant cycle-to-cycle, often a sign that the closed-loop combustion controller has lost authority or the sensor signal is filtered.

t: Cylinder 12 Pressure Variation High
s: Cylinder-to-cylinder pressure variation on cylinder 12 is above the calibrated maximum — combustion is unstable cycle-to-cycle, typical of partial misfire or injector seat erosion.

t: Cylinder 12 Combustion Performance
s: The closed-loop combustion controller reports that combustion performance on cylinder 12 is below the calibrated quality metric. Computed from the cylinder pressure trace — IMEP (indicated mean effective pressure), CA50 (50% mass burnt angle) or peak pressure deviates from the model.

t: Fuel Volume Regulator B Control Circuit Open
s: An open circuit is detected on the control line for the second fuel volume regulator (B) used on twin high-pressure-pump diesel systems. Without this regulator the high-pressure pump cannot meter fuel for its assigned cylinder bank, leading to rail-pressure faults and limp-home behaviour.

t: Fuel Volume Regulator B Control Circuit Performance
s: The ECU sees implausible feedback on the control loop for fuel volume regulator B - duty cycle does not match measured rail pressure response. The regulator either reacts too slowly, sticks, or has shifted from its calibration.

t: Fuel Volume Regulator B Control Circuit Low
s: Same as P2370 with the control line shorted to ground. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Fuel Volume Regulator B Control Circuit High
s: Same as P2370 with the control line shorted to supply. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Turbocharger Inlet Pressure Sensor B Circuit
s: The pressure sensor at the inlet of the second turbocharger (B) - on twin-turbo or sequential-turbo systems - is reporting a circuit fault. The ECU cannot determine inlet pressure and falls back to a substitute value, which limits boost control accuracy on this stage.

t: Turbocharger Inlet Pressure Sensor B Circuit Range or Performance
s: Same as P2374 but with the signal in plausible range yet not matching the engine model under boost. Boost control tracks commanded vs. actual manifold pressure; persistent deviation means wastegate, vanes, hose or actuator are not delivering the requested pressure.

t: Turbocharger Inlet Pressure Sensor B Circuit Low
s: Same as P2374 with the signal voltage below the plausible range. Boost control tracks commanded vs. actual manifold pressure; persistent deviation means wastegate, vanes, hose or actuator are not delivering the requested pressure.

t: Turbocharger Inlet Pressure Sensor B Circuit High
s: Same as P2374 with the signal voltage above the plausible range. Boost control tracks commanded vs. actual manifold pressure; persistent deviation means wastegate, vanes, hose or actuator are not delivering the requested pressure.

t: Turbocharger Inlet Pressure Sensor B Circuit Intermittent or Erratic
s: Same as P2374 but the signal is intermittent or has spikes that the ECU cannot reconcile - typically a wiring or connector fault.

t: O2 Sensor Out of Range During Deceleration Bank 2 Sensor 2
s: During fuel cutoff on overrun the post-catalyst lambda sensor on bank 2 should drift toward a fixed lean reference voltage. If it stays outside the expected window the ECU sets this fault, indicating either a slow or stuck downstream sensor or an exhaust leak that draws unburnt gas across it.

t: O2 Sensor Exhaust Sample Error Bank 1 Sensor 2
s: The post-catalyst lambda sensor on bank 1 cannot acquire a valid exhaust sample - the signal does not respond to the expected exhaust composition during the diagnostic window. Typical causes are a fouled sensing element, a clogged sensor body or an installation leak that bypasses exhaust around the sensor.

t: O2 Sensor Exhaust Sample Error Bank 2 Sensor 2
s: The post-catalyst lambda sensor on bank 2 cannot acquire a valid exhaust sample - the signal does not respond to the expected exhaust composition during the diagnostic window. Typical causes are a fouled sensing element, a clogged sensor body or an installation leak that bypasses exhaust around the sensor.

t: Exhaust Heat Exchanger Bypass Valve A Control Circuit Open
s: An open circuit is detected on the control line for the bypass flap A of the exhaust heat exchanger. This valve directs exhaust either through the heat exchanger (cold start, fast warm-up) or around it (normal operation). Without working control the warm-up strategy is impaired and emissions during cold start increase.

t: Exhaust Heat Exchanger Bypass Valve A Control Circuit Low
s: Same as P237C with the control line shorted to ground. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Exhaust Heat Exchanger Bypass Valve A Control Circuit High
s: Same as P237C with the control line shorted to supply. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Exhaust Heat Exchanger Bypass Valve A Control Performance
s: The bypass flap A of the exhaust heat exchanger does not move as commanded - the position feedback or downstream temperature does not respond to the control demand. The flap is mechanically stuck, the actuator gearing is worn, or the position sensor is offset.

t: Wait to Start Lamp Control Circuit
s: The ECU detects an electrical fault on the wait-to-start indicator output of the diesel preheat system. The driver may not get a correct preheat indication.

t: EGR Sensor D Circuit High
s: The fourth exhaust-gas recirculation sensor (D) reports a circuit voltage above the plausible upper limit. The ECU loses one of its EGR feedback channels and falls back to a substitute value, which limits EGR rate accuracy and emissions performance.

t: EGR Sensor D Circuit Low
s: Same as P2381 but with the signal voltage below the plausible lower limit - typically the signal line shorted to ground or an internal sensor short.

t: EGR Sensor D Circuit Range or Performance
s: Same as P2381 but with the signal in plausible voltage range yet not matching the engine model - drift, partial blockage of the reference port, or a slow sensor.

t: EGR Sensor D Circuit Intermittent
s: Same as P2381 but the signal is intermittent - typically a connector or harness fault. EGR mixes inert exhaust into intake to lower combustion peak temperature — too little raises NOx, too much causes misfires and rough running.

t: EGR Sensor E Circuit
s: A circuit fault is detected on the fifth exhaust-gas recirculation sensor (E). The ECU loses this EGR feedback channel and falls back to a substitute value; emissions and EGR rate accuracy on this stage are reduced until repair.

t: EGR Sensor E Circuit High
s: The fourth exhaust-gas recirculation sensor (D) reports a circuit voltage above the plausible upper limit. The ECU loses one of its EGR feedback channels and falls back to a substitute value, which limits EGR rate accuracy and emissions performance.

t: EGR Sensor E Circuit Low
s: Same as P2382 on EGR sensor E. EGR mixes inert exhaust into intake to lower combustion peak temperature — too little raises NOx, too much causes misfires and rough running.

t: EGR Sensor E Circuit Range or Performance
s: Same as P2383 on EGR sensor E. EGR mixes inert exhaust into intake to lower combustion peak temperature — too little raises NOx, too much causes misfires and rough running.

t: EGR Sensor E Circuit Intermittent
s: Same as P2384 on EGR sensor E. EGR mixes inert exhaust into intake to lower combustion peak temperature — too little raises NOx, too much causes misfires and rough running.

t: Water in Fuel Sensor B Circuit
s: A general circuit fault on the secondary water-in-fuel sensor B - typically used on diesel systems with two filter housings (pre-pump and post-pump). The ECU loses water-detection feedback on this stage; risk of water reaching the high-pressure pump and injectors.

t: Water in Fuel Sensor B Circuit Range or Performance
s: Same as P238A with the signal in plausible range yet not matching the engine model.

t: Water in Fuel Sensor B Circuit Low
s: Same as P238A with the signal voltage below the plausible range. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Water in Fuel Sensor B Circuit High
s: Same as P238A with the signal voltage above the plausible range. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Water in Fuel Sensor B Circuit Intermittent
s: Same as P238A with intermittent or erratic signal. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Water in Fuel Condition Sensor B
s: Sensor B reports water in the fuel filter housing - drain the water trap immediately. Continued operation may cause damage to the high-pressure pump and injectors.

t: Diesel Intake Air Flow B Control Circuit Open
s: Long-term plausibility checks show that the front oxygen sensor on bank 1 reacts too slowly or stays too close to a fixed value. The signal does not show normal cross-counts during closed-loop operation.

t: Diesel Intake Air Flow B Control Performance
s: Same as P2390 with the actuator electrically intact yet not delivering the commanded position - sticky valve, broken linkage, or position offset.

t: Diesel Intake Air Flow B Control Circuit Low
s: Same as P2390 with the control line shorted to ground. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Diesel Intake Air Flow B Control Circuit High
s: Same as P2390 with the control line shorted to supply. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Diesel Intake Air Flow B Control Stuck Open
s: Diesel intake air flow valve B is mechanically stuck open - the ECU cannot close it for anti-shudder shutdown or EGR mixing assistance.

t: Diesel Intake Air Flow B Control Stuck Closed
s: Same as P2394 with the valve stuck closed - intake airflow blocked even when the engine should run with full air.

t: Diesel Intake Air Flow B Position Sensor Circuit
s: A general circuit fault on the position sensor of diesel intake air flow valve B.

t: Diesel Intake Air Flow B Position Sensor Range or Performance
s: Same as P2396 with the signal in plausible range yet not matching the actuator command.

t: Diesel Intake Air Flow B Position Sensor Circuit Low
s: Same as P2396 with the signal voltage below the plausible range. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Diesel Intake Air Flow B Position Sensor Circuit High
s: Same as P2396 with the signal voltage above the plausible range. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Diesel Intake Air Flow B Position Sensor Circuit Intermittent or Erratic
s: Same as P2396 with intermittent or erratic signal. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Diesel Intake Air Flow B Control Motor Current Range or Performance
s: The motor current of the actuator on diesel intake air flow B is outside the calibrated window - typically excessive (sticky valve), or too low (worn brushes).

t: Diesel Intake Air Flow B Control System High Air Flow Detected
s: With diesel intake air flow valve B commanded closed the measured airflow is above the calibrated threshold - valve does not seat or position sensor offset.

t: Diesel Intake Air Flow B Control System Low Air Flow Detected
s: Same as P239C with the airflow below the threshold when the valve is commanded open - valve does not open fully or the path is restricted.

t: O2 Sensor Out of Range During Deceleration Bank 1 Sensor 2
s: During fuel cutoff on overrun the post-catalyst lambda sensor on bank 1 should drift toward a fixed lean reference voltage. If it stays outside the expected window the ECU sets this fault, indicating either a slow or stuck downstream sensor or an exhaust leak that draws unburnt gas across it.

t: O2 Sensor Out of Range During Deceleration Bank 2 Sensor 2
s: During fuel cutoff on overrun the post-catalyst lambda sensor on bank 2 should drift toward a fixed lean reference voltage. If it stays outside the expected window the ECU sets this fault, indicating either a slow or stuck downstream sensor or an exhaust leak that draws unburnt gas across it.

t: O2 Sensor Exhaust Sample Error Bank 1 Sensor 2
s: The post-catalyst lambda sensor on bank 1 cannot acquire a valid exhaust sample - the signal does not respond to the expected exhaust composition during the diagnostic window. Typical causes are a fouled sensing element, a clogged sensor body or an installation leak that bypasses exhaust around the sensor.

t: O2 Sensor Exhaust Sample Error Bank 2 Sensor 2
s: The post-catalyst lambda sensor on bank 2 cannot acquire a valid exhaust sample - the signal does not respond to the expected exhaust composition during the diagnostic window. Typical causes are a fouled sensing element, a clogged sensor body or an installation leak that bypasses exhaust around the sensor.

t: Exhaust Heat Exchanger Bypass Valve B Control Circuit Open
s: An open circuit is detected on the control line for the bypass flap A of the exhaust heat exchanger. This valve directs exhaust either through the heat exchanger (cold start, fast warm-up) or around it (normal operation). Without working control the warm-up strategy is impaired and emissions during cold start increase.

t: Exhaust Heat Exchanger Bypass Valve B Control Circuit Low
s: Same as P237D on bypass valve B. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Exhaust Heat Exchanger Bypass Valve B Control Circuit High
s: Same as P237E on bypass valve B. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Exhaust Heat Exchanger Bypass Valve B Control Performance
s: The bypass flap A of the exhaust heat exchanger does not move as commanded - the position feedback or downstream temperature does not respond to the control demand. The flap is mechanically stuck, the actuator gearing is worn, or the position sensor is offset.

t: Exhaust Heat Exchanger Bypass Valve B Stuck Open
s: Bypass valve B is stuck in the open position - exhaust bypasses the heat exchanger continuously, slowing engine warm-up.

t: Exhaust Heat Exchanger Bypass Valve B Stuck Closed
s: Same as P23A6 with bypass valve B stuck closed - exhaust always passes through the heat exchanger.

t: Exhaust Heat Exchanger Bypass Valve B Position Sensor Stop Performance
s: During calibration the position sensor of bypass valve B does not reach one of its mechanical end stops as expected.

t: Communication Fault Forced Engine Shutdown
s: A safety-critical communication loss between control modules has triggered a forced engine shutdown. The ECU could not validate signals it needs to operate safely (e.g. throttle commands, brake state) and stopped the engine.

t: Turbocharger Bypass Valve A Stuck
s: The turbocharger bypass valve A (compressor bypass for surge protection or wastegate-style boost relief) is mechanically stuck. Boost control is impaired - either no relief on overspeed or no boost build-up depending on which position the valve is stuck in.

t: NOx Sensor Processor Performance Bank 1 Sensor 3
s: The ECU has detected an internal performance fault in the processing of accelerator pedal position - the redundant signals are tracked internally and one of them shows a software-side anomaly.

t: NOx Sensor Circuit Bank 1 Sensor 3
s: A general circuit fault on the third NOx sensor of bank 1.

t: NOx Sensor Circuit Range or Performance Bank 1 Sensor 3
s: Same as P23AC with the signal in plausible range yet not matching the engine model.

t: NOx Sensor Circuit Low Bank 1 Sensor 3
s: Same as P23AC with the signal voltage below the plausible range. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: NOx Sensor Circuit High Bank 1 Sensor 3
s: Same as P23AC with the signal voltage above the plausible range. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: NOx Sensor Circuit Intermittent Bank 1 Sensor 3
s: Same as P23AC with intermittent or erratic signal. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: NOx Sensor Heater Control Circuit Open Bank 1 Sensor 3
s: The heater control circuit for the NOx sensor on bank 1 sensor 3 is open. Without the heater the sensor cannot reach operating temperature; readings stay invalid.

t: NOx Sensor Heater Control Circuit Low Bank 1 Sensor 3
s: Same as P23B1 with the heater control line shorted to ground. The sensor heater warms the element to operating temperature (~600 °C); without it the lambda sensor stays inactive in closed loop and the ECM cannot trim mixture.

t: NOx Sensor Heater Control Circuit High Bank 1 Sensor 3
s: Same as P23B1 with the heater control line shorted to supply. The sensor heater warms the element to operating temperature (~600 °C); without it the lambda sensor stays inactive in closed loop and the ECM cannot trim mixture.

t: NOx Sensor Heater Sense Circuit Bank 1 Sensor 3
s: A general circuit fault on the heater sense feedback for the bank 1 sensor 3 NOx sensor - the line over which the ECU verifies that the heater is actually drawing current.

t: NOx Sensor Heater Sense Circuit Range or Performance Bank 1 Sensor 3
s: Same as P23B4 with the sense voltage in plausible range yet not matching the heater model.

t: NOx Sensor Supply Voltage Circuit Bank 1 Sensor 3
s: The supply voltage to the bank 1 sensor 3 NOx sensor is outside the calibrated window.

t: NOx Sensor Heater Control Circuit Range or Performance Bank 1 Sensor 3
s: Same as P23B1 with the heater control loop in plausible range yet not matching the heater model - aged heater or harness resistance.

t: NOx Sensor Heater Sense Circuit Low Bank 1 Sensor 3
s: Same as P23B4 with the sense voltage below the plausible range. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: NOx Sensor Heater Sense Circuit High Bank 1 Sensor 3
s: Same as P23B4 with the sense voltage above the plausible range. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: NOx Sensor Heater Sense Circuit Intermittent Bank 1 Sensor 3
s: Same as P23B4 with intermittent or erratic signal. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: NOx Sensor Heater Resistance Bank 1 Sensor 3
s: The resistance of the NOx sensor heater on bank 1 sensor 3 is outside the calibrated band - aged element or contact resistance increased.

t: NOx Sensor Calibration Memory Bank 1 Sensor 3
s: The NOx sensor on bank 1 sensor 3 reports a calibration memory check-sum failure - factory calibration data corrupted or unreadable.

t: NOx Sensor Performance Signal Biased or Stuck High Bank 1 Sensor 3
s: The NOx sensor on bank 1 sensor 3 reports a value that stays at the upper end of its range despite changing engine conditions - sensor element biased or stuck high.

t: NOx Sensor Performance Signal Biased or Stuck Low Bank 1 Sensor 3
s: Same as P23BD with the sensor stuck low.

t: NOx Sensor Performance Slow Response Low to High Bank 1 Sensor 3
s: The NOx sensor on bank 1 sensor 3 takes longer than the diagnostic threshold to follow rising NOx levels - aged element.

t: NOx Sensor Performance Slow Response High to Low Bank 1 Sensor 3
s: The NOx sensor on bank 1 sensor 3 takes longer than the diagnostic threshold to follow rising NOx levels - aged element.

t: NOx Sensor Sensing Element Bank 1 Sensor 3
s: The downstream NOx sensor on bank 1 reports that its internal ZrO2 sensing element is no longer producing valid pumping currents - the element has lost its electrochemical response, typically because the active ceramic layer has been poisoned, cracked, or burnt out.

t: NOx Sensor Delayed Response High to Low Bank 1 Sensor 3
s: Same as P23C0 with a measurable delay before the sensor begins to track falling levels - the start of the response is delayed, not just the slope.

t: Engine Coolant Bypass Valve C Position Sensor Circuit
s: A general circuit fault on the position sensor of the third engine coolant bypass valve C - typically used in advanced thermal management systems with multiple coolant loops.

t: Engine Coolant Bypass Valve C Position Sensor Circuit Low
s: Same as P23C3 with the signal voltage below the plausible range. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Engine Coolant Bypass Valve C Position Sensor Circuit High
s: Same as P23C3 with the signal voltage above the plausible range. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Engine Coolant Bypass Valve C Control Circuit Driver Current or Temperature Too High
s: The driver stage of the coolant bypass valve C reports excessive current draw or thermal protection trip - typically a sticking valve drawing too much motor current.

t: Fuel Heater Control Module Power Control Circuit Open
s: The control line that energises the fuel heater control module - used on diesel systems for cold-weather fuel preheating - is open.

t: Fuel Heater Control Module Power Control Circuit Low
s: Same as P23C7 with the control line shorted to ground. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Fuel Heater Control Module Power Control Circuit High
s: Same as P23C7 with the control line shorted to supply. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Fuel Rail Heater Control Circuit Open
s: The fuel rail heater - on common-rail diesels for cold-start - has an open control circuit. The rail cannot be heated, slowing cold-start fuel atomisation.

t: Fuel Rail Heater Control Circuit Low
s: Same as P23CA with the control line shorted to ground. The sensor heater warms the element to operating temperature (~600 °C); without it the lambda sensor stays inactive in closed loop and the ECM cannot trim mixture.

t: Fuel Rail Heater Control Circuit High
s: Same as P23CA with the control line shorted to supply. The sensor heater warms the element to operating temperature (~600 °C); without it the lambda sensor stays inactive in closed loop and the ECM cannot trim mixture.

t: Fuel Heater Control Module Feedback Signal Open
s: The feedback line from the fuel heater control module to the ECM - reporting heater status - is open.

t: Fuel Heater Control Module Feedback Signal Performance
s: Same as P23CD with feedback in plausible range yet not matching the commanded heater state.

t: Fuel Heater Control Module Feedback Signal Circuit Low
s: Same as P23CD with the feedback voltage below the plausible range. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Fuel Heater Control Module Feedback Signal Circuit High
s: Same as P23CD with the feedback voltage above the plausible range. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Cylinder 1 Fuel Heater Control Circuit Open
s: Per-cylinder fuel heaters (used on some heavy-duty diesels for cold-start atomisation) on cylinder 1 have an open control circuit.

t: Cylinder 1 Fuel Heater Control Circuit High
s: Same as P23D1 with the control line shorted to supply. The sensor heater warms the element to operating temperature (~600 °C); without it the lambda sensor stays inactive in closed loop and the ECM cannot trim mixture.

t: Cylinder 1 Fuel Heater Control Circuit Low
s: Same as P23D1 with the control line shorted to ground. The sensor heater warms the element to operating temperature (~600 °C); without it the lambda sensor stays inactive in closed loop and the ECM cannot trim mixture.

t: Cylinder 1 Fuel Heater Performance
s: Cylinder 1 fuel heater is electrically intact yet does not deliver the expected heat - aged element or contact resistance high.

t: Cylinder 2 Fuel Heater Control Circuit Open
s: Per-cylinder fuel heaters (used on some heavy-duty diesels for cold-start atomisation) on cylinder 2 have an open control circuit.

t: Cylinder 2 Fuel Heater Control Circuit High
s: Same as P23D2 on cylinder 2. The sensor heater warms the element to operating temperature (~600 °C); without it the lambda sensor stays inactive in closed loop and the ECM cannot trim mixture.

t: Cylinder 2 Fuel Heater Control Circuit Low
s: Same as P23D3 on cylinder 2. The sensor heater warms the element to operating temperature (~600 °C); without it the lambda sensor stays inactive in closed loop and the ECM cannot trim mixture.

t: Cylinder 2 Fuel Heater Performance
s: Cylinder 2 fuel heater is electrically intact yet does not deliver the expected heat - aged element or contact resistance high.

t: Cylinder 3 Fuel Heater Control Circuit Open
s: Per-cylinder fuel heaters (used on some heavy-duty diesels for cold-start atomisation) on cylinder 3 have an open control circuit.

t: Cylinder 3 Fuel Heater Control Circuit High
s: Same as P23D2 on cylinder 3. The sensor heater warms the element to operating temperature (~600 °C); without it the lambda sensor stays inactive in closed loop and the ECM cannot trim mixture.

t: Cylinder 3 Fuel Heater Control Circuit Low
s: Same as P23D3 on cylinder 3. The sensor heater warms the element to operating temperature (~600 °C); without it the lambda sensor stays inactive in closed loop and the ECM cannot trim mixture.

t: Cylinder 3 Fuel Heater Performance
s: Cylinder 3 fuel heater is electrically intact yet does not deliver the expected heat - aged element or contact resistance high.

t: Cylinder 4 Fuel Heater Control Circuit Open
s: Per-cylinder fuel heaters (used on some heavy-duty diesels for cold-start atomisation) on cylinder 4 have an open control circuit.

t: Cylinder 4 Fuel Heater Control Circuit High
s: Same as P23D2 on cylinder 4. The sensor heater warms the element to operating temperature (~600 °C); without it the lambda sensor stays inactive in closed loop and the ECM cannot trim mixture.

t: Cylinder 4 Fuel Heater Control Circuit Low
s: Same as P23D3 on cylinder 4. The sensor heater warms the element to operating temperature (~600 °C); without it the lambda sensor stays inactive in closed loop and the ECM cannot trim mixture.

t: Cylinder 4 Fuel Heater Performance
s: Cylinder 4 fuel heater is electrically intact yet does not deliver the expected heat - aged element or contact resistance high.

t: EGR C Control Circuit Open
s: A general open-circuit fault on the third EGR valve C control - typically a third EGR loop in heavy-duty engines or specific Euro-7 systems.

t: EGR C Control Circuit Performance
s: Same as P23E1 with the actuator electrically intact but not delivering the commanded position. EGR mixes inert exhaust into intake to lower combustion peak temperature — too little raises NOx, too much causes misfires and rough running.

t: EGR C Control Circuit Low
s: Same as P23E1 with the control line shorted to ground. EGR mixes inert exhaust into intake to lower combustion peak temperature — too little raises NOx, too much causes misfires and rough running.

t: EGR C Control Circuit High
s: Same as P23E1 with the control line shorted to supply. EGR mixes inert exhaust into intake to lower combustion peak temperature — too little raises NOx, too much causes misfires and rough running.

t: EGR C Control Stuck Open
s: EGR valve C is stuck in the open position - exhaust gas continuously dilutes intake air, reducing power and risking misfires at idle.

t: EGR C Control Stuck Closed
s: Same as P23E5 with valve C stuck closed - no EGR through this stage; NOx emissions rise. EGR mixes inert exhaust into intake to lower combustion peak temperature — too little raises NOx, too much causes misfires and rough running.

t: EGR C Flow Insufficient Detected
s: Same as P049B on EGR loop C. EGR mixes inert exhaust into intake to lower combustion peak temperature — too little raises NOx, too much causes misfires and rough running.

t: EGR C Flow Excessive Detected
s: Same as P049C on EGR loop C. EGR mixes inert exhaust into intake to lower combustion peak temperature — too little raises NOx, too much causes misfires and rough running.

t: Camshaft A Position Signal Output Circuit Open Bank 2
s: The output line that the ECU uses to forward the bank 2 cam A position signal to other modules (e.g. dashboard, transmission, hybrid module) is open.

t: Camshaft A Position Signal Output Circuit Low Bank 2
s: Same as P23E9 with the output shorted to ground. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Camshaft A Position Signal Output Circuit High Bank 2
s: Same as P23E9 with the output shorted to supply. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Camshaft B Position Signal Output Circuit Open Bank 1
s: The output line that the ECU uses to forward the bank 2 cam A position signal to other modules (e.g. dashboard, transmission, hybrid module) is open.

t: Camshaft B Position Signal Output Circuit Low Bank 1
s: Same as P23EA on cam B bank 1. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Camshaft B Position Signal Output Circuit High Bank 1
s: Same as P23EB on cam B bank 1. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Camshaft B Position Signal Output Circuit Open Bank 2
s: The output line that the ECU uses to forward the bank 2 cam A position signal to other modules (e.g. dashboard, transmission, hybrid module) is open.

t: Camshaft B Position Signal Output Circuit Low Bank 2
s: Same as P23EA on cam B bank 2. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Camshaft B Position Signal Output Circuit High Bank 2
s: Same as P23EB on cam B bank 2. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Cylinder 1 Injector B Air-Fuel Ratio Imbalance
s: On dual-injector systems (typical for some heavy-duty diesels and some petrol/gas bi-fuel applications) the secondary injector B at cylinder 1 cannot keep its mixture in balance with the rest of the bank.

t: Cylinder 2 Injector B Air-Fuel Ratio Imbalance
s: On dual-injector systems (typical for some heavy-duty diesels and some petrol/gas bi-fuel applications) the secondary injector B at cylinder 2 cannot keep its mixture in balance with the rest of the bank.

t: Cylinder 3 Injector B Air-Fuel Ratio Imbalance
s: On dual-injector systems (typical for some heavy-duty diesels and some petrol/gas bi-fuel applications) the secondary injector B at cylinder 3 cannot keep its mixture in balance with the rest of the bank.

t: Cylinder 4 Injector B Air-Fuel Ratio Imbalance
s: On dual-injector systems (typical for some heavy-duty diesels and some petrol/gas bi-fuel applications) the secondary injector B at cylinder 4 cannot keep its mixture in balance with the rest of the bank.

t: Cylinder 5 Injector B Air-Fuel Ratio Imbalance
s: On dual-injector systems (typical for some heavy-duty diesels and some petrol/gas bi-fuel applications) the secondary injector B at cylinder 5 cannot keep its mixture in balance with the rest of the bank.

t: Cylinder 6 Injector B Air-Fuel Ratio Imbalance
s: On dual-injector systems (typical for some heavy-duty diesels and some petrol/gas bi-fuel applications) the secondary injector B at cylinder 6 cannot keep its mixture in balance with the rest of the bank.

t: Cylinder 7 Injector B Air-Fuel Ratio Imbalance
s: On dual-injector systems (typical for some heavy-duty diesels and some petrol/gas bi-fuel applications) the secondary injector B at cylinder 7 cannot keep its mixture in balance with the rest of the bank.

t: Cylinder 8 Injector B Air-Fuel Ratio Imbalance
s: On dual-injector systems (typical for some heavy-duty diesels and some petrol/gas bi-fuel applications) the secondary injector B at cylinder 8 cannot keep its mixture in balance with the rest of the bank.

t: Cylinder 9 Injector B Air-Fuel Ratio Imbalance
s: On dual-injector systems (typical for some heavy-duty diesels and some petrol/gas bi-fuel applications) the secondary injector B at cylinder 9 cannot keep its mixture in balance with the rest of the bank.

t: Cylinder 10 Injector B Air-Fuel Ratio Imbalance
s: On dual-injector systems (typical for some heavy-duty diesels and some petrol/gas bi-fuel applications) the secondary injector B at cylinder 10 cannot keep its mixture in balance with the rest of the bank.

t: Cylinder 11 Injector B Air-Fuel Ratio Imbalance
s: On dual-injector systems (typical for some heavy-duty diesels and some petrol/gas bi-fuel applications) the secondary injector B at cylinder 11 cannot keep its mixture in balance with the rest of the bank.

t: Cylinder 12 Injector B Air-Fuel Ratio Imbalance
s: On dual-injector systems (typical for some heavy-duty diesels and some petrol/gas bi-fuel applications) the secondary injector B at cylinder 12 cannot keep its mixture in balance with the rest of the bank.

t: Bank 1 System B Air-Fuel Ratio Imbalance
s: The secondary fuel system B on bank 1 (e.g. port-injection rail in a dual-injection engine, or LPG/CNG rail in a bi-fuel system) does not maintain calibrated mixture - lambda response on bank 1 is biased by an offset on the B system.

t: Bank 2 System B Air-Fuel Ratio Imbalance
s: The secondary fuel system B on bank 2 (e.g. port-injection rail in a dual-injection engine, or LPG/CNG rail in a bi-fuel system) does not maintain calibrated mixture - lambda response on bank 2 is biased by an offset on the B system.

t: EVAP Leak Detection Pump Control Circuit Open
s: The control circuit to the EVAP leak-detection pump is open. Without that pump the ECU cannot pressurise the fuel-vapour system to test for leaks.

t: EVAP Leak Detection Pump Control Circuit Low
s: Drive voltage on the leak-detection pump line is below the lower limit. Often a short to ground in the harness.

t: EVAP Leak Detection Pump Control Circuit High
s: Drive voltage on the leak-detection pump line is above the upper limit. Usually a short to supply or open ground.

t: EVAP System Leak Detection Pump Sense Circuit Open
s: The leak detection pump (LDP) - which actively pressurises the EVAP system to find leaks - has an open sense circuit. The ECU cannot read the pump status during the diagnostic cycle.

t: EVAP Leak Detection Pump Sense Circuit Range/Performance
s: The sense (reed-switch or current-feedback) signal from the leak-detection pump is electrically valid but does not match expectations during a leak test. Often a worn pump diaphragm or a sticking reed contact.

t: EVAP System Leak Detection Pump Sense Circuit Low
s: Same as P2403 with the sense voltage below the plausible range. The evaporative system captures fuel vapor from the tank and purges it into intake; leaks or stuck purge valves leak hydrocarbons and cause emissions failures.

t: EVAP System Leak Detection Pump Sense Circuit High
s: Same as P2403 with the sense voltage above the plausible range. The evaporative system captures fuel vapor from the tank and purges it into intake; leaks or stuck purge valves leak hydrocarbons and cause emissions failures.

t: EVAP Leak Detection Pump Sense Circuit Range/Performance
s: The leak detection pump returns a sense signal that is electrically valid but does not match the expected response during the pump's self-test or pressure cycle.

t: Fuel Cap Sensor/Switch Circuit Range/Performance
s: On vehicles with a sensed fuel cap, the cap-status signal is electrically valid but does not change as expected when the cap is opened or closed.

t: Fuel Cap Sensor or Switch Circuit Range or Performance
s: The sensor or switch that detects whether the fuel cap is properly closed reports a value within plausible range yet not matching driver action.

t: EVAP System Leak Detection Pump Heater Control Circuit Open
s: The leak detection pump heater - keeps the pump diaphragm warm in cold weather - has an open control circuit.

t: EVAP System Leak Detection Pump Heater Control Circuit Low
s: Same as P240A with the control line shorted to ground. The sensor heater warms the element to operating temperature (~600 °C); without it the lambda sensor stays inactive in closed loop and the ECM cannot trim mixture.

t: EVAP System Leak Detection Pump Heater Control Circuit High
s: Same as P240A with the control line shorted to supply. The sensor heater warms the element to operating temperature (~600 °C); without it the lambda sensor stays inactive in closed loop and the ECM cannot trim mixture.

t: Alternative Fuel Low Pressure System Leak
s: A leak detected in the low-pressure side of an alternative fuel system (LPG, CNG between regulator and rail, or H2 between vapouriser and rail). Risk of escape into the engine bay.

t: Alternative Fuel High Pressure System Leak
s: A leak detected in the low-pressure side of an alternative fuel system (LPG, CNG between regulator and rail, or H2 between vapouriser and rail). Risk of escape into the engine bay.

t: EGR Slow Response
s: The EGR system reacts to commanded changes more slowly than calibrated - typically a partly clogged EGR cooler, sticky valve, or worn actuator.

t: Fuel Cap Sensor/Switch Stuck
s: The fuel cap status switch is stuck in one position — the ECU never sees the expected transition between open and closed.

t: Fuel Cap Sensor/Switch Circuit
s: A general electrical fault on the fuel cap sensor/switch circuit — open or short.

t: Fuel Cap Sensor or Switch Circuit Intermittent or Erratic
s: Same as P2409 with intermittent or erratic signal. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Exhaust Gas Recirculation System Performance
s: The ECU monitors EGR flow indirectly through MAF, MAP or differential-pressure feedback and finds that actual EGR flow does not match the commanded value over a sustained period.

t: O2 Sensor Exhaust Sample Error Bank 1 Sensor 1
s: Same as P237A but on the upstream sensor B1S1 (pre-catalyst). A drifted or sluggish lambda sensor lets the mixture run lean or rich without correction — fuel economy and emissions suffer immediately.

t: EVAP Switching Valve Circuit Open
s: The ECU detects an open circuit on the EVAP switching valve drive line. The valve cannot be commanded between its purge and isolation positions.

t: EVAP Switching Valve Performance
s: The EVAP switching valve responds electrically but does not produce the expected pressure change. Often a stuck valve seat or a torn diaphragm.

t: EVAP Switching Valve Stuck Closed
s: The EVAP switching valve cannot reach the open position. Purge flow is blocked and the EVAP system cannot vent the canister.

t: EVAP Switching Valve Control Circuit Performance
s: The evaporative switching valve — used on multi-path EVAP systems to route purge or pressure-test flow — does not respond as expected. Self-test fails because tank pressure does not change in the calibrated direction.

t: EVAP Switching Valve Stuck Open
s: The EVAP switching valve cannot close. Tank pressure cannot be isolated for the leak detection test, and purge routing may bypass the canister.

t: O2 Sensor Signals Swapped Bank 1 Sensor 1 and Sensor 2
s: The pre- and post-catalyst lambda signals on bank 1 are swapped - the upstream sensor responds like a downstream sensor and vice versa. Typically the harness was reversed during a sensor change.

t: O2 Sensor Signals Swapped Bank 2 Sensor 1 and Sensor 2
s: The pre- and post-catalyst lambda signals on bank 2 are swapped - the upstream sensor responds like a downstream sensor and vice versa. Typically the harness was reversed during a sensor change.

t: Throttle Actuator Control System Ice Blockage Bank 2
s: The throttle actuator on bank 2 cannot move because of ice formation - typical for very cold ambient temperature with humid intake. Engine de-ices on warm-up.

t: SCR Inducement Forced Engine Shutdown
s: The SCR inducement strategy (mandatory regulatory measure to enforce AdBlue refilling and emissions compliance) has reached the final stage and shut down the engine. Refill AdBlue and clear inducement fault to restart.

t: Reductant Heater Coolant Control Valve Stuck Closed
s: The valve that routes engine coolant to heat the AdBlue lines is stuck closed - AdBlue lines do not warm up and may freeze in cold weather, blocking dosing.

t: EGR Cooler B Efficiency Below Threshold
s: The temperature drop across EGR cooler B is below the calibrated threshold - cooler is fouled with soot, has internal scaling, or coolant flow is restricted. Recirculated gas reaches the intake hotter than designed, increasing NOx and combustion temperatures.

t: EVAP Switching Valve Stuck Closed
s: The EVAP switching valve cannot open when commanded. Purge or test flow cannot reach the appropriate path and the leak monitor aborts.

t: EVAP Vent Valve Stuck Open
s: The evaporative emission system vent valve does not close on command. The ECU detects that the evap system cannot hold pressure or vacuum because the canister is permanently vented to atmosphere.

t: EVAP Canister Vent Valve Stuck Closed
s: The vent valve at the canister end of the EVAP system cannot open. Without ambient air entering the canister, fresh fuel cannot purge the stored vapors and refueling may trigger ORVR issues. Common with rusted or rodent-damaged vent valves.

t: HC Adsorption Catalyst Efficiency Below Threshold Bank 1
s: The ECU determines that the hydrocarbon adsorption catalyst on bank 1 no longer holds enough HC during cold start to meet emission targets. Often due to ageing or thermal damage.

t: HC Adsorption Catalyst Efficiency Below Threshold Bank 2
s: The hydrocarbon adsorption catalyst on bank 2 - which traps HC during cold-start until the main catalyst lights off - is degraded; HC slip during cold start is above the calibrated threshold.

t: EGR Cooling Valve Control Circuit Open
s: The valve that admits coolant flow to the EGR cooler has an open control circuit - cooling cannot be enabled and EGR temperatures stay too high.

t: EGR Cooling Valve Control Circuit Low
s: Same as P2425 with the control line shorted to ground. EGR mixes inert exhaust into intake to lower combustion peak temperature — too little raises NOx, too much causes misfires and rough running.

t: EGR Cooling Valve Control Circuit High
s: Same as P2425 with the control line shorted to supply. EGR mixes inert exhaust into intake to lower combustion peak temperature — too little raises NOx, too much causes misfires and rough running.

t: Exhaust Gas Temperature Too High Bank 1
s: An EGT sensor on bank 1 reports a temperature above the safety threshold for prolonged operation - typical for blocked DPF, restricted exhaust, or excessive fuelling. Risk of catalyst, DPF or turbo damage.

t: Exhaust Gas Temperature Too High Bank 2
s: An EGT sensor on bank 2 reports a temperature above the safety threshold for prolonged operation - typical for blocked DPF, restricted exhaust, or excessive fuelling. Risk of catalyst, DPF or turbo damage.

t: Exhaust Gas Temperature Sensor Circuit Bank 1 Sensor 3
s: A general circuit fault is detected on the EGT sensor at bank 1 sensor 3 (typically just downstream of the turbo or before the catalyst). The ECU loses thermal protection feedback for the catalyst and DPF.

t: Exhaust Gas Temperature Sensor Circuit Range or Performance Bank 1 Sensor 3
s: Same as P242A with the signal in plausible range yet not matching the engine model.

t: Exhaust Gas Temperature Sensor Circuit Low Bank 1 Sensor 3
s: Same as P242A with the signal voltage below the plausible range. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Exhaust Gas Temperature Sensor Circuit High Bank 1 Sensor 3
s: Same as P242A with the signal voltage above the plausible range. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Exhaust Gas Temperature Sensor Circuit Intermittent or Erratic Bank 1 Sensor 3
s: Same as P242A with intermittent or erratic signal. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Particulate Filter Restriction Ash Accumulation Bank 1
s: The diesel particulate filter on bank 1 reports backpressure attributable to ash (non-combustible residue from oil and fuel additives, not removable by regeneration). The filter has reached the end of its service life and needs cleaning or replacement.

t: AIR System Air Flow or Pressure Sensor Circuit Bank 1
s: A general circuit fault on the secondary air injection (AIR) system flow or pressure sensor on bank 1 - the sensor that verifies air pump flow during cold-start enrichment compensation.

t: Secondary Air Injection System Air Flow/Pressure Sensor Circuit
s: The ECU detects a general fault in the secondary air injection system air flow or pressure sensor circuit. The diagnostic cannot verify proper SAI operation.

t: Secondary Air Injection System Air Flow/Pressure Sensor Circuit Range/Performance
s: The signal of the SAI air flow or pressure sensor is implausible during operation. The reading does not change as expected when the air pump is commanded.

t: AIR System Air Flow or Pressure Sensor Circuit High Bank 1
s: Same as P2430 with the signal voltage above the plausible range. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: AIR System Air Flow or Pressure Sensor Circuit Intermittent or Erratic Bank 1
s: Same as P2430 with intermittent or erratic signal. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: AIR System Air Flow or Pressure Sensor Circuit Bank 2
s: A general circuit fault on the secondary air injection (AIR) system flow or pressure sensor on bank 2 - the sensor that verifies air pump flow during cold-start enrichment compensation.

t: AIR System Air Flow or Pressure Sensor Circuit Range or Performance Bank 2
s: Same as P2430 on bank 2 with the signal in plausible range yet not matching the engine model.

t: AIR System Air Flow or Pressure Sensor Circuit Low Bank 2
s: A general circuit fault on the secondary air injection (AIR) system flow or pressure sensor on bank 2 - the sensor that verifies air pump flow during cold-start enrichment compensation.

t: AIR System Air Flow or Pressure Sensor Circuit High Bank 2
s: Same as P2433 on bank 2. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: AIR System Air Flow or Pressure Sensor Circuit Intermittent or Erratic Bank 2
s: Same as P2434 on bank 2. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Particulate Filter Restriction Ash Accumulation Bank 2
s: The diesel particulate filter on bank 2 reports backpressure attributable to ash (non-combustible residue from oil and fuel additives, not removable by regeneration). The filter has reached the end of its service life and needs cleaning or replacement.

t: Particulate Filter Restriction Forced Limited Power Bank 2
s: DPF backpressure on bank 2 is so high that the ECU enforces limp-home power limitation to protect the engine. Regeneration must be performed (or the DPF cleaned) to clear the limit.

t: Particulate Filter Regeneration Frequency Bank 2
s: The DPF on bank 2 needs regeneration more often than calibrated. Possible causes are an oil-burning engine, fuel-injector leak, short-trip operation, or a sensor offset.

t: Particulate Filter Regeneration Duration Bank 2
s: Regenerations on bank 2 take longer than calibrated to complete - typically a low EGT during the cycle (cold weather, short trips) or a partially aged catalyst before the DPF.

t: Particulate Filter Regeneration Incomplete Bank 2
s: A regeneration cycle on bank 2 was started but did not bring the DPF backpressure down sufficiently - either the cycle was interrupted (driver stopped before completion) or thermal conditions were not met.

t: Particulate Filter Restriction Soot Accumulation Too High Bank 2
s: Soot loading on bank 2 DPF has reached a level that exceeds even the high-load regeneration capability - manual workshop regeneration or DPF cleaning required.

t: Secondary Air Injection System Switching Valve Stuck Open (Bank 1)
s: The switching/check valve in the secondary air injection plumbing on bank 1 is stuck in the open position. With the valve permanently open, exhaust gas can back-feed into the air pump and corrode it, while the downstream O2 sensor sees ambient air constantly.

t: Secondary Air Injection System Switching Valve Stuck Closed (Bank 1)
s: The switching valve on bank 1 will not open when commanded, so no secondary air reaches the exhaust during cold start. Catalyst light-off is delayed and emissions during the cold-start window rise.

t: Secondary Air Injection System Switching Valve Stuck Open (Bank 2)
s: The switching/check valve in the secondary air injection plumbing on bank 2 is stuck in the open position. With the valve permanently open, exhaust gas can back-feed into the air pump and corrode it, while the downstream O2 sensor sees ambient air constantly.

t: Secondary Air Injection System Switching Valve Stuck Closed (Bank 2)
s: The switching valve on bank 2 will not open when commanded, so no secondary air reaches the exhaust during cold start. Catalyst light-off is delayed and emissions during the cold-start window rise.

t: Secondary Air Injection System Pump Stuck Off (Bank 1)
s: The secondary-air pump does not run when commanded during cold start. Often a seized pump (after exhaust back-feed corroded the impeller), a blown fuse, or a failed relay.

t: Secondary Air Injection System Pump Stuck On (Bank 1)
s: The secondary-air pump continues to run after the cold-start window, when the ECU has commanded it off. Usually a stuck-closed relay welding the pump permanently to battery voltage.

t: Secondary Air Injection Pump Stuck Off (Bank 2)
s: Bank 2 secondary air pump does not deliver air when commanded. The catalyst light-off process is delayed and cold-start emissions rise.

t: Secondary Air Injection Pump Stuck On (Bank 2)
s: Bank 2 secondary air pump runs continuously. Either a stuck-on relay or a shorted control output keeps the pump powered when it should be off.

t: AIR System High Air Flow Bank 1
s: The secondary air injection system on bank 1 delivers more flow than calibrated when the pump is commanded on - typically a stuck-open switching valve allowing free flow.

t: AIR System High Air Flow Bank 2
s: The secondary air injection system on bank 2 delivers more flow than calibrated when the pump is commanded on - typically a stuck-open switching valve allowing free flow.

t: Diesel Particulate Filter Differential Pressure Too Low
s: The differential pressure across the DPF is below the calibrated minimum even at higher loads. Often a sign of a damaged or missing filter substrate, or a leak in the sensing line.

t: Diesel Particulate Filter Differential Pressure Sensor Offset
s: At engine off, the DPF differential pressure sensor does not return to zero within the allowed offset window. The static offset is too large to be ignored.

t: Exhaust Temperature Too Low For Particulate Filter Regeneration Bank 1
s: During an attempted regeneration of the bank 1 DPF the EGT did not reach the temperature window needed for soot oxidation. Regeneration could not start or was held. Driving in higher load is needed.

t: Exhaust Temperature Too High For Particulate Filter Regeneration Bank 1
s: Same as P244C with the EGT too high - regeneration paused to protect filter substrate from melt damage.

t: Exhaust Temperature Too Low For Particulate Filter Regeneration Bank 2
s: During an attempted regeneration of the bank 2 DPF the EGT did not reach the temperature window needed for soot oxidation. Regeneration could not start or was held. Driving in higher load is needed.

t: Exhaust Temperature Too High For Particulate Filter Regeneration Bank 2
s: Same as P244D on bank 2.

t: EVAP System Switching Valve Performance/Stuck
s: The EVAP switching valve does not respond to commands as expected. The valve is mechanically stuck or unable to switch between purge and vent paths, blocking proper leak diagnosis.

t: EVAP System Switching Valve Stuck Closed
s: The EVAP switching valve - which directs vapor flow between purge and leak-detection paths - is stuck in the closed position. The diagnostic cycle cannot complete.

t: Diesel Particulate Filter Pressure Sensor Circuit
s: A general electrical fault on the DPF differential-pressure sensor circuit. Without a valid pressure reading the ECU cannot estimate soot load and may inhibit regeneration.

t: Diesel Particulate Filter Pressure Sensor Circuit Range/Performance
s: The DPF differential-pressure sensor produces a value that is electrically valid but does not match expectations — for example a non-zero reading with engine off or no change between idle and load.

t: Diesel Particulate Filter Pressure Sensor Circuit Low
s: Voltage on the DPF differential-pressure sensor signal line is below the lower threshold. Typically a short to ground or an open in the supply line.

t: Diesel Particulate Filter Pressure Sensor Circuit High
s: Voltage on the DPF pressure sensor line exceeds the upper threshold. Usually a short to supply or an open ground on the sensor.

t: DPF Differential Pressure Sensor Circuit Intermittent
s: The DPF differential-pressure sensor delivers an intermittent signal — usually a marginal connector or a chafed wiring section that breaks contact during temperature or vibration.

t: EGR Cooler Performance
s: The EGR cooler does not lower exhaust gas temperature as expected — typically internal coolant fouling, a partial blockage, or a cracked cooler that mixes coolant with exhaust gas.

t: DPF Regeneration Duration
s: The DPF regeneration cycle takes longer than the calibrated maximum. The ECU sets this code when the soot load is not being burned off within the expected window — common after many short trips or with degraded post-injection.

t: Diesel Particulate Filter Regeneration Frequency
s: Active DPF regenerations are being requested more often than the calibration window allows. Indicates that soot accumulates faster than the filter can burn it off — typically due to short-trip use, leaking injectors, or excessive engine oil consumption.

t: EGR Cooler Bypass Control Circuit Open Bank 1
s: The EGR cooler bypass valve on bank 1 - routes EGR around the cooler during cold-start to keep cabin and engine warm-up fast - has an open control circuit.

t: EGR Cooler Bypass Control Circuit Range or Performance Bank 1
s: Same as P245A with the actuator electrically intact yet not delivering the commanded position. EGR mixes inert exhaust into intake to lower combustion peak temperature — too little raises NOx, too much causes misfires and rough running.

t: EGR Cooler Bypass Control Circuit Low Bank 1
s: Same as P245A with the control line shorted to ground. EGR mixes inert exhaust into intake to lower combustion peak temperature — too little raises NOx, too much causes misfires and rough running.

t: EGR Cooler Bypass Control Circuit High Bank 1
s: Same as P245A with the control line shorted to supply. EGR mixes inert exhaust into intake to lower combustion peak temperature — too little raises NOx, too much causes misfires and rough running.

t: Particulate Filter Pressure Sensor B Circuit
s: A general circuit fault on the secondary differential pressure sensor B across the DPF - measures backpressure for the regeneration strategy.

t: Particulate Filter Pressure Sensor B Circuit Range or Performance
s: Same as P245E with the signal in plausible range yet not matching the engine model.

t: DPF Restriction — Forced Limited Power
s: The DPF restriction has reached a level that forces the ECU to limit engine power. The driver must allow a forced regeneration or have the filter serviced before normal power is restored.

t: DPF Restriction — Forced Limited
s: The DPF restriction has reached the limit defined for forced limited operation. Vehicle behaviour depends on the manufacturer — usually a hard torque cap with a service warning.

t: Diesel Particulate Filter Restriction - Soot Accumulation
s: The DPF differential pressure sensor reports a backpressure rise consistent with excessive soot accumulation. The filter cannot regenerate fast enough, eventually causing limp mode.

t: Diesel Particulate Filter Restriction — Soot Accumulation
s: The estimated soot mass in the DPF has crossed the upper limit and the ECU could not complete a regeneration to bring it back down. Very common on cars used on short trips. Continued driving in this state risks a runaway regeneration that destroys the filter.

t: Diesel Particulate Filter Differential Pressure Too High
s: The differential pressure across the DPF exceeds the calibrated upper threshold during operation. Indicates severe blockage of the filter element.

t: Diesel Particulate Filter Differential Pressure Sensor Range/Performance
s: The DPF differential pressure sensor signal is implausible compared to engine load and exhaust flow. Either the sensor or the pressure tubes leading to it are at fault.

t: Exhaust Gas Temperature Sensor Circuit Bank 2 Sensor 3
s: A general circuit fault is detected on the EGT sensor at bank 2 sensor 3 (typically just downstream of the turbo or before the catalyst). The ECU loses thermal protection feedback for the catalyst and DPF.

t: Exhaust Gas Temperature Sensor Circuit Range or Performance Bank 2 Sensor 3
s: Same as P242B on bank 2 sensor 3.

t: Exhaust Gas Temperature Sensor Circuit Low Bank 2 Sensor 3
s: Same as P242C on bank 2 sensor 3. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Exhaust Gas Temperature Sensor Circuit High Bank 2 Sensor 3
s: Same as P242D on bank 2 sensor 3. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Exhaust Gas Temperature Sensor Circuit Intermittent or Erratic Bank 2 Sensor 3
s: Same as P242E on bank 2 sensor 3. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Vehicle Conditions Incorrect for Particulate Filter Regeneration
s: The driving pattern (short trips, low speed, frequent stops) does not provide the load and EGT needed for passive or active DPF regeneration. Filter loading rises until limit reached.

t: Particulate Filter Restriction Forced Limited Power Bank 1
s: DPF backpressure on bank 2 is so high that the ECU enforces limp-home power limitation to protect the engine. Regeneration must be performed (or the DPF cleaned) to clear the limit.

t: Particulate Filter Pressure Sensor A and B Correlation
s: The two DPF differential pressure sensors A and B disagree by more than the diagnostic tolerance - one of them has drifted.

t: Exhaust Gas Temperature Sensor Circuit Bank 1 Sensor 4
s: A general circuit fault is detected on the EGT sensor at bank 1 sensor 4 (typically just downstream of the turbo or before the catalyst). The ECU loses thermal protection feedback for the catalyst and DPF.

t: Reductant System Cold Start Performance
s: At cold start the SCR system is unable to begin reductant dosing within the required time. Typical cause on diesel cars in winter — frozen AdBlue in the tank or lines, failed tank heater, or a slow-warming SCR catalyst.

t: Exhaust Gas Temperature Sensor Circuit Intermittent Bank 1 Sensor 3
s: The exhaust gas temperature sensor 3 on bank 1 (often after the DPF) shows intermittent dropouts. Erratic readings disturb DPF regeneration logic.

t: Exhaust Gas Temperature Sensor Circuit Range/Performance Bank 1 Sensor 3
s: EGT sensor 3 on bank 1 reports values that are not plausible for current operating conditions. Either drift or wiring resistance changes the signal away from the expected value.

t: Exhaust Gas Temperature Sensor Circuit Low Bank 1 Sensor 3
s: The voltage from EGT sensor 3 on bank 1 is below the calibrated minimum. The signal indicates either a short to ground or a sensor returning impossibly low temperature.

t: Exhaust Gas Temperature Sensor Circuit High Bank 1 Sensor 3
s: The voltage of EGT sensor 3 on bank 1 is above the calibrated maximum. Often a sign of an open sensor circuit or a short to supply voltage.

t: Exhaust Gas Temperature Sensor Circuit Range or Performance Bank 2 Sensor 4
s: Same as P242B on bank 2 sensor 4.

t: Exhaust Gas Temperature Sensor Circuit Low Bank 2 Sensor 4
