s: Same as P242C on bank 2 sensor 4. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Exhaust Gas Temperature Sensor Circuit High Bank 2 Sensor 4
s: Same as P242D on bank 2 sensor 4. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Exhaust Gas Temperature Sensor Circuit Intermittent or Erratic Bank 2 Sensor 4
s: Same as P242E on bank 2 sensor 4. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Exhaust Gas Temperature Out of Range Bank 1 Sensor 1
s: The EGT sensor at bank 1 sensor 1 reports a value outside its plausible operating range over time - typically a degraded element giving an offset that the substitute logic flags as implausible.

t: Exhaust Gas Temperature Out of Range Bank 1 Sensor 2
s: The EGT sensor at bank 1 sensor 2 reports a value outside its plausible operating range over time - typically a degraded element giving an offset that the substitute logic flags as implausible.

t: Exhaust Gas Temperature Out of Range Bank 1 Sensor 3
s: The EGT sensor at bank 1 sensor 3 reports a value outside its plausible operating range over time - typically a degraded element giving an offset that the substitute logic flags as implausible.

t: Exhaust Gas Temperature Out of Range Bank 1 Sensor 4
s: The EGT sensor at bank 1 sensor 4 reports a value outside its plausible operating range over time - typically a degraded element giving an offset that the substitute logic flags as implausible.

t: Exhaust Gas Temperature Out of Range Bank 2 Sensor 1
s: The EGT sensor at bank 2 sensor 1 reports a value outside its plausible operating range over time - typically a degraded element giving an offset that the substitute logic flags as implausible.

t: Exhaust Gas Temperature Out of Range Bank 2 Sensor 2
s: The EGT sensor at bank 2 sensor 2 reports a value outside its plausible operating range over time - typically a degraded element giving an offset that the substitute logic flags as implausible.

t: Exhaust Gas Temperature Out of Range Bank 2 Sensor 3
s: The EGT sensor at bank 2 sensor 3 reports a value outside its plausible operating range over time - typically a degraded element giving an offset that the substitute logic flags as implausible.

t: Exhaust Gas Temperature Out of Range Bank 2 Sensor 4
s: The EGT sensor at bank 2 sensor 4 reports a value outside its plausible operating range over time - typically a degraded element giving an offset that the substitute logic flags as implausible.

t: Exhaust Gas Temperature Sensor Circuit Bank 2 Sensor 3
s: General electrical fault on EGT sensor 3 on bank 2. The ECU loses the temperature reference required for DPF regeneration management on bank 2.

t: Exhaust Gas Temperature Sensor Circuit Low Bank 1 Sensor 5
s: Same as P242C on bank 1 sensor 5. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Exhaust Gas Temperature Sensor Circuit High Bank 1 Sensor 5
s: Same as P242D on bank 1 sensor 5. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Exhaust Gas Temperature Sensor Circuit Range or Performance Bank 1 Sensor 5
s: Same as P242B on bank 1 sensor 5.

t: Exhaust Gas Temperature Sensor Circuit Intermittent or Erratic Bank 1 Sensor 5
s: Same as P242E on bank 1 sensor 5. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Exhaust Gas Temperature Sensor Circuit Open Bank 2 Sensor 5
s: A general circuit fault is detected on the EGT sensor at bank 2 sensor 5 (typically just downstream of the turbo or before the catalyst). The ECU loses thermal protection feedback for the catalyst and DPF.

t: Exhaust Gas Temperature Sensor Circuit Low Bank 2 Sensor 5
s: Same as P242C on bank 2 sensor 5. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Exhaust Gas Temperature Sensor Circuit High Bank 2 Sensor 5
s: Same as P242D on bank 2 sensor 5. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Exhaust Gas Temperature Sensor Circuit Range or Performance Bank 2 Sensor 5
s: Same as P242B on bank 2 sensor 5.

t: Exhaust Gas Temperature Sensor Circuit Intermittent or Erratic Bank 2 Sensor 5
s: Same as P242E on bank 2 sensor 5. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Reductant Heater A Sense Circuit Low
s: The sense feedback line for the AdBlue heater A measures voltage below the plausible range - typically a short to ground or open in the heater element.

t: Reductant Heater A Sense Circuit High
s: Same as P248A with the sense voltage above the plausible range. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Reductant Heater B Sense Circuit Low
s: The sense feedback line for the AdBlue heater A measures voltage below the plausible range - typically a short to ground or open in the heater element.

t: Reductant Heater B Sense Circuit High
s: Same as P248B on heater B. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: EGR Cooler Bypass Control Circuit Open Bank 2
s: The EGR cooler bypass valve on bank 2 - routes EGR around the cooler during cold-start to keep cabin and engine warm-up fast - has an open control circuit.

t: EGR Cooler Bypass Control Circuit Range or Performance Bank 2
s: Same as P245B on bank 2. EGR mixes inert exhaust into intake to lower combustion peak temperature — too little raises NOx, too much causes misfires and rough running.

t: EGR Cooler Bypass Control Circuit Low Bank 2
s: Same as P245C on bank 2. EGR mixes inert exhaust into intake to lower combustion peak temperature — too little raises NOx, too much causes misfires and rough running.

t: EGR Cooler Bypass Control Circuit High Bank 2
s: Same as P245D on bank 2. EGR mixes inert exhaust into intake to lower combustion peak temperature — too little raises NOx, too much causes misfires and rough running.

t: EGR Cooler Bypass Position Sensor Circuit Bank 1
s: A general circuit fault on the position feedback sensor of the EGR cooler bypass valve on bank 1. EGR mixes inert exhaust into intake to lower combustion peak temperature — too little raises NOx, too much causes misfires and rough running.

t: EGR Cooler Bypass Position Sensor Range or Performance Bank 1
s: Same as P2492 with the signal in plausible range yet not matching the actuator command. EGR mixes inert exhaust into intake to lower combustion peak temperature — too little raises NOx, too much causes misfires and rough running.

t: EGR Cooler Bypass Position Sensor Circuit Low Bank 1
s: Same as P2492 with the signal voltage below the plausible range. EGR mixes inert exhaust into intake to lower combustion peak temperature — too little raises NOx, too much causes misfires and rough running.

t: EGR Cooler Bypass Position Sensor Circuit High Bank 1
s: Same as P2492 with the signal voltage above the plausible range. EGR mixes inert exhaust into intake to lower combustion peak temperature — too little raises NOx, too much causes misfires and rough running.

t: EGR Cooler Bypass Position Sensor Circuit Intermittent or Erratic Bank 1
s: Same as P2492 with intermittent or erratic signal. EGR mixes inert exhaust into intake to lower combustion peak temperature — too little raises NOx, too much causes misfires and rough running.

t: EGR Cooler Bypass Position Sensor Circuit Bank 2
s: A general circuit fault on the position feedback sensor of the EGR cooler bypass valve on bank 2. EGR mixes inert exhaust into intake to lower combustion peak temperature — too little raises NOx, too much causes misfires and rough running.

t: EGR Cooler Bypass Position Sensor Range or Performance Bank 2
s: Same as P2493 on bank 2. EGR mixes inert exhaust into intake to lower combustion peak temperature — too little raises NOx, too much causes misfires and rough running.

t: EGR Cooler Bypass Position Sensor Circuit Low Bank 2
s: Same as P2494 on bank 2. EGR mixes inert exhaust into intake to lower combustion peak temperature — too little raises NOx, too much causes misfires and rough running.

t: EGR Cooler Bypass Position Sensor Circuit High Bank 2
s: Same as P2495 on bank 2. EGR mixes inert exhaust into intake to lower combustion peak temperature — too little raises NOx, too much causes misfires and rough running.

t: EGR Cooler Bypass Position Sensor Circuit Intermittent or Erratic Bank 2
s: Same as P2496 on bank 2. EGR mixes inert exhaust into intake to lower combustion peak temperature — too little raises NOx, too much causes misfires and rough running.

t: Excessive Time To Enter Closed Loop Reductant Injection Control
s: After cold start the AdBlue injection runs open-loop until SCR temperature is reached. The transition into closed-loop is taking longer than calibrated.

t: Closed Loop Reductant Injection Control At Limit Flow Too Low
s: With reductant injection running closed-loop the controller drives the dosing valve to maximum opening but cannot reach the required AdBlue flow.

t: Closed Loop Reductant Injection Control At Limit Flow Too High
s: Same as P249D with the controller fully closing the dosing valve yet still measuring more flow than needed - the valve does not seat fully.

t: Excessive Time To Enter Closed Loop Particulate Filter Regeneration Control
s: The DPF regeneration controller cannot transition into its closed-loop temperature control quickly enough - typically because EGT does not rise to setpoint within the calibrated window.

t: Closed Loop Particulate Filter Regeneration Control At Limit Temperature Too Low
s: With regeneration running closed-loop the controller is at maximum heat-input demand yet cannot raise EGT to setpoint - typical for a load-poor drive cycle.

t: Closed Loop Particulate Filter Regeneration Control At Limit Temperature Too High
s: Same as P24A0 with the controller fully reducing heat input yet EGT still rising - typical for a runaway exotherm in the DPF (oil contamination).

t: Particulate Filter Regeneration Incomplete Bank 1
s: A regeneration cycle on bank 2 was started but did not bring the DPF backpressure down sufficiently - either the cycle was interrupted (driver stopped before completion) or thermal conditions were not met.

t: Particulate Filter Restriction Soot Accumulation Bank 2
s: The DPF on bank 2 has accumulated soot beyond the normal operating window. Active regeneration will be requested.

t: Particulate Filter Restriction Soot Accumulation Too High Bank 1
s: Soot loading on bank 2 DPF has reached a level that exceeds even the high-load regeneration capability - manual workshop regeneration or DPF cleaning required.

t: EGR Cooler Bypass Control Stuck Open Bank 1
s: The EGR cooler bypass valve on bank 1 is mechanically stuck in the open (bypassing) position - EGR always bypasses the cooler, gas reaches the intake hotter than designed.

t: EGR Cooler Bypass Control Stuck Open Bank 2
s: The EGR cooler bypass valve on bank 2 is mechanically stuck in the open (bypassing) position - EGR always bypasses the cooler, gas reaches the intake hotter than designed.

t: EGR Cooler Coolant Pump Control Circuit Open
s: The dedicated coolant pump for the EGR cooler has an open control circuit - the pump cannot run, EGR cooler temperature rises beyond the safety threshold.

t: EGR Cooler Coolant Pump Performance Stuck Off
s: The pump is electrically intact but does not deliver flow when commanded - typically a worn impeller or seized rotor.

t: EGR Cooler Coolant Pump Control Circuit Low
s: Same as P24A7 with the control line shorted to ground. EGR mixes inert exhaust into intake to lower combustion peak temperature — too little raises NOx, too much causes misfires and rough running.

t: EGR Cooler Coolant Pump Control Circuit High
s: Same as P24A7 with the control line shorted to supply. EGR mixes inert exhaust into intake to lower combustion peak temperature — too little raises NOx, too much causes misfires and rough running.

t: EGR Cooler Coolant Pump Stuck On
s: The pump or its driver is stuck on - the pump runs continuously, even with the engine off, draining battery and bleeding heat from the engine.

t: EGR Cooler Coolant Pump Feedback Circuit Low
s: The feedback line that confirms pump speed reads below the plausible range - either pump not turning or feedback line shorted to ground.

t: EGR Cooler Coolant Pump Feedback Circuit High
s: Same as P24AC with the feedback line shorted to supply. EGR mixes inert exhaust into intake to lower combustion peak temperature — too little raises NOx, too much causes misfires and rough running.

t: Particulate Matter Sensor Circuit
s: A general circuit fault on the particulate matter (PM) sensor - measures soot in the exhaust downstream of the DPF to verify regeneration.

t: Particulate Matter Sensor Circuit Range or Performance
s: Same as P24AE with the signal in plausible range yet not matching the engine model.

t: Particulate Matter Sensor Circuit Low
s: Same as P24AE with the signal voltage below the plausible range. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Particulate Matter Sensor Circuit High
s: Same as P24AE with the signal voltage above the plausible range. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Particulate Matter Sensor Circuit Intermittent
s: Same as P24AE with intermittent or erratic signal. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Particulate Matter Sensor Heater Control Circuit Open
s: The PM sensor heater (regenerates the sensor by burning off accumulated soot) has an open control circuit.

t: Particulate Matter Sensor Heater Control Circuit Range or Performance
s: Same as P24B3 with the heater control loop in plausible range yet not matching the heater model. The sensor heater warms the element to operating temperature (~600 °C); without it the lambda sensor stays inactive in closed loop and the ECM cannot trim mixture.

t: Particulate Matter Sensor Heater Control Circuit Low
s: Same as P24B3 with the control line shorted to ground. The sensor heater warms the element to operating temperature (~600 °C); without it the lambda sensor stays inactive in closed loop and the ECM cannot trim mixture.

t: Particulate Matter Sensor Heater Control Circuit High
s: Same as P24B3 with the control line shorted to supply. The sensor heater warms the element to operating temperature (~600 °C); without it the lambda sensor stays inactive in closed loop and the ECM cannot trim mixture.

t: Particulate Matter Sensor Heater Resistance
s: The resistance of the PM sensor heater is outside the calibrated band.

t: EVAP System Leak Detection Pump Pressure Sensor Circuit
s: A general circuit fault on the LDP internal pressure sensor. The evaporative system captures fuel vapor from the tank and purges it into intake; leaks or stuck purge valves leak hydrocarbons and cause emissions failures.

t: EVAP System Leak Detection Pump Pressure Sensor Circuit Range or Performance
s: Same as P24B8 with the signal in plausible range yet not matching the pump operating curve. The evaporative system captures fuel vapor from the tank and purges it into intake; leaks or stuck purge valves leak hydrocarbons and cause emissions failures.

t: EVAP System Leak Detection Pump Pressure Sensor Circuit Low
s: Same as P24B8 with the signal voltage below the plausible range. The evaporative system captures fuel vapor from the tank and purges it into intake; leaks or stuck purge valves leak hydrocarbons and cause emissions failures.

t: EVAP System Leak Detection Pump Pressure Sensor Circuit High
s: Same as P24B8 with the signal voltage above the plausible range. The evaporative system captures fuel vapor from the tank and purges it into intake; leaks or stuck purge valves leak hydrocarbons and cause emissions failures.

t: EVAP System Leak Detection Pump Pressure Sensor Circuit Intermittent
s: Same as P24B8 with intermittent or erratic signal. The evaporative system captures fuel vapor from the tank and purges it into intake; leaks or stuck purge valves leak hydrocarbons and cause emissions failures.

t: EVAP System Leak Detection Pump Switching Valve Control Circuit Open
s: The switching valve in the LDP - directs pump output between EVAP test path and reference orifice - has an open control circuit.

t: EVAP System Leak Detection Pump Switching Valve Control Circuit Low
s: Same as P24BD with the control line shorted to ground. The evaporative system captures fuel vapor from the tank and purges it into intake; leaks or stuck purge valves leak hydrocarbons and cause emissions failures.

t: EVAP System Leak Detection Pump Switching Valve Control Circuit High
s: Same as P24BD with the control line shorted to supply. The evaporative system captures fuel vapor from the tank and purges it into intake; leaks or stuck purge valves leak hydrocarbons and cause emissions failures.

t: EVAP System Leak Detection Pump Switching Valve Stuck On
s: The LDP switching valve is stuck in the activated position. The evaporative system captures fuel vapor from the tank and purges it into intake; leaks or stuck purge valves leak hydrocarbons and cause emissions failures.

t: EVAP System Leak Detection Pump Switching Valve Performance Stuck Off
s: Same as P24C0 with the valve stuck off. The evaporative system captures fuel vapor from the tank and purges it into intake; leaks or stuck purge valves leak hydrocarbons and cause emissions failures.

t: Exhaust Gas Temperature Measurement System Multiple Sensor Correlation Bank 1
s: The multiple EGT sensors on bank 1 disagree by more than the diagnostic tolerance under stable conditions - one of them has drifted relative to the others.

t: Exhaust Gas Temperature Measurement System Multiple Sensor Correlation Bank 2
s: The multiple EGT sensors on bank 2 disagree by more than the diagnostic tolerance under stable conditions - one of them has drifted relative to the others.

t: EGR Cooler Bypass Position Sensor Exceeded Learning Limit Bank 1
s: The bypass valve position sensor learning has reached its software-side limit on bank 1 - typically heavy soot deposits have shifted the mechanical zero beyond the design adaptation range.

t: EGR Cooler Bypass Position Sensor Exceeded Learning Limit Bank 2
s: The bypass valve position sensor learning has reached its software-side limit on bank 2 - typically heavy soot deposits have shifted the mechanical zero beyond the design adaptation range.

t: Particulate Matter Sensor Temperature Circuit
s: A general circuit fault on the temperature element of the PM sensor - used to verify that the sensor is hot enough for measurement after regeneration.

t: Particulate Matter Sensor Temperature Circuit Range or Performance
s: Same as P24C6 with the signal in plausible range yet not matching the engine model.

t: Particulate Matter Sensor Temperature Circuit Low
s: Same as P24C6 with the signal voltage below the plausible range. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Particulate Matter Sensor Temperature Circuit High
s: Same as P24C6 with the signal voltage above the plausible range. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Particulate Matter Sensor Temperature Circuit Intermittent
s: Same as P24C6 with intermittent or erratic signal. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Reductant Tank Cap Switch Circuit
s: A general circuit fault on the AdBlue tank cap switch - reports whether the cap is properly closed.

t: Reductant Tank Cap Switch Circuit Range or Performance
s: Same as P24CB with input in plausible range yet not matching driver action.

t: Reductant Tank Cap Switch Circuit Low
s: Same as P24CB with the signal shorted to ground. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Reductant Tank Cap Switch Circuit High
s: Same as P24CB with the signal shorted to supply. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Reductant Tank Cap Switch Circuit Intermittent or Erratic
s: Same as P24CB with intermittent or erratic signal. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Particulate Matter Sensor Supply Voltage Circuit Low
s: The PM sensor supply voltage is below the plausible range. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Particulate Matter Sensor Regeneration Incomplete
s: A sensor regeneration cycle (heating to burn off accumulated soot) did not complete - sensor cannot return to measurement mode.

t: Reductant Heater A Sense Circuit
s: A general circuit fault on the AdBlue heater A sense feedback line.

t: Reductant Heater B Sense Circuit
s: A general circuit fault on the AdBlue heater A sense feedback line.

t: EGR Cooler Coolant Pump Feedback Circuit
s: A general circuit fault on the feedback line that the EGR cooler coolant pump reports speed on. EGR mixes inert exhaust into intake to lower combustion peak temperature — too little raises NOx, too much causes misfires and rough running.

t: EVAP System Pressure Sensor or Switch B Circuit
s: A general circuit fault on the secondary EVAP system pressure sensor or switch B. The evaporative system captures fuel vapor from the tank and purges it into intake; leaks or stuck purge valves leak hydrocarbons and cause emissions failures.

t: EVAP System Pressure Sensor or Switch B Circuit Range or Performance
s: Same as P24D5 with the signal in plausible range yet not matching the engine model. The evaporative system captures fuel vapor from the tank and purges it into intake; leaks or stuck purge valves leak hydrocarbons and cause emissions failures.

t: EVAP System Pressure Sensor or Switch B Circuit Low
s: Same as P24D5 with the signal voltage below the plausible range. The evaporative system captures fuel vapor from the tank and purges it into intake; leaks or stuck purge valves leak hydrocarbons and cause emissions failures.

t: EVAP System Pressure Sensor or Switch B Circuit High
s: Same as P24D5 with the signal voltage above the plausible range. The evaporative system captures fuel vapor from the tank and purges it into intake; leaks or stuck purge valves leak hydrocarbons and cause emissions failures.

t: EVAP System Pressure Sensor or Switch B Circuit Intermittent
s: Same as P24D5 with intermittent or erratic signal. The evaporative system captures fuel vapor from the tank and purges it into intake; leaks or stuck purge valves leak hydrocarbons and cause emissions failures.

t: Particulate Matter Sensor Exhaust Sample Error Bank 1
s: The PM sensor on bank 1 cannot acquire a valid exhaust sample - probe fouled, exhaust path obstructed at sensor, or sensor seal leaking.

t: Reductant Purge Control Valve B Circuit Open
s: The secondary reductant purge valve B - which clears AdBlue from injector and lines at shutdown to prevent crystallisation - has an open control circuit.

t: Reductant Purge Control Valve B Performance
s: Same as P24DB with the actuator electrically intact yet not delivering the commanded purge flow. The evaporative system captures fuel vapor from the tank and purges it into intake; leaks or stuck purge valves leak hydrocarbons and cause emissions failures.

t: Reductant Purge Control Valve B Circuit Low
s: Same as P24DB with the control line shorted to ground. The evaporative system captures fuel vapor from the tank and purges it into intake; leaks or stuck purge valves leak hydrocarbons and cause emissions failures.

t: Reductant Purge Control Valve B Circuit High
s: Same as P24DB with the control line shorted to supply. The evaporative system captures fuel vapor from the tank and purges it into intake; leaks or stuck purge valves leak hydrocarbons and cause emissions failures.

t: Reductant Purge Control Valve B Stuck Open
s: Purge valve B is mechanically stuck open - AdBlue lines drain continuously, dosing pressure cannot build.

t: Reductant Purge Control Valve B Stuck Closed
s: Same as P24DF with valve B stuck closed - AdBlue cannot be cleared at shutdown, risk of crystallisation in injector and lines.

t: NH3 Sensor Circuit
s: A general circuit fault on the NH3 (ammonia) sensor - measures ammonia slip downstream of the SCR catalyst to detect AdBlue overdose.

t: NH3 Sensor Circuit Range or Performance
s: Same as P24E1 with the signal in plausible range yet not matching the engine model.

t: NH3 Sensor Circuit Low
s: Same as P24E1 with the signal voltage below the plausible range. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: NH3 Sensor Circuit High
s: Same as P24E1 with the signal voltage above the plausible range. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: NH3 Sensor Circuit Intermittent/Erratic
s: The ammonia (NH3) slip sensor downstream of the SCR catalyst delivers an intermittent or erratic signal. The ECM expects a smooth voltage proportional to ammonia concentration in the tailpipe — sudden jumps, dropouts or noise mean the wiring, connector or sensing element is failing. Used to close-loop the AdBlue/DEF dosing strategy.

t: NH3 Sensor Heater Circuit/Open
s: The heater inside the ammonia (NH3) slip sensor warms the sensing cell to operating temperature so it can read tailpipe ammonia. The ECM detects an open in the heater control circuit — no current flows when the heater should be active, so the sensor never reaches its working point and SCR closed-loop control is suspended.

t: NH3 Sensor Heater Circuit Low
s: The heater control circuit of the ammonia (NH3) slip sensor reads below the expected voltage range. Either the wiring is shorted to ground or the heater driver in the SCR/dosing module is stuck low. The sensor element never warms up, NH3 readings stay invalid and the SCR strategy falls back to open-loop dosing.

t: NH3 Sensor Heater Circuit High
s: The heater control circuit of the NH3 slip sensor reads above the expected voltage range. Typically a short to battery voltage or a heater driver stuck high — duty-cycle control is lost, the heater either runs continuously or not at all, and the sensor element risks overheating before the ECM disables it.

t: NH3 Sensor Heater Circuit Performance
s: The heater of the NH3 slip sensor is electrically intact, but the warm-up profile or steady-state temperature feedback does not match the ECM model. The sensor takes too long to reach operating temperature or drifts away from setpoint, so SCR closed-loop control is delayed or held off.

t: NH3 Sensor Supply Voltage Circuit
s: The supply rail that powers the NH3 slip sensor electronics (typically 5 V or 12 V from the dosing/SCR module) is out of tolerance. Without a stable supply the sensor cannot deliver a valid ammonia reading, and the ECM disables the closed-loop SCR strategy.

t: NH3 Sensor Supply Voltage Circuit Low
s: The NH3 slip sensor supply voltage drops below the lower limit. Usually a partial short to ground in the supply line, an undersized ground path with high resistance, or an internal supply collapse in the SCR/dosing module. Sensor output is held invalid until the supply recovers.

t: NH3 Sensor Supply Voltage Circuit High
s: The NH3 slip sensor supply voltage rises above the upper limit. Either the supply is shorted to battery voltage or an internal regulator in the dosing module has failed open. The sensor electronics may be damaged if the condition persists, so the ECM disables the input.

t: NH3 Sensor Calibration Memory
s: The NH3 slip sensor stores its individual calibration coefficients in non-volatile memory (EEPROM/flash). The ECM cannot read valid calibration values — either checksum fails, the memory is empty after replacement, or communication with the sensor electronics is broken. Without the trim values the raw ammonia reading is unreliable.

t: NH3 Sensor Processor Performance
s: The microcontroller inside the smart NH3 slip sensor reports an internal performance fault — failed self-test, watchdog reset, or out-of-range plausibility checks. The sensor cannot guarantee its output value, the ECM marks the channel invalid and disables SCR closed-loop control on this branch.

t: Control Module Wake-up Circuit/Open
s: The wake-up line that brings a sleeping ECU back from low-power mode is open. Many networks use a dedicated hardware wake-up wire (door-latch trigger, terminal 15, CAN wake-up) so modules can stay asleep with the ignition off. Without it, the module either fails to wake up at start-up or stays awake and drains the battery.

t: Control Module Wake-up Circuit Low
s: The wake-up input on the control module reads stuck low — usually a short to ground or a permanently asserted wake signal. The module either keeps waking up unnecessarily and drains the battery, or it cannot distinguish a real wake event from the stuck low level.

t: Control Module Wake-up Circuit High
s: The wake-up input reads above the expected range — typically a short to battery voltage. The module sees a permanent wake-up request, never enters sleep mode and drains the battery while parked.

t: EGR Temperature/Charge Air Cooler Temperature Correlation
s: After a long key-off soak both the EGR temperature sensor and the charge-air cooler outlet temperature sensor must read approximately the same ambient temperature. The ECM compares them and detects an implausible delta. One of the sensors drifts, is wired wrong, or is exposed to a heat source the model does not know about.

t: Reductant Tank Temperature/Fuel Temperature Correlation
s: After a long key-off soak the AdBlue/DEF tank temperature and the fuel tank temperature should read close to each other (both at ambient). The ECM detects an implausible spread, which usually means one of the two sensors drifts, is wired wrong, or has a heat source nearby that the plausibility model does not account for.

t: NOx Sensor Delayed Response - High to Low Bank 1 Sensor 1
s: The pre-catalyst NOx sensor on bank 1 (sensor 1) reacts too slowly when NOx in the exhaust drops from a high to a low level — for example after a fuel cut or transition into low-load. Aged ceramic cells, soot deposits or condensation inside the sensor protective tube slow the diffusion of NOx to the measuring element.

t: NOx Sensor Delayed Response - High to Low Bank 1 Sensor 2
s: The post-catalyst NOx sensor on bank 1 (sensor 2) reacts too slowly when NOx in the exhaust drops from a high to a low level. Same failure mechanism as the upstream sensor — aged element, soot or condensate — but post-catalyst the consequence is a degraded SCR conversion-efficiency monitor.

t: Exhaust Aftertreatment Fuel Air Purge Valve Stuck Open
s: On systems that inject HC dosing fuel or AIR-assisted reductant into the aftertreatment, a purge valve clears the line after each dosing event. The ECM detects the valve stuck open — pressure or flow does not build up against a closed valve, so the dosing line cannot be charged. Common on diesel HC-doser hardware (DPF regen support).

t: Exhaust Aftertreatment Fuel Air Purge Valve Stuck Closed
s: The aftertreatment HC dosing or reductant purge valve is stuck closed. The ECM commands purge but the line is not cleared, leaving residual fluid that can foul nozzles or freeze in cold weather. Subsequent dosing events also fail because backpressure does not match the model.

t: Exhaust Aftertreatment Fuel Air Purge Valve Control Circuit
s: General electrical fault in the control circuit of the aftertreatment fuel/air purge valve. The ECM cannot drive the valve coil according to plausibility checks — open, short or unexpected feedback voltage. Until cleared, dosing line purge is suspended.

t: Exhaust Aftertreatment Fuel Air Purge Valve Control Circuit Range/Performance
s: The valve coil is electrically intact, but the response (current rise time, return-current decay, position feedback) does not match the ECM model. Typical for solenoids losing magnetic strength after long thermal cycling, or armatures with increased friction from coking.

t: Exhaust Aftertreatment Fuel Air Purge Valve Control Circuit Low
s: The control circuit of the aftertreatment purge valve reads stuck low — usually a short to ground or a failed low-side driver. The valve is energised continuously when it should be off, or the diagnostic feedback voltage is pulled below threshold.

t: Exhaust Aftertreatment Fuel Air Purge Valve Control Circuit High
s: The control circuit of the aftertreatment purge valve reads stuck high — typically a short to battery voltage or an open ground/return path. The driver cannot pull the line low to actuate the valve.

t: Particulate Matter Sensor Heater Control Circuit Driver Current/Temperature Too High
s: The PM (soot) sensor heater is driven by a current-limited stage so it can burn off accumulated soot before each measurement. The driver reports current or junction temperature above the safe limit and disables itself. Often a partial short across the heater or sensor element after coolant intrusion.

t: Particulate Filter A Incorrect
s: The ECM identifies the installed particulate filter A as the wrong part — backpressure signature, regeneration efficiency or coded part-number do not match the application. Common after a non-OEM aftermarket DPF or a wrong-application replacement.

t: SCR Catalyst Incorrect
s: The ECM identifies the installed SCR catalyst as the wrong part — NOx conversion efficiency, NH3 storage capacity or coded part-number do not match the expected application. Typical after fitting a non-OEM SCR brick or after a coding error following replacement.

t: Reductant Temperature Too High
s: The temperature of the AdBlue/DEF in the dosing system exceeds the safe limit — risk of thermal decomposition (NH3/biuret formation) and clogged dosing nozzles. Often caused by a stuck-on tank heater, a stalled coolant return or proximity to a hot exhaust component.

t: Generator Lamp/L-Terminal Circuit Low
s: The L-terminal feedback line from the alternator (charging warning lamp circuit) reads below the lower limit. The lamp may stay on, and battery voltage may drift if regulation feedback is also affected.

t: Generator Lamp/L-Terminal Circuit High
s: The L-terminal feedback from the alternator reads above the upper limit — short to supply or open ground side. Charging diagnostics cannot run cleanly.

t: Charging System Voltage
s: The ECU detects an out-of-range charging system voltage without specifying high or low. Indicates a generic charging fault that requires further sub-code investigation.

t: Charging System Voltage Low
s: The ECU detects that battery voltage during operation stays below the regulated target. The alternator output is insufficient to maintain charge under load, eventually leading to battery depletion.

t: Charging System Voltage High
s: The ECU detects a battery voltage above the regulated upper limit. The voltage regulator is not limiting alternator output, risking damage to electronic modules and battery overcharge.

t: ECM/PCM Power Input Signal
s: The ECM/PCM monitors the main switched battery feed (typically terminal 15 / ignition-switched supply via the main relay). A general fault is reported — either the input is missing entirely or it does not match the expected timing relative to ignition state. Engine may not start or stay running.

t: ECM/PCM Power Input Signal Range/Performance
s: The switched-battery input to the ECM/PCM is present, but the level or transient behaviour is out of plausibility — sagging during cranking, slow ramp-up after main-relay close, or noisy. Often caused by aged main relay contacts or a weak battery feed under load.

t: ECM/PCM Power Input Signal Low
s: The ECM/PCM switched-battery input reads below threshold while the ignition is on. Either the main relay does not close, the supply wire is broken or shorted to ground, or the battery is too low. Engine usually fails to start until the input recovers.

t: ECM/PCM Power Input Signal High
s: The ECM/PCM switched-battery input reads above the upper threshold. Most often caused by a charging system overvoltage (failed alternator regulator) or wrong jump-start procedure. Sustained high voltage risks damaging ECU electronics.

t: ECM/PCM Power Input Signal Intermittent
s: The ECU detects sporadic interruptions of its supply voltage. The fault is set when supply drops below a threshold for short periods, often during cranking or due to a poor connection.

t: Engine Oil Level Sensor Circuit
s: General electrical fault on the engine oil level sensor circuit. The ECM cannot read a plausible level signal (analog level, ultrasonic or capacitive sender). Without a valid level reading the ECM disables oil-low warnings and dynamic service-interval calculation.

t: Engine Oil Level Sensor Circuit Range/Performance
s: The oil level sensor is electrically intact, but the level reading is implausible — for example a constant value across drives, jumps that cannot match real oil consumption, or readings outside the design range. Often a contaminated or aged float/ultrasonic sender.

t: Engine Oil Level Sensor Circuit Low
s: The oil level sensor signal voltage reads below the lower limit, typically a short to ground or an open at the reference end of the sender. The ECM treats the input as invalid and ignores oil-low warnings until the circuit is repaired.

t: Engine Oil Level Sensor Circuit High
s: The oil level sensor signal voltage reads above the upper limit, typically a short to reference voltage or an open signal return. Pegged at maximum, the ECM cannot distinguish full from invalid and disables the level monitor.

t: Engine Oil Level Sensor Circuit Intermittent/Erratic
s: The oil level signal jumps or drops out intermittently — usually loose contacts, vibration cracks at the sender or oil sloshing on bumpy roads. The ECM filters short transients but flags the input when the noise pattern exceeds the diagnostic limits.

t: Engine Oil Level Too Low
s: The oil level monitor reports the engine oil level below the safe minimum after sensor checks pass. Continued operation risks bearing damage and oil-pump cavitation, so the ECM sets a critical warning, may reduce torque and asks the driver to add oil immediately.

t: ECM/PCM Power Relay Sense Circuit Range/Performance
s: The voltage reported on the main power relay sense line for the engine controller does not match expectation. The ECU may shut down too early or stay awake too long, draining the battery.

t: ECM/PCM Power Relay Sense Circuit Intermittent
s: The sense (feedback) line that lets the ECM verify the main power relay has actually closed shows intermittent dropouts. The ECM expects to see battery voltage on this line whenever the relay is energised — short losses suggest aged contacts, vibration or chafed wiring.

t: Event Data Recorder Request Circuit/Open
s: The Event Data Recorder (EDR / black box) request line — used by the ECM or restraint module to trigger the EDR to capture pre-crash data — is open. The ECM cannot trigger an event capture, so any subsequent crash event would not be recorded reliably.

t: Event Data Recorder Request Circuit Low
s: The EDR request line reads stuck low — typically a short to ground or a permanently asserted request. The ECM either sees a constant request (which it cannot service) or cannot pulse the line high to fire a real event capture.

t: Event Data Recorder Request Circuit High
s: The EDR request line reads stuck high — typically a short to battery voltage. The ECM cannot pulse the line back low to release the request, so the EDR may either ignore further requests or sit in a permanent triggered state.

t: A/C Refrigerant Pressure Sensor B Circuit
s: General electrical fault in the second A/C refrigerant pressure sensor circuit. Sensor B usually monitors the low-side or compressor-suction pressure (in addition to high-side sensor A). The ECM cannot read a plausible value and disables variable compressor control to avoid evaporator freeze-up or overpressure.

t: A/C Refrigerant Pressure Sensor B Circuit Range/Performance
s: A/C refrigerant pressure sensor B is electrically intact, but its reading does not match the system model — for example pressures that do not fall when the compressor is off, do not change with ambient temperature, or contradict pressure sensor A. Often a refrigerant charge issue or a contaminated sensor.

t: A/C Refrigerant Pressure Sensor B Circuit Low
s: A/C refrigerant pressure sensor B signal voltage reads below the lower limit. Either a short to ground in the signal wire, an open reference voltage at the sensor, or an internal short. The ECM ignores the reading and disables A/C closed-loop control until the circuit recovers.

t: A/C Refrigerant Pressure Sensor B Circuit High
s: A/C refrigerant pressure sensor B signal voltage reads above the upper limit. Typically a short to reference voltage in the signal line, an open signal return, or an overpressure event briefly pegging the sensor. ECM disables variable compressor control to protect the system.

t: A/C Request A Circuit
s: General electrical fault on the A/C compressor request input A. This input tells the ECM that the HVAC controller wants the A/C compressor engaged. With the input invalid, the ECM cannot enable the compressor clutch — A/C does not cool, idle-up compensation is not applied.

t: PTO Enable Switch Circuit/Open
s: The Power Take-Off (PTO) enable switch input to the ECM is open. PTO is used on trucks and work vehicles to drive auxiliary equipment from the engine — without a valid enable signal the ECM refuses to enter PTO operating mode, idle speed control changes do not apply.

t: PTO Enable Switch Circuit Low
s: The PTO enable switch input reads stuck low — typically a short to ground or a permanently closed switch. The ECM either sees PTO enabled when the operator has not requested it, or cannot release the PTO mode after work.

t: PTO Enable Switch Circuit High
s: The PTO enable switch input reads stuck high — typically a short to battery voltage. The ECM cannot pull the input low, so the switch state is unreadable and PTO mode is locked out for safety.

t: PTO Engine Shutdown Circuit/Open
s: The PTO engine-shutdown command line is open. This safety input lets a connected work attachment (or operator switch) request an immediate engine shutdown — for example when an interlock is opened on a stationary aerial work platform. With the line broken, the safety shutdown cannot be commanded.

t: PTO Engine Shutdown Circuit Low
s: The PTO engine-shutdown command line reads stuck low — usually a short to ground or a permanently asserted shutdown request. The ECM may treat this as an active shutdown command and refuse to start or keep the engine running while in PTO mode.

t: PTO Engine Shutdown Circuit High
s: The PTO engine-shutdown command line reads stuck high — typically a short to battery voltage. The ECM cannot pulse the line low to acknowledge a shutdown request, so the operator-side safety chain is effectively disabled.

t: A/C Request A Circuit Low
s: The A/C compressor request input A reads stuck low — typically a short to ground or a permanently asserted request. The ECM either sees A/C demanded all the time (cannot release idle-up, raises fuel use) or cannot distinguish a real request from the stuck low level.

t: A/C Request A Circuit High
s: The A/C request input A reads stuck high — typically a short to battery voltage. The ECM cannot pull the line low to read switch state, so the A/C compressor request is treated as invalid and the clutch stays disengaged.

t: A/C Request B Circuit
s: General electrical fault on the second A/C request input B. Many vehicles have two HVAC zones, a rear evaporator request, or a redundant request line — input B carries that signal. With the input invalid, the ECM cannot honour zone B compressor demand or rear-evap operation.

t: A/C Request B Circuit Low
s: The A/C request input B reads stuck low — short to ground or permanently asserted request. Same effect as input A stuck low, applied to the second HVAC channel.

t: A/C Request B Circuit High
s: The A/C request input B reads stuck high — typically a short to battery voltage. The ECM cannot read the switch state, request B is treated as invalid.

t: Vacuum Reservoir Pressure Sensor Circuit
s: General electrical fault on the vacuum reservoir pressure sensor circuit. The reservoir stores manifold or pump-generated vacuum for systems like brake booster, EGR actuators or HVAC dampers. Without a valid reading the ECM disables vacuum-pump duty cycle control.

t: Vacuum Reservoir Pressure Sensor Circuit Range/Performance
s: The vacuum reservoir pressure sensor is electrically intact, but the reading does not match the model — vacuum that does not build up after pump activation, does not bleed off when consumers draw, or stays at one value. Often a partly clogged hose, a leaking reservoir or an aged sensor.

t: Vacuum Reservoir Pressure Sensor Circuit Low
s: Sensor signal voltage below the lower limit — usually a short to ground or open reference. The ECM disables vacuum pump control until the input recovers.

t: Vacuum Reservoir Pressure Sensor Circuit High
s: Sensor signal voltage above the upper limit — usually a short to reference voltage or open signal return. ECM disables vacuum pump control to avoid overshooting.

t: Vacuum Reservoir Pressure Sensor Circuit Intermittent
s: The sensor signal jumps or drops out intermittently — loose connector, vibration, or chafed harness. The ECM filters short transients but flags the input when noise exceeds limits.

t: Engine Oil Quality Sensor Circuit
s: General electrical fault on the engine oil quality sensor — typically a capacitive/dielectric sensor that estimates oil aging from permittivity, viscosity proxies and conductivity. With no valid reading the dynamic service-interval algorithm falls back to the fixed-interval default.

t: Engine Oil Quality Sensor Circuit Range/Performance
s: The oil quality sensor is electrically intact, but the readings do not match the model — for example permittivity that does not change after an oil change, or shifts inconsistent with km/h driven. Often a contaminated electrode or oil that does not fit the sensor calibration.

t: Engine Oil Quality Sensor Circuit Low
s: Sensor signal voltage below the lower limit — typically a short to ground in the signal line or an internal short across the sensor electrode.

t: Engine Oil Quality Sensor Circuit High
s: Sensor signal voltage above the upper limit — typically a short to reference voltage or an open signal return.

t: Engine Oil Quality Sensor Circuit Intermittent/Erratic
s: The oil quality sensor signal jumps or drops out — usually a loose connector at the oil pan, an oil-soaked harness or vibration cracks at the sensor housing.

t: Engine Oil Level Too High
s: The oil level monitor reports the engine oil level above the safe maximum after sensor checks pass. Overfilling raises foaming, blow-by and crankcase pressure — and on diesels with regen-by-injection, fuel dilution can drive the level up by itself. The ECM warns the driver and may flag for service.

t: Ignition Switch Run Position Circuit
s: The ECU detects an electrical fault on the ignition switch run position input. The signal indicating that the ignition is in the run position is missing or implausible.

t: Ignition Switch Run Position Circuit Low
s: The ignition switch Run-position input reads stuck low — short to ground or a permanently closed contact. The ECM cannot tell when the operator releases the switch from Run, so accessory and crank logic gets confused.

t: Ignition Switch Run Position Circuit High
s: The ignition switch Run-position input reads stuck high — short to battery voltage or open switch return. The ECM cannot detect when the operator turns the switch back to off/accessory, so module sleep and accessory power timeouts do not work correctly.

t: Ignition Switch On/Start Position Circuit
s: General electrical fault on the ignition switch On/Start (terminal 50 / crank) input. The ECM uses this signal to know when the starter is energised — without it the ECM cannot raise fuel and timing for cranking, the engine struggles to start or stalls right after.

t: Ignition Switch On/Start Position Circuit Low
s: The On/Start input reads stuck low — short to ground or permanently closed contact. The ECM may see crank request when the key is at rest, blocking accessory functions or causing unexpected starter cut-off logic.

t: Ignition Switch On/Start Position Circuit High
s: The On/Start input reads stuck high — short to battery voltage. The ECM cannot read the crank state, modules dependent on this signal are unable to time their wake-up correctly.

t: Ignition Switch Accessory Position Circuit
s: General electrical fault on the ignition switch accessory (terminal 75 / ACC) input. ACC tells the ECM that radio, fans, accessory loads should be powered without the engine running. With the input invalid, the body controller may misjudge accessory power timing.

t: Ignition Switch Accessory Position Circuit Low
s: The ACC input reads stuck low — short to ground or permanently closed contact. The ECM may see ACC active continuously and refuse to enter sleep, draining the battery.

t: Ignition Switch Accessory Position Circuit High
s: The ACC input reads stuck high — short to battery voltage. The ECM cannot detect when the operator releases the switch from ACC, accessory power timeouts do not work.

t: Low Pressure Fuel System Sensor Circuit
s: General electrical fault on the low-pressure fuel system sensor — typically the lift-pump or pre-supply pressure sensor that monitors the rail feeding the high-pressure pump. With no valid reading, the ECM cannot verify that lift pressure is sufficient for HP-pump intake.

t: PTO Sense Circuit/Open
s: The PTO sense (engagement feedback) line is open. This input tells the ECM whether the PTO clutch or driveline is actually engaged after a request — it closes the loop on the PTO enable command. With it broken, the ECM cannot confirm engagement and refuses to ramp up to PTO speed setpoints.

t: PTO Sense Circuit Range/Performance
s: The PTO sense circuit is electrically intact, but the engagement feedback does not match the command. The ECM requests engage and sees no engaged feedback (or vice versa) within the expected time window — drive-line slip, sticky clutch, or a mis-adjusted limit switch.

t: PTO Sense Circuit Low
s: The PTO sense input reads stuck low — short to ground or limit switch stuck closed. The ECM may see PTO continuously engaged.

t: PTO Sense Circuit High
s: The PTO sense input reads stuck high — short to battery voltage or open switch return. The ECM cannot detect engagement.

t: PTO Sense Circuit Intermittent/Erratic
s: The PTO sense signal jumps or drops out — usually loose connectors, a chattering limit switch, or vibration on the work attachment.

t: Engine Oil Deteriorated
s: The oil quality monitor concludes that the engine oil has reached the end of its useful life — based on permittivity, soot content, fuel dilution and total operating hours. The ECM requests an oil change before the next regen cycle to prevent accelerated bearing wear.

t: Low Pressure Fuel System Sensor Circuit
s: The ECU detects a general fault in the low-pressure fuel system pressure sensor circuit. Without a valid signal the ECU cannot verify lift-pump pressure or fuel rail pre-pressure.

t: Low Pressure Fuel System Sensor Circuit Range/Performance
s: The signal of the low-pressure fuel sensor does not match the expected value during operation. Pressure does not rise as expected when the lift pump is commanded.

t: Low Pressure Fuel System Sensor Circuit High
s: The low-pressure fuel system sensor signal voltage is above the upper limit. Either a short to reference voltage in the signal line, an open signal return, or a sensor reading pegged high after an internal failure. The HP-pump pressure-control strategy falls back to default.

t: Power Demand Signal Circuit
s: The ECU detects an electrical fault on the power demand input signal used to coordinate accessory loads (e.g. AC compressor, alternator load). The signal is missing or implausible.

t: Torque Management Request Input Signal A
s: The ECU detects an invalid torque-management request signal A from another control unit (e.g. transmission, ESP, ABS) on the CAN bus. Cooperative torque control cannot be performed as expected.

t: Torque Management Request Input Signal A Range/Performance
s: The ECM receives torque-management requests from other modules (TCM during shifts, ABS/ESC during traction events, hybrid controller). Input A is electrically intact but its values do not match the system model — out-of-range steps, jumps, or persistent values that other modules cannot confirm.

t: Torque Management Request Input Signal A Low
s: Torque management input A reads stuck low — analog short to ground or message stuck at minimum value. The ECM cannot trust the request and falls back to a safe default.

t: Torque Management Request Input Signal A High
s: Torque management input A reads stuck high — analog short to reference voltage or message stuck at maximum value. The ECM rejects the request to avoid running unintended high-torque commands.

t: Torque Management Request Input Signal B
s: General electrical fault on the second torque-management request input B. Used when more than one module needs to request torque adjustments — typically TCM on input A and ABS/ESC on input B, or chassis vs powertrain. With B invalid, only the A-side request is honoured.

t: Torque Management Request Input Signal B Range/Performance
s: Input B is electrically intact but the values are implausible — same failure mode as the A-side performance fault, applied to the second torque-management channel.

t: PTO Speed Selector Sensor/Switch 1 Circuit/Open
s: The PTO speed-selector switch 1 input is open. This switch lets the operator pick a preset PTO operating speed (idle, 800, 1200 rpm, etc.). With the input broken, the ECM cannot apply any operator-selected speed and falls back to a default speed.

t: PTO Speed Selector Sensor/Switch 1 Range/Performance
s: The selector input is electrically intact but the value is implausible — multiple positions asserted at once, or a value that cannot map to any of the configured speed presets. Often a sticky multi-position switch or a contaminated rotary contact.

t: PTO Speed Selector Sensor/Switch 1 Circuit Low
s: PTO speed selector 1 input reads stuck low — short to ground or analog signal pegged at minimum. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: PTO Speed Selector Sensor/Switch 1 Circuit High
s: PTO speed selector 1 input reads stuck high — short to battery voltage or analog signal pegged at maximum.

t: PTO Speed Selector Sensor/Switch 1 Circuit Intermittent/Erratic
s: The PTO speed selector signal jumps or drops out — usually a worn rotary contact or vibration on the work attachment.

t: Engine Hood Switch Circuit
s: General electrical fault on the engine hood (bonnet) switch circuit. The hood switch tells the body controller and (on hybrids) the high-voltage system that the hood is open — used for security, alarm and HV-safety interlock. With the input invalid, alarm logic and HV interlock fall back to a safe state.

t: Torque Management Request Input Signal B Low
s: Torque management input B reads stuck low — short to ground or message at minimum. ECM rejects the request, only A-side is used.

t: Torque Management Request Input Signal B High
s: Torque management input B reads stuck high — short to reference voltage or message at maximum. ECM rejects the request to avoid unintended high-torque commands.

t: Throttle/Fuel Inhibit A Circuit
s: General electrical fault on the throttle/fuel inhibit input A. This input lets external safety functions (immobilizer, hybrid HV interlock, anti-theft) override the throttle or cut fuel delivery. With the input invalid the safety override is unreliable, so the ECM falls back to its safe default — typically inhibit asserted to err on the side of safety.

t: Throttle/Fuel Inhibit A Circuit Range/Performance
s: The throttle/fuel inhibit A signal is electrically intact but its value does not match the system context — for example asserted while the immobilizer reports OK, or de-asserted while a hybrid HV interlock is active. The ECM cannot trust the signal.

t: Throttle/Fuel Inhibit A Circuit Low
s: Throttle/fuel inhibit A reads stuck low — short to ground or permanently asserted. ECM may treat it as continuous inhibit and refuse normal operation.

t: Throttle/Fuel Inhibit A Circuit High
s: Throttle/fuel inhibit A reads stuck high — short to battery voltage. ECM cannot read the inhibit state and falls back to the safe default.

t: Engine Coolant Level Sensor/Switch Circuit
s: General electrical fault on the engine coolant level sensor/switch circuit. Many vehicles use a simple float-switch in the expansion tank, others use a continuous-level analog sensor. With the input invalid the ECM cannot warn for low coolant before overheating starts.

t: Engine Coolant Level Sensor/Switch Circuit Range/Performance
s: The coolant level sensor is electrically intact, but the readings are implausible — for example a level that does not move at all over many drive cycles, or jumps that cannot match real coolant loss. Often a stuck float, contaminated electrode or air-locked sensor.

t: Engine Coolant Level Sensor/Switch Circuit Low
s: Coolant level sensor signal voltage below the lower limit — short to ground or internal short. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Engine Coolant Level Sensor/Switch Circuit High
s: Coolant level sensor signal voltage above the upper limit — short to reference voltage or open signal return.

t: PTO Speed Selector Sensor/Switch 2 Circuit/Open
s: The second PTO speed-selector switch input is open. Switch 2 typically encodes a binary or multi-bit code together with switch 1 — together they select one of several preset PTO speeds. With switch 2 broken, only the half of the bit pattern is available, so several presets cannot be reached.

t: PTO Speed Selector Sensor/Switch 2 Range/Performance
s: PTO speed selector switch 2 is electrically intact but its value does not match a valid bit pattern — multiple positions asserted, or values that do not map to any preset.

t: PTO Speed Selector Sensor/Switch 2 Circuit Low
s: PTO speed selector 2 input reads stuck low — short to ground or analog signal pegged at minimum. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: PTO Speed Selector Sensor/Switch 2 Circuit High
s: PTO speed selector 2 input reads stuck high — short to battery voltage or analog signal pegged at maximum.

t: PTO Speed Selector Sensor/Switch 2 Circuit Intermittent/Erratic
s: PTO speed selector 2 signal jumps or drops out — usually a worn rotary contact or vibration on the work attachment.

t: A/C Request A Circuit Range/Performance
s: The A/C request input A is electrically intact, but its value does not match the system context — for example asserted while the HVAC controller reports off, toggling rapidly, or sustained while compressor pressures cannot support it. The ECM disables A/C clutch engagement until the input becomes plausible.

t: Engine Coolant Level Low
s: The coolant level monitor reports the engine coolant below the safe minimum after sensor checks pass. Continued operation risks overheating, head warping and head-gasket failure. The driver is warned and the ECM may reduce engine power until the level is restored.

t: A/C Control Module Requested MIL Illumination
s: The A/C / HVAC control module has requested the ECM to illuminate the malfunction indicator (MIL) because of an emissions-relevant fault on its side. The original fault is stored in the HVAC module — this code only signals that the ECM has honoured the request.

t: Turbocharger Boost Control Position Sensor A Circuit
s: General electrical fault on the turbocharger boost control position sensor A — the feedback sensor on the wastegate or VGT actuator (often a contactless Hall sensor). Without valid position feedback the ECM cannot close the loop on boost control and falls back to open-loop duty cycle.

t: Turbocharger Boost Control Position Sensor Circuit
s: The position feedback sensor on the variable-geometry or wastegate turbocharger actuator is not communicating or is implausible. Without position feedback, boost control runs in default mode and power is reduced.

t: Turbocharger Boost Control Position Sensor A Circuit Low
s: Position sensor A signal voltage below the lower limit — short to ground in the signal line or open reference voltage at the sensor.

t: Turbocharger Boost Control Position Sensor A Circuit High
s: Position sensor A signal voltage above the upper limit — short to reference voltage in the signal line or open signal return.

t: Turbocharger Boost Control Position Sensor Range/Performance
s: The turbocharger actuator position sensor reports a value, but it does not track commanded vane or wastegate position within tolerance. Often a sticky VTG mechanism due to soot.

t: Turbocharger Boost Control Position Sensor Circuit Low
s: The turbocharger position sensor signal is below the lower limit — short to ground or sensor at zero stop.

t: Turbocharger Boost Control Position Sensor Circuit High
s: The turbocharger position sensor signal is above the upper limit — short to supply, open ground or sensor failed high.

t: Direct Ozone Reduction Catalyst Temperature Sensor Circuit Low
s: Direct Ozone Reduction (DOR) catalysts are coated radiator surfaces or dedicated catalysts that decompose tropospheric ozone passing over the radiator — used for low-emissions vehicles. The temperature sensor monitoring the DOR brick reads stuck low — short to ground or sensor short.

t: Engine Idle Speed Selector Sensor/Switch Circuit/Open
s: The idle-speed selector input is open. On commercial vehicles this switch lets the operator pick a fast-idle setpoint (for example to keep auxiliary loads supplied). With the input broken, the ECM cannot apply an operator-selected idle speed.

t: Engine Idle Speed Selector Sensor/Switch Range/Performance
s: The idle-speed selector input is electrically intact but the value does not match a valid preset — multiple positions or a non-mappable value. Often a sticky multi-position switch.

t: Engine Idle Speed Selector Sensor/Switch Circuit Low
s: Idle-speed selector input reads stuck low — short to ground or analog signal at minimum. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Engine Idle Speed Selector Sensor/Switch Circuit High
s: Idle-speed selector input reads stuck high — short to battery voltage or analog signal at maximum. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Engine Idle Speed Selector Sensor/Switch Circuit Intermittent/Erratic
s: The idle-speed selector signal jumps or drops out — usually a worn rotary contact or vibration. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: A/C Request B Circuit Range/Performance
s: A/C request input B is electrically intact but its value does not match the system context — same failure mode as the input A performance fault, applied to the second HVAC channel.

t: Direct Ozone Reduction Catalyst Temperature Sensor Circuit High
s: The DOR catalyst temperature sensor reads stuck high — short to reference voltage or open signal return. Without a valid temperature the ECM cannot enable the DOR efficiency monitor.

t: Direct Ozone Reduction Catalyst Temperature Sensor Circuit Intermittent/Erratic
s: The DOR catalyst temperature sensor signal jumps or drops out — typically loose connectors or chafed wiring near the radiator pack.

t: Direct Ozone Reduction Catalyst Deterioration Sensor Circuit
s: General electrical fault on the DOR catalyst deterioration sensor circuit. This sensor estimates the remaining ozone-conversion capacity of the DOR coating — coating worn off the radiator surface, contaminated by salt or grime, or thermally aged. Without a valid signal the deterioration monitor is disabled.

t: Direct Ozone Reduction Catalyst Deterioration Sensor Circuit Range/Performance
s: The DOR deterioration sensor is electrically intact but its readings drift or do not match the model — for example a deterioration value that does not move with km/h driven, or moves implausibly fast.

t: Direct Ozone Reduction Catalyst Deterioration Sensor Circuit Low
s: The DOR deterioration sensor signal voltage reads below the lower limit — short to ground or internal short.

t: Direct Ozone Reduction Catalyst Deterioration Sensor Circuit High
s: The DOR deterioration sensor signal voltage reads above the upper limit — short to reference voltage or open signal return.

t: Direct Ozone Reduction Catalyst Deterioration Sensor Circuit Intermittent/Erratic
s: The DOR deterioration sensor signal jumps or drops out — typically loose connectors or chafed wiring near the radiator.

t: Direct Ozone Reduction Catalyst Efficiency Below Threshold
s: The DOR catalyst no longer reduces tropospheric ozone with the required efficiency, based on the deterioration sensor and operating-time model. Coating worn off the radiator surface, contaminated by road salt and grime, or thermally aged after many heat cycles. Replacing the affected radiator/coating restores function.

t: Turbocharger/Supercharger Speed Sensor A Circuit
s: General electrical fault on the turbocharger/supercharger shaft speed sensor A — a high-frequency Hall or eddy-current sensor on the compressor shaft. The ECM uses turbo speed for overspeed protection (typically 200,000+ rpm) and for closed-loop boost on VGT systems. With no valid speed the ECM limits boost.

t: Turbocharger/Supercharger Speed Sensor A Circuit Range/Performance
s: Turbo speed sensor A is electrically intact, but the speed reading does not match the model — for example a speed that does not climb when boost rises, drifts away from the air-mass-flow estimate, or jumps. Often blade-fouling, a contaminated sensor tip or aged element.

t: Vacuum Reservoir Control Circuit/Open
s: The control circuit that drives the vacuum reservoir control valve (or vacuum pump motor) is open. The ECM cannot energise the actuator, so vacuum is no longer maintained on demand and consumers like brake booster, EGR or HVAC dampers may run out of vacuum.

t: Vacuum Reservoir Control Circuit Low
s: The vacuum reservoir control circuit reads stuck low — short to ground or low-side driver stuck on. The actuator may be energised continuously when it should be off.

t: Vacuum Reservoir Control Circuit High
s: The vacuum reservoir control circuit reads stuck high — short to battery voltage or open coil. The driver cannot pull the line low to actuate the valve/motor.

t: Engine Hood Switch Circuit Range/Performance
s: The hood switch is electrically intact, but its state is implausible — for example reporting hood-open while vehicle speed is rising, or rapid toggles. Usually a worn switch with high contact bounce or a misaligned hood actuator.

t: Engine Hood Switch Circuit Low
s: The hood switch input reads stuck low — short to ground or stuck-closed switch. The body controller may treat the hood as permanently open and refuse to arm the alarm or close the HV interlock.

t: Engine Hood Switch Circuit High
s: The hood switch input reads stuck high — short to battery voltage or open switch return. The body controller cannot detect when the hood is opened.

t: Turbocharger/Supercharger Speed Sensor A Circuit Low
s: Turbo speed sensor A signal voltage below the lower limit — short to ground in the signal line or open supply.

t: Turbocharger/Supercharger Speed Sensor A Circuit High
s: Turbo speed sensor A signal voltage above the upper limit — short to reference voltage or open signal return.

t: Turbocharger/Supercharger Speed Sensor A Circuit Intermittent
s: Turbo speed sensor A signal jumps or drops out — vibration, oil mist on the sensor tip, or chafed wiring near the hot turbo.

t: Cruise Control Front Distance Range Sensor Single Sensor or Center
s: General fault on the radar/lidar/camera distance-range sensor used by adaptive cruise control. On systems with a single sensor or three sensors with a center unit, this code refers to the central sensor — without it, ACC distance control is disabled and the system falls back to plain cruise.

t: Fuel Additive Control Module Requested MIL Illumination
s: The fuel additive control module — typical for diesel cars with a separate Eolys/Cerine additive tank that aids DPF regeneration — has requested the ECM to illuminate the MIL because of an emissions-relevant fault on its side. The original fault is in the additive module itself.

t: Fuel Additive Control Module Warning Lamp Request
s: The fuel additive control module asks for the additive-low warning lamp to be illuminated — the additive tank (Eolys/Cerine, used to lower DPF regen temperature) is approaching empty or has reached a service threshold. Continued driving without refilling will eventually disable DPF regeneration.

t: Turbocharger Boost Control Position Sensor B Circuit
s: General electrical fault on the turbocharger boost control position sensor B — second position sensor on the same actuator (redundant Hall) or on a second turbo (twin-turbo, sequential or two-stage). With B invalid the ECM cannot cross-check actuator A and falls back to limited boost control.

t: Turbocharger Boost Control Position Sensor B Circuit Range/Performance
s: Position sensor B is electrically intact, but the reading does not track sensor A or the actuator-command model. Often a misaligned redundant sensor or aged element on a second turbo.

t: Turbocharger Boost Control Position Sensor B Circuit Low
s: Position sensor B signal voltage below the lower limit — short to ground or open reference. Boost control tracks commanded vs. actual manifold pressure; persistent deviation means wastegate, vanes, hose or actuator are not delivering the requested pressure.

t: Turbocharger Boost Control Position Sensor B Circuit High
s: Position sensor B signal voltage above the upper limit — short to reference voltage or open signal return.

t: Vacuum Pump Control Circuit A/Open
s: The control circuit driving the electric vacuum pump (used to maintain brake-booster vacuum on direct-injection or hybrid powertrains where the engine is not always running) is open. The ECM cannot run the pump on demand — booster vacuum drops, pedal feel hardens, brake assist may be reduced.

t: Vacuum Pump Control Circuit A Range/Performance
s: The vacuum pump control circuit is electrically intact, but the pump response does not match the model — pump runs longer than expected to reach setpoint, current draw rises (mechanically tight) or vacuum does not build at all. Aged motor brushes, dry vanes or a leaking line are typical causes.

t: Vacuum Pump Control Circuit A Low
s: The electric vacuum pump control circuit reads stuck low — short to ground or low-side driver stuck on. The pump may run continuously, drawing current and overheating, until the ECM disables the channel.

t: Vacuum Pump Control Circuit A High
s: The vacuum pump control circuit reads stuck high — short to battery voltage or open coil/return. The driver cannot pull the line low to start the pump.

t: PTO Enable Switch Performance
s: The PTO enable switch is electrically intact but its state does not match operating conditions — for example asserted while the vehicle is rolling above the PTO speed limit, or asserted/de-asserted in fast succession. The ECM rejects the request to avoid unsafe PTO engagement.

t: Torque Management Request Output Signal
s: The ECM/PCM produces a torque-management output signal for downstream consumers (TCM during shifts, hybrid module, transfer case) and detects a fault on this output — open, short, or feedback voltage outside expected range. Without it, downstream modules cannot synchronise their behaviour with the engine.

t: Turbocharger Boost Control Position Sensor B Circuit Intermittent/Erratic
s: Position sensor B signal jumps or drops out — vibration on the actuator, oil mist contamination or chafed wiring near the hot turbo.

t: Cruise Control Front Distance Range Sensor Left
s: General fault on the left-side front distance-range sensor used by adaptive cruise. Multi-sensor radar arrays use left/center/right sensors for wider field-of-view and lane discrimination — without left, ACC may degrade to single-target tracking.

t: Cruise Control Front Distance Range Sensor Right
s: General fault on the right-side front distance-range sensor used by adaptive cruise. Same role as the left sensor but covering the right field-of-view.

t: Turbocharger Speed Sensor B Circuit
s: General electrical fault on the second turbocharger speed sensor B — used on twin-turbo, sequential or two-stage installations to read the second compressor shaft speed. Same role as sensor A on the second turbo.

t: Turbocharger Speed Sensor B Circuit Range/Performance
s: Turbo speed sensor B is electrically intact, but the speed reading does not match the boost or air-mass model — same failure mode as sensor A performance fault, applied to the second turbo.

t: Turbocharger Speed Sensor B Circuit Low
s: Turbo speed sensor B signal voltage below the lower limit — short to ground or open supply. Boost control tracks commanded vs. actual manifold pressure; persistent deviation means wastegate, vanes, hose or actuator are not delivering the requested pressure.

t: Turbocharger Speed Sensor B Circuit High
s: Turbo speed sensor B signal voltage above the upper limit — short to reference voltage or open signal return.

t: Turbocharger Speed Sensor B Circuit Intermittent
s: Turbo speed sensor B signal jumps or drops out — vibration, oil mist or chafed wiring at the second turbo.

t: Turbocharger Boost Control Position Sensor A Performance - Stuck Low
s: Position sensor A reports a value stuck near the low end of its travel even when the actuator is commanded to move. Either the actuator is mechanically stuck closed (wastegate jammed shut, VGT vanes seized at minimum opening) or the sensor itself is pegged low. Boost is uncontrolled — overboost on diesels with VGT, underboost on wastegate-stuck systems.

t: Turbocharger Boost Control Position Sensor A Performance - Stuck High
s: Position sensor A reports a value stuck near the high end of its travel — actuator stuck open (wastegate held wide open, VGT vanes seized at maximum opening) or sensor pegged high. Boost suffers — typically underboost on VGT, overboost-protection-off on wastegate.

t: Turbocharger Boost Control Position Sensor B Performance - Stuck Low
s: Same condition as the A-side stuck-low fault, but on the second sensor / second turbo. Position sensor B reads stuck near the low end while the actuator is commanded.

t: Turbocharger Boost Control Position Sensor B Performance - Stuck High
s: Same as the A-side stuck-high fault, applied to sensor B / turbo B. Boost control tracks commanded vs. actual manifold pressure; persistent deviation means wastegate, vanes, hose or actuator are not delivering the requested pressure.

t: Excessive Time To Enter Closed Loop Turbocharger A Boost Control
s: After start-up the boost-control loop on turbo A takes longer than the calibration limit to transition from open-loop to closed-loop control. Typically a slow-warming actuator (VGT vanes carbonised), a sluggish position sensor or a leaky pneumatic actuator that cannot follow setpoints quickly enough.

t: Excessive Time To Enter Closed Loop Turbocharger B Boost Control
s: Same condition as the turbo A excessive-warm-up fault, applied to the second turbo. Boost control tracks commanded vs. actual manifold pressure; persistent deviation means wastegate, vanes, hose or actuator are not delivering the requested pressure.

t: Turbocharger A Boost Control Position At Low Limit
s: The boost-control actuator on turbo A has been operating at the low end of its travel for too long — the closed-loop controller is saturated against the lower stop trying to reach the boost setpoint. Indicates either a leak (wastegate held closed but boost still escapes) or commanded boost above what the engine can produce.

t: Turbocharger A Boost Control Position At High Limit
