s: The boost-control actuator on turbo A has been operating at the high end of its travel — the controller is saturated against the upper stop trying to bleed off boost. Indicates either a wastegate stuck closed, oversized turbo for the calibration, or commanded boost limits being exceeded by ambient conditions (cold dense air).

t: Turbocharger B Boost Control Position At Low Limit
s: Same condition as the turbo A low-limit saturation, applied to the second turbo. Boost control tracks commanded vs. actual manifold pressure; persistent deviation means wastegate, vanes, hose or actuator are not delivering the requested pressure.

t: Turbocharger B Boost Control Position At High Limit
s: Same condition as the turbo A high-limit saturation, applied to the second turbo. Boost control tracks commanded vs. actual manifold pressure; persistent deviation means wastegate, vanes, hose or actuator are not delivering the requested pressure.

t: Brake System Control Module A Requested MIL Illumination
s: The primary brake system control module (ABS/ESC/iBooster) has requested the ECM to illuminate the MIL because of an emissions-relevant fault on its side — for example a regenerative braking fault on a hybrid that affects driveline torque flow. The original fault is in the brake module.

t: Engine Hood Open
s: After plausibility checks the body controller has concluded that the engine hood is genuinely open while the vehicle is operating. Used to suspend HV operation on hybrids/EVs (HV interlock), block remote engine start, and trigger the alarm if armed.

t: Fuel Mode Select Switch Circuit
s: General electrical fault on the fuel mode select switch — used on bi-fuel vehicles (CNG/LPG/H2/gasoline) so the driver can pick the active fuel. With the input invalid, the ECM falls back to the default fuel and disables operator-selectable switching.

t: Fuel Mode Select Switch Performance
s: The fuel mode select switch is electrically intact, but its state does not match operating conditions — switched to gas while gas tank is empty, or fast toggling. The ECM rejects the request to avoid stalling.

t: Fuel Mode Select Switch Circuit Low
s: Fuel mode switch input reads stuck low — short to ground or stuck-closed contact. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Fuel Mode Select Switch Circuit High
s: Fuel mode switch input reads stuck high — short to battery voltage. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Fuel Mode Select Switch Circuit Intermittent/Erratic
s: Fuel mode switch signal jumps or drops out — worn contact or vibration. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Piston Cooling Oil Control Circuit/Open
s: The piston-cooling oil-jet control circuit is open. Modern engines with under-piston oil jets switch them on under high load to cool the piston crown — controlled by a solenoid valve in the oil gallery. With the circuit broken, jets cannot be commanded on or stay locked off, raising piston temperatures under load.

t: Piston Cooling Oil Control Circuit Low
s: The piston-cooling oil-jet control circuit reads stuck low — short to ground or low-side driver stuck on. The jets may be enabled continuously, raising oil pump load and lowering oil pressure to other consumers.

t: Piston Cooling Oil Control Circuit High
s: Control circuit stuck high — short to battery voltage or open coil/return. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Piston Cooling Oil Control Circuit Performance/Stuck Off
s: The control circuit is electrically intact, but the solenoid valve does not open when commanded — confirmed by oil-pressure or oil-temperature feedback that does not change after the activation pulse. Piston cooling does not engage under high load — risk of piston-crown overheating and ring-land cracks.

t: Piston Cooling Oil Control Circuit Stuck On
s: The piston cooling solenoid does not close when commanded off — jets remain active when not needed. Oil pressure to top-end consumers may sag, and warm-up takes longer because the cooling jets remove heat from the pistons during cold start.

t: Piston Cooling Oil Pressure Too Low
s: With the piston-cooling jets commanded on, the dedicated oil-pressure sensor in that gallery reads below the safe threshold. Insufficient flow to the jets means real piston cooling is not happening — the ECM warns the driver and may reduce torque to protect the pistons.

t: Coolant Temperature Control Module Requested MIL Illumination
s: A dedicated coolant-temperature control module (used on engines with electric coolant pumps and electronic thermostats to keep the cylinder head and block at independent setpoints) has requested the ECM to illuminate the MIL because of an emissions-relevant fault on its side. Original fault is in that module.

t: Fuel Level Sensor A Stuck
s: Fuel level sensor A (typically the float in the main fuel tank section) reports a value that does not change over a long driving distance — the float is mechanically stuck against the tank wall, baffle or pump module. Without level changes the EVAP monitor cannot run and remaining-range estimates are wrong.

t: Fuel Level Sensor B Stuck
s: Same condition as sensor A stuck, applied to the second fuel level sensor B. Saddle tanks (often AWD vehicles) and large trucks use two sensors — one per tank chamber — averaged for total level. A stuck B leaves only A and reduces accuracy.

t: Fuel Level Sensor A or B Stuck
s: The ECM detects that one of the two level sensors is stuck but cannot identify which from the available signals — for example because the saddle-tank algorithm sees implausible delta only intermittently. Both sensors should be inspected.

t: Turbocharger/Supercharger Wastegate A Stuck Open
s: Wastegate A is mechanically stuck open. Exhaust gas bypasses the turbine even at low load — turbo cannot reach target speed, boost is permanently low and the engine feels asthmatic. Common causes are seized linkage, broken return spring or carbon-locked wastegate flap.

t: Turbocharger/Supercharger Wastegate A Stuck Closed
s: Wastegate A is mechanically stuck closed. The turbo cannot bleed off exhaust at high load — boost overshoots the setpoint, risking overboost cut-off and damage to the turbo bearing. Carbon-locked flap, seized linkage or aged actuator are typical causes.

t: Turbocharger/Supercharger Wastegate B Stuck Open
s: Same as wastegate A stuck open, applied to the second wastegate (twin-turbo, sequential or two-stage). Boost control tracks commanded vs. actual manifold pressure; persistent deviation means wastegate, vanes, hose or actuator are not delivering the requested pressure.

t: Turbocharger/Supercharger Wastegate B Stuck Closed
s: Same as wastegate A stuck closed, applied to the second wastegate. Boost control tracks commanded vs. actual manifold pressure; persistent deviation means wastegate, vanes, hose or actuator are not delivering the requested pressure.

t: Auxiliary Ignition Switch On/Start Position Circuit
s: General electrical fault on the auxiliary ignition switch On/Start input. Used on commercial vehicles or upfit applications where a second ignition switch (in a sleeper cab, work platform or crew compartment) can crank the engine.

t: Auxiliary Ignition Switch On/Start Position Circuit Low
s: Auxiliary ignition switch On/Start input reads stuck low — short to ground or stuck-closed contact. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Auxiliary Ignition Switch On/Start Position Circuit High
s: Auxiliary ignition switch On/Start input reads stuck high — short to battery voltage. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Regeneration Control Switch Circuit/Open
s: The DPF/SCR forced-regeneration request switch — used on commercial vehicles to let the operator force a parked regeneration when the automatic regen has been postponed too often — is open. The ECM cannot honour an operator request for forced regen.

t: Regeneration Control Switch Circuit Low
s: Regeneration control switch input reads stuck low — short to ground or stuck-closed contact. The ECM may see a constant regen request which it cannot service while the vehicle is moving.

t: Regeneration Control Switch Circuit High
s: Regeneration control switch input reads stuck high — short to battery voltage. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Unmetered Fuel - Forced Engine Shutdown
s: The engine controller commanded a forced shutdown after detecting unmetered fuel reaching the combustion chambers. Common on diesels with leaking turbo seals or cracked intercoolers that drip oil into the intake — the engine then runs on the oil and can overspeed.

t: Alternative Fuel Disable Control Circuit / Open
s: The controller cannot drive the alternative-fuel disable line that switches a bi-fuel vehicle (CNG, LPG, hydrogen) back to gasoline. An open detected on the control side blocks the safety shutoff for the gas system.

t: Alternative Fuel Disable Control Circuit High
s: Voltage on the alternative-fuel disable control line is above the expected window. Often a short to battery upstream of the relay or a stuck-on driver in the control module.

t: Alternative Fuel Disable Control Circuit Low
s: Voltage on the alternative-fuel disable control line is below the expected window. Usually a short to ground or a failed driver pulling the line low.

t: Alternative Fuel Disable Signal Circuit
s: The feedback signal that confirms the alt-fuel disable command was carried out is missing or out of range. The fuel system cannot verify that the gas circuit is shut.

t: Alternative Fuel Disable Signal Circuit High
s: The disable feedback signal sits above its valid window. Typical cause is a short to battery on the feedback wire or a failed pull-up in the input stage.

t: Alternative Fuel Disable Signal Circuit Low
s: The disable feedback signal sits below its valid window. Usually a short to ground or an open pull-up that lets the input float low.

t: Brake Booster Temperature Sensor A Circuit
s: The temperature sensor monitoring the electric brake booster (iBooster, eBooster) returns no usable signal. Without temperature feedback the controller derates booster assist to protect the motor.

t: Brake Booster Temperature Sensor A Circuit Range/Performance
s: The booster temperature sensor reading is implausible relative to commanded assist or coolant temperature. The signal moves too slowly, sticks, or never reaches expected values during operation.

t: Brake Booster Temperature Sensor A Circuit Low
s: The booster temperature signal is below the lower diagnostic threshold. Typical causes are a shorted sensor element or a short to ground in the harness.

t: Brake Booster Temperature Sensor A Circuit High
s: The booster temperature signal is above the upper diagnostic threshold. Common causes are an open sensor or a short to a 5 V reference in the harness.

t: Brake Booster Temperature Sensor A Circuit Intermittent/Erratic
s: The booster temperature signal jumps or drops out for short moments. Usually a loose connector or a chafed wire that opens during vibration or thermal expansion.

t: Brake System Control Module B Requested MIL Illumination
s: A second brake system control module (typically the redundant secondary unit on by-wire or AV-grade brake systems) has signalled an emissions-relevant fault and requested the engine controller to switch on the MIL.

t: Intake Camshaft Actuator Park Lock Control Circuit / Open Bank 1
s: The controller cannot drive the park-lock pin that secures the intake camshaft phaser on bank 1 in its base position at engine off. Without the lock, the next start can suffer poor combustion or rough idle until the phaser builds oil pressure.

t: Intake Camshaft Actuator Park Lock Control Circuit Low Bank 1
s: The intake camshaft park-lock control line on bank 1 is below the expected voltage window. Usually a short to ground or a low-side driver shorted internally.

t: Intake Camshaft Actuator Park Lock Control Circuit High Bank 1
s: The intake camshaft park-lock control line on bank 1 is above the expected voltage window. Typical causes are a short to battery or a stuck-on driver in the controller.

t: Intake Camshaft Actuator Park Lock Control Circuit / Open Bank 2
s: The controller cannot drive the park-lock pin that secures the intake camshaft phaser on bank 2 in its base position at engine off. Without the lock, the next start can suffer poor combustion or rough idle until the phaser builds oil pressure.

t: Intake Camshaft Actuator Park Lock Control Circuit Low Bank 2
s: The intake camshaft park-lock control line on bank 2 is below the expected voltage window. Usually a short to ground or a low-side driver shorted internally.

t: Intake Camshaft Actuator Park Lock Control Circuit High Bank 2
s: The intake camshaft park-lock control line on bank 2 is above the expected voltage window. Typical causes are a short to battery or a stuck-on driver in the controller.

t: Exhaust Camshaft Actuator Park Lock Control Circuit / Open Bank 1
s: The controller cannot drive the park-lock pin that secures the exhaust camshaft phaser on bank 1 in its base position at engine off. Without the lock, the next start can suffer poor combustion or rough idle until the phaser builds oil pressure.

t: Exhaust Camshaft Actuator Park Lock Control Circuit Low Bank 1
s: The exhaust camshaft park-lock control line on bank 1 is below the expected voltage window. Usually a short to ground or a low-side driver shorted internally.

t: Exhaust Camshaft Actuator Park Lock Control Circuit High Bank 1
s: The exhaust camshaft park-lock control line on bank 1 is above the expected voltage window. Typical causes are a short to battery or a stuck-on driver in the controller.

t: Exhaust Camshaft Actuator Park Lock Control Circuit / Open Bank 2
s: The controller cannot drive the park-lock pin that secures the exhaust camshaft phaser on bank 2 in its base position at engine off. Without the lock, the next start can suffer poor combustion or rough idle until the phaser builds oil pressure.

t: Exhaust Camshaft Actuator Park Lock Control Circuit Low Bank 2
s: The exhaust camshaft park-lock control line on bank 2 is below the expected voltage window. Usually a short to ground or a low-side driver shorted internally.

t: Exhaust Camshaft Actuator Park Lock Control Circuit High Bank 2
s: The exhaust camshaft park-lock control line on bank 2 is above the expected voltage window. Typical causes are a short to battery or a stuck-on driver in the controller.

t: Exhaust Heat Exchanger Exhaust Bypass Valve A Position Sensor Stop Performance
s: During self-calibration of the exhaust heat exchanger bypass valve A, the position sensor never reached the expected mechanical stop. The controller cannot zero the position reference, so commanded openings will be inaccurate.

t: Fuel Level Sensor C Circuit
s: A third fuel level sensor (C) used on multi-tank trucks, dual-saddle SUVs, or auxiliary fuel systems returns no usable signal. Without it, calculated fuel level and EVAP monitor enabling will be off.

t: Fuel Level Sensor C Performance
s: The third fuel level sensor reading does not change as expected with consumption or refuelling. Float may be sticking, or the resistor track has localised wear.

t: Fuel Level Sensor C Circuit Low
s: The third fuel level sensor signal is below the lower diagnostic threshold. Usually a shorted sender or a short to ground in the harness.

t: Fuel Level Sensor C Circuit High
s: The third fuel level sensor signal is above the upper diagnostic threshold. Common causes are an open sender or a short to the 5 V reference.

t: Fuel Level Sensor C Circuit Intermittent
s: The third fuel level sensor signal jumps or drops out for short moments. Usually a loose connector or a chafed wire that opens during driving vibration.

t: Fuel Level Sensor C Stuck
s: The third fuel level sensor reads a constant value across known consumption or refuelling events. Float arm or resistor wiper is mechanically stuck.

t: A Camshaft Position - Stuck In Locked Position Bank 1
s: Camshaft phaser A on bank 1 will not unlock from its base position. The phaser stays at the park-lock angle and cannot be advanced or retarded as commanded.

t: A Camshaft Position - Unable to Achieve Locked Position Bank 1
s: Camshaft phaser A on bank 1 cannot reach its base position at engine shutdown so the lock pin never engages. Next cold start may run rough until oil pressure builds.

t: A Camshaft Position Control Performance Bank 1
s: Camshaft phaser A on bank 1 follows the commanded angle too slowly, oscillates, or shows steady-state error outside tolerance. Closed-loop control of cam timing is degraded.

t: A Camshaft Position - Stuck In Locked Position Bank 2
s: Camshaft phaser A on bank 2 will not unlock from its base position. The phaser stays at the park-lock angle and cannot be advanced or retarded as commanded.

t: A Camshaft Position - Unable to Achieve Locked Position Bank 2
s: Camshaft phaser A on bank 2 cannot reach its base position at engine shutdown so the lock pin never engages. Next cold start may run rough until oil pressure builds.

t: A Camshaft Position Control Performance Bank 2
s: Camshaft phaser A on bank 2 follows the commanded angle too slowly, oscillates, or shows steady-state error outside tolerance. Closed-loop control of cam timing is degraded.

t: B Camshaft Position - Stuck In Locked Position Bank 1
s: Camshaft phaser B on bank 1 will not unlock from its base position. The phaser stays at the park-lock angle and cannot be advanced or retarded as commanded.

t: B Camshaft Position - Unable to Achieve Locked Position Bank 1
s: Camshaft phaser B on bank 1 cannot reach its base position at engine shutdown so the lock pin never engages. Next cold start may run rough until oil pressure builds.

t: B Camshaft Position Control Performance Bank 1
s: Camshaft phaser B on bank 1 follows the commanded angle too slowly, oscillates, or shows steady-state error outside tolerance. Closed-loop control of cam timing is degraded.

t: B Camshaft Position - Stuck In Locked Position Bank 2
s: Camshaft phaser B on bank 2 will not unlock from its base position. The phaser stays at the park-lock angle and cannot be advanced or retarded as commanded.

t: B Camshaft Position - Unable to Achieve Locked Position Bank 2
s: Camshaft phaser B on bank 2 cannot reach its base position at engine shutdown so the lock pin never engages. Next cold start may run rough until oil pressure builds.

t: B Camshaft Position Control Performance Bank 2
s: Camshaft phaser B on bank 2 follows the commanded angle too slowly, oscillates, or shows steady-state error outside tolerance. Closed-loop control of cam timing is degraded.

t: Supercharger Disconnect Clutch Stuck Open
s: The clutch that engages the mechanical supercharger to the engine drive will not close. The supercharger stays disengaged so boost from this stage is unavailable, which limits torque and may also affect cooling pump drives shared with the same belt.

t: Supercharger Disconnect Clutch Stuck Closed
s: The clutch that engages the mechanical supercharger to the engine drive will not open. The supercharger remains coupled at all loads, increasing parasitic loss and fuel consumption when boost is not needed.

t: Supercharger Disconnect Clutch Circuit Range/Performance
s: The supercharger clutch control current does not match the commanded value within tolerance. Either coil resistance has drifted or the driver feedback is implausible.

t: Supercharger Disconnect Clutch Circuit Low
s: Voltage on the supercharger clutch control line is below the expected window. Usually a short to ground or a shorted clutch coil.

t: Supercharger Disconnect Clutch Circuit High
s: Voltage on the supercharger clutch control line is above the expected window. Typical causes are a short to battery or an open coil that lets the driver float high.

t: Vacuum Pump Control Circuit B / Open
s: The controller cannot drive the secondary electric vacuum pump (B). Brake servo or HVAC actuators that depend on engine vacuum may not get enough vacuum during low-load operation or with engine off in stop-start mode.

t: Vacuum Pump Control Circuit B Range/Performance
s: The secondary vacuum pump does not produce the expected vacuum within the commanded run time. Leak in the pump line or worn pump diaphragm reduces output.

t: Vacuum Pump Control Circuit B Low
s: Voltage on the secondary vacuum pump control line is below the expected window. Usually a short to ground or a shorted motor winding.

t: Vacuum Pump Control Circuit B High
s: Voltage on the secondary vacuum pump control line is above the expected window. Typical causes are a short to battery or an open motor that lets the driver float high.

t: Turbocharger/Supercharger Boost Sensor C Circuit
s: A third boost pressure sensor (C) used on twin-stage or sequential boost systems returns no usable signal. Without it, boost control of the third stage runs open-loop or is disabled.

t: Turbocharger/Supercharger Boost Sensor C Circuit Range/Performance
s: The third boost pressure sensor reading is implausible compared with manifold pressure or commanded boost. Either drift, leak in the sensing line, or contamination on the sensor diaphragm.

t: Turbocharger/Supercharger Boost Sensor C Circuit Low
s: The third boost pressure sensor signal is below the lower diagnostic threshold. Usually a shorted sensor element or a short to ground.

t: Turbocharger/Supercharger Boost Sensor C Circuit High
s: The third boost pressure sensor signal is above the upper diagnostic threshold. Common causes are an open sensor or a short to the 5 V reference.

t: AIR System B
s: A general fault in the second secondary air injection (AIR) circuit. Detected by the O2 sensors which expect a lean swing during cold-start AIR pump operation but do not see one.

t: AIR System B Incorrect Flow Detected
s: The second AIR system delivers measurably more or less air than expected. Often a partially blocked pipe, a worn pump, or a leak between pump and check valve.

t: Turbocharger/Supercharger Bypass Valve C Control Circuit
s: A third bypass (recirculation or wastegate) valve used in three-stage charging systems is not responding to control commands. Boost balancing across stages can no longer be achieved.

t: Turbocharger/Supercharger Bypass Valve C Control Circuit Low
s: Voltage on the third bypass valve control line is below the expected window. Usually a short to ground or a shorted solenoid coil.

t: Turbocharger/Supercharger Bypass Valve C Control Circuit High
s: Voltage on the third bypass valve control line is above the expected window. Typical causes are a short to battery or an open solenoid coil.

t: Turbocharger/Supercharger Bypass Valve C Control Circuit Range/Performance
s: The third bypass valve current does not match the commanded value within tolerance. Drifted coil resistance or high-resistance contact in the connector.

t: Turbocharger/Supercharger Bypass Valve C - Mechanical
s: The third bypass valve is mechanically jammed. Solenoid is energised correctly but the valve flap or disc does not move as expected.

t: Turbocharger/Supercharger Bypass Valve C Temperature Too High
s: The third bypass valve has reached over-temperature. Either coil duty cycle is too high for too long, or cooling air flow around the valve is restricted.

t: Supercharger Disconnect Clutch
s: A general performance fault in the supercharger disconnect clutch. Engagement or release is slow, sticky, or out of tolerance without a clear electrical-side cause.

t: Electrical System Voltage Stabilizer Control Module Performance
s: The voltage stabiliser module (DC/DC converter for stop-start engine cranking events) does not maintain the regulated 12 V output during high transient loads. Sensitive electronics may reset during cranking.

t: Coolant Pump A Control Circuit / Open
s: The controller cannot drive the electric coolant pump A control line. Without circulation, engine and aftertreatment can overheat or warm up incorrectly.

t: Coolant Pump A Control Circuit Performance/Stuck Off
s: The electric coolant pump A is commanded on but does not run. Coolant temperature climbs faster than expected with no flow detected.

t: Coolant Pump Control Circuit Open
s: The ECU detects an open circuit on the electric coolant pump control line. Without pump control the engine cannot actively manage warm-up and cool-down phases.

t: Coolant Pump Control Circuit Low
s: The electric coolant pump drive line reads below the lower threshold — typically a short to ground or a failed driver stage.

t: Coolant Pump Control Circuit High
s: The electric coolant pump drive line reads above the upper threshold — usually a short to positive or a stuck-on driver stage.

t: Intake Air Heater B Circuit / Open
s: The controller cannot drive the secondary intake air heater (heater grid B) used on diesel engines for cold-start support. The grid stays cold so cold-start emissions and white smoke increase.

t: Intake Air Heater B Circuit Range/Performance
s: The secondary intake air heater current does not match expected resistance. Either the grid is partially open or the relay contacts have high resistance.

t: Intake Air Heater B Circuit Low
s: Voltage on the secondary intake air heater control line is below the expected window. Usually a short to ground or a shorted relay coil.

t: Intake Air Heater B Circuit High
s: Voltage on the secondary intake air heater control line is above the expected window. Typical causes are a short to battery or an open relay coil.

t: Intake Air Heater System Performance
s: The intake air heater system did not raise intake temperature by the expected amount during cold-start support. Suggests low current draw, poor connection to the grid, or a failed temperature feedback path.

t: PTO Control Circuit / Open
s: The controller cannot drive the power take-off (PTO) engagement line on commercial vehicles. The PTO will not engage even when commanded by the body controller or operator switch.

t: PTO Control Circuit Low
s: Voltage on the PTO engagement control line is below the expected window. Usually a short to ground or a shorted solenoid coil.

t: PTO Control Circuit High
s: Voltage on the PTO engagement control line is above the expected window. Typical causes are a short to battery or an open solenoid coil.

t: PTO Engaged Lamp Control Circuit
s: The dashboard PTO engaged lamp control circuit is faulty. Driver may not see whether the PTO is currently driving an external accessory.

t: Particulate Filter Regeneration Lamp Control Circuit
s: The DPF regeneration warning lamp circuit is faulty. The driver may not be alerted to a required highway run that supports passive regeneration.

t: EVAP System Monitoring Processor Performance
s: The dedicated EVAP monitoring processor (often a small auxiliary CPU inside the engine controller or a separate module) does not pass its self-test or runs out of step with the main monitor logic.

t: ECM/PCM Internal Engine Off Timer Performance
s: The engine controller's internal soak-time/engine-off timer fails its plausibility check — for example readings claim instant cool-down or never-cooled. Cold-start enrichment, EVAP and catalyst monitors depend on this timer; a fault freezes those readiness monitors.

t: A/C Refrigerant Distribution Valve A Control Circuit / Open
s: The A/C refrigerant distribution valve A (used to switch refrigerant flow between front and rear evaporators or between cabin and battery cooling on EVs) cannot be driven by the controller.

t: A/C Refrigerant Distribution Valve A Control Circuit Low
s: Voltage on the A/C distribution valve A control line is below the expected window. Usually a short to ground or a shorted solenoid coil.

t: A/C Refrigerant Distribution Valve A Control Circuit High
s: Voltage on the A/C distribution valve A control line is above the expected window. Typical causes are a short to battery or an open solenoid coil.

t: Camshaft Position Output Circuit/Open
s: The camshaft position output signal sent from the ECU to other modules is interrupted. Other systems that rely on the cam reference (e.g. transmission, hybrid module) do not receive a valid signal.

t: Camshaft A Position Signal Output Circuit Low Bank 1
s: The buffered camshaft A position output that the engine controller sends to other modules (transmission, body, ignition module) is below the expected level on bank 1. Other modules cannot use the cam phase reference for synchronisation.

t: Camshaft A Position Signal Output Circuit High Bank 1
s: The buffered camshaft A position output is above the expected level on bank 1. Typical causes are a short to battery or an open output stage.

t: Crankshaft Position Signal Output Circuit / Open
s: The buffered crankshaft position output that the engine controller sends to ignition modules and the transmission cannot drive its line. Open detected on the output side.

t: Crankshaft Position Signal Output Circuit Low
s: The buffered crankshaft position output is below the expected level. Usually a short to ground on the output line or a failed buffer.

t: Crankshaft Position Signal Output Circuit High
s: The buffered crankshaft position output is above the expected level. Typical causes are a short to battery or an open output stage.

t: Coolant Pump B Control Circuit / Open
s: The controller cannot drive the second electric coolant pump (B). Often used for aftertreatment, turbo cool-down, or hybrid battery loops — fault stops circulation in that loop.

t: Coolant Pump B Control Circuit Performance/Stuck Off
s: The second electric coolant pump is commanded on but does not run. Cooling demand of the connected loop is not satisfied.

t: Coolant Pump B Control Circuit Low
s: Voltage on the second coolant pump control line is below the expected window. Usually a short to ground or a shorted motor winding.

t: Coolant Pump B Control Circuit High
s: Voltage on the second coolant pump control line is above the expected window. Typical causes are a short to battery or an open motor winding.

t: Coolant Pump B Control Circuit Stuck On
s: The second coolant pump runs even when no command is active. Driver is stuck on or the wire is shorted to a permanent supply.

t: Coolant Pump A Control Circuit Stuck On
s: The first coolant pump runs even when no command is active. Driver is stuck on or the wire is shorted to a permanent supply.

t: Throttle Position Output Circuit
s: A general electrical fault on the ECU output that broadcasts the throttle position signal to other modules — for example to the transmission control or hybrid drive controller.

t: Throttle Position Output Circuit Low
s: The buffered throttle position output that the engine controller sends to the transmission and body modules is below the expected level. Usually a short to ground or a failed output stage.

t: Throttle Position Output Circuit High
s: The buffered throttle position output is above the expected level. Typical causes are a short to battery or an open output stage.

t: Injector Control Pressure Regulator Circuit / Open
s: The Injector Control Pressure (ICP) regulator on hydraulically actuated injectors (HEUI diesel) or the high-pressure regulator on common-rail systems cannot be driven. Injection pressure cannot be controlled, engine may not start.

t: Injector Control Pressure Regulator Circuit Low
s: Voltage on the ICP regulator control line is below the expected window. Usually a short to ground or a shorted regulator coil.

t: Injector Control Pressure Regulator Circuit High
s: Voltage on the ICP regulator control line is above the expected window. Typical causes are a short to battery or an open regulator coil.

t: O2 Sensor Pumping Current Trim Circuit/Open Bank 1 Sensor 1
s: The ECU detects an open in the pumping current trim circuit of the wide-band oxygen sensor on bank 1 sensor 1. Without trim correction, the lambda value reported by the sensor is not accurate.

t: O2 Sensor Pumping Current Trim Circuit Low Bank 1 Sensor 1
s: On a wide-band (broadband) O2 sensor, the trim resistor circuit that calibrates the pumping current is reading low on bank 1 sensor 1. Air-fuel feedback gain is wrong, causing rich or lean offsets.

t: O2 Sensor Pumping Current Trim Circuit High Bank 1 Sensor 1
s: The wide-band trim resistor circuit on bank 1 sensor 1 reads high. Air-fuel feedback gain is wrong, causing offsets that the controller cannot compensate.

t: O2 Sensor Pumping Current Trim Circuit / Open Bank 2 Sensor 1
s: The wide-band trim resistor circuit on bank 2 sensor 1 is open. Air-fuel feedback for bank 2 cannot be calibrated, fuel control runs degraded.

t: Fuel Injector - Pilot Injection Not Learned
s: The pilot (pre) injection learning routine on a common-rail diesel did not complete successfully. Without learned pilot quantity, combustion noise increases and NOx may rise.

t: Control Module Power Off Timer Performance
s: The internal power-down timer of the engine controller does not run as expected. The module either stays awake too long after key-off (battery drain) or shuts down too early (no aftertreatment cool-down or learn-write).

t: Sensor Reference Voltage G Circuit / Open
s: The 5 V reference G output that the engine controller supplies to a group of sensors is open. All sensors fed from this rail will report invalid readings.

t: Sensor Reference Voltage G Circuit Low
s: The 5 V reference G voltage is below the expected level. Usually a short to ground or excessive load from a downstream sensor pulling the rail down.

t: Sensor Reference Voltage G Circuit High
s: The 5 V reference G voltage is above the expected level. Typical causes are a short to battery or an internal regulator failure.

t: Sensor Reference Voltage G Circuit Range/Performance
s: The 5 V reference G voltage is within range but unstable or offset against expected. Loose ground or marginal regulator load.

t: O2 Sensor Pumping Current Trim Circuit Low Bank 2 Sensor 1
s: The wide-band trim resistor circuit on bank 2 sensor 1 reads low. Air-fuel feedback gain on bank 2 is wrong, causing rich or lean offsets.

t: O2 Sensor Pumping Current Trim Circuit High Bank 2 Sensor 1
s: The wide-band trim resistor circuit on bank 2 sensor 1 reads high. Air-fuel feedback gain on bank 2 is wrong, the controller cannot compensate the offset.

t: Fuel Pump B Control Circuit / Open
s: The controller cannot drive the secondary fuel pump (B). Common on dual-tank trucks, GDI lift-pump systems, or alt-fuel installations — fault stops fuel delivery from this stage.

t: Fuel Pump B Control Circuit Low
s: Voltage on the second fuel pump control line is below the expected window. Usually a short to ground or a shorted motor winding.

t: Fuel Pump B Control Circuit High
s: Voltage on the second fuel pump control line is above the expected window. Typical causes are a short to battery or an open motor winding.

t: Fuel Pump A Low Flow / Performance
s: The ECU detects insufficient fuel flow from fuel pump A. Either the pump is worn out, the supply line is restricted, or the pressure regulator does not allow proper flow.

t: Fuel Pump B Low Flow/Performance
s: The second fuel pump runs but does not deliver expected volume or pressure. Worn pump, restricted filter, or leaking pressure regulator.

t: Torque Management Feedback Signal A
s: The torque feedback signal A from the transmission or other torque user is missing or invalid. The engine controller cannot reduce torque on shift requests, which can cause harsh shifts.

t: Torque Management Feedback Signal A Range/Performance
s: The torque feedback signal A is present but its value is implausible against measured engine load and speed. Sender calibration or sensor offset is suspected.

t: Torque Management Feedback Signal A Low
s: The torque feedback signal A reads below its valid range. Either the source reports a value below physical possibility or the bus signal is stuck low.

t: MIL Control Circuit Low
s: Voltage on the malfunction indicator lamp control line is below the expected window. Usually a short to ground or a shorted bulb driver.

t: MIL Control Circuit High
s: Voltage on the malfunction indicator lamp control line is above the expected window. Typical causes are a short to battery or an open lamp.

t: Glow Plug Control Driver Performance
s: The glow plug control driver does not respond to commands within tolerance. Cold-start glow function is degraded so cold starting can be delayed and white smoke increases.

t: Reductant Heater Driver Performance
s: The reductant (AdBlue) tank or line heater driver does not switch as expected. AdBlue may freeze in cold weather, blocking SCR dosing.

t: Glow Plug Control Module 1 Over Temperature
s: The first glow plug control module reports over-temperature. Sustained current draw or insufficient cooling has driven the module above its rated temperature.

t: Glow Plug Control Module 2 Over Temperature
s: The second glow plug control module reports over-temperature. Sustained current draw or insufficient cooling has driven the module above its rated temperature.

t: Torque Management Feedback Signal A High
s: The torque feedback signal A reads above its valid range. Source module sends an implausibly high value or the bus signal is stuck high.

t: Torque Management Feedback Signal B
s: The torque feedback signal B from a second torque user is missing or invalid. The engine controller cannot coordinate torque reduction with this user.

t: Torque Management Feedback Signal B Range/Performance
s: The torque feedback signal B is present but its value is implausible against measured engine load and speed. Sender calibration or sensor offset is suspected.

t: Torque Management Feedback Signal B Low
s: The torque feedback signal B reads below its valid range. Source sends a value below physical possibility or the bus signal is stuck low.

t: Torque Management Feedback Signal B High
s: The torque feedback signal B reads above its valid range. Source sends an implausibly high value or the bus signal is stuck high.

t: A Rocker Arm Actuator Control Circuit / Open Bank 1
s: The controller cannot drive the rocker arm switching actuator A on bank 1 (used in cylinder deactivation systems such as VTEC, AFM, MDS). The valve lift mode cannot be switched.

t: A Rocker Arm Actuator System Performance/Stuck Off Bank 1
s: The rocker arm actuator A on bank 1 is commanded to engage (high lift or all-cylinder mode) but does not switch. Engine stays in low-lift or deactivated mode under load, causing reduced power.

t: A Rocker Arm Actuator System Stuck On Bank 1
s: On variable valve lift systems (e.g. Honda VTEC) the ECU detects that the bank 1 rocker arm actuator is stuck in the engaged (high lift) position. Idle quality and fuel economy are affected.

t: A Rocker Arm Actuator Control Circuit Low Bank 1
s: Voltage on the rocker arm actuator A control line on bank 1 is below the expected window. Usually a short to ground or a shorted solenoid coil.

t: A Rocker Arm Actuator Control Circuit High Bank 1
s: Voltage on the rocker arm actuator A control line on bank 1 is above the expected window. Typical causes are a short to battery or an open solenoid coil.

t: A Rocker Arm Actuator Position Sensor Circuit Bank 1
s: The position sensor that confirms the A rocker arm switching state on bank 1 returns no usable signal. Closed-loop control of valve lift cannot verify the state.

t: A Rocker Arm Actuator Position Sensor Circuit Range/Performance Bank 1
s: The position sensor reading does not transition cleanly between the two valve lift modes on bank 1. Sensor drift or mechanical wear leaves the signal between expected values.

t: A Rocker Arm Actuator Position Sensor Circuit Low Bank 1
s: The position sensor signal is below the lower diagnostic threshold. Usually a shorted sensor or short to ground in the harness.

t: A Rocker Arm Actuator Position Sensor Circuit High Bank 1
s: The position sensor signal is above the upper diagnostic threshold. Common causes are an open sensor or a short to the 5 V reference.

t: A Rocker Arm Actuator Position Sensor Circuit Intermittent/Erratic Bank 1
s: The position sensor signal jumps or drops out intermittently. Usually a loose connector or a chafed wire in the harness.

t: Engine Serial Number Not Programmed or Incompatible
s: The engine controller cannot read a valid engine serial number from its non-volatile memory, or the stored value does not match the expected pattern. Calibration sets keyed to the serial number cannot be applied.

t: B Rocker Arm Actuator Control Circuit / Open Bank 1
s: The controller cannot drive the rocker arm switching actuator B on bank 1. The second valve lift mode cannot be commanded.

t: B Rocker Arm Actuator System Performance/Stuck Off Bank 1
s: The rocker arm actuator B on bank 1 is commanded to engage but does not switch. Engine stays in the wrong lift or cylinder mode under load.

t: B Rocker Arm Actuator System Stuck On Bank 1
s: The rocker arm actuator B on bank 1 stays in the activated state when commanded off. Engine cannot return to the alternate lift or cylinder mode.

t: B Rocker Arm Actuator Control Circuit Low Bank 1
s: Voltage on the rocker arm actuator B control line on bank 1 is below the expected window. Usually a short to ground or a shorted solenoid coil.

t: B Rocker Arm Actuator Control Circuit High Bank 1
s: Voltage on the rocker arm actuator B control line on bank 1 is above the expected window. Typical causes are a short to battery or an open solenoid coil.

t: A Rocker Arm Actuator Control Circuit / Open Bank 2
s: The controller cannot drive the rocker arm switching actuator A on bank 2. The valve lift mode cannot be switched on this bank.

t: A Rocker Arm Actuator System Performance/Stuck Off Bank 2
s: The rocker arm actuator A on bank 2 is commanded to engage but does not switch. Engine stays in the wrong lift mode under load on this bank.

t: A Rocker Arm Actuator System Stuck On Bank 2
s: The rocker arm actuator A on bank 2 stays in the activated state when commanded off.

t: A Rocker Arm Actuator Control Circuit Low Bank 2
s: Voltage on the rocker arm actuator A control line on bank 2 is below the expected window. Usually a short to ground or a shorted solenoid coil.

t: A Rocker Arm Actuator Control Circuit High Bank 2
s: Voltage on the rocker arm actuator A control line on bank 2 is above the expected window. Typical causes are a short to battery or an open solenoid coil.

t: B Rocker Arm Actuator Position Sensor Circuit Bank 1
s: The position sensor that confirms the B rocker arm switching state on bank 1 returns no usable signal.

t: B Rocker Arm Actuator Position Sensor Circuit Range/Performance Bank 1
s: The position sensor reading does not transition cleanly between the two valve lift modes for actuator B on bank 1.

t: B Rocker Arm Actuator Position Sensor Circuit Low Bank 1
s: The position sensor signal for actuator B on bank 1 is below the lower diagnostic threshold. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: B Rocker Arm Actuator Position Sensor Circuit High Bank 1
s: The position sensor signal for actuator B on bank 1 is above the upper diagnostic threshold. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: B Rocker Arm Actuator Position Sensor Circuit Intermittent/Erratic Bank 1
s: The position sensor signal for actuator B on bank 1 jumps or drops out intermittently. Usually a loose connector or a chafed wire.

t: Variable A/C Compressor Control Circuit Range/Performance
s: The variable-displacement A/C compressor control valve does not respond to commands within tolerance. Refrigerant flow regulation is degraded so cabin temperature varies or efficiency drops.

t: B Rocker Arm Actuator Control Circuit / Open Bank 2
s: The controller cannot drive the rocker arm switching actuator B on bank 2.

t: B Rocker Arm Actuator System Performance/Stuck Off Bank 2
s: The rocker arm actuator B on bank 2 is commanded to engage but does not switch.

t: B Rocker Arm Actuator System Stuck On Bank 2
s: The rocker arm actuator B on bank 2 stays in the activated state when commanded off.

t: B Rocker Arm Actuator Control Circuit Low Bank 2
s: Voltage on the rocker arm actuator B control line on bank 2 is below the expected window. Usually a short to ground or a shorted solenoid coil.

t: B Rocker Arm Actuator Control Circuit High Bank 2
s: Voltage on the rocker arm actuator B control line on bank 2 is above the expected window. Typical causes are a short to battery or an open solenoid coil.

t: Fuel Shutoff Valve B Control Circuit / Open
s: The controller cannot drive the secondary fuel shutoff valve. Engine cannot be commanded off through this path during a safety event.

t: Fuel Shutoff Valve B Control Circuit Low
s: Voltage on the secondary fuel shutoff valve control line is below the expected window. Usually a short to ground or a shorted solenoid coil.

t: Fuel Shutoff Valve B Control Circuit High
s: Voltage on the secondary fuel shutoff valve control line is above the expected window. Typical causes are a short to battery or an open solenoid coil.

t: Fuel Mode Indicator Lamp Control Circuit
s: The fuel mode indicator lamp circuit (showing whether a bi-fuel vehicle currently runs on gasoline or alternative fuel) is faulty. Driver may not know the active fuel.

t: Actuator Supply Voltage B Circuit / Open
s: The second power feed used to supply actuators (relays, solenoids, motor drivers) is open. All loads on this rail are powerless and report follow-on faults.

t: A Rocker Arm Actuator Position Sensor Circuit Bank 2
s: The position sensor that confirms the A rocker arm switching state on bank 2 returns no usable signal.

t: A Rocker Arm Actuator Position Sensor Circuit Range/Performance Bank 2
s: The position sensor reading does not transition cleanly between the two valve lift modes for actuator A on bank 2.

t: A Rocker Arm Actuator Position Sensor Circuit Low Bank 2
s: The position sensor signal for actuator A on bank 2 is below the lower diagnostic threshold. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: A Rocker Arm Actuator Position Sensor Circuit High Bank 2
s: The position sensor signal for actuator A on bank 2 is above the upper diagnostic threshold. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: A Rocker Arm Actuator Position Sensor Circuit Intermittent/Erratic Bank 2
s: The position sensor signal for actuator A on bank 2 jumps or drops out intermittently. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: A/C Refrigerant Distribution Valve A Control Circuit Driver Current/Temperature Too High
s: The driver stage for the A/C refrigerant distribution valve A is in current limit or over-temperature. Sustained overload from a partially shorted load or excessive switching frequency.

t: Actuator Supply Voltage B Circuit Low
s: The supply voltage to actuator group B (e.g. solenoids, valves driven by ECU) is below the calibrated threshold. The affected actuators may be slow or unable to switch.

t: Actuator Supply Voltage B Circuit High
s: The second actuator supply rail is above the expected voltage. Likely a charging system overvoltage or a short to a higher-voltage source.

t: Injection Pump Timing Offset
s: The high-pressure injection pump timing is offset from the expected reference. On distributor pumps with a timing solenoid, the actuator does not match the commanded position.

t: Injection Pump Timing Calibration Not Learned
s: The injection pump timing learning routine did not complete successfully. Without learned values, injection timing runs from default tables and combustion is suboptimal.

t: Injection Pump Fuel Calibration Not Learned
s: The injection pump fuel quantity calibration did not complete successfully. Without learned fuel calibration, AFR runs from default tables and emissions or smoke can rise.

t: Air Cleaner Inlet Control Circuit / Open
s: The controller cannot drive the air cleaner inlet control valve (warm-air or snorkel switching for cold-start emissions). Stuck in default position so cold-start fueling is sub-optimal.

t: Air Cleaner Inlet Control Circuit Low
s: Voltage on the air cleaner inlet valve control line is below the expected window. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Air Cleaner Inlet Control Circuit High
s: Voltage on the air cleaner inlet valve control line is above the expected window. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Coolant Degassing Valve Control Circuit / Open
s: The controller cannot drive the coolant degassing valve. Air pockets in the cooling system can form and cause local overheating.

t: Coolant Degassing Valve Control Circuit Low
s: Voltage on the coolant degassing valve control line is below the expected window. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: B Rocker Arm Actuator Position Sensor Circuit Bank 2
s: The position sensor that confirms the B rocker arm switching state on bank 2 returns no usable signal.

t: B Rocker Arm Actuator Position Sensor Circuit Range/Performance Bank 2
s: The position sensor reading does not transition cleanly between the two valve lift modes for actuator B on bank 2.

t: B Rocker Arm Actuator Position Sensor Circuit Low Bank 2
s: The position sensor signal for actuator B on bank 2 is below the lower diagnostic threshold. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: B Rocker Arm Actuator Position Sensor Circuit High Bank 2
s: The position sensor signal for actuator B on bank 2 is above the upper diagnostic threshold. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: B Rocker Arm Actuator Position Sensor Circuit Intermittent/Erratic Bank 2
s: The position sensor signal for actuator B on bank 2 jumps or drops out intermittently. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Control Module Internal Temperature Sensor A Circuit Intermittent/Erratic
s: The internal temperature sensor of the control module shows intermittent jumps or dropouts. Module thermal protection may falsely activate or fail to activate when needed.

t: Coolant Degassing Valve Control Circuit High
s: Voltage on the coolant degassing valve control line is above the expected window. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Engine Coolant Bypass Valve A Control Circuit / Open
s: The controller cannot drive the engine coolant bypass valve A. The valve usually routes coolant around the radiator during warm-up; without it warm-up takes longer and emissions during the warm-up phase rise.

t: Engine Coolant Bypass Valve A Control Circuit Low
s: Voltage on the coolant bypass valve A control line is below the expected window. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Engine Coolant Bypass Valve A Control Circuit High
s: Voltage on the coolant bypass valve A control line is above the expected window. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Actuator Supply Voltage C Circuit / Open
s: The third actuator supply rail is open. All loads on this rail are powerless and report follow-on faults.

t: Actuator Supply Voltage C Circuit Low
s: The third actuator supply rail is below the expected voltage. Battery weakness, blown fuse, or excessive current draw collapses the supply.

t: Actuator Supply Voltage C Circuit High
s: The third actuator supply rail is above the expected voltage. Likely a charging system overvoltage or a short to a higher-voltage source.

t: Fuel Supply Heater Control Circuit / Open
s: The controller cannot drive the diesel fuel filter or supply line heater. In cold weather diesel waxing can clog the filter.

t: Fuel Supply Heater Control Circuit Low
s: Voltage on the fuel supply heater control line is below the expected window. The sensor heater warms the element to operating temperature (~600 °C); without it the lambda sensor stays inactive in closed loop and the ECM cannot trim mixture.

t: Fuel Supply Heater Control Circuit High
s: Voltage on the fuel supply heater control line is above the expected window. The sensor heater warms the element to operating temperature (~600 °C); without it the lambda sensor stays inactive in closed loop and the ECM cannot trim mixture.

t: Fuel Injector Calibration Not Learned/Programmed
s: After injector replacement the engine controller did not receive the per-injector flow calibration codes (IMA, ISA, IMV). Cylinder balance and emissions are out of tolerance.

t: High Pressure Fuel Pump Calibration Not Learned/Programmed
s: After high-pressure pump replacement the engine controller did not receive the pump-specific calibration data. Rail pressure regulation runs from default values.

t: Cylinder 1 Injector Data Incompatible
s: The flow calibration data programmed for cylinder 1 injector does not match the format expected by the controller software. Calibration was applied for the wrong injector type or family.

t: Cylinder 2 Injector Data Incompatible
s: The flow calibration data programmed for cylinder 2 injector does not match the format expected by the controller software.

t: Cylinder 3 Injector Data Incompatible
s: The flow calibration data programmed for cylinder 3 injector does not match the format expected by the controller software.

t: Cylinder 4 Injector Data Incompatible
s: The flow calibration data programmed for cylinder 4 injector does not match the format expected by the controller software.

t: Cylinder 5 Injector Data Incompatible
s: The flow calibration data programmed for cylinder 5 injector does not match the format expected by the controller software.

t: Cylinder 6 Injector Data Incompatible
s: The flow calibration data programmed for cylinder 6 injector does not match the format expected by the controller software.

t: Cylinder 7 Injector Data Incompatible
s: The flow calibration data programmed for cylinder 7 injector does not match the format expected by the controller software.

t: Cylinder 8 Injector Data Incompatible
s: The flow calibration data programmed for cylinder 8 injector does not match the format expected by the controller software.

t: Cylinder 9 Injector Data Incompatible
s: The flow calibration data programmed for cylinder 9 injector does not match the format expected by the controller software.

t: Cylinder 10 Injector Data Incompatible
s: The flow calibration data programmed for cylinder 10 injector does not match the format expected by the controller software.

t: Injector Data Incompatible
s: Generic counterpart to the cylinder-specific incompatibility codes. The injector calibration data set as a whole does not match the controller software.

t: Exhaust Aftertreatment Fuel Injector A Circuit / Open
s: The 7th injector (HC dosing for DPF active regeneration) cannot be driven. Active regeneration cannot raise exhaust temperature, soot accumulates in the DPF.

t: Exhaust Aftertreatment Fuel Injector A Performance
s: The 7th injector responds to commands but does not deliver expected fuel during regeneration. Coking on the nozzle or worn pressure regulation reduces flow.

t: Exhaust Aftertreatment Fuel Injector A Circuit Low
s: Voltage on the 7th injector control line is below the expected window. Usually a short to ground or a shorted solenoid coil.

t: Exhaust Aftertreatment Fuel Injector A Circuit High
s: Voltage on the 7th injector control line is above the expected window. Typical causes are a short to battery or an open solenoid coil.

t: Exhaust Aftertreatment Glow Plug Control Circuit / Open
s: The control line for the aftertreatment glow plug (used on some diesels to ignite injected HC during DPF regeneration) is open. Active regeneration cannot start.

t: Exhaust Aftertreatment Glow Plug Control Performance
s: The aftertreatment glow plug control responds to commands but the resulting current draw is out of tolerance. Worn glow plug or high-resistance contact.

t: Exhaust Aftertreatment Glow Plug Control Circuit Low
s: Voltage on the aftertreatment glow plug control line is below the expected window. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Exhaust Aftertreatment Glow Plug Control Circuit High
s: Voltage on the aftertreatment glow plug control line is above the expected window. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Exhaust Aftertreatment Glow Plug Circuit / Open
s: The aftertreatment glow plug element itself is open. No current flows even with the relay closed.

t: Exhaust Aftertreatment Glow Plug Performance
s: The aftertreatment glow plug heats but exhaust temperature does not rise as expected. Combustion of injected HC may not start.

t: Exhaust Aftertreatment Glow Plug Circuit Low
s: The aftertreatment glow plug current is below expected. Either the element is partially open or supply is undersized.

t: Exhaust Aftertreatment Glow Plug Circuit High
s: The aftertreatment glow plug current is above expected. Internal short reduces resistance. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Engine Coolant Bypass Valve A Range/Performance
s: The coolant bypass valve A responds to commands but the resulting coolant temperature behaviour is not as expected. Possibly stuck partially open or contaminated bore.

t: Engine Coolant Bypass Valve A Position Sensor Circuit
s: The position sensor on the coolant bypass valve A returns no usable signal. Closed-loop control of bypass position is lost.

t: Engine Coolant Bypass Valve A Position Sensor Circuit Range/Performance
s: The bypass valve A position sensor reading does not correlate with commanded position. Sensor drift or worn linkage.

t: Engine Coolant Bypass Valve A Position Sensor Circuit Low
s: The bypass valve A position sensor signal is below the lower diagnostic threshold. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Engine Coolant Bypass Valve A Position Sensor Circuit High
s: The bypass valve A position sensor signal is above the upper diagnostic threshold. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Engine Coolant Bypass Valve A Position Sensor Circuit Intermittent/Erratic
s: The bypass valve A position sensor signal jumps or drops out intermittently. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Engine Coolant Bypass Valve A Position Sensor Stop/Minimum Stop Performance
s: During self-calibration the bypass valve A position sensor never reached the minimum mechanical stop. The position reference cannot be zeroed.

t: Engine Coolant Bypass Valve A Position Sensor Maximum Stop Performance
s: During self-calibration the bypass valve A position sensor never reached the maximum mechanical stop.

t: Engine Coolant Bypass Valve A Stuck/Open
s: The coolant bypass valve A is stuck in the open position. Coolant always bypasses the radiator, leading to overheating under load.

t: Engine Coolant Bypass Valve B Control Circuit / Open
s: The controller cannot drive the second coolant bypass valve. The associated cooling sub-loop cannot be regulated.

t: Engine Coolant Bypass Valve B Control Circuit Low
s: Voltage on the second coolant bypass valve control line is below the expected window. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Engine Coolant Bypass Valve B Control Circuit High
s: Voltage on the second coolant bypass valve control line is above the expected window. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Engine Coolant Bypass Valve B Stuck/Open
s: The second coolant bypass valve is stuck in the open position. Coolant always bypasses the second radiator path.

t: Engine Coolant Bypass Valve B Range/Performance
s: The second coolant bypass valve responds to commands but the resulting coolant temperature behaviour is not as expected.

t: Engine Coolant Bypass Valve A Stuck Closed
s: The coolant bypass valve A is stuck closed. Coolant cannot bypass the radiator during warm-up so warm-up takes longer and emissions in the warm-up phase rise.

t: Engine Coolant Bypass Valve B Stuck Closed
s: The second coolant bypass valve is stuck closed.

t: Fuel Shutoff Valve A Control Circuit Performance/Stuck Off
s: The first fuel shutoff valve is commanded on but does not open. Fuel cannot reach the engine via this path.

t: Fuel Shutoff Valve A Control Circuit Stuck On
s: The first fuel shutoff valve stays open when commanded closed. Fuel cannot be cut off via this path during a safety event.

t: Fuel Shutoff Valve B Control Circuit Performance/Stuck Off
s: The second fuel shutoff valve is commanded on but does not open.

t: Fuel Shutoff Valve B Control Circuit Stuck On
s: The second fuel shutoff valve stays open when commanded closed.

t: Engine Coolant Bypass Valve C Control Circuit / Open
s: The controller cannot drive the third coolant bypass valve.

t: Engine Coolant Bypass Valve C Control Circuit Low
s: Voltage on the third coolant bypass valve control line is below the expected window. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Engine Coolant Bypass Valve C Control Circuit High
s: Voltage on the third coolant bypass valve control line is above the expected window. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Engine Coolant Bypass Valve C Stuck/Open
s: The third coolant bypass valve is stuck open.

t: Engine Coolant Bypass Valve C Range/Performance
s: The third coolant bypass valve responds to commands but the resulting coolant temperature behaviour is not as expected.

t: Engine Coolant Bypass Valve C Stuck Closed
s: The third coolant bypass valve is stuck closed.

t: Engine Coolant Bypass Valve D Control Circuit / Open
s: The controller cannot drive the fourth coolant bypass valve.

t: Engine Coolant Bypass Valve D Control Circuit Low
s: Voltage on the fourth coolant bypass valve control line is below the expected window. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Engine Coolant Bypass Valve D Control Circuit High
s: Voltage on the fourth coolant bypass valve control line is above the expected window. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Engine Coolant Bypass Valve D Stuck/Open
s: The fourth coolant bypass valve is stuck open.

t: Engine Coolant Bypass Valve D Range/Performance
s: The fourth coolant bypass valve responds to commands but the resulting coolant temperature behaviour is not as expected.

t: Engine Coolant Bypass Valve D Stuck Closed
s: The fourth coolant bypass valve is stuck closed.

t: Internal Control Module Transmission Range Sensor Performance
s: The internal transmission range sensor inside the control module (used for redundancy on shift-by-wire systems) does not match the external sensor reading.

t: Internal Control Module Clutch Pedal Performance
s: The internal clutch pedal interpretation logic in the control module does not match the external clutch switch state. Manual transmission start interlock or stop-start enable can misbehave.

t: Exhaust Flow Control Valve A Control Circuit / Open
s: The controller cannot drive the exhaust flow control valve A (used for exhaust acoustics, EGR boost, or aftertreatment heating).

t: Exhaust Flow Control Valve A Control Circuit Low
s: Voltage on the exhaust flow control valve A control line is below the expected window. The exhaust control valve modulates exhaust backpressure or routing (EGR feed, sound, regen support); a malfunction shifts torque mapping and may trigger limp mode.

t: Exhaust Flow Control Valve A Control Circuit High
s: Voltage on the exhaust flow control valve A control line is above the expected window. The exhaust control valve modulates exhaust backpressure or routing (EGR feed, sound, regen support); a malfunction shifts torque mapping and may trigger limp mode.

t: Chassis Control Module 1 Requested MIL Illumination
s: A chassis control module (typically active suspension, AWD coupling, or steering controller) has signalled an emissions-relevant fault and requested the engine controller to switch on the MIL.

t: Chassis Control Module 2 Requested MIL Illumination
s: A second chassis control module has signalled an emissions-relevant fault and requested the engine controller to switch on the MIL.

t: Engine Coolant Pump Control Circuit / Open
s: The controller cannot drive the main electric coolant pump. Without flow the engine and aftertreatment will overheat under load.

t: Engine Coolant Pump Performance/Stuck Off
s: The main electric coolant pump is commanded on but does not run.

t: Engine Coolant Pump Control Circuit Low
s: Voltage on the engine coolant pump control line is below the expected window. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Engine Coolant Pump Control Circuit High
s: Voltage on the engine coolant pump control line is above the expected window. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Engine Coolant Pump Overspeed/Air In System
s: The coolant pump speed feedback is higher than the commanded value with no resistance from coolant. Indicates air in the cooling loop or low coolant level so the pump spins free.

t: Engine Coolant Pump Control Module System Voltage
s: The integrated controller of the smart coolant pump reports a system voltage out of operating range.

t: Engine Coolant Pump Control Module System Voltage Low
s: The smart coolant pump module reports the system voltage below operating range. Pump output is reduced or disabled to protect the module.

t: Engine Coolant Pump Control Module System Voltage High
s: The smart coolant pump module reports the system voltage above operating range. Pump output is reduced or disabled to protect the module.

t: Engine Coolant Pump Control Module Over Temperature
s: The smart coolant pump controller has reached over-temperature. Sustained high duty or insufficient cooling air flow.

t: Engine Coolant Pump Supply Voltage Circuit
s: The dedicated supply voltage line to the coolant pump module is faulty. Pump cannot operate without battery feed.

t: Engine Coolant Pump Supply Voltage Circuit Low
s: The dedicated supply voltage to the coolant pump module is below operating range. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Engine Coolant Pump Supply Voltage Circuit High
s: The dedicated supply voltage to the coolant pump module is above operating range. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Engine Coolant Pump Clutch Control Circuit / Open
s: The mechanical clutch on a belt-driven coolant pump (used to disengage the pump for fast warm-up) cannot be driven. Pump always engaged so warm-up takes longer.

t: Engine Coolant Pump Clutch Performance/Stuck Off
s: The coolant pump clutch is commanded on but does not engage. Coolant flow is missing under load, leading to overheating.

t: Engine Coolant Pump Clutch Stuck On
s: The coolant pump clutch remains engaged when commanded off. Pump runs continuously, slowing warm-up and increasing parasitic load.

t: Engine Coolant Pump Clutch Control Circuit Low
s: Voltage on the coolant pump clutch control line is below the expected window. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Engine Coolant Pump Clutch Control Circuit High
s: Voltage on the coolant pump clutch control line is above the expected window. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Engine Sound Control A Circuit / Open
s: The active engine sound generator A (Active Sound Design or AVAS — Acoustic Vehicle Alerting System for EV/hybrid) cannot be driven. EV pedestrian warning sound or sport-mode engine sound is missing.

t: Engine Sound Control A Circuit Low
s: Voltage on the sound generator A control line is below the expected window. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Engine Sound Control A Circuit High
s: Voltage on the sound generator A control line is above the expected window. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Engine Sound Control A Circuit Performance
s: The sound generator A circuit responds to commands but the resulting acoustic feedback is not as expected. Speaker damage or feedback path noise.

t: Sensor Power Supply D Circuit / Open
s: The 5 V supply rail D for sensors is open. All sensors fed from this rail report invalid readings.

t: Sensor Power Supply D Circuit Low
s: The 5 V supply rail D is below the expected level. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Sensor Power Supply D Circuit High
s: The 5 V supply rail D is above the expected level. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Fuel Mode Indicator Lamp Control Circuit Low
s: Voltage on the fuel mode indicator lamp control line is below the expected window. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Fuel Mode Indicator Lamp Control Circuit High
s: Voltage on the fuel mode indicator lamp control line is above the expected window. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Starter Relay B Circuit
s: The control circuit for the secondary starter relay (used on twin-starter heavy diesels or stop-start systems with a dedicated stop-start starter) returns no usable signal.

t: Starter Relay B Circuit Low
s: Voltage on the secondary starter relay control line is below the expected window. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Starter Relay B Circuit High
s: Voltage on the secondary starter relay control line is above the expected window. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Actuator Supply Voltage D Circuit / Open
s: The fourth actuator supply rail is open. All loads on this rail are powerless and report follow-on faults.

t: Actuator Supply Voltage D Circuit Low
s: The fourth actuator supply rail is below the expected voltage. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Actuator Supply Voltage D Circuit High
s: The fourth actuator supply rail is above the expected voltage. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Fuel Pump Control Module B
s: A general fault in the second fuel pump control module (smart pump controller, separate from the ECU). Internal self-test failed or communication with the ECU is degraded.

t: Alternative Fuel Control Module System Voltage
s: The alternative fuel control module reports a system voltage out of operating range. CNG, LPG, or hydrogen fuelling can be disabled until voltage is in spec.

t: Engine Sound Control B Circuit / Open
s: The second active sound generator (B) cannot be driven.

t: Engine Sound Control B Circuit Low
s: Voltage on the sound generator B control line is below the expected window. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Engine Sound Control B Circuit High
s: Voltage on the sound generator B control line is above the expected window. Wiring faults of this kind typically come from chafing, water ingress at connector seals, or pin push-back — a wiggle test on the harness while the code is active often confirms the location.

t: Engine Sound Control B Circuit Performance
s: The sound generator B circuit responds but the resulting acoustic feedback is not as expected.

t: Starter Relay A Stuck On
s: The first starter relay stays closed when commanded off. Starter motor remains engaged after the engine is running, which can destroy the starter pinion or ring gear.

t: Starter Relay A Stuck Off
s: The first starter relay does not close when commanded on. Starter motor cannot crank, the engine will not start.

t: Starter Relay B Stuck On
s: The second starter relay stays closed when commanded off. Starter motor B remains engaged after the engine is running, which can destroy the starter pinion or ring gear.

t: Starter Relay B Stuck Off
s: The second starter relay does not close when commanded on. The associated starter motor cannot crank.

t: Coolant Pump C Control Circuit / Open
s: The controller cannot drive the third electric coolant pump. Cooling demand of the connected loop (often hybrid power electronics or battery cooling) is not satisfied.

t: Coolant Pump C Control Circuit Low
s: Voltage on the third coolant pump control line is below the expected window. Usually a short to ground or a shorted motor winding.

t: Coolant Pump C Control Circuit High
s: Voltage on the third coolant pump control line is above the expected window. Typical causes are a short to battery or an open motor winding.

t: Coolant Pump C Supply Voltage Circuit
s: The dedicated supply voltage line to the third coolant pump module is faulty. Pump cannot operate without battery feed.

t: Coolant Pump C Stuck/Stalled
s: The third coolant pump is commanded on but the rotor does not turn. Sediment, frozen coolant, or a foreign object jams the impeller.

t: Coolant Pump C Control Module Overtemperature
s: The smart controller of the third coolant pump has reached over-temperature. Sustained high duty or insufficient cooling air flow around the pump electronics.

t: Coolant Pump C Overspeed/Air in System
s: The third coolant pump speed feedback is higher than commanded with no resistance from coolant. Indicates air in the cooling loop or low coolant level so the pump spins free.
